topics.title: Types of Decision Making Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTIC FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Decision Making Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of Operations Research, Management Science, and Heuristic Decision Making, a **Decision Making Environment** refers to the external and informational context within which a decision-maker (DM) operates. It is defined by the **degree of knowledge** the DM possesses about:

1. The complete set of **available alternatives** (acts / strategies / courses of action).
2. The **state of nature** (events / scenarios) that may occur.
3. The **payoff / outcome** associated with each alternative–event combination.

Based on these three parameters, KTU 2024 Scheme classifies decision environments into **four canonical types**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme - Core Classification**
> 1. **Decision Making under Certainty** (Deterministic Environment)
> 2. **Decision Making under Risk** (Probabilistic / Stochastic Environment)
> 3. **Decision Making under Uncertainty** (Ambiguous / Incomplete Information Environment)
> 4. **Decision Making under Conflict / Competition** (Adversarial / Game-Theoretic Environment)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **picnic tomorrow**.

| Scenario | Your Knowledge | Decision Type |
|---|---|---|
| Weather forecast says **100% sunny, no clouds** | You know the exact outcome | **Certainty** |
| Forecast says **70% sunny, 30% rain** with probability values | You know the likelihood of outcomes | **Risk** |
| You are **locked in a windowless room** with no forecast at all | You have no idea what may happen | **Uncertainty** |
| A **rival group** also wants the same picnic spot and may move before you | An intelligent opponent acts against you | **Conflict** |

This simple mental model immediately captures how decision environments are differentiated by the **information gradient** available to the decision-maker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Lock** (For KTU Board Examinations):
> - **Act / Alternative ($a_i$):** A course of action available to the DM.
> - **State of Nature ($s_j$):** A future event not under the DM's control.
> - **Payoff ($v_{ij}$):** Outcome (profit / utility / cost) of pairing act $a_i$ with state $s_j$.
> - **Payoff Matrix:** Tabular representation of all $v_{ij}$ values.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Type 1 — Decision Making under Certainty

**Operational Logic:**
- The DM has **complete, perfect, and reliable information** about the state of nature.
- Each alternative leads to a **single, deterministic payoff**.
- Only **one state of nature** is assumed to occur.

**Decision Heuristics Used:** Linear Programming (LP), Assignment, Transportation, Integer Programming, CPM/PERT (deterministic version).

**Example:** A company knows with certainty that producing 500 units will yield a profit of ₹50,000. There is no ambiguity.

### 2.2 Type 2 — Decision Making under Risk

**Operational Logic:**
- The DM does **not** know which state will occur, but can assign **objective or subjective probabilities** $P(s_j)$ to each state.
- The probabilities satisfy: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) = 1$ and $P(s_j) \ge 0$.
- The DM chooses the alternative that **optimizes expected payoff**.

**Decision Heuristics Used:** Expected Monetary Value (EMV), Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL), Decision Trees, Bayesian Analysis, Stochastic Programming.

### 2.3 Type 3 — Decision Making under Uncertainty

**Operational Logic:**
- The DM is **completely ignorant** about which state will occur.
- **No probabilities** can be assigned (objective or subjective).
- Selection is driven by the DM's **attitude toward ambiguity** (optimism, pessimism, regret, etc.).

**Decision Heuristics Used:**
- Maximax (Optimism Criterion)
- Maximin (Pessimism Criterion)
- Hurwicz $\alpha$-Criterion (Compromise Criterion)
- Minimax Regret (Savage Criterion)
- Laplace Criterion (Principle of Insufficient Reason)
- Equal Likelihood Criterion

### 2.4 Type 4 — Decision Making under Conflict (Competition)

**Operational Logic:**
- The outcome depends not only on the DM's action but also on the **intelligent, rational action of a competitor/opponent**.
- The DM's payoff is a **function of joint strategies**: $v = f(\text{DM's strategy}, \text{Opponent's strategy})$.
- The opponent's goal is typically **adversarial** (zero-sum) or **strategic** (non-zero-sum).

**Decision Heuristics Used:** Game Theory — Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium, Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium, Dominance, Maximin–Minimax Saddle Point.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — Memorize for KTU ESE.

| # | Decision Environment | Governing Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Certainty | $\text{Optimal Act} = \arg\max_i v_i$ | $v_i$ = deterministic payoff of act $a_i$ |
| 2 | Risk (EMV) | $EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} \cdot P(s_j)$ | $v_{ij}$ = payoff, $P(s_j)$ = probability |
| 3 | Risk (EOL) | $EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \cdot P(s_j)$ | $R_{ij}$ = regret / opportunity loss |
| 4 | Maximax | $a^* = \arg\max_i \left( \max_j v_{ij} \right)$ | Optimism criterion |
| 5 | Maximin | $a^* = \arg\max_i \left( \min_j v_{ij} \right)$ | Pessimism criterion |
| 6 | Hurwicz | $H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_j v_{ij}$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ = optimism index |
| 7 | Minimax Regret | $R_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$; $a^* = \arg\min_i \left( \max_j R_{ij} \right)$ | $R_{ij}$ = regret matrix element |
| 8 | Laplace | $L_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij}$ | Equal likelihood assumption |
| 9 | Saddle Point (Game) | $\max_i \min_j v_{ij} = \min_j \max_i v_{ij} = v_{s}$ | Pure strategy equilibrium |

> **Note on Notation:** In expressions involving magnitude or absolute values (e.g., $\lvert v_{ij} \rvert$), the vertical pipe symbol is replaced with `\lvert` or `\vert` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Certainty:** Production planning, deterministic inventory replenishment.
- **Risk:** Insurance underwriting, capital budgeting with market probability estimates, queuing systems.
- **Uncertainty:** Strategic R&D investment, new product launch in a novel market, defense technology procurement.
- **Conflict:** Auction bidding, telecommunications spectrum allocation, competitive bidding in construction tenders, cyber-security game models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Criterion

The EMV criterion underpins **Decision Making under Risk**. We derive it from first principles of probability-weighted aggregation.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
For each act $a_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$) and state of nature $s_j$ ($j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), there is an associated payoff $v_{ij}$.

**Step 2 — Probability Assignment:**
The DM assigns probability $P(s_j)$ to each state, with the axioms:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) = 1, \quad P(s_j) \ge 0 \; \forall j$$

**Step 3 — Expected Value Definition:**
The expected payoff of act $a_i$ is the probability-weighted sum of all payoffs across states:

$$EMV(a_i) = P(s_1) \cdot v_{i1} + P(s_2) \cdot v_{i2} + \cdots + P(s_n) \cdot v_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Compact Sigma Notation:**

$$EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot v_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Selection Rule:**

$$a^* = \arg\max_{i} \; EMV(a_i) \quad \text{(for profit-oriented DM)}$$

For cost minimization problems, the rule becomes $\arg\min_{i} EMV(a_i)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hurwicz $\alpha$-Criterion

The Hurwicz criterion blends optimism and pessimism into a single weighted index.

**Step 1 — Best Outcome of Each Act:**

$$\text{Best}_i = \max_{j} v_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Worst Outcome of Each Act:**

$$\text{Worst}_i = \min_{j} v_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Hurwicz Weighted Index:**

$$H_i = \alpha \cdot \text{Best}_i + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{Worst}_i$$

**Step 4 — Selection Rule:**

$$a^* = \arg\max_i H_i$$

**Boundary Interpretations:**

$$\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow H_i = \max_j v_{ij} \;\; \text{(Pure Optimism / Maximax)}$$

$$\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow H_i = \min_j v_{ij} \;\; \text{(Pure Pessimism / Maximin)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Minimax Regret (Savage) Criterion

This criterion operates on the principle of **minimizing the maximum opportunity loss** after the fact.

**Step 1 — Construct the Regret Matrix:**
For each column (state) $j$, identify the maximum payoff:

$$M_j = \max_{k} v_{kj}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Regret Entries:**

$$R_{ij} = M_j - v_{ij}$$

This represents the **forgone profit** had the DM chosen the best act in state $s_j$.

**Step 3 — Maximum Regret of Each Act:**

$$\text{MaxRegret}_i = \max_{j} R_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Selection Rule:**

$$a^* = \arg\min_i \; \text{MaxRegret}_i$$

### 3.4 Saddle Point Condition in Two-Person Zero-Sum Games (Conflict Environment)

**Step 1 — Row Minimums (Worst-Case for DM):**

$$r_i = \min_{j} v_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Column Maximums (Worst-Case for Opponent):**

$$c_j = \max_{i} v_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Maximin Value:**

$$\underline{v} = \max_i r_i = \max_i \min_j v_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Minimax Value:**

$$\overline{v} = \min_j c_j = \min_j \max_i v_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Saddle Point Test:**
A pure strategy solution exists if and only if:

$$\underline{v} = \overline{v} = v_s \quad \text{(Saddle Point)}$$

If strict inequality $\underline{v} < \overline{v}$ holds, then a **mixed strategy** solution must be sought.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparative Table** — Frequently tested in KTU 2-mark questions.

| Property | Certainty | Risk | Uncertainty | Conflict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| State of Nature | Known | Probabilistic | Unknown | Strategic Opponent |
| Probability Available? | N/A (single state) | Yes ($P(s_j)$) | No | Implicit via opponent |
| Solution Tool | LP, Assignment | EMV, Decision Tree | Maximax / Hurwicz / Savage | Game Theory |
| Information Required | Full & perfect | Partial + probabilistic | Minimal | Behavioral (rational opponent) |
| Decision Maker Posture | Optimizer | Risk-aware | Ambiguity-averse / seeking | Strategic |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Decision Environments

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Decision Making Environment"]:::root
    A --> B1["Certainty"]:::c1
    A --> B2["Risk"]:::c2
    A --> B3["Uncertainty"]:::c3
    A --> B4["Conflict"]:::c4

    B1 --> C1["Deterministic Payoffs"]
    B1 --> C2["Linear Programming"]
    B1 --> C3["Assignment Models"]

    B2 --> D1["Objective Probability"]
    B2 --> D2["Subjective Probability"]
    D1 --> D11["EMV Criterion"]
    D1 --> D12["EOL Criterion"]
    D2 --> D21["Bayesian Revision"]

    B3 --> E1["Maximax Optimism"]
    B3 --> E2["Maximin Pessimism"]
    B3 --> E3["Hurwicz Alpha"]
    B3 --> E4["Minimax Regret"]
    B3 --> E5["Laplace Equal"]

    B4 --> F1["Two Person Zero Sum"]
    B4 --> F2["Non Zero Sum"]
    F1 --> F11["Pure Strategy"]
    F1 --> F12["Mixed Strategy"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Engine Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In["Input: Problem Context"]:::inp --> Env["Environment Classifier Module"]:::cls
    Env -->|"Deterministic"| Det["Certainty Solver LP/Assignment"]:::s1
    Env -->|"Probabilistic"| Rsk["Risk Solver EMV/EOL/Decision Tree"]:::s2
    Env -->|"No Probability Data"| Uns["Uncertainty Solver Maximin/Minimax Regret/Hurwicz"]:::s3
    Env -->|"Strategic Opponent Present"| Cnf["Conflict Solver Game Theory Pure/Mixed"]:::s4

    Det --> Out["Recommended Act a* and Payoff v*"]:::out

    Uns --> Out
    Cnf --> Out

    Out --> Feas["Feasibility Validation"]:::fes
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between decision making under **risk** and decision making under **uncertainty**. Give one real-life example for each.

**Model Answer (Board-Standard, 3 Marks):**

| Basis | Decision Making under Risk | Decision Making under Uncertainty |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of States | States are not known, but **probabilities are assigned** | States are not known and **no probabilities** can be assigned |
| Information Level | **Partial** quantitative information | **Minimal / no** quantitative information |
| Solution Criterion | EMV, EOL, Decision Tree | Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Minimax Regret, Laplace |
| Real-Life Example | Insurance premium calculation (actuarial tables give mortality probabilities) | Strategic investment in a brand-new technology with no historical data |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
What is meant by **decision making under conflict**? Name the heuristic used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Decision making under conflict is a situation in which the **outcome depends on the actions of an intelligent, rational opponent** as well as the decision-maker's own action. The decision-maker cannot optimize in isolation; instead, the best act is conditional on the opponent's strategy.

**Heuristic Used:** **Game Theory** — specifically, the **Saddle Point test** (for pure strategy) or **Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium** (when no saddle point exists). Examples include competitive bidding, military strategy, and zero-sum pricing wars.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Decision Making under Uncertainty Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain any **four criteria** for decision making under uncertainty with suitable formulas. For each criterion, state the underlying psychological assumption of the decision-maker.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company must choose between three products A, B, C to launch in a new market. The payoff table (in ₹ lakhs) under four possible market states is given below:

| Product \ State | $S_1$ (Boom) | $S_2$ (Stable) | $S_3$ (Decline) | $S_4$ (Crash) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 200 | 150 | -50 | -100 |
| B | 250 | 100 | -25 | -150 |
| C | 175 | 175 | 50 | -75 |

Apply **Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$), and Minimax Regret** criteria. Recommend the best product.

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**1. Maximax (Optimism Criterion):**

$$\text{Choose } a^* = \arg\max_i \left[ \max_j v_{ij} \right]$$

*Psychological Assumption:* DM is a **bold optimist** who believes the best state will occur.

**2. Maximin (Pessimism Criterion):**

$$a^* = \arg\max_i \left[ \min_j v_{ij} \right]$$

*Assumption:* DM is a **pessimist** who safeguards against the worst-case state.

**3. Hurwicz $\alpha$-Criterion (Compromise):**

$$H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_j v_{ij}; \quad a^* = \arg\max_i H_i$$

*Assumption:* DM has a **mixed attitude** between optimism and pessimism, parameterized by $\alpha \in [0,1]$.

**4. Minimax Regret (Savage Criterion):**

$$R_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}; \quad a^* = \arg\min_i \left[ \max_j R_{ij} \right]$$

*Assumption:* DM aims to **minimize post-decision regret**, irrespective of which state occurs.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Maximax:**
- Best of A: $\max(200, 150, -50, -100) = 200$
- Best of B: $\max(250, 100, -25, -150) = 250$
- Best of C: $\max(175, 175, 50, -75) = 175$

Decision: $\max(200, 250, 175) = \mathbf{250 \Rightarrow \text{Choose Product B}}$

**Step 2 — Maximin:**
- Worst of A: $\min(200, 150, -50, -100) = -100$
- Worst of B: $\min(250, 100, -25, -150) = -150$
- Worst of C: $\min(175, 175, 50, -75) = -75$

Decision: $\max(-100, -150, -75) = \mathbf{-75 \Rightarrow \text{Choose Product C}}$

**Step 3 — Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$):**

$$H_A = 0.6(200) + 0.4(-100) = 120 - 40 = 80$$

$$H_B = 0.6(250) + 0.4(-150) = 150 - 60 = 90$$

$$H_C = 0.6(175) + 0.4(-75) = 105 - 30 = 75$$

Decision: $\max(80, 90, 75) = \mathbf{90 \Rightarrow \text{Choose Product B}}$

**Step 4 — Minimax Regret:**

Construct regret matrix. Column maxes: $S_1 = 250, S_2 = 175, S_3 = 50, S_4 = -75$.

| Product | $R_{S1}$ | $R_{S2}$ | $R_{S3}$ | $R_{S4}$ | Max Regret |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 250-200=50 | 175-150=25 | 50-(-50)=100 | -75-(-100)=25 | **100** |
| B | 250-250=0 | 175-100=75 | 50-(-25)=75 | -75-(-150)=75 | **75** |
| C | 250-175=75 | 175-175=0 | 50-50=0 | -75-(-75)=0 | **75** |

Decision: $\min(100, 75, 75) = \mathbf{75 \Rightarrow \text{Choose Product B or C (tie)}}$

**Final Recommendation:**
Three of four criteria point to **Product B** as optimal. Since it dominates in Maximax and Hurwicz and ties in Minimax Regret, the board-recommended choice is **Product B**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Decision Making under Risk + Conflict Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** criterion and the **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** criterion for decision making under risk. Show that $\text{EMV}^* + \text{EOL}^* = \text{constant}$, where the constant equals the payoff of the best act under the best state.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm must decide whether to **Enter (E)** or **Not Enter (N)** a new market. A rival firm can simultaneously choose to **Enter (e)** or **Stay Out (s)**. The payoff matrix (Firm's profit, ₹ lakhs) is:

| Firm \ Rival | e | s |
|---|---|---|
| E | 50 | 200 |
| N | 0 | 0 |

Identify the **type of decision environment**, test for a **saddle point**, and determine the optimal strategies.

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**EMV Criterion:**

$$EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot v_{ij}, \quad a^* = \arg\max_i EMV(a_i)$$

**EOL Criterion:**
First, construct regret matrix $R_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$.

$$EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot R_{ij}, \quad a^* = \arg\min_i EOL(a_i)$$

**Proof of Identity:**
Let $M_j = \max_k v_{kj}$ denote the column maximum. Then:

$$EMV(a_i) + EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot v_{ij} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot (M_j - v_{ij})$$

$$\Rightarrow EMV(a_i) + EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot M_j$$

The right-hand side is **independent of $i$**, hence a constant $C$. Therefore:

$$EMV^* + EOL^* = C = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(s_j) \cdot M_j$$

This is the payoff of the **best act under the most favorable state**, weighted by state probabilities — a **board-favorite 7-mark derivation**.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Type of Environment:** **Decision Making under Conflict (Two-Person Non-Zero-Sum Game)** — because the payoffs are not negatives of each other (not strictly zero-sum).

**Saddle Point Test:**

Row minimums:
- $r_E = \min(50, 200) = 50$
- $r_N = \min(0, 0) = 0$

Column maximums:
- $c_e = \max(50, 0) = 50$
- $c_s = \max(200, 0) = 200$

Compute:

$$\underline{v} = \max(r_E, r_N) = \max(50, 0) = 50$$

$$\overline{v} = \min(c_e, c_s) = \min(50, 200) = 50$$

Since $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = 50$, a **saddle point exists**.

**Optimal Strategies:**
- Firm should choose **Enter (E)**.
- Rival should choose **Enter (e)**.
- **Equilibrium Payoff:** ₹50 lakhs.

This is a classic **Prisoner's Dilemma**-type setup — both firms would be better off staying out (joint payoff 200 vs. 50), but rational self-interest leads both to enter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "Risk" with "Uncertainty":** If the question states "no probabilities available," it is **Uncertainty**, not Risk. Applying EMV here is a **guaranteed 0** for that sub-part.
> 2. **Hurwicz Miscomputation:** Many students compute $H_i = \alpha \cdot \max + \alpha \cdot \min$ instead of $\alpha \cdot \max + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min$. The $(1-\alpha)$ on the pessimistic term is **frequently missed** — deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Regret Matrix Sign Errors:** Regret is $M_j - v_{ij}$, not $v_{ij} - M_j$. Always **subtract the cell from the column maximum**.
> 4. **Saddle Point Equality:** Forgetting to write the equality $\underline{v} = \overline{v}$ before concluding "saddle point exists" costs a mark.
> 5. **Decision Making under Certainty vs. LP:** Do not list LP as a separate environment — LP is a **solution heuristic for the Certainty environment**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four canonical environments:** Certainty, Risk, Uncertainty, Conflict.
- **Information gradient** is the master differentiator: full → probabilistic → none → opponent-driven.
- **Certainty** = deterministic payoffs → LP/Assignment family.
- **Risk** = probabilities available → EMV/EOL/Decision Trees/Bayesian.
- **Uncertainty** = no probabilities → five classic criteria (Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Minimax Regret, Laplace).
- **Conflict** = opponent's rational choice → Game Theory (Saddle Point / Nash Equilibrium).
- **Hurwicz formula:** $H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_j v_{ij}$ — boundary cases $\alpha = 1$ (pure optimism), $\alpha = 0$ (pure pessimism).
- **Regret matrix:** $R_{ij} = M_j - v_{ij}$ where $M_j$ is column-wise maximum.
- **EMV–EOL complementarity:** $EMV^* + EOL^* = C$ (constant).
- **Saddle Point Test:** $\underline{v} = \overline{v}$ → pure strategy; else mixed strategy.
- **Payoff matrix size:** For KTU, expect $2 \times 2$, $2 \times 3$, or $3 \times 3$ matrices.
- **Examiner Traps:** Misclassification of environment, wrong Hurwicz weighting, missing boundary conditions, no justification for criterion choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Mnemonic — "C-R-U-C":**
> **C**ertainty (Concrete data) →
> **R**isk (Rates/Probabilities) →
> **U**ncertainty (Unknown likelihoods) →
> **C**onflict (Competitor moves).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision Making Under Uncertainty and Under Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Decision Making Under Uncertainty and Under Risk

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Analysis (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Decision analysis is a systematic, quantitative, and visual approach to making strategic business or engineering decisions under conditions of uncertainty. It provides a framework for identifying and analyzing all possible alternatives, the uncertainties (states of nature), and the payoffs/consequences associated with each combination.

In any decision problem, we identify three fundamental components:

1. **Decision Alternatives ($A_i$)**: The set of mutually exclusive actions or choices available to the decision-maker (DM). For $n$ alternatives, $A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$.

2. **States of Nature ($S_j$)**: The future conditions or events over which the DM has no direct control. There are $m$ possible states, $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$.

3. **Payoff Matrix ($V_{ij}$)**: A tabular representation of the outcome (profit, cost, utility, time, etc.) obtained by choosing alternative $A_i$ when state $S_j$ occurs.

The classification of the decision environment depends critically on the **knowledge of the probabilities** of the states of nature:

| Environment Type | Knowledge of State Probabilities | Information Level |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Certainty** | $P(S_j) = 1$ for one state | Complete |
| **Risk** | $P(S_j)$ are **known/estimated** | Partial but quantifiable |
| **Uncertainty** | $P(S_j)$ are **completely unknown** | Incomplete |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Farmer and the Weather** 🌾
> Imagine a farmer in Kerala deciding whether to plant **Rice (Paddy)** or **Tapioca (Cassava)** for the upcoming monsoon. The farmer cannot control the rainfall.
> - **Certainty**: The farmer has a magical weather crystal ball — rain is 100% guaranteed. The choice is trivial.
> - **Risk**: The farmer checks 30 years of IMD (India Meteorological Department) data and concludes there is a **70% chance of normal monsoon** and **30% chance of drought**. Here, probabilities are known, but outcomes are not.
> - **Uncertainty**: The farmer is planting a brand-new hybrid crop where **no historical rainfall data exists**. The probabilities of rainfall states are completely unknown. The DM must rely on **subjective criteria** (pessimism, optimism, regret aversion) rather than statistical probability.

---

### 1.3 Types of Decision Environments

#### A. Decision Making Under Uncertainty
When the decision-maker has **no basis for assigning probabilities** to the states of nature. The DM relies on **subjective criteria** based on personal attitude (pessimism, optimism, regret). Common criteria include:
- Maximax (Optimistic)
- Maximin (Pessimistic)
- Hurwicz (Compromise)
- Laplace (Principle of Insufficient Reason)
- Minimax Regret (Savage Criterion)

#### B. Decision Making Under Risk
When the decision-maker can **assign probabilities** to each state of nature (either from historical data, expert judgment, or statistical inference). Common criteria include:
- Expected Monetary Value (EMV) / Expected Value (EV)
- Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) / Expected Regret
- Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)
- Decision Tree Analysis

### 1.4 The Payoff Matrix (Decision Table)

A payoff matrix is constructed with alternatives as rows and states of nature as columns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alternatives} \quad \backslash \quad \text{States} \quad &\rightarrow \quad S_1 \quad S_2 \quad \cdots \quad S_m \\
A_1 \quad &\rightarrow \quad V_{11} \quad V_{12} \quad \cdots \quad V_{1m} \\
A_2 \quad &\rightarrow \quad V_{21} \quad V_{22} \quad \cdots \quad V_{2m} \\
\vdots \quad &\rightarrow \quad \vdots \quad \vdots \quad \ddots \quad \vdots \\
A_n \quad &\rightarrow \quad V_{n1} \quad V_{n2} \quad \cdots \quad V_{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Payoff Convention**:
> - If the entries represent **profits, revenue, or desirability** → use the **Maximization** framework.
> - If the entries represent **costs, losses, or penalties** → use the **Minimization** framework (e.g., Minimin, Minimin, Minimax Regret on cost).

**Engineering Example**: A civil engineer choosing between three bridge designs (Steel, Concrete, Composite) under three possible load conditions (Light, Medium, Heavy Traffic) where the cost of construction is the payoff.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decision Making Under Uncertainty — The Five Classical Criteria

### Criterion 1: Maximax (Optimistic Criterion)
**Philosophy**: "The best will happen. I will shoot for the stars."
**Logic**: For each alternative $A_i$, find the **maximum payoff** in its row, then select the alternative with the **largest of these maximums**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^*_{Maximax} &= \arg\max_{i} \left\{ \max_{j} V_{ij} \right\} \\
\text{Value} &= \max_{i} \left\{ \max_{j} V_{ij} \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Best for**: Entrepreneurs, aggressive bidders, low-stake reversible decisions.
- **Worst feature**: Ignores all bad outcomes; can lead to catastrophic loss.

### Criterion 2: Maximin (Pessimistic Criterion / Wald's Criterion)
**Philosophy**: "The worst will happen. I will play safe."
**Logic**: For each alternative $A_i$, find the **minimum payoff** in its row, then select the alternative with the **largest of these minimums** (i.e., maximize the worst case).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^*_{Maximin} &= \arg\max_{i} \left\{ \min_{j} V_{ij} \right\} \\
\text{Value} &= \max_{i} \left\{ \min_{j} V_{ij} \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Best for**: Safety-critical engineering, military, insurance, risk-averse DM.
- **Worst feature**: Overly conservative; ignores potential for large gains.

### Criterion 3: Hurwicz Criterion (Alpha Criterion / Optimism-Pessimism Index)
**Philosophy**: "A weighted balance between hope and fear."
**Logic**: Combines the best and worst payoffs of each alternative using a coefficient of optimism $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.
- $\alpha = 1$ → Pure Optimist (becomes Maximax)
- $\alpha = 0$ → Pure Pessimist (becomes Maximin)
- The DM's attitude is quantified by $\alpha$.

The **Hurwicz value** for alternative $A_i$ is computed as:

$$
H_i = \alpha \cdot \left( \max_{j} V_{ij} \right) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \left( \min_{j} V_{ij} \right)
$$

Then the optimal alternative is:

$$
A^*_{Hurwicz} = \arg\max_{i} H_i
$$

- **Best for**: When DM has a quantifiable degree of optimism.
- **Worst feature**: Choosing $\alpha$ is subjective; the result is highly sensitive to $\alpha$.

### Criterion 4: Laplace Criterion (Principle of Insufficient Reason)
**Philosophy**: "I have no reason to prefer one state over another, so treat them equally."
**Logic**: If the states of nature are equally likely (i.e., $P(S_j) = 1/m$ for all $j$), then the expected value of each alternative is simply its **arithmetic mean**.

$$
L_i = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_{ij}
$$

The optimal alternative is:

$$
A^*_{Laplace} = \arg\max_{i} L_i
$$

- **Best for**: When states are truly indistinguishable, or as a neutral baseline.
- **Worst feature**: Often unrealistic; nature rarely treats all outcomes as equally probable.

### Criterion 5: Minimax Regret Criterion (Savage Criterion)
**Philosophy**: "I want to minimize my post-decision sorrow."
**Logic**: Construct a **Regret Matrix (Opportunity Loss Table)**. For each state $S_j$, find the best payoff $\max_i V_{ij}$. The regret for cell $(i, j)$ is:

$$
R_{ij} = \max_{i} V_{ij} - V_{ij}
$$

(For cost matrices: $R_{ij} = V_{ij} - \min_i V_{ij}$)

Then for each alternative, find its **maximum regret**, and select the alternative with the **minimum of these maximum regrets**:

$$
A^*_{Savage} = \arg\min_{i} \left\{ \max_{j} R_{ij} \right\}
$$

- **Best for**: When post-decision regret carries a high psychological or financial cost.
- **Worst feature**: Computationally heavier; results can be very different from Maximin/EMV.

---

## 2.2 Decision Making Under Risk — Probabilistic Criteria

When state probabilities $P(S_j) = p_j$ are known (with $\sum p_j = 1$):

### Criterion 6: Expected Monetary Value (EMV) / Expected Value (EV)
**Logic**: Compute the probability-weighted average payoff of each alternative.

$$
EMV_i = E[V_i] = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j \cdot V_{ij}
$$

$$
A^*_{EMV} = \arg\max_{i} EMV_i
$$

For **cost problems**, this becomes **Expected Cost**, and we select $\arg\min$.

### Criterion 7: Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) / Expected Regret
**Logic**: Compute the expected value of the regret matrix.

$$
EOL_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j \cdot R_{ij}
$$

$$
A^*_{EOL} = \arg\min_{i} EOL_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of Decision Theory**
> The alternative that **maximizes EMV** is **identical** to the alternative that **minimizes EOL**. This is a universally tested result in KTU exams.
> $\min_i EOL_i = \max_i EMV_i - \max_j \left( \sum_i V_{ij} p_j \right)$ — they lead to the same choice.

### Criterion 8: Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)
**Philosophy**: "What is the most I should pay to know the future with certainty?"
**Logic**: 
- $EMV^*$ = Maximum EMV (best decision without perfect information)
- $EPPL$ = Expected Payoff with Perfect Information = $\sum_j p_j \cdot \max_i V_{ij}$ (for each state, pick the best alternative)

$$
EVPI = EPPL - EMV^*
$$

EVPI is the **upper bound** on the price one should pay for a perfect forecast (e.g., a market research study, a sophisticated sensor system).

### Criterion 9: Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)
EVSI is the value of obtaining *imperfect* (sample) information before making the final decision. Used in Bayesian decision theory.

$$
EVSI = EMV_{with\_sample} - EMV^*_{without\_sample}
$$

Efficiency of information: $\eta = \dfrac{EVSI}{EVPI} \times 100\%$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Criterion | Formula | Decision Rule | Environment |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Maximax** | $V_{MM} = \max_i \max_j V_{ij}$ | Select $A_i$ giving $V_{MM}$ | Uncertainty |
| 2 | **Maximin** | $V_{mM} = \max_i \min_j V_{ij}$ | Select $A_i$ giving $V_{mM}$ | Uncertainty |
| 3 | **Hurwicz** | $H_i = \alpha \max_j V_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \min_j V_{ij}$ | $A^* = \arg\max_i H_i$ | Uncertainty |
| 4 | **Laplace** | $L_i = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_{ij}$ | $A^* = \arg\max_i L_i$ | Uncertainty |
| 5 | **Minimax Regret** | $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$ | $A^* = \arg\min_i \max_j R_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 6 | **EMV (EV)** | $EMV_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j \cdot V_{ij}$ | $A^* = \arg\max_i EMV_i$ | Risk |
| 7 | **EOL** | $EOL_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j \cdot R_{ij}$ | $A^* = \arg\min_i EOL_i$ | Risk |
| 8 | **EVPI** | $EVPI = \sum_j p_j \max_i V_{ij} - \max_i EMV_i$ | Compare to info cost | Risk |

**Boundary Conditions & Special Notes**:
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$: $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ Maximax; $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ Maximin
- $p_j \in [0, 1]$, $\sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j = 1$
- $R_{ij} \geq 0$ always (regret is non-negative)
- $\min_i EOL_i = EVPI_{equivalent}$ relationship holds: $\max_i EMV_i + \min_i EOL_i = EPPL$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Applications**:
> - **Civil Engineering**: Selecting a foundation type (Pile, Raft, Isolated) under uncertain soil conditions.
> - **Mechanical Engineering**: Choosing a manufacturing process under uncertain demand for cost minimization.
> - **Electrical Engineering**: Optimal power generation mix under uncertain renewable energy availability.
> - **Project Management**: Make-or-Buy decisions under uncertain market prices.
> - **Finance**: Portfolio selection under uncertain asset returns.
> - **Strategic Management**: Market entry decisions under competitor response uncertainty.

# 3. Step-by-Step Worked Examples & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Solved Example 1: Decision Under Uncertainty (All Five Criteria)

**Problem Statement**: A construction company in Kochi must decide on the scale of a new commercial complex. Three alternatives ($A_1, A_2, A_3$) and four possible economic states ($S_1$: Boom, $S_2$: Stable, $S_3$: Mild Recession, $S_4$: Severe Recession) are considered. The payoffs (profits in ₹ Lakhs) are:

| Alternative $\backslash$ State | $S_1$ (Boom) | $S_2$ (Stable) | $S_3$ (Mild) | $S_4$ (Severe) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $A_1$: Small Complex | 50 | 40 | 20 | 10 |
| $A_2$: Medium Complex | 80 | 50 | 30 | -10 |
| $A_3$: Large Complex | 120 | 60 | 25 | -30 |

Assume the DM is moderately optimistic with $\alpha = 0.6$.

---

### Step 1: Maximax Criterion

Find maximum of each row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_j V(A_1, S_j) &= \max\{50, 40, 20, 10\} = 50 \\
\max_j V(A_2, S_j) &= \max\{80, 50, 30, -10\} = 80 \\
\max_j V(A_3, S_j) &= \max\{120, 60, 25, -30\} = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the alternative with the maximum of these:

$$
\text{Maximax Value} = \max\{50, 80, 120\} = 120 \Rightarrow A_3 \text{ (Large Complex)}
$$

---

### Step 2: Maximin Criterion

Find minimum of each row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_j V(A_1, S_j) &= \min\{50, 40, 20, 10\} = 10 \\
\min_j V(A_2, S_j) &= \min\{80, 50, 30, -10\} = -10 \\
\min_j V(A_3, S_j) &= \min\{120, 60, 25, -30\} = -30
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the alternative with the maximum of these minima:

$$
\text{Maximin Value} = \max\{10, -10, -30\} = 10 \Rightarrow A_1 \text{ (Small Complex)}
$$

---

### Step 3: Hurwicz Criterion ($\alpha = 0.6$)

For each alternative, compute $H_i = \alpha \cdot \max + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 0.6 \times 50 + 0.4 \times 10 = 30 + 4 = 34 \\
H_2 &= 0.6 \times 80 + 0.4 \times (-10) = 48 - 4 = 44 \\
H_3 &= 0.6 \times 120 + 0.4 \times (-30) = 72 - 12 = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the alternative with the maximum Hurwicz value:

$$
\text{Hurwicz} = \max\{34, 44, 60\} = 60 \Rightarrow A_3 \text{ (Large Complex)}
$$

---

### Step 4: Laplace Criterion (Equal Probabilities $p_j = 1/4$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \frac{1}{4}(50 + 40 + 20 + 10) = \frac{120}{4} = 30 \\
L_2 &= \frac{1}{4}(80 + 50 + 30 - 10) = \frac{150}{4} = 37.5 \\
L_3 &= \frac{1}{4}(120 + 60 + 25 - 30) = \frac{175}{4} = 43.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the alternative with the maximum Laplace value:

$$
\text{Laplace} = \max\{30, 37.5, 43.75\} = 43.75 \Rightarrow A_3 \text{ (Large Complex)}
$$

---

### Step 5: Minimax Regret (Savage) Criterion

**Step 5a**: Find the column maximum (best in each state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_i V(A_i, S_1) &= \max\{50, 80, 120\} = 120 \\
\max_i V(A_i, S_2) &= \max\{40, 50, 60\} = 60 \\
\max_i V(A_i, S_3) &= \max\{20, 30, 25\} = 30 \\
\max_i V(A_i, S_4) &= \max\{10, -10, -30\} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5b**: Construct the regret matrix: $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{11} &= 120 - 50 = 70 \quad &R_{12} &= 60 - 40 = 20 \\
R_{13} &= 30 - 20 = 10 \quad &R_{14} &= 10 - 10 = 0 \\
R_{21} &= 120 - 80 = 40 \quad &R_{22} &= 60 - 50 = 10 \\
R_{23} &= 30 - 30 = 0 \quad &R_{24} &= 10 - (-10) = 20 \\
R_{31} &= 120 - 120 = 0 \quad &R_{32} &= 60 - 60 = 0 \\
R_{33} &= 30 - 25 = 5 \quad &R_{34} &= 10 - (-30) = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | $\max_j R_{ij}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $A_1$ | 70 | 20 | 10 | 0 | **70** |
| $A_2$ | 40 | 10 | 0 | 20 | **40** |
| $A_3$ | 0 | 0 | 5 | 40 | **40** |

Select the alternative with the minimum of these maximum regrets:

$$
\text{Savage} = \min\{70, 40, 40\} = 40 \Rightarrow A_2 \text{ or } A_3 \text{ (tie)}
$$

In case of tie, either may be selected. Convention: pick the one with the lower index or higher EMV.

---

## 3.2 Solved Example 2: Decision Under Risk (EMV, EOL, EVPI)

**Problem Statement**: Using the same payoff matrix as above, suppose the chief economist estimates the following probabilities based on macroeconomic indicators:

$$
P(S_1) = 0.20, \quad P(S_2) = 0.45, \quad P(S_3) = 0.25, \quad P(S_4) = 0.10
$$

Note that $0.20 + 0.45 + 0.25 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓

---

### Step 1: Compute EMV for each alternative

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_1) &= 0.20(50) + 0.45(40) + 0.25(20) + 0.10(10) \\
&= 10.0 + 18.0 + 5.0 + 1.0 = 34.0 \\
EMV(A_2) &= 0.20(80) + 0.45(50) + 0.25(30) + 0.10(-10) \\
&= 16.0 + 22.5 + 7.5 - 1.0 = 45.0 \\
EMV(A_3) &= 0.20(120) + 0.45(60) + 0.25(25) + 0.10(-30) \\
&= 24.0 + 27.0 + 6.25 - 3.0 = 54.25
\end{aligned}
$$

| Alternative | EMV (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $A_1$ | 34.00 |
| $A_2$ | 45.00 |
| $A_3$ | **54.25** ✓ |

**Decision**: Select $A_3$ (Large Complex) with $EMV^* = 54.25$ Lakhs.

---

### Step 2: Compute EOL for each alternative (Verification)

Using the regret matrix from Example 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_1) &= 0.20(70) + 0.45(20) + 0.25(10) + 0.10(0) \\
&= 14.0 + 9.0 + 2.5 + 0 = 25.5 \\
EOL(A_2) &= 0.20(40) + 0.45(10) + 0.25(0) + 0.10(20) \\
&= 8.0 + 4.5 + 0 + 2.0 = 14.5 \\
EOL(A_3) &= 0.20(0) + 0.45(0) + 0.25(5) + 0.10(40) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 1.25 + 4.0 = 5.25
\end{aligned}
$$

| Alternative | EOL (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $A_1$ | 25.50 |
| $A_2$ | 14.50 |
| $A_3$ | **5.25** ✓ |

**Decision**: Select $A_3$ with minimum EOL = 5.25 Lakhs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Equivalence Theorem**:
> $\max_i EMV_i + \min_i EOL_i = EPPL$?
> $54.25 + 5.25 = 59.50$
> 
> Now compute EPPL:
> $EPPL = \sum_j p_j \max_i V_{ij} = 0.20(120) + 0.45(60) + 0.25(30) + 0.10(10) = 24 + 27 + 7.5 + 1.0 = 59.50$ ✓
> 
> **Theorem verified!**

---

### Step 3: Compute Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPPL &= 0.20(120) + 0.45(60) + 0.25(30) + 0.10(10) \\
&= 24.0 + 27.0 + 7.5 + 1.0 = 59.50 \\
EMV^* &= 54.25 \\
EVPI &= EPPL - EMV^* = 59.50 - 54.25 = 5.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The construction company should pay **at most ₹ 5.25 Lakhs** to obtain perfectly reliable economic forecasts. Any market research report priced above this is not worth purchasing.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Summary Table (Both Examples)

| Criterion | Recommended Alternative | Value (₹ Lakhs) | Type of DM |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Maximax | $A_3$ | 120 | Optimist |
| Maximin | $A_1$ | 10 (worst-case) | Pessimist |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$) | $A_3$ | 60 | Balanced-Optimist |
| Laplace | $A_3$ | 43.75 | Neutral |
| Minimax Regret | $A_2$ or $A_3$ | 40 | Regret-Averse |
| **EMV (Risk)** | **$A_3$** | **54.25** | **Rational** |
| **EOL (Risk)** | **$A_3$** | **5.25** | **Rational** |
| **EVPI** | — | **5.25** | Info Value |

```mermaid
    nodeUncert --> nodeUnc3
    nodeUncert --> nodeUnc4
    nodeUncert --> nodeUnc5
    nodeRisk2 --> nodeRk1
    nodeRisk2 --> nodeRk2
    nodeRisk2 --> nodeRk3
    nodeRisk2 --> nodeRk4
    nodeRisk2 --> nodeRk5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between decision-making under uncertainty and decision-making under risk. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer**:
- **Decision Making Under Uncertainty**: The decision-maker has **no information** (or basis) to assign probabilities to the various states of nature. The DM must rely on subjective criteria such as Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, or Minimax Regret.
- **Decision Making Under Risk**: The decision-maker can **assign probabilities** (objective or subjective) to each state of nature, often derived from historical data, statistical inference, or expert judgment. Probabilistic criteria like EMV, EOL, and EVPI are used.
- **Example (Uncertainty)**: An entrepreneur launching a radically new product with no market data, choosing between three pricing strategies.
- **Example (Risk)**: A KSEB engineer choosing between thermal, hydro, and solar power expansion plans, using historical rainfall and demand data to assign probabilities.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**State and explain the Minimax Regret criterion. How is the regret matrix constructed?**

**Model Answer**:
- **Minimax Regret Criterion (Savage Criterion)**: It is a decision-making rule under uncertainty where the DM chooses the alternative that minimizes the maximum possible regret (opportunity loss) after the state of nature is revealed.
- **Construction of the Regret Matrix**:
  1. For each state of nature $S_j$, identify the maximum payoff in that column: $V_j^* = \max_i V_{ij}$.
  2. For each cell $(i, j)$, compute the regret: $R_{ij} = V_j^* - V_{ij}$ (for profit matrices) or $R_{ij} = V_{ij} - \min_i V_{ij}$ (for cost matrices).
  3. For each alternative (row), find the maximum regret: $\max_j R_{ij}$.
  4. Select the alternative with the **minimum of these maximum regrets**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, Savage

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** A manufacturing company must choose one of three production strategies for a new product. The payoffs (profits in ₹ Lakhs) under four demand conditions are given below:

| Strategy | Low Demand | Medium Demand | High Demand | Very High Demand |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $S_1$: Conservative | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
| $S_2$: Balanced | 10 | 50 | 60 | 70 |
| $S_3$: Aggressive | -20 | 30 | 80 | 100 |

Determine the best strategy using the **(i) Maximax, (ii) Maximin, and (iii) Laplace** criteria. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the same payoff matrix in (a), determine the optimal strategy using the **(iv) Hurwicz criterion** (with $\alpha = 0.7$) and **(v) Minimax Regret (Savage) criterion**. Justify your recommendations. (7 marks)

---

### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Maximax**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_j V(S_1) &= \max\{30, 40, 50, 60\} = 60 \\
\max_j V(S_2) &= \max\{10, 50, 60, 70\} = 70 \\
\max_j V(S_3) &= \max\{-20, 30, 80, 100\} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Best: $\max\{60, 70, 100\} = 100 \Rightarrow \boxed{S_3 \text{ (Aggressive)}}$

**(ii) Maximin**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_j V(S_1) &= 30 \quad \min_j V(S_2) = 10 \quad \min_j V(S_3) = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

Best: $\max\{30, 10, -20\} = 30 \Rightarrow \boxed{S_1 \text{ (Conservative)}}$

**(iii) Laplace** ($p_j = 1/4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \frac{1}{4}(30 + 40 + 50 + 60) = \frac{180}{4} = 45 \\
L_2 &= \frac{1}{4}(10 + 50 + 60 + 70) = \frac{190}{4} = 47.5 \\
L_3 &= \frac{1}{4}(-20 + 30 + 80 + 100) = \frac{190}{4} = 47.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Best: $\max\{45, 47.5, 47.5\} = 47.5 \Rightarrow \boxed{S_2 \text{ or } S_3 \text{ (tie)}}$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(iv) Hurwicz** ($\alpha = 0.7$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 0.7(60) + 0.3(30) = 42 + 9 = 51 \\
H_2 &= 0.7(70) + 0.3(10) = 49 + 3 = 52 \\
H_3 &= 0.7(100) + 0.3(-20) = 70 - 6 = 64
\end{aligned}
$$

Best: $\max\{51, 52, 64\} = 64 \Rightarrow \boxed{S_3 \text{ (Aggressive)}}$

**(v) Savage (Minimax Regret)**:

Column max: $S_1 = 30, S_2 = 50, S_3 = 80, S_4 = 100$

Regret Matrix:

| Strategy | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $S_1$ | 0 | 10 | 30 | 40 |
| $S_2$ | 20 | 0 | 20 | 30 |
| $S_3$ | 50 | 20 | 0 | 0 |

Maximum regret of each row:
- $S_1$: 40
- $S_2$: 30
- $S_3$: 50

Best: $\min\{40, 30, 50\} = 30 \Rightarrow \boxed{S_2 \text{ (Balanced)}}$

**Justification**: The DM should choose $S_2$ (Balanced) to minimize the worst possible regret, as the regret of 30 is the smallest among the maximum regrets.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — EMV, EOL, EVPI

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A logistics company is evaluating three distribution routes. The profits (in ₹ Lakhs) under four demand scenarios with associated probabilities are:

| Route | Low (0.15) | Medium (0.40) | High (0.30) | Very High (0.15) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $R_1$ | 20 | 35 | 50 | 65 |
| $R_2$ | 15 | 40 | 55 | 60 |
| $R_3$ | -10 | 25 | 60 | 90 |

Compute the **EMV** for each route and identify the optimal route. (7 marks)

**(b)** Construct the **opportunity loss (regret) matrix**, compute the **EOL** for each route, and calculate the **EVPI**. Verify the equivalence: $\max EMV + \min EOL = EPPL$. (7 marks)

---

### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(R_1) &= 0.15(20) + 0.40(35) + 0.30(50) + 0.15(65) \\
&= 3.0 + 14.0 + 15.0 + 9.75 = 41.75 \\
EMV(R_2) &= 0.15(15) + 0.40(40) + 0.30(55) + 0.15(60) \\
&= 2.25 + 16.0 + 16.5 + 9.0 = 43.75 \\
EMV(R_3) &= 0.15(-10) + 0.40(25) + 0.30(60) + 0.15(90) \\
&= -1.5 + 10.0 + 18.0 + 13.5 = 40.0
\end{aligned}
$$

| Route | EMV (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $R_1$ | 41.75 |
| $R_2$ | **43.75** ✓ |
| $R_3$ | 40.00 |

**Optimal Route**: $\boxed{R_2 \text{ with } EMV^* = 43.75 \text{ Lakhs}}$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1**: Column maxima of payoff matrix:
- Low: $\max\{20, 15, -10\} = 20$
- Medium: $\max\{35, 40, 25\} = 40$
- High: $\max\{50, 55, 60\} = 60$
- Very High: $\max\{65, 60, 90\} = 90$

**Step 2**: Regret Matrix $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$:

| Route | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $R_1$ | 0 | 5 | 10 | 25 |
| $R_2$ | 5 | 0 | 5 | 30 |
| $R_3$ | 30 | 15 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3**: EOL Computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(R_1) &= 0.15(0) + 0.40(5) + 0.30(10) + 0.15(25) \\
&= 0 + 2.0 + 3.0 + 3.75 = 8.75 \\
EOL(R_2) &= 0.15(5) + 0.40(0) + 0.30(5) + 0.15(30) \\
&= 0.75 + 0 + 1.5 + 4.5 = 6.75 \\
EOL(R_3) &= 0.15(30) + 0.40(15) + 0.30(0) + 0.15(0) \\
&= 4.5 + 6.0 + 0 + 0 = 10.5
\end{aligned}
$$

| Route | EOL (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $R_1$ | 8.75 |
| $R_2$ | **6.75** ✓ |
| $R_3$ | 10.50 |

**Optimal by EOL**: $R_2$ (matches EMV choice) ✓

**Step 4**: EVPI Computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPPL &= \sum_j p_j \max_i V_{ij} \\
&= 0.15(20) + 0.40(40) + 0.30(60) + 0.15(90) \\
&= 3.0 + 16.0 + 18.0 + 13.5 = 50.5 \\
EMV^* &= 43.75 \\
EVPI &= EPPL - EMV^* = 50.5 - 43.75 = 6.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Verify Equivalence Theorem:

$$
\max EMV + \min EOL = 43.75 + 6.75 = 50.5 = EPPL \quad ✓
$$

**Conclusion**: The logistics company should choose Route $R_2$ with $EMV = 43.75$ Lakhs. The maximum amount they should pay for perfect demand information is ₹ 6.75 Lakhs (EVPI).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Sign Error in Regret**: For profit matrices, $R_{ij} = \max - V_{ij}$ (always non-negative). For cost matrices, $R_{ij} = V_{ij} - \min$. Mixing these up is the most common mark-loss cause (loses 1-2 marks).
> 2. **Hurwicz $\alpha$ Definition**: If the textbook defines $\alpha$ as the pessimism coefficient, the formula inverts to $H_i = (1-\alpha)\max + \alpha\min$. Always verify the convention. KTU convention: $\alpha$ = **optimism** coefficient.
> 3. **Probability Sum**: Forgetting to verify $\sum p_j = 1$ loses a mark in EMV questions.
> 4. **EMV vs. EOL Equivalence**: Both must give the same optimal alternative. If they don't, check arithmetic.
> 5. **Hurwicz $\alpha$ Sensitivity**: If $\alpha$ is not given, state the assumed value clearly (typically $\alpha = 0.5$ unless otherwise specified).
> 6. **Laplace Justification**: Always state "by the principle of insufficient reason, all states are assumed equally likely" before writing $p_j = 1/m$.
> 7. **Tie-Breaking**: In case of ties (e.g., Minimax Regret, Laplace), explicitly state the tie and choose one with justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

### A. Decision Environments (3 Types)
- **Certainty** → Single state known → Trivial
- **Risk** → Probabilities known → EMV, EOL, EVPI
- **Uncertainty** → Probabilities unknown → Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, Savage

### B. Five Criteria Under Uncertainty (Memorize the Acronym: **M-M-H-L-S**)
1. **M**aximax → $\max_i \max_j V_{ij}$ → Optimist
2. **M**aximin → $\max_i \min_j V_{ij}$ → Pessimist (Wald)
3. **H**urwicz → $\alpha \max + (1-\alpha) \min$ → $\alpha$ = optimism index
4. **L**aplace → Average of row → Equal probability assumption
5. **S**avage (Minimax Regret) → $\min_i \max_j R_{ij}$ → Regret averse

### C. Three Criteria Under Risk
1. **EMV** = $\sum p_j V_{ij}$ → Maximize
2. **EOL** = $\sum p_j R_{ij}$ → Minimize
3. **EVPI** = $EPPL - \max EMV$ → Upper bound on info price

### D. The Equivalence Theorem
$$
\max_i EMV_i + \min_i EOL_i = \sum_j p_j \max_i V_{ij} = EPPL
$$
**The same alternative maximizes EMV and minimizes EOL.**

### E. Regret Matrix Construction
- Profit matrix: $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$
- Cost matrix: $R_{ij} = V_{ij} - \min_i V_{ij}$

### F. Hurwicz Boundary Behavior
- $\alpha = 1$ → Maximax (pure optimist)
- $\alpha = 0$ → Maximin (pure pessimist)
- $\alpha = 0.5$ → Equal weight compromise

### G. EVPI Interpretation
- It is the **maximum amount** the DM should pay for a perfectly reliable forecast.
- If actual info cost $C_{info} < EVPI$ → buy the information.
- If actual info cost $C_{info} \geq EVPI$ → don't buy the information.

### H. Common KTU Exam Question Patterns
- "Apply all 5 criteria" (14 marks) → Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, Savage
- "Apply EMV, EOL, EVPI" (14 marks) → Three probabilistic computations
- "Compare results across criteria" (conceptual 3-mark question)
- "Verify equivalence theorem" (computational verification)
- "Justify which criterion to use in scenario X" (decision reasoning)

### I. Numerical Safeguards
- Always carry 2 decimal places in EMV/EOL computations
- $\sum p_j$ must equal **1.00** (always verify)
- Regret entries must be $\geq 0$ (negative regret is a red flag for errors)
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ (if outside, the problem has a typo)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Decision Tree Analysis — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Decision Tree Analysis (DTA)** is a structured, quantitative heuristic tool used in decision theory and operations research to evaluate a sequence of interconnected decisions, chance events, and payoffs over time. It models a problem as a rooted, acyclic, branching graph (a *tree*) in which each branch represents a possible course of action or outcome, and each leaf represents the final consequence (typically measured in monetary value, utility, or loss). The technique is particularly powerful for sequential, multi-stage decisions under **risk** and **uncertainty**, because it explicitly captures both *managerial control variables* (decisions) and *uncontrollable variables* (states of nature), allowing the decision-maker to compute an **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** at every node and roll it back to the root to identify the optimal policy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEIET634 — Heuristics for Decision Making), Decision Tree Analysis is positioned as the foundational **quantitative** counterpart to purely qualitative heuristics. It bridges deterministic decision analysis (payoff tables) with probabilistic reasoning (Bayesian revision of state probabilities) and utility-based judgments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Decision Tree Analysis is taught alongside Payoff Matrices, Expected Value Criterion, Expected Opportunity Loss, Expected Value of Perfect/Imperfect Information, and Utility Theory. Mastery of this topic requires fluency in computing **EMV, EVPI, EOL, and Roll-Back Values** — these are perennial high-frequency questions in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Road-Junction Metaphor

Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Delhi. At every major junction (Trivandrum, Coimbatore, Bengaluru, Hyderabad), the road splits into multiple lanes. Each lane leads to a different sub-junction or destination, and your fuel consumption (cost) and arrival time (payoff) depend on traffic (an *uncontrollable* factor) as well as your choice of lane (a *controllable* decision). A **decision tree** is the *map* of these junctions drawn on paper. You trace every possible route, write down the cost and time for each segment, and then — working *backwards* from the destination — pick the route that minimizes cost and time on average.

Equivalently, think of a corporate manager deciding whether to **launch a new product**, **improve an existing product**, or **do nothing**. Each choice leads to a *chance node* where the market may turn *Favorable* or *Unfavorable*. At the end of each branch is a profit (or loss). The decision tree forces the manager to **map the entire decision space visually** before committing to a course of action — converting an intuitive gamble into a *calculable* policy.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Lexicon)

> [!NOTE]
> The following vocabulary is **mandatory** for KTU answers. Examiners award specific marks for the correct use of these terms.

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Decision Node** | □ (square) | A point where the decision-maker *controls* the choice among alternatives. |
| **Chance Node** | ○ (circle) | A point where an *uncontrollable* state of nature occurs; probabilities are attached. |
| **End Point / Consequence** | △ (triangle) | A terminal leaf showing the final payoff (profit, cost, or utility). |
| **Branch** | — | An edge representing one alternative (from a decision node) or one state of nature (from a chance node). |
| **Payoff** | $V_{ij}$ | The numerical consequence ($₹$, utility, or loss) at a leaf. |
| **State of Nature** | $S_j$ | A mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcome beyond the decision-maker's control. |
| **Prior Probability** | $P(S_j)$ | The initial probability of state $S_j$ *before* any information is gathered. |
| **Expected Monetary Value** | $\text{EMV}$ | The probability-weighted average of payoffs at a chance node. |
| **Roll-Back Value** | $\text{RBV}$ | The propagated optimal value at a decision node after comparing alternative branches. |
| **Expected Value of Perfect Information** | $\text{EVPI}$ | The maximum amount a rational decision-maker should pay for flawless foresight. |
| **Expected Opportunity Loss** | $\text{EOL}$ | The probability-weighted average of regrets (missed opportunity costs). |

## 1.4 Classification of Decision Environments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 requires students to **explicitly classify** the decision environment before applying any heuristic. Failing to do so is a common deduction in ESE valuation.

1. **Certainty** — Single state of nature; outcome is known with probability **1.0**. DTA degenerates to a simple comparison.
2. **Risk** — Multiple states with known (objective or subjective) probabilities. **DTA is the canonical method.**
3. **Uncertainty** — Multiple states with **unknown** probabilities. Heuristics like Maximax, Maximin, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz, and Laplace are applied (covered in later topics).
4. **Conflict** — Two or more competing decision-makers; game-theoretic models apply (covered later in Module 2/3).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Decision Tree

A decision tree is constructed in **chronological (left-to-right)** order and solved in **reverse (right-to-left, "roll-back")** order. The construction follows a strict protocol:

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Identify the initial decision** and draw a square (decision node) on the far left.
2. **Branch out** the decision node into all feasible alternatives (typically 2–4 in KTU problems).
3. At the tip of each decision branch, if the outcome depends on uncontrollable factors, draw a **chance node** (circle).
4. Branch the chance node into all **mutually exclusive and exhaustive** states of nature.
5. Assign a **prior probability** $P(S_j)$ to each chance branch, ensuring $\sum_j P(S_j) = 1$.
6. At the end of each chance branch, write the **payoff** (₹, utility, or loss).
7. If a **secondary decision** can be made *after* the chance event resolves, attach another decision node at the end of that chance branch and repeat from Step 2.
8. Solve the tree from **right to left**: at each chance node compute EMV, at each decision node select the branch with the most preferred value, and propagate this as the **Roll-Back Value (RBV)** to the parent.
9. The optimal policy is read off by tracing the bold (or marked) branches from the root to the leaves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why roll-back works:** Because decision trees encode *sequential* rationality, the optimal policy at any decision node depends only on the best EMV of the sub-tree rooted at that node. Working backwards propagates this local optimality to the root — a direct application of the **Principle of Optimality** (Bellman, 1957).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — these **six equations** account for ~80% of KTU ESE marks on Decision Tree Analysis.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Expected Monetary Value** at a chance node | $\text{EMV}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V_{ij}$ | $V_{ij}$ is the payoff of alternative $i$ under state $S_j$. Unit: $₹$ (or any currency). |
| 2 | **Optimal Decision** (maximization) | $i^* = \arg\max_i \text{EMV}_i$ | For cost-minimization problems, replace max with min. |
| 3 | **Expected Value with Perfect Information** | $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot \max_i V_{ij}$ | Take the best payoff in each column, then weight by probabilities. |
| 4 | **Expected Value of Perfect Information** | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \text{EMV}^*$ | Upper bound on what perfect information is worth. Always $\geq 0$. |
| 5 | **Regret (Opportunity Loss)** | $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij}$ | Best payoff in column $j$ minus actual payoff. Unit: $₹$. |
| 6 | **Expected Opportunity Loss** | $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot R_{ij}$ | Optimal decision = $\arg\min_i \text{EOL}_i$. |

### Supplementary Equations (Advanced)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | **Expected Value of Imperfect / Sample Information** | $\text{EVSI} = \text{EMV}^*_{\text{w/SI}} - \text{EMV}^*$ | Net gain from a non-perfect test. |
| 8 | **Efficiency of Information** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{EVSI}}{\text{EVPI}} \times 100\%$ | $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$. |
| 9 | **Bayes' Theorem (Posterior Update)** | $P(S_j \mid E_k) = \dfrac{P(E_k \mid S_j) \cdot P(S_j)}{\sum_{i} P(E_k \mid S_i) \cdot P(S_i)}$ | Used to revise priors after a test/indicator. |
| 10 | **Certainty Equivalent** | $U(\text{CE}) = E[U(X)]$ | The guaranteed amount giving the same utility as the lottery. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Managerial Utility

Decision Tree Analysis is the workhorse technique in:

- **Capital Budgeting** — Sequenced R&D, pilot, and scale-up decisions.
- **Product Portfolio Management** — Launch, modify, or abandon decisions under market uncertainty.
- **Reliability Engineering** — "Inspect, repair, or replace" maintenance policies.
- **Medical Diagnosis** — Sequence of tests and treatments under probabilistic symptoms.
- **Software Engineering** — Agile vs. waterfall project selection.
- **Energy Sector** — Investment in solar/wind under demand uncertainty.
- **Supply Chain** — Make-or-buy with demand variability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Heuristics? Why not always maximize EMV?** In high-stakes decisions (e.g., firm survival, life-safety), the EMV criterion may recommend a bet that *could* bankrupt the company with non-trivial probability. Decision trees are therefore often augmented with **Utility Theory** (concave utility curves for risk-averse decision-makers) to model real preferences. This is a recurrent 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The KTU "Product Launch" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **canonical KTU-style 14-mark problem**. Every step is shown; no shortcut is taken.

### Problem Statement

A consumer-electronics company in Kerala is evaluating three alternatives for the upcoming fiscal year:

- **D₁:** Launch a brand-new product line.
- **D₂:** Launch an improved version of the existing best-seller.
- **D₃:** Do not launch any new product (retain current portfolio).

The marketing department estimates the following two states of nature with their prior probabilities:

- **S₁:** Favorable market response, $P(S_1) = 0.6$
- **S₂:** Unfavorable market response, $P(S_2) = 0.4$

The expected payoffs (profits in **₹ lakhs**) for the first year are tabulated below.

| Alternative | $S_1$ (Favorable) | $S_2$ (Unfavorable) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **D₁: New product** | 200 | -100 |
| **D₂: Improved product** | 100 | -20 |
| **D₃: No launch** | 0 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Draw the Decision Tree (chronological construction)

The root is a **decision node** (square). From it, three branches go to D₁, D₂, D₃. At the tip of each, a **chance node** (circle) splits into S₁ and S₂ with their probabilities. Each terminal leaf carries the corresponding payoff.

### Step 2 — Compute EMV at each chance node

We work *right-to-left*. The "right-most" calculations are the EMVs at the chance nodes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(D_1) &= P(S_1)\cdot V_{11} + P(S_2)\cdot V_{12} \\
&= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times (-100) \\
&= 120 - 40 \\
&= \mathbf{80 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(D_2) &= P(S_1)\cdot V_{21} + P(S_2)\cdot V_{22} \\
&= 0.6 \times 100 + 0.4 \times (-20) \\
&= 60 - 8 \\
&= \mathbf{52 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(D_3) &= P(S_1)\cdot V_{31} + P(S_2)\cdot V_{32} \\
&= 0.6 \times 0 + 0.4 \times 0 \\
&= \mathbf{0 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Roll-Back at the decision node

At the root decision node, the manager selects the alternative with the **maximum** EMV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i^* &= \arg\max\{\text{EMV}(D_1), \text{EMV}(D_2), \text{EMV}(D_3)\} \\
&= \arg\max\{80, 52, 0\} \\
&= D_1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Optimal Decision} = D_1 \text{ (Launch new product); } \text{EMV}^* = 80 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

### Step 4 — Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

**First**, compute the Expected Value **with** Perfect Information ($\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}}$). If we could foresee the state of nature, we would pick the best alternative *for each state*:

- Under $S_1$: best is D₁ with payoff 200.
- Under $S_2$: best is D₃ (or D₁, D₂ — all yield $\leq 0$; the best non-negative is D₃ with payoff 0).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} &= P(S_1)\cdot \max_i V_{i1} + P(S_2)\cdot \max_i V_{i2} \\
&= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times 0 \\
&= 120 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{120 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Second**, the EVPI is the *advantage* of having perfect information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVPI} &= \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \text{EMV}^* \\
&= 120 - 80 \\
&= \mathbf{40 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The company should pay **at most ₹40 lakhs** for a perfectly reliable market research forecast. Any consultant charging more is not worth it *in expectation*.

### Step 5 — Opportunity Loss (Regret) Matrix

Compute the regret $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij}$ for every cell:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{11} &= \max\{200, 100, 0\} - 200 = 0 \\
R_{21} &= 200 - 100 = 100 \\
R_{31} &= 200 - 0 = 200 \\
R_{12} &= \max\{-100, -20, 0\} - (-100) = 0 - (-100) = 100 \\
R_{22} &= 0 - (-20) = 20 \\
R_{32} &= 0 - 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Regret Matrix** is:

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **D₁** | 0 | 100 |
| **D₂** | 100 | 20 |
| **D₃** | 200 | 0 |

### Step 6 — Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EOL}(D_1) &= 0.6 \times 0 + 0.4 \times 100 = 0 + 40 = \mathbf{40} \\
\text{EOL}(D_2) &= 0.6 \times 100 + 0.4 \times 20 = 60 + 8 = \mathbf{68} \\
\text{EOL}(D_3) &= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times 0 = 120 + 0 = \mathbf{120}
\end{aligned}
$$

Optimal decision = $\arg\min\{\text{EOL}(D_1), \text{EOL}(D_2), \text{EOL}(D_3)\} = \arg\min\{40, 68, 120\} = D_1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of the Fundamental Identity:** $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}_i = 40$ lakhs. This identity is *always* true and is a frequent 2-mark "show that" question.

### Step 7 — Sensitivity Analysis (Bonus)

The break-even probability $p^*$ for D₁ vs. D₃ is found by setting $\text{EMV}(D_1) = \text{EMV}(D_3) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p^* \cdot 200 + (1 - p^*)\cdot(-100) &= 0 \\
200 p^* - 100 + 100 p^* &= 0 \\
300 p^* &= 100 \\
p^* &= \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the prior $P(S_1) = 0.6 > 0.333$, D₁ remains optimal with a comfortable **margin of safety of ~0.267**. KTU examiners often ask for this margin.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Recommended Criterion | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Repeated, low-stakes, symmetric payoffs | **EMV / Maximize Expected Profit** | Law of Large Numbers averages out the variance. |
| One-shot, firm-threatening loss possible | **Utility Theory / Certainty Equivalent** | A 10% chance of bankruptcy is not offset by a 90% chance of moderate gain. |
| Probabilities are *not* reliable (deep uncertainty) | **Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, Minimax Regret** | Avoids spurious precision from guesswork probabilities. |
| Competitor response is the main driver | **Game Theory (Minimax, Saddle Point)** | Opponent's strategy matters as much as chance. |
| A reliable forecast can be purchased | **EVPI-based "buy or not buy" decision** | Pay ≤ EVPI for the information source. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Decision Tree Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT PROBLEM] --> B[IDENTIFY DECISIONS AND STATES]
    B --> C[CONSTRUCT PAYOFF MATRIX]
    C --> D[ASSIGN PRIOR PROBABILITIES]
    D --> E[DRAW TREE LEFT TO RIGHT]
    E --> F[COMPUTE EMV AT CHANCE NODES]
    F --> G[ROLL BACK AT DECISION NODES]
    G --> H{OPTIMAL POLICY}
    H --> I[COMPUTE EV WITH PI]
    I --> J[COMPUTE EVPI]
    C --> K[COMPUTE REGRET MATRIX]
    K --> L[COMPUTE EOL]
    L --> M[VERIFY MIN EOL EQUALS EVPI]
    J --> N[DECIDE BUY OR NOT BUY INFO]
    G --> O[SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS]
    O --> P[BREAK EVEN PROBABILITY]

    style A fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2da44e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#9e6a03,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P fill:#6f42c1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

This block diagram shows the **end-to-end solution pipeline** a KTU student should follow when faced with a 14-mark Decision Tree Analysis question. Skipping any box typically costs 1–2 marks.
