# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)]`

**Q1(a).** *Distinguish between a decision node and a chance node in a decision tree. Draw the standard symbols used for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **decision node** represents a point where the decision-maker has full control over the choice among alternative courses of action; it is conventionally drawn as a **square (□)**. A **chance node**, in contrast, represents a point where the outcome is governed by an uncontrollable state of nature governed by probabilities; it is drawn as a **circle (○)**. Branches emanating from a decision node represent *alternatives*, whereas branches emanating from a chance node represent *states of nature* with associated probabilities that sum to unity. The end points (leaves) of the tree are typically marked as **triangles (△)** and carry the numerical payoff. (3 marks)

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1(b)]`

**Q1(b).** *Define Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI). What does a high EVPI indicate about a decision problem?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EVPI is the **maximum additional expected profit** a decision-maker can obtain by having access to perfect (100% accurate) information about which state of nature will occur before making the decision. Mathematically, $\text{EVPI} = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \text{EMV}^*$. A high EVPI indicates that the decision is **highly sensitive to the state of nature**, that the opportunity cost of being wrong is large, and that it may be economically justified to invest in additional research, market surveys, or expert consultation *up to* the EVPI value. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q2]`

### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

A startup company must choose between two strategies for launching a software product: **S₁ = Aggressive Launch** (large marketing spend) and **S₂ = Conservative Launch** (minimal marketing). The market response can be **High (H)** with probability 0.4 or **Low (L)** with probability 0.6. The payoffs (in ₹ lakhs) are:

| Strategy | H | L |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| S₁ | 300 | -50 |
| S₂ | 120 | 30 |

**(a)** Draw the decision tree and determine the optimal strategy using the **EMV criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **EVPI** and the **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** for each strategy. Verify the identity $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Decision Tree & EMV**

Construction: Root (decision) → branches S₁, S₂ → each splits at a chance node into H ($P=0.4$) and L ($P=0.6$) → leaves carry payoffs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(S_1) &= 0.4 \times 300 + 0.6 \times (-50) \\
&= 120 - 30 \\
&= \mathbf{90 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(S_2) &= 0.4 \times 120 + 0.6 \times 30 \\
&= 48 + 18 \\
&= \mathbf{66 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Optimal Strategy} = S_1 \text{ (Aggressive Launch); } \text{EMV}^* = 90 \text{ lakhs}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

**Part (b) — EVPI & EOL**

Expected value *with* perfect information — pick the best payoff in each column:

- Under H: best is S₁ (300).
- Under L: best is S₂ (30).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} &= 0.4 \times 300 + 0.6 \times 30 \\
&= 120 + 18 \\
&= \mathbf{138 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVPI} &= \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \text{EMV}^* \\
&= 138 - 90 \\
&= \mathbf{48 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Regret matrix $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{11} &= 300 - 300 = 0 \\
R_{21} &= 300 - 120 = 180 \\
R_{12} &= 30 - (-50) = 80 \\
R_{22} &= 30 - 30 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Regret table:

| Strategy | H | L |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| S₁ | 0 | 80 |
| S₂ | 180 | 0 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EOL}(S_1) &= 0.4 \times 0 + 0.6 \times 80 = 0 + 48 = \mathbf{48} \\
\text{EOL}(S_2) &= 0.4 \times 180 + 0.6 \times 0 = 72 + 0 = \mathbf{72}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $\min_i \text{EOL}_i = 48 = \text{EVPI}$. ✓

$$
\boxed{\text{The company should pay at most ₹48 lakhs for perfect market information; and the optimal EOL-based decision is also } S_1. \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]`

A manufacturer can lease a small plant (**L₁**) or a large plant (**L₂**) for a new product with a 5-year useful life. Demand can be **High (H)** with probability 0.5 or **Low (L)** with probability 0.5. The annual cash-flow equivalents (in ₹ lakhs) are:

| Plant | H | L |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| L₁ (Small) | 50 | 30 |
| L₂ (Large) | 90 | 10 |

**(a)** Construct the decision tree, compute the EMV for each alternative, and identify the optimal lease choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the manufacturer can commission a market survey *before* leasing. The survey has a 70% chance of correctly predicting "High" when demand is indeed high, and a 60% chance of correctly predicting "Low" when demand is low. Use **Bayes' Theorem** to compute the posterior probabilities and decide whether the survey is worth its **₹25 lakh** cost. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — EMV under priors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(L_1) &= 0.5 \times 50 + 0.5 \times 30 = 25 + 15 = \mathbf{40 \text{ lakhs}} \\
\text{EMV}(L_2) &= 0.5 \times 90 + 0.5 \times 10 = 45 + 5 = \mathbf{50 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Optimal Choice} = L_2 \text{ (Large plant); } \text{EMV}^* = 50 \text{ lakhs}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

**Part (b) — Bayesian update and EVSI**

Let $F_H$ = survey forecasts "High" and $F_L$ = survey forecasts "Low". Given:

- $P(F_H \mid H) = 0.7$, hence $P(F_L \mid H) = 0.3$
- $P(F_L \mid L) = 0.6$, hence $P(F_H \mid L) = 0.4$

Prior: $P(H) = P(L) = 0.5$.

Posterior probability of H given forecast $F_H$ (Bayes' Theorem):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H \mid F_H) &= \frac{P(F_H \mid H)\cdot P(H)}{P(F_H \mid H)\cdot P(H) + P(F_H \mid L)\cdot P(L)} \\
&= \frac{0.7 \times 0.5}{0.7 \times 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.5} \\
&= \frac{0.35}{0.35 + 0.20} = \frac{0.35}{0.55} = \frac{7}{11} \approx 0.6364
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $P(L \mid F_H) = 1 - 7/11 = 4/11 \approx 0.3636$.

Similarly for forecast $F_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H \mid F_L) &= \frac{0.3 \times 0.5}{0.3 \times 0.5 + 0.6 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.15}{0.45} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333 \\
P(L \mid F_L) &= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**EMV under each forecast (using posteriors):**

If survey says "High" (use posteriors $P(H \mid F_H)=7/11$, $P(L \mid F_H)=4/11$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(L_1 \mid F_H) &= \frac{7}{11}\cdot 50 + \frac{4}{11}\cdot 30 = \frac{350 + 120}{11} = \frac{470}{11} \approx 42.73 \\
\text{EMV}(L_2 \mid F_H) &= \frac{7}{11}\cdot 90 + \frac{4}{11}\cdot 10 = \frac{630 + 40}{11} = \frac{670}{11} \approx 60.91
\end{aligned}
$$

Optimal when $F_H$: choose **L₂**, with EMV ≈ 60.91 lakhs.

If survey says "Low" (use $P(H \mid F_L)=1/3$, $P(L \mid F_L)=2/3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(L_1 \mid F_L) &= \frac{1}{3}\cdot 50 + \frac{2}{3}\cdot 30 = \frac{50 + 60}{3} = \frac{110}{3} \approx 36.67 \\
\text{EMV}(L_2 \mid F_L) &= \frac{1}{3}\cdot 90 + \frac{2}{3}\cdot 10 = \frac{90 + 20}{3} = \frac{110}{3} \approx 36.67
\end{aligned}
$$

Optimal when $F_L$: **indifferent** between L₁ and L₂; both yield ≈ 36.67 lakhs (tie).

**Expected EMV with sample information:**

Probability of $F_H$: $P(F_H) = 0.7\times 0.5 + 0.4\times 0.5 = 0.55$.
Probability of $F_L$: $P(F_L) = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}^*_{\text{w/SI}} &= 0.55 \times 60.91 + 0.45 \times 36.67 \\
&\approx 33.50 + 16.50 \\
&\approx \mathbf{50.00 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**EVSI and decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVSI} &= \text{EMV}^*_{\text{w/SI}} - \text{EMV}^*_{\text{w/o SI}} \\
&= 50.00 - 50.00 \\
&= \mathbf{0 \text{ lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Exactly zero, since the survey is symmetric and adds no decision-relevant information given the symmetric posteriors — this is a beautiful pedagogical coincidence; in general, EVSI > 0.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net gain from the survey** $= \text{EVSI} - \text{Cost} = 0 - 25 = -25$ lakhs. Therefore, the survey is **NOT worth purchasing** at ₹25 lakhs. The manufacturer should retain the **large plant** decision based on prior information alone. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Decision Tree Analysis (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Drawing decision and chance nodes backwards** — Students often place chance nodes *before* decision nodes, which is logically invalid. **Rule:** A decision node is always *first* (left-most) at any stage.
> 2. **Forgetting to ensure probabilities sum to 1** — If you write $P(S_1) = 0.6$ and $P(S_2) = 0.5$, you will lose 1 full mark for "incomplete probability model."
> 3. **Not showing the roll-back step** — EMV calculations at chance nodes are not enough; you must explicitly write $\text{EMV}(D_i) = X$ *and* $\text{Optimal} = \arg\max$.
> 4. **Confusing EVPI with EVSI** — EVPI is the value of *perfect* information; EVSI is the value of *imperfect/sample* information. The latter requires Bayes' update.
> 5. **Sign errors in regret matrices** — Regret is *always* non-negative. A negative regret indicates you took the best alternative in that column. Cross-check by ensuring the minimum element in each column is exactly **0**.
> 6. **Forgetting utility transformation** — In a 14-mark question that mentions "risk-averse investor" or "utility function," pure EMV is *not* sufficient. You must compute the Expected Utility and roll that back instead. **Loss: up to 4 marks.**
> 7. **Unit omission** — Always write "₹ lakhs" or "₹ crores" with the numerical answer. Examiners are trained to deduct for ambiguous units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Decision Tree = square (decision) + circle (chance) + triangle (payoff).**
- [ ] Construct **left-to-right**; solve **right-to-left (roll-back).**
- [ ] $\text{EMV}_i = \sum_j P(S_j) \cdot V_{ij}$; optimal decision is $\arg\max \text{EMV}_i$ (or $\arg\min$ for cost).
- [ ] $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} = \sum_j P(S_j) \cdot \max_i V_{ij}$ — *best-in-column-then-weight*.
- [ ] $\text{EVPI} = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \text{EMV}^* \geq 0$ — upper bound on the price of perfect information.
- [ ] Regret $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij} \geq 0$; $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_j P(S_j) \cdot R_{ij}$.
- [ ] **Verification identity (always true):** $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$.
- [ ] **EVSI** = expected gain from a non-perfect test = $\text{EMV}^*_{\text{w/SI}} - \text{EMV}^*$.
- [ ] **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(S_j \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid S_j)\,P(S_j)}{\sum_i P(E \mid S_i)\,P(S_i)}$ — used to update priors after sample information.
- [ ] **Sensitivity / break-even probability:** Solve $p^* \cdot V_{\text{best under } S_1} + (1-p^*) \cdot V_{\text{worst under } S_2} = 0$ to find the threshold.
- [ ] **Utility theory is the fix** when EMV recommends an unacceptable gamble (risk-aversion, firm survival).
- [ ] Always state **units** (₹ lakhs, ₹ crores, utility units) and **round** EMVs to two decimal places.
- [ ] KTU "show that" questions often verify $\text{EVPI} = \min \text{EOL}$ — practice this identity.
- [ ] KTU "explain" questions on decision tree symbols, roll-back, and EVPI definitions are **3-mark givers** — never leave them unattempted.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts and Evolutionary Algorithms: Genetic Algorithm (GA) - Types of Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICSB FOR DECISION MAKING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concepts and Evolutionary Algorithms: Genetic Algorithm (GA) - Types of Encoding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Evolutionary Algorithm (EA)? An **Evolutionary Algorithm (EA)** is a subset of evolutionary computation, a generic population-based meta-heuristic optimization algorithm that uses mechanisms inspired by biological evolution, such as reproduction, mutation, recombination, and selection. EA operates on a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals*), iteratively improving them with respect to a defined objective (fitness) function. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** "Evolutionary Algorithms are stochastic search procedures based on principles of natural selection and genetics, where a population of candidate solutions evolves toward better regions of the search space through the repeated application of selection, crossover, and mutation operators." ## 1.2 What is a Genetic Algorithm (GA)? A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is the most widely recognized class of Evolutionary Algorithms, developed by **John Holland (1975)** and popularized by **David Goldberg (1989)**. It is a meta-heuristic inspired by the process of natural selection that belongs to the larger class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EA)**. GAs are commonly used to generate high-quality solutions to optimization and search problems by relying on bio-inspired operators such as *selection*, *crossover*, and *mutation*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Distinction:** In KTU examinations, a GA is **not** a single algorithm but a *framework* — a problem-solving paradigm. The fitness function, encoding scheme, and genetic operators are problem-specific. Always specify the encoding type when describing a GA problem. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Survival of the Fittest Chromosome" Imagine you are a botanist trying to grow the **tallest, most drought-resistant plant** in a giant greenhouse. You have **1000 seeds**, each with slightly different genetic codes. You cannot test every single genetic combination directly. - **Population** → The collection of 1000 seeds (candidate solutions). - **Chromosome** → The DNA strand of each seed (encoded solution). - **Gene** → A single parameter (e.g., root length, leaf thickness). - **Fitness Function** → How well the grown plant survives in dry conditions. - **Selection** → You keep the best-growing plants as parents for the next season. - **Crossover** → You cross-pollinate two strong plants to mix their DNA. - **Mutation** → Occasionally, random small changes occur in the DNA (like sun radiation causing mutations). - **Generation** → Each planting cycle is a new "generation" of plants, generally fitter than the last. After many generations, you obtain a near-optimal plant — this is exactly how a **GA mimics biological evolution** to solve complex engineering optimization problems like route planning, scheduling, neural network weight tuning, and antenna design. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners love analogies. Writing one short real-world analogy in the introduction (2 marks) demonstrates higher-order Bloom's Level (Analyze/Apply) and earns easy partial credit. ## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology of GAs | Term | Biological Meaning | GA Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **Individual** | A single organism | A single candidate solution | | **Population** | A group of organisms | A set of candidate solutions | | **Chromosome** | DNA strand of an organism | An encoded solution string | | **Gene** | A single DNA segment | A single decision variable | | **Allele** | A variant value of a gene | A possible value of a variable | | **Genotype** | Genetic makeup | The encoded representation | | **Phenotype** | Physical expression | The decoded actual solution | | **Fitness** | Reproductive success | Quality measure (objective value) | | **Generation** | One reproductive cycle | One iteration of the GA loop | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical GA Pipeline Every Genetic Algorithm follows a structured 5-step iterative pipeline. Understanding this is **mandatory** for KTU Module 1 questions. ### Step 1 — Initialization A population of $N$ chromosomes is generated randomly (or via a heuristic seeding). Each chromosome is a string of genes representing one candidate solution to the optimization problem. ### Step 2 — Fitness Evaluation Each chromosome is decoded from its **genotype** (encoded form) into its **phenotype** (actual variable values) and evaluated using the **objective/fitness function** $f(x)$. ### Step 3 — Selection Parents are selected from the current population for mating. Higher fitness = higher probability of selection. Common selection methods include **Roulette Wheel**, **Tournament Selection**, **Rank Selection**, and **Truncation Selection**. ### Step 4 — Crossover (Recombination) Selected parents exchange genetic material to produce offspring. The probability of crossover is governed by the **crossover rate** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ (typical KTU value). ### Step 5 — Mutation Random alterations are introduced into offspring genes with a small **mutation rate** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ to maintain genetic diversity and prevent premature convergence. ### Termination The loop terminates when one of the following criteria is met: - **Maximum generations** $G_{max}$ reached - **Convergence** detected (fitness plateau) - **Time budget** exhausted - **Target fitness** $f_{target}$ achieved ## 2.2 Types of Encoding in Genetic Algorithms **Encoding** (also called *representation* or *chromosome design*) is the **most critical design decision** in any GA. It defines the structure of the chromosome and dictates which genetic operators (crossover/mutation) are valid. There are **four primary encoding schemes** prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. ### 2.2.1 Binary Encoding Each chromosome is a string of **binary digits** $\{0, 1\}$. It is the classical GA representation introduced by Holland. **Example Chromosome:** $$ \text{Chromosome} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Decoding Formula (unsigned integer in range $[a, b]$):** $$ x = a + \frac{(b-a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} \cdot \left( \sum_{i=0}^{\ell-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i} \right) $$ where $\ell$ is the chromosome length, and $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $i^{th}$ bit. **Strengths:** - Simple to implement; classical crossover (single-point, two-point, uniform) works directly. - Small alphabet $\Rightarrow$ high schema processing capability (Schema Theorem). **Weaknesses:** - **Hamming Cliff Problem** — integers 7 (0111) and 8 (1000) differ in 4 bits, yet are numerically adjacent. - Poor precision for continuous problems (requires long chromosomes). ### 2.2.2 Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding Each gene is a real number $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$, typically bounded within a domain $[L_i, U_i]$. Most suitable for continuous optimization. **Example Chromosome:** $$ \text{Chromosome} = \begin{bmatrix} 12.34 & -5.67 & 0.892 & 43.21 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Strengths:** - Eliminates the decoding step $\Rightarrow$ genotype = phenotype. - High precision; no Hamming cliff issues. - Efficient for large-dimensional continuous problems. **Weaknesses:** - Requires specialized operators (SBX crossover, polynomial mutation, Gaussian mutation). - Schema theorem does not directly apply. ### 2.2.3 Integer / Discrete-Valued Encoding Each gene takes a value from a **finite set of integers** $x_i \in \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$. Used when variables are naturally discrete (e.g., number of machines, choice of material). **Example Chromosome:** $$ \text{Chromosome} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 7 & 1 & 5 & 9 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Strengths:** - Compact representation for discrete combinatorial problems. - Easy constraint handling. **Weaknesses:** - May require custom crossover/mutation to preserve integer semantics. ### 2.2.4 Permutation Encoding Used exclusively for **ordering problems** (TSP, scheduling, assignment). Each chromosome is a permutation of $n$ unique integers $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. **Example Chromosome (for $n = 8$):** $$ \text{Chromosome} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 7 & 2 & 8 & 1 & 5 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Strengths:** - Naturally represents permutations. - Can be combined with specialized operators (PMX, OX, CX, Edge Recombination). **Weaknesses:** - Standard crossover produces invalid permutations $\Rightarrow$ must use permutation-aware operators. ### 2.2.5 Tree Encoding (Bonus — Mention in KTU) Used in **Genetic Programming (GP)**. Chromosomes are tree structures where leaves are terminals and internal nodes are functions. The depth and structure of the tree is variable. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | |---|---|---| | Binary Decoding | $x = a + \dfrac{(b-a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\ell-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | Converts binary chromosome to real value in $[a,b]$ | | Population Diversity | $D(t) = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d(c_i, \bar{c})$ | Diversity measure at generation $t$ | | Roulette Wheel Probability | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Probability of selecting individual $i$ | | Expected Count (Roulette) | $E[n_i] = N \cdot \dfrac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | Expected copies of individual $i$ in mating pool | | Tournament Size Effect | $P(\text{win}) \approx p^{k-1}$ for $k$-tournament, $p$ = rank probability | Probability of winning $k$-tournament | | Crossover Rate | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | Standard recommended range | | Mutation Rate | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ | Standard recommended range | | Schema Theorem | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$ | Holland's Schema Theorem (implicit parallelism) | | Schema Order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions in schema $H$ | | Defining Length | $\delta(H) = \text{last}(H) - \text{first}(H)$ | Distance between outermost fixed bits | | Real-valued Decoding | $x_i = L_i + (U_i - L_i) \cdot g_i$ | For $g_i \in [0,1]$ (when gene is normalized) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of GA Encoding | Encoding Type | Engineering Application | |---|---| | **Binary** | Feature selection in ML, simple knapsack problem, Boolean logic minimization | | **Real-valued** | Neural network weight optimization, aerodynamic shape design, controller tuning | | **Integer** | Job-shop scheduling, resource allocation, structural member selection | | **Permutation** | Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), vehicle routing, university course timetabling | | **Tree** | Symbolic regression, automatic program synthesis, financial model discovery | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Engineering Insight:** In real industrial applications, **real-valued encoding has largely replaced binary encoding** for continuous problems because of the Hamming cliff issue. However, **binary GA remains academically important** for KTU theory questions and schema-theorem analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Binary Decoding **Problem (KTU Style):** A GA uses a binary chromosome of length $\ell = 8$ to represent a variable $x \in [0, 10]$. Decode the chromosome $c = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0]$ to its corresponding real value $x$. ### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Identify the relevant formula from the KTU Formula Sheet. $$ x = a + \frac{(b-a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} \cdot \left( \sum_{i=0}^{\ell-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i} \right) $$ Here, $a = 0$, $b = 10$, $\ell = 8$. **Step 2:** Compute the integer value of the binary string. Assume the chromosome is indexed left-to-right as $b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ (MSB to LSB). $$ c = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0] $$ So we have: $$ b_7 = 1, \quad b_6 = 0, \quad b_5 = 1, \quad b_4 = 1 $$ $$ b_3 = 0, \quad b_2 = 1, \quad b_1 = 0, \quad b_0 = 0 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the weighted binary sum. $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i \cdot 2^{i} &= (1)(2^7) + (0)(2^6) + (1)(2^5) + (1)(2^4) + (0)(2^3) + (1)(2^2) + (0)(2^1) + (0)(2^0) \\ &= 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 \\ &= 180 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the scaling factor. $$ \frac{(b - a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} = \frac{10 - 0}{2^8 - 1} = \frac{10}{255} = 0.03921568\ldots $$ **Step 5:** Compute the decoded real value. $$ \begin{aligned} x &= 0 + \left( \frac{10}{255} \right) \cdot 180 \\ &= \frac{1800}{255} \\ &= \frac{120}{17} \\ &\approx 7.0588 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $x \approx 7.0588$ > [!TIP] > **Verification Trick for KTU:** The maximum representable value is when all bits are 1, i.e., binary $11111111 = 255$, which maps to $x = 10$. Our decoded value $7.0588$ lies within $[0, 10]$ ✓ ## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Roulette Wheel Selection **Problem:** Consider a GA population of $N = 4$ individuals with fitness values $f = [10, 30, 40, 20]$. Compute the selection probability and expected count for each individual. ### Solution **Step 1:** Compute total population fitness. $$ F = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i = 10 + 30 + 40 + 20 = 100 $$ **Step 2:** Compute selection probability for each individual using the Roulette Wheel formula $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{F}$. $$ P_1 = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10 \quad (\text{or } 10\%) $$ $$ P_2 = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30 \quad (\text{or } 30\%) $$ $$ P_3 = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40 \quad (\text{or } 40\%) $$ $$ P_4 = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20 \quad (\text{or } 20\%) $$ **Step 3:** Compute cumulative probability $C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_j$ for the spinning wheel. $$ C_1 = 0.10, \quad C_2 = 0.40, \quad C_3 = 0.80, \quad C_4 = 1.00 $$ **Step 4:** Compute expected count $E_i = N \cdot P_i$. $$ E_1 = 4 \times 0.10 = 0.4, \quad E_2 = 4 \times 0.30 = 1.2 $$ $$ E_3 = 4 \times 0.40 = 1.6, \quad E_4 = 4 \times 0.20 = 0.8 $$ **Analytical Matrix — Selection Outcome:** | Individual | Fitness $f_i$ | Probability $P_i$ | Cumulative $C_i$ | Expected Count $E_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 10 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.4 | | 2 | 30 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 1.2 | | 3 | 40 | 0.40 | 0.80 | 1.6 | | 4 | 20 | 0.20 | 1.00 | 0.8 | | **Total** | **100** | **1.00** | — | **4.0** | **Observation:** Individual 3 (fitness 40) has the highest probability of being selected, which is the desired behavior of a fitness-proportional selection scheme. ## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Schema Theorem Application **Problem:** Consider a binary GA with population size $N = 50$, average fitness $\bar{f} = 100$. The fitness of schema $H = 1 * * 0$ is $f(H) = 130$. The schema order is $o(H) = 2$ and the defining length is $\delta(H) = 3$. Given $p_c = 0.7$ and $p_m = 0.005$, compute the lower bound on the expected number of schema instances in the next generation. (Use $\ell = 5$.) ### Solution **Step 1:** Recall Holland's Schema Theorem lower bound: $$ E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right] $$ **Step 2:** Assume $m(H, t) = 10$ (i.e., 10 instances of $H$ in current population). **Step 3:** Compute the ratio $\dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$: $$ \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} = \frac{130}{100} = 1.3 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the survival probability bracket. $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H) &= 1 - (0.7) \cdot \frac{3}{5-1} - (0.005) \cdot 2 \\ &= 1 - (0.7) \cdot (0.75) - (0.01) \\ &= 1 - 0.525 - 0.01 \\ &= 0.465 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Compute the lower bound. $$ \begin{aligned} E[m(H, t+1)] &\geq 10 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 0.465 \\ &= 13 \cdot 0.465 \\ &= 6.045 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Despite losses due to crossover and mutation, the above-average fitness schema $H$ is expected to grow from 10 instances to **at least 6.045 instances** in the next generation. Schemas with above-average fitness grow exponentially — this is the **implicit parallelism** of GAs. ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Encoding | Criterion | Binary | Real-Valued | Integer | Permutation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Problem Type** | Discrete/Boolean | Continuous | Discrete finite | Ordering | | **Precision** | Low (Hamming cliff) | High | Exact | Exact | | **Operator Compatibility** | Standard (1pt, 2pt, UX) | Custom (SBX, PM) | Standard + Repair | Custom (PMX, OX) | | **Constraint Handling** | Easy via bit masking | Penalty methods | Lookup tables | Permutation repair | | **Memory Footprint** | Compact (1 bit/gene) | Large (32–64 bits/gene) | Compact (8–32 bits) | Compact | | **Search Space** | $2^{\ell}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ | $\prod k_i$ | $n!$ | | **Convergence Speed** | Slow for continuous | Fast for continuous | Fast for discrete | Fast for ordering | | **Best Use Case** | Combinatorial | Engineering optimization | Resource allocation | TSP, Scheduling | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note > All node IDs in the following diagrams are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword safeguard. Labels are kept clean — no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside double-quoted node text. ## 4.2 The GA Main Loop — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([START GA]) --> nodeB[Initialize Population
N random chromosomes] nodeB --> nodeC[Decode Genotype to Phenotype] nodeC --> nodeD[Evaluate Fitness Function fx] nodeD --> nodeE{Termination
Criterion Met} nodeE -- NO --> nodeF[SELECTION
Roulette or Tournament] nodeF --> nodeG[CROSSOVER
Combine Parent Genes] nodeG --> nodeH[MUTATION
Random Gene Perturbation] nodeH --> nodeI[Form New Population
Next Generation] nodeI --> nodeC nodeE -- YES --> nodeJ[Return Best Solution] nodeJ --> nodeK([END GA]) ``` **Reading Guide for KTU Students:** - The **outer loop** corresponds to one *generation* of evolution. - **Selection → Crossover → Mutation** is the canonical GA "engine" — this exact triple is worth **3 marks** in a typical KTU 2-mark question. - The fitness evaluation is performed *before* selection, NOT after — a common student mistake. ## 4.3 Encoding Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{What is the
variable type?} q1 -- Binary Boolean --> e1[BINARY ENCODING
Example 10110] q1 -- Continuous Real --> q2{Domain size} q1 -- Discrete Count --> e2[INTEGER ENCODING
Example 1 3 7 5] q1 -- Ordering Permutation --> e3[PERMUTATION ENCODING
Example 3 7 2 8 1] q2 -- Bounded range --> e4[REAL VALUED ENCODING
Example 12.34 0.892] q2 -- Tree structured --> e5[TREE ENCODING
Used in GP] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Encoding Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHENO[PHENOTYPE DOMAIN] pA[Real World Solution] end subgraph GENO[GENOTYPE DOMAIN] gA[Encoded Chromosome] end subgraph OPS[GA OPERATORS] op1[Selection] op2[Crossover] op3[Mutation] end pA -->|Encoding Function E| gA gA -->|Decoding Function D| pA gA --> op1 op1 --> op2 op2 --> op3 op3 -->|New Generation| gA ``` **Key Insight (KTU 2024):** The **encoding/decoding functions** $E$ and $D$ form the *bridge* between problem space and GA space. Any error or non-invertibility in this mapping causes loss of solution quality. ## 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart — Binary GA with Single-Point Crossover ```mermaid flowchart TD p1[Parent 1: 10110010] p2[Parent 2: 01101101] pivot{Crossover Point
Position 4} p1 --> pivot p2 --> pivot pivot -- Cut Here --> c1[Child 1: 10110101] pivot -- Cut Here --> c2[Child 2: 01101010] m1{Mutate Bit 6} c1 --> m1 m1 -- Flip Bit --> f1[Final Offspring 1: 10111101] c2 --> m2{Mutate Bit 2} m2 -- Flip Bit --> f2[Final Offspring 2: 00101010] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > Explain the concept of a Genetic Algorithm. List and briefly describe the main types of encoding used in GAs. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Genetic Algorithm (GA) is a population-based meta-heuristic optimization technique inspired by the principles of natural selection and genetics, developed by John Holland in 1975. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (chromosomes) that evolve over generations through three primary operators: **selection**, **crossover**, and **mutation**, with the goal of maximizing (or minimizing) a fitness function. The main types of encoding in GAs are: 1. **Binary Encoding** — Chromosome is a string of 0s and 1s. Simple but suffers from Hamming cliff. 2. **Real-Valued Encoding** — Genes are continuous real numbers; ideal for engineering optimization. 3. **Integer Encoding** — Genes take discrete integer values from a finite set. 4. **Permutation Encoding** — Chromosome is a permutation of integers; used for ordering problems like TSP. ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > Differentiate between binary and real-valued encoding schemes in Genetic Algorithms. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Binary Encoding | Real-Valued Encoding | |---|---|---| | **Alphabet** | $\{0, 1\}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ within bounds | | **Decoding** | Required (binary $\to$ real) | Not required (direct) | | **Precision** | Limited by chromosome length | High (machine precision) | | **Hamming Cliff** | Yes, present | No issue | | **Operators** | Standard 1-point, 2-point crossover | Custom (SBX, polynomial mutation) | | **Best For** | Discrete/combinatorial | Continuous optimization | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** > **(a)** Describe the fundamental architecture of a Genetic Algorithm with a neat block diagram. Explain in detail the role of the **fitness function**, **selection mechanism**, and **crossover operator** in the evolutionary loop. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A binary GA uses an 8-bit chromosome to represent a variable $x \in [2.5, 12.5]$. Given the chromosome $c = [1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]$, decode it to obtain the corresponding real value of $x$. Using the roulette wheel selection method, also compute the selection probability for an individual with fitness $f = 75$ in a population of 5 individuals with total fitness $F = 250$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — GA Architecture (7 marks) The Genetic Algorithm operates as an iterative population-based search procedure with the following architecture: **1. Initialization:** A population of $N$ chromosomes is created randomly. Each chromosome encodes one candidate solution to the optimization problem. **2. Fitness Evaluation:** The objective function $f(x)$ is evaluated for every individual. This step assigns a quality score to each candidate solution, driving the entire selection pressure of the algorithm. **3. Selection Mechanism:** Selection emulates the *survival of the fittest* principle. Individuals with higher fitness are preferentially chosen as parents for the next generation. Common schemes include: - **Roulette Wheel** (fitness-proportional) - **Tournament Selection** (pairwise competition) - **Rank-Based Selection** (order-based, scale-free) **4. Crossover Operator:** Crossover (recombination) mixes genetic material from two parents to produce offspring. It is the primary *exploitation* operator. Types include: - Single-point crossover - Two-point crossover - Uniform crossover **5. Mutation Operator:** Mutation introduces small random changes to maintain genetic diversity and prevent premature convergence. It is the primary *exploration* operator. **6. Termination:** The loop terminates when convergence criteria are met (e.g., maximum generations, fitness plateau, target fitness). **Role Summary:** - **Fitness Function:** Quantifies solution quality; defines the optimization landscape. - **Selection Mechanism:** Directs the search toward promising regions. - **Crossover Operator:** Combines building blocks (schema) of good solutions. #### Part (b) — Binary Decoding and Roulette Wheel (7 marks) **Step 1: Binary Decoding** Using the KTU formula: $$ x = a + \frac{(b-a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\ell-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i} $$ Given $a = 2.5$, $b = 12.5$, $\ell = 8$, and $c = [1,1,0,0,1,0,1,0]$. **Step 2: Binary to Integer Conversion** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i \cdot 2^{i} &= (1)(128) + (1)(64) + (0)(32) + (0)(16) + (1)(8) + (0)(4) + (1)(2) + (0)(1) \\ &= 128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 \\ &= 202 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Scaling Factor** $$ \frac{(b - a)}{(2^{\ell} - 1)} = \frac{12.5 - 2.5}{255} = \frac{10}{255} = 0.03921568 $$ **Step 4: Compute Real Value** $$ \begin{aligned} x &= 2.5 + (0.03921568)(202) \\ &= 2.5 + 7.9215 \\ &= 10.4215 \end{aligned} $$ **Final decoded value:** $x \approx 10.4215$ **Step 5: Roulette Wheel Probability** Given $f_i = 75$ and $F = 250$: $$ P_i = \frac{f_i}{F} = \frac{75}{250} = 0.30 \;\; (\text{or } 30\%) $$ This means the individual has a 30% probability of being selected as a parent in each mating event. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** > **(a)** Compare and contrast the four major types of encoding schemes in Genetic Algorithms. For each scheme, provide one engineering application and a representative chromosome example. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A real-valued GA optimizes a 3-variable function. The current population consists of 4 chromosomes with fitness values $f = [25, 50, 75, 50]$. Apply **Roulette Wheel Selection** to compute the selection probability and expected count for each individual. If **single-point crossover** is performed between Chromosome 1 (genotype) $= [1.2, 3.4, 5.6]$ and Chromosome 2 (genotype) $= [4.8, 7.0, 2.1]$ at crossover point 1, write the resulting offspring chromosomes. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Comparison of Encoding Types (7 marks) | Encoding Type | Chromosome Example | Application | |---|---|---| | **Binary** | $1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0$ | Boolean knapsack problem, feature subset selection in ML | | **Real-Valued** | $12.34, -5.67, 0.892$ | Neural network weight tuning, aerodynamic shape optimization | | **Integer** | $3, 7, 1, 5, 9$ | Job-shop scheduling, selection of structural materials | | **Permutation** | $3, 7, 2, 8, 1, 5$ | Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), vehicle routing | **Key Comparative Insights:** - **Binary** is the original Holland representation, but suffers from the **Hamming cliff** (e.g., $0111 \to 1000$ is 4-bit change for adjacent integers). - **Real-valued** is the most common in modern engineering because genotype = phenotype, eliminating decoding overhead and supporting high precision. - **Integer** encoding is suitable when variables have a finite set of meaningful discrete values; standard bit-string operators can still be used. - **Permutation** encoding is *exclusively* for ordering problems; standard crossover would produce duplicates, so specialized operators like **Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)** or **Order Crossover (OX)** are mandatory. #### Part (b) — Roulette Wheel Selection and Crossover (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute Total Fitness** $$ F = 25 + 50 + 75 + 50 = 200 $$ **Step 2: Compute Selection Probability $P_i = f_i / F$** $$ P_1 = \frac{25}{200} = 0.125, \quad P_2 = \frac{50}{200} = 0.250 $$ $$ P_3 = \frac{75}{200} = 0.375, \quad P_4 = \frac{50}{200} = 0.250 $$ **Step 3: Compute Expected Count $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ (with $N=4$)** $$ E_1 = 4 \times 0.125 = 0.5, \quad E_2 = 4 \times 0.250 = 1.0 $$ $$ E_3 = 4 \times 0.375 = 1.5, \quad E_4 = 4 \times 0.250 = 1.0 $$ **Analytical Matrix:** | Individual | Fitness $f_i$ | Probability $P_i$ | Cumulative $C_i$ | Expected Count $E_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 25 | 0.125 | 0.125 | 0.5 | | 2 | 50 | 0.250 | 0.375 | 1.0 | | 3 | 75 | 0.375 | 0.750 | 1.5 | | 4 | 50 | 0.250 | 1.000 | 1.0 | | **Total** | **200** | **1.000** | — | **4.0** | **Step 4: Apply Single-Point Crossover at Point 1** Parent 1: $[1.2, 3.4, 5.6]$ Parent 2: $[4.8, 7.0, 2.1]$ **Cut between index 0 and index 1:** - Offspring 1: First gene from Parent 1, rest from Parent 2 → $[1.2, 7.0, 2.1]$ - Offspring 2: First gene from Parent 2, rest from Parent 1 → $[4.8, 3.4, 5.6]$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Confusing decoding direction:** Students often swap MSB/LSB. Always index from the rightmost bit as $2^0$ unless the question specifies otherwise. > 2. **Forgetting the range limits:** The decoded value $x$ must lie in $[a, b]$; a value outside the range indicates a calculation error. > 3. **Probability sum check:** Cumulative probabilities MUST equal 1.0. If your sum is 0.99 or 1.01, you have a rounding or transcription error. > 4. **Crossover point indexing:** In single-point crossover, *crossover point 1* means the cut occurs *after* index 0 (i.e., between gene 0 and gene 1). This is the most common interpretation in KTU papers. > 5. **Mixing encoding types:** A common mistake is applying binary crossover to real-valued chromosomes. Always use encoding-appropriate operators. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: GA Types of Encoding** - **GA = Population + Fitness + Selection + Crossover + Mutation** (memorize the 5 components). - The GA is a **meta-heuristic framework**, not a single algorithm — operators are problem-specific. - **Encoding is the FIRST design decision** in any GA implementation. - **Binary Encoding** — string of $\{0,1\}$; compact; suffers from **Hamming cliff**; classical Holland representation. - **Real-Valued Encoding** — genes are real numbers; genotype = phenotype; best for continuous optimization; uses **SBX crossover** and **polynomial mutation**. - **Integer Encoding** — genes from a finite set; used in scheduling and resource allocation. - **Permutation Encoding** — for ordering problems (TSP, scheduling); requires **PMX/OX** crossover. - **Tree Encoding** — for Genetic Programming; variable-depth tree structures. - **Decoding Formula:** $x = a + \dfrac{(b-a)}{2^{\ell} - 1} \cdot \sum_{i} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$. - **Roulette Wheel Probability:** $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_j f_j}$. - **Selection pressure** must be balanced — too high causes premature convergence; too low causes slow progress. - **Crossover rate** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$; **Mutation rate** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ (memorize these ranges). - **Schema Theorem** (Holland): Above-average fitness schemas grow exponentially in the population, giving GAs **implicit parallelism**. - **Real-world applications:** TSP, knapsack, neural network training, antenna design, job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, symbolic regression. - **Termination criteria:** max generations, fitness plateau, time budget, target fitness. - **Engineering rule of thumb:** Use **real-valued encoding** for continuous problems; **permutation encoding** for ordering; **binary** for educational/Boolean problems. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Genetic Algorithm: Types of Encoding** > *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Selection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Heuristics for Decision Making**, **Selection** is a fundamental dual-meaning concept that appears in two distinct yet complementary forms:

**(A) The Selection Problem (Classical Algorithmic Sense):**
Given an unsorted collection of **n** comparable elements and an integer **k** (where **1 ≤ k ≤ n**), the *kth-order selection problem* asks us to identify the element that would occupy the *k*th position if the collection were sorted in non-decreasing (or non-increasing) order. The special case **k = ⌈n/2⌉** is called the **Median Selection Problem**.

**(B) Selection Operator (Metaheuristic / Evolutionary Sense):**
Within population-based metaheuristics (Genetic Algorithms, Evolution Strategies, Genetic Programming, Swarm Intelligence, etc.), **Selection** is the stochastic or deterministic mechanism that probabilistically chooses parent individuals from the current population to drive the next generation, simulating Darwinian *survival of the fittest*. This governs **selection pressure**, which directly balances *exploitation* versus *exploration* in the search space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 focus on *Selection* bridges deterministic algorithmic design (Quick Select, Median-of-Medians) with stochastic heuristic mechanisms (Roulette Wheel, Tournament, Rank-Based, Boltzmann, Truncation). Both interpretations will be examined.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy 1 — "The Cricket Auction Room" (Classical Selection)
Imagine a room full of **200 unsorted cricket players** (heights, runs, speeds all mixed up), and the coach shouts: *"Find me the 73rd best batsman by strike rate — do NOT sort everyone!"* A naive student sorts all 200 — cost **O(n log n)**. A clever coach says: *"Pick a random captain, partition the room into 'better than captain' and 'worse than captain' — you'll throw away half the room in one move!"* This is the essence of **Quick Select**, achieving average **O(n)** time.

### Analogy 2 — "The Reality TV Elimination" (Metaheuristic Selection)
Picture a reality show where **100 contestants** compete weekly. Three elimination strategies are possible:
- **Proportional / Roulette Wheel:** "Better-looking contestants get more votes to survive" — fitness proportionate.
- **Tournament:** "Pick 5 random contestants, the fittest wins the slot" — stochastic with tunable pressure.
- **Truncation / Elitist:** "Top 10% automatically survive; bottom 90% are discarded" — high-pressure, deterministic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Selection, in both senses, is the act of *pivoting* — either on a partition element (algorithm) or on fitness quality (heuristic) — to extract a specific item or subset without exhaustive enumeration. The **pivot** is the *heuristic heart* of selection.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Role |
|---|---|---|
| Population size | **N** | Typically 50 – 500 in GA |
| Tournament size | **k_t** | Typically 2 – 7 |
| Selection pressure | **β** | Higher = greedier (e.g., **β = 1.5** rank-based) |
| Pivot position rank | **k** | 1 ≤ k ≤ n |
| Partitioning index | **q** | Position of pivot after partition |
| Median index | **⌈n/2⌉** | Special kth-order statistic |
| Group size (MoM) | **5** | Standard deterministic group size |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical Selection Problem — Operational Decomposition

The **Selection Problem** is decomposed into the following logical steps (Bulleted Logic):

- **Step 1 — Base Case Truncation:** If **n ≤ 1**, return the only element (trivially, it is the *k*th element for *k = 1*).
- **Step 2 — Pivot Selection:** Choose a pivot element *p* from the input. *Heuristic quality of the pivot governs algorithm performance.*
   - *Naïve choice:* First / last element — leads to O(n²) on sorted inputs.
   - *Random choice:* Expected O(n) — robust on adversarial input.
   - *Median-of-Medians choice:* Worst-case O(n) — guaranteed linear.
- **Step 3 — Partition:** Rearrange the array into three sub-arrays: **L** (elements < p), **E** (elements = p), **G** (elements > p). Compute **q** = index of the pivot's rank.
- **Step 4 — Branching Decision:**
   - If **k ≤ |L|**, recursively select in **L**.
   - If **|L| < k ≤ |L| + |E|**, return *p* (we have found the *k*th element).
   - If **k > |L| + |E|**, recursively select in **G** with *k′ = k − |L| − |E|*.
- **Step 5 — Convergence:** Because each recursive call operates on a strictly smaller sub-array, the algorithm terminates in finite steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Step 4:** After partitioning, we have *disqualified* an entire region (either L or G) from further consideration. This is the *information-theoretic gain* — we gain at least one bit of information per partition step, unlike sorting which gains **log n** bits.

## 2.2 Quick Select — Average-Case Expected Time

Quick Select (Hoare, 1961) uses **Lomuto** or **Hoare partition** schemes. The expected number of comparisons on a random input of size *n* is:

$$E[T(n)] \;\leq\; 2n$$

The recurrence relation captures the expected cost:

$$E[T(n)] \;\leq\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{q=1}^{n}\bigl(T(q-1) + T(n-q)\bigr) + (n-1)$$

Solving via induction:

$$E[T(n)] \;\leq\; 2(n-1) \;\in\; \Theta(n)$$

## 2.3 Worst-Case Analysis (Adversarial Input)

If the pivot is the *minimum* or *maximum* element at every step:

$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) \;=\; T(n-1) + (n-1) \;\Longrightarrow\; T_{\text{worst}}(n) \;\in\; \Theta(n^2)$$

This quadratic blowup is the *failure mode* that **Median-of-Medians (MoM)** is engineered to eliminate.

## 2.4 Median-of-Medians (BFPRT) — Deterministic O(n)

The **Blum-Floyd-Pratt-Rivest-Tarjan (BFPRT)** algorithm, also called the **Median of Medians** algorithm, guarantees worst-case linear time through the following **deterministic pivot selection**:

1. Divide the **n** elements into ⌈n/5⌉ groups of **5** elements each (with one leftover group of size *n* mod 5).
2. Find the median of each group by sorting (cost: 5 log 5 = constant per group).
3. Recursively apply MoM to find the median **M** of the ⌈n/5⌉ group-medians — the **pivot**.
4. Partition around **M** and recurse on the appropriate sub-array.

### The Master Recurrence

$$T_{\text{BFPRT}}(n) \;\leq\; T\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{n}{5}\right\rceil\right) + T\!\left(\frac{7n}{10} + 6\right) + O(n)$$

The 7/10 factor arises because at least **30%** of elements are provably excluded in each step (geometric argument on 5-element groups). Solving the recurrence by the **Master Theorem** (or Akra-Bazzi method) yields:

$$T_{\text{BFPRT}}(n) \;\in\; \Theta(n)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Algorithm / Concept | Formula / Recurrence | Time Complexity | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naïve Sort-then-Index | Sort in O(n log n), then index | **O(n log n)** | O(1) – O(n) |
| 2 | Quick Select (Random Pivot) | $E[T(n)] = T(q-1) + T(n-q) + O(n)$ | **O(n)** expected | O(log n) stack |
| 3 | Quick Select (Worst Pivot) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | **O(n²)** worst | O(n) stack |
| 4 | Median of Medians (BFPRT) | $T(n) \le T(\lceil n/5 \rceil) + T(7n/10+6) + O(n)$ | **O(n)** worst | O(log n) |
| 5 | Counting Sort Selection | $T(n) = O(n + u)$ where *u* = universe | O(n + u) | O(u) |
| 6 | Tournament Size Effect | $p_{\text{win}} \approx 1 - (1 - f_i)^k$ | Probability | — |
| 7 | Fitness-Proportionate | $p_i = f_i / \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$ | Selection prob. | O(N) per draw |
| 8 | Rank-Based (Linear) | $p_i = \frac{1}{N}\left[\beta - (2\beta - 2)\frac{i-1}{N-1}\right]$ | Selection prob. | O(N log N) |
| 9 | Boltzmann Selection | $p_i = \frac{e^{f_i / T}}{\sum_j e^{f_j / T}}$ | Selection prob. | O(N) |
| 10 | Truncation Threshold | Top *τ* fraction (e.g., **τ = 0.1**) | Elitist | O(N log N) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Reminder:** Every `|x|` (absolute value) inside a markdown table is rendered as `\vert x \vert` to prevent the table-pipe from breaking the rendering. KTU 2024 valuation will award **0.5 marks** for correctly formatted equations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quick Select** powers database query optimizers and **Order-Statistic Trees** in OS scheduling.
- **BFPRT** is used in real-time embedded systems where deterministic worst-case latency is mandated (aerospace flight control, automotive ECU).
- **Tournament Selection** is the de-facto standard in commercial-grade **Genetic Algorithm libraries** (e.g., MATLAB `ga()`, Python `DEAP`, NSGA-II).
- **Roulette Wheel** is preferred in *Dynamic* objective landscapes where fitness scaling prevents premature convergence.
- **Rank-Based Selection** mitigates the *fiat-dominance problem* in highly skewed fitness distributions (e.g., TSP, neural architecture search).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Time of Randomized Quick Select

We consider the expected number of comparisons **C(n)** when the pivot is chosen uniformly at random and the array is a random permutation of distinct elements.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Recurrence

When pivot is at position *q* (with *q* uniformly distributed in [1, n]), the algorithm recurses on a sub-array of size *q − 1* (if *k ≤ q − 1*) or *n − q* (if *k > q*). Averaging over all *q* and over the two cases:

$$C(n) \;\le\; n - 1 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{q=1}^{n}\bigl[\,C(q-1) + C(n-q)\,\bigr]$$

### Step 2 — Symmetry Simplification

By symmetry, $C(q-1)$ and $C(n-q)$ produce the same sum:

$$\sum_{q=1}^{n}\bigl[C(q-1) + C(n-q)\bigr] = 2\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} C(i)$$

So the recurrence becomes:

$$C(n) \;\le\; n - 1 + \frac{2}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} C(i)$$

### Step 3 — Form the Difference Equation

Let $D(n) = C(n) - C(n-1)$. From the recurrence:

$$C(n) - C(n-1) \;\le\; 1 + \frac{2C(n-1)}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Closed-Form Bound

Assuming *C(0) = 0* and inductively $C(n-1) \le 2(n-1)$:

$$C(n) \;\le\; 1 + \frac{4(n-1)}{n} \;<\; 5$$

This yields the crude bound $C(n) \le 5n$. Refining with harmonic series:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{i} = H_n \approx \ln n + \gamma$$

gives the tight result:

$$C(n) \;\le\; 2n \;\in\; \Theta(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** Step 4 uses the inequality $1/i \le 2/(i+1)$, which telescopes to the constant-factor bound 2.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Median-of-Medians Recurrence Solution

The Master Recurrence (restated):

$$T(n) \;\le\; c_1 n + T\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{n}{5}\right\rceil\right) + T\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{7n}{10}\right\rceil + 6\right)$$

### Step 1 — Decompose the Cost

The term $c_1 n$ accounts for the cost of grouping, intra-group sorting, and the final partition pass.

### Step 2 — Bound the Recursive Magnitude

Claim: At least **3/10 of elements** are excluded in each recursive step. Geometric proof: in each 5-element group, at least 3 elements are guaranteed to fall on the *correct side* of the median-of-medians pivot. With ⌈n/5⌉ groups:

$$\text{Excluded elements} \;\ge\; 3 \cdot \left\lceil\frac{n}{5}\right\rceil \;\ge\; \frac{3n}{5} - 3$$

The smaller recursive call therefore acts on at most:

$$n - \left(\frac{3n}{5} - 3\right) - 1 \;=\; \frac{2n}{5} + 2 \;\le\; \frac{7n}{10} + 6 \quad \text{(for } n \ge 60\text{)}$$

### Step 3 — Solve via Substitution

We prove by strong induction that $T(n) \le 20 c_1 n$ for $n \ge 1$:

$$T(n) \;\le\; c_1 n + 20 c_1 \cdot \frac{n}{5} + 20 c_1\!\left(\frac{7n}{10} + 6\right)$$

$$= c_1 n + 4 c_1 n + 14 c_1 n + 120 c_1$$

$$= 19 c_1 n + 120 c_1 \;\le\; 20 c_1 n \quad \text{(for } n \ge 120\text{)}$$

This confirms $T(n) \in \Theta(n)$ — the celebrated **BFPRT** result.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tournament Selection Probability

In **k_t-sized tournament selection**, the probability that individual *i* with fitness *f_i* wins the tournament against a random opponent is:

$$p_{\text{win},i} \;=\; f_i \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{f}}$$

where $\bar{f}$ is the average fitness in the *contestant pool*. For a tournament of size $k_t$, the probability of winning *all* $k_t$ matches is:

$$p_{\text{select},i} \;\approx\; \left(\frac{f_i}{\bar{f}}\right)^{k_t}$$

### Interpretation

Increasing $k_t$ **exponentially amplifies** the selection pressure — small fitness differences are magnified. This is why **k_t = 2** is the canonical default in **NSGA-II** and **DEAP**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Selection Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Selection Pressure | Premature Convergence Risk | Parameter Sensitivity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Roulette Wheel (FPS)** | Low–Medium | High (fitness scaling needed) | Fitness distribution | Smooth landscapes |
| **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** | Low | Medium | N (population size) | Noisy fitness |
| **Tournament (k_t = 2)** | Medium | Low | $k_t$ | General-purpose |
| **Tournament (k_t = 5+)** | High | High | $k_t$ | Highly multimodal |
| **Rank-Based (Linear)** | Controllable via **β** | Low | β ∈ [1.0, 2.0] | Skewed fitness |
| **Truncation** | Very High | Very High | τ (threshold) | Micro-GA, elitism |
| **Boltzmann** | Annealed | Annealed | Temperature *T* | Dynamic scheduling |
| **SUS + Elitism** | Controlled | Minimal | Combination | Production GAs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Quick Select Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input A of size n, target k"] --> B{"n <= 1?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Return A[1] as the kth element"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Pick pivot p from A uniformly at random"]
    D --> E["Partition A into L less than p, E equal to p, G greater than p"]
    E --> F["Compute q = position rank of p after partition"]
    F --> G{"k vs q Comparison"}
    G -- "k < q" --> H["Recurse SELECT on L with same k"]
    G -- "k equals q" --> I["Return p as the kth element"]
    G -- "k greater than q" --> J["Recurse SELECT on G with k minus |L| minus |E|"]
    H --> K["Output returned value"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    C --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Median of Medians (BFPRT) Pivot Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Input: array of n elements"] --> B0["Divide into ceil n by 5 groups of size 5"]
    B0 --> C0["For each group, sort and extract the median"]
    C0 --> D0["Collect all group medians into array M of size ceil n by 5"]
    D0 --> E0["Recursively apply BFPRT to M to obtain pivot p"]
    E0 --> F0["Partition original array around pivot p"]
    F0 --> G0{"Compare k with pivot rank"}
    G0 -- "Less" --> H0["Recurse on left partition"]
    G0 -- "Equal" --> I0["Return pivot p"]
    G0 -- "Greater" --> J0["Recurse on right partition with adjusted k"]
    H0 --> K0["Final kth element"]
    I0 --> K0
    J0 --> K0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Selection Mechanisms in Metaheuristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POP["Current Population Pool"]
        I1["Individual 1 with fitness f1"]
        I2["Individual 2 with fitness f2"]
        I3["Individual 3 with fitness f3"]
        IN["Individual N with fitness fN"]
    end

    POP --> SEL["Selection Operator Module"]

    SEL --> M1["Roulette Wheel FPS"]
    SEL --> M2["Stochastic Universal Sampling"]
    SEL --> M3["Tournament Selection k t"]
    SEL --> M4["Rank Based Linear"]
    SEL --> M5["Truncation Elitist"]
    SEL --> M6["Boltzmann Annealed"]

    M1 --> MAT["Mating Pool"]
    M2 --> MAT
    M3 --> MAT
    M4 --> MAT
    M5 --> MAT
    M6 --> MAT

    MAT --> OUT["Selected Parents for Crossover and Mutation"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Tournament Selection Round

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GA as GA Engine
    participant POOL as Population
    participant T as Tournament Buffer
    GA->>POOL: Request k_t random individuals
    POOL-->>T: Return k_t candidates
    GA->>T: Evaluate fitness of each candidate
    GA->>T: Compare pairwise, retain the fittest
    T-->>GA: Return single winner
    GA->>GA: Add winner to mating pool
    GA->>GA: Repeat until pool size equals N
```
