# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the *kth-order selection problem. Differentiate between Quick Select and Median-of-Medians in terms of worst-case time complexity and pivot selection strategy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The *kth-order selection problem* seeks the element that would occupy the *k*th position in the sorted order of an unsorted collection of *n* elements, without fully sorting the array. The special case *k = ⌈n/2⌉* is the **median** problem.
- **Quick Select (1 mark):** Uses *random* or *first/last* pivot; expected O(n), worst-case **O(n²)** on adversarial (sorted) input.
- **Median-of-Medians (1 mark):** Uses *deterministic* median-of-group-medians pivot; guarantees worst-case **O(n)** via the BFPRT recurrence, at the cost of higher constant factors and 5-element grouping overhead.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**Explain *Tournament Selection* with *k_t = 3*. Compute the probability that the fittest individual is selected.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Mechanism (1.5 marks):** In *k_t*-sized tournament selection, *k_t* individuals are drawn uniformly at random from the population; they compete (pairwise or sequential), and the fittest is copied to the mating pool. The process repeats until the mating pool is filled.
- **Probability (1.5 marks):** Assuming the fittest individual has fitness *f\** and the average opponent fitness is $\bar{f}$:
$$p_{\text{win}} \;\approx\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{f^*}{\bar{f}}\right)^{k_t}$$
For $k_t = 3$ and $f^* / \bar{f} = 2$: $p_{\text{win}} \approx 1 - (1 - 0.5)^3 = 1 - 0.125 = 0.875$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A. (a) Describe the Quick Select algorithm with a suitable pseudocode. Derive its expected time complexity and explain why it fails on sorted input. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pseudocode (3 marks):**
```text
QUICK_SELECT(A, low, high, k):
    if low == high:
        return A[low]
    pivot_index = PARTITION(A, low, high)
    rank = pivot_index - low + 1
    if k == rank:
        return A[pivot_index]
    else if k < rank:
        return QUICK_SELECT(A, low, pivot_index - 1, k)
    else:
        return QUICK_SELECT(A, pivot_index + 1, high, k - rank)
```

**Expected Time Derivation (3 marks):**
The recurrence with random pivot:
$$C(n) = n - 1 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{q=1}^{n}\bigl[C(q-1) + C(n-q)\bigr]$$
Solving yields $C(n) \le 2n$ — expected linear.

**Failure on Sorted Input (1 mark):**
If the pivot is the *first* or *last* element and the input is sorted, the partition is maximally unbalanced: one sub-array has size *n − 1* and the other has size 0. The recurrence becomes $T(n) = T(n-1) + n - 1 = \Theta(n^2)$, defeating the linear expectation.

**A. (b) Explain the Median-of-Medians (BFPRT) algorithm. Prove that it runs in O(n) worst-case time using the master recurrence. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):**
BFPRT deterministically selects the median-of-medians as the pivot:
1. Divide *n* elements into ⌈n/5⌉ groups of 5.
2. Sort each group; extract the median.
3. Recursively find the median of the medians — this is the pivot.
4. Partition around the pivot; recurse on the relevant side.

**Master Recurrence (2 marks):**
$$T(n) \;\le\; c_1 n + T\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{n}{5}\right\rceil\right) + T\!\left(\frac{7n}{10} + 6\right)$$

**Inductive Proof of O(n) (2 marks):**
Assume $T(n) \le 20c_1 n$ for all smaller *n*. Substituting:
$$T(n) \;\le\; c_1 n + 20c_1 \cdot \frac{n}{5} + 20c_1 \cdot \frac{7n}{10} + 120c_1 = 19c_1 n + 120c_1 \le 20c_1 n$$
The base case $n \le 140$ is verified by direct computation. Therefore $T(n) \in \Theta(n)$ in the worst case.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**B. (a) Discuss various parent selection mechanisms in Genetic Algorithms. Compare Roulette Wheel, Tournament, and Rank-Based selection with respect to selection pressure and premature convergence. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism Descriptions (3 marks):**
- **Roulette Wheel (FPS):** Probability of selection = $f_i / \sum_j f_j$. Biased towards fittest; sensitive to fitness scaling.
- **Tournament:** Draw *k_t* individuals; the fittest wins. Parameter *k_t* controls pressure.
- **Rank-Based:** Sort by fitness; assign selection probability by rank. $p_i = \frac{1}{N}\left[\beta - (2\beta - 2)\frac{i-1}{N-1}\right]$ with $\beta \in [1.0, 2.0]$.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Roulette | Tournament | Rank-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selection Pressure | Variable | Tunable via *k_t* | Tunable via β |
| Premature Convergence | High | Low–Medium | Low |
| Fitness Sensitivity | High | Low | Insensitive |
| Computation | O(N) | O(k_t) | O(N log N) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
For general-purpose GAs, **Tournament with k_t = 2** is the recommended default due to its balance of pressure, robustness, and O(1) per-draw cost.

**B. (b) Formulate the Boltzmann Selection mechanism. Explain how the temperature parameter *T* controls the exploration–exploitation tradeoff during the GA run. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Formulation (3 marks):**
Boltzmann selection assigns selection probability:
$$p_i \;=\; \frac{e^{f_i / T}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{f_j / T}}$$
where *T* is the **temperature** (analogous to statistical mechanics).

**High-Temperature Behavior (2 marks):**
As $T \to \infty$, $e^{f_i/T} \to 1$ for all *i*, so $p_i \to 1/N$ — **uniform random selection** (pure exploration, no pressure).

**Low-Temperature Behavior (2 marks):**
As $T \to 0^+$, the dominant term is $e^{f_{\max}/T}$, and only the fittest individual(s) have non-vanishing probability — **pure exploitation** (deterministic elitism).

**Annealing Schedule (extra credit, KTU bonus):**
A common cooling schedule is $T(t) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^t$ with $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.99]$, gradually transitioning from exploration to exploitation — analogous to *simulated annealing* by Kirkpatrick et al. (1983).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Selection Topic):**
> 1. **Confusing Selection Problem with Sorting:** Students often default to O(n log n) sorting — KTU explicitly penalizes this (lose 2–3 marks). Always justify *why* O(n) is achievable.
> 2. **Misstating BFPRT group size:** The correct grouping is **5**; using 3 or 7 changes the constant factor and breaks the worst-case linear guarantee.
> 3. **Forgetting to recompute *k* in recursion:** When recursing on the right sub-array, the new *k′ = k − |L| − |E|*. Many students pass the *same k* — this is a **3-mark error**.
> 4. **Tournament probability formula:** Students often write $p = f_i / \bar{f}$ *without* raising to the $k_t$-th power for multi-round tournaments.
> 5. **Boltzmann units:** The exponent $f_i / T$ must be **dimensionless** — divide by $k_B$ (Boltzmann constant) if comparing to physical systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Selection**

- **Selection problem = find the kth smallest of n elements** without full sorting.
- **Quick Select** uses partition + recursion — **O(n) expected, O(n²) worst**.
- **Random pivot** breaks adversarial input degeneracy.
- **Median-of-Medians (BFPRT)** guarantees **worst-case O(n)** via 5-element grouping.
- **BFPRT master recurrence:** $T(n) \le T(n/5) + T(7n/10) + O(n) \Rightarrow \Theta(n)$.
- **Selection operator in metaheuristics** governs the *exploitation–exploration* balance.
- **Roulette Wheel (FPS):** $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$ — sensitive to fitness scaling.
- **Tournament Selection:** Stochastic, parameter $k_t$; default $k_t = 2$ (NSGA-II).
- **Rank-Based Selection:** Parameter $\beta \in [1, 2]$; immune to fitness scaling.
- **Truncation:** Top *τ* survive; very high pressure, used in micro-GAs.
- **Boltzmann Selection:** $p_i = e^{f_i/T} / \sum e^{f_j/T}$; **T anneals** from ∞ to 0.
- **SUS (Stochastic Universal Sampling):** Low-variance variant of FPS; one spin, N pointers.
- **Elitism** preserves the best individual across generations — never discard the global best.
- **Selection pressure (β or k_t)** is the *dial* a practitioner tunes to control GA convergence.
- **Premature convergence** = population collapses to a non-optimal genotype — controlled by *decreasing* pressure over time.
- **KTU favorite questions:** Median-of-Medians derivation, Tournament probability, Boltzmann temperature analysis.
- **Pitfall to memorize:** Always *adjust k* when recursing on the right partition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crossover and Mutation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crossover and Mutation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Crossover (Recombination Operator)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Crossover* (also called *Recombination*) is a **stochastic search operator** in **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** that generates one or more *offspring* (child chromosomes) by combining segments of the *genetic material* (encoding) taken from two or more *parent* solutions. It is the principal **exploitation mechanism** of a Genetic Algorithm (GA), exploiting the high-fitness *schemata* already discovered in the population.

- **Parents** → two or more selected candidate solutions (chromosomes).
- **Offspring** → newly produced solutions intended to inherit the "good traits" of both parents.
- **Crossover Rate (p_c)** → the probability (typically **0.6 ≤ p_c ≤ 0.95**) with which the crossover operator is applied to a selected mating pair.

### 1.2 Mutation Operator

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Mutation* is a **unary stochastic perturbation operator** that alters one or more *genes* of a single chromosome with a small probability (**p_m**), thereby introducing **genetic diversity** into the population. It is the principal **exploration mechanism** of a GA, protecting the population from premature convergence to local optima.

- **Mutation Rate (p_m)** → the probability of flipping/perturbing a single gene (typically **0.001 ≤ p_m ≤ 0.1**).
- **Purpose** → preserves **genetic diversity** and prevents the *loss of alleles*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Family Recipe Book":**
> Imagine two master chefs, Chef A and Chef B, each holding a secret recipe book.
> - **Crossover** is like both chefs sitting at a table, ripping out the first 5 pages from Chef A's book and the last 5 pages from Chef B's book, and stapling them together into a **brand-new hybrid recipe book** for the apprentice. The apprentice now benefits from "the best of both worlds" — that is exploitation of proven knowledge.
> - **Mutation** is like the apprentice, while cooking, *accidentally* adding a pinch of an unusual spice that wasn't in either original recipe. Sometimes it ruins the dish, but sometimes it accidentally invents a new Michelin-star flavor — that is exploration of brand-new regions of taste-space.
>
> **Why both?** A recipe book made *only* by stapling will eventually become a static cookbook (premature convergence). A recipe book made *only* by random spice-tossing will never settle on a coherent dish (no exploitation). **The combination is the genius of Genetic Algorithms.**

### 1.4 Why Crossover + Mutation are Complementary

| Aspect | Crossover (Exploitation) | Mutation (Exploration) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operates on** | Two or more parents | Single individual |
| **Effect** | Combines existing building blocks | Introduces new building blocks |
| **Risk if used alone** | Premature convergence | Random walk (no convergence) |
| **Biological metaphor** | Sexual reproduction (meiosis) | Asexual genetic error (copying mistake) |
| **Typical probability** | **High (≈ 0.8)** | **Low (≈ 0.01)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Examiners explicitly test whether students understand that **crossover is an EXPLOITATION operator** and **mutation is an EXPLORATION operator** — confusing the two is a guaranteed 3-mark loss in Module 1 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crossover — Taxonomy and Operational Steps

There are **two broad families** of crossover operators:

**(A) Crossover for Binary / Real-Valued Encodings**
1. **Single-Point Crossover**
2. **Two-Point (Multi-Point) Crossover**
3. **Uniform (Bit-wise) Crossover**
4. **Arithmetic Crossover** (for real-coded GAs)

**(B) Crossover for Permutation Encodings (e.g., TSP)**
1. **Order Crossover (OX)**
2. **Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)**
3. **Cycle Crossover (CX)**

> [!NOTE]
> For Module 1, the KTU syllabus emphasizes the **first family (binary / real-valued)**. Permutation crossovers are introduced in higher modules under Combinatorial Optimization.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Single-Point Crossover

1. Two parent chromosomes P₁ and P₂ of length **L** are selected via a selection scheme (Roulette-Wheel, Tournament, Rank-based).
2. A random integer *cut-point* **k** is sampled uniformly from **{1, 2, …, L − 1}**.
3. With probability **p_c**, crossover is performed:
   - Offspring C₁ = P₁[1 : k]  concatenated with  P₂[k+1 : L]
   - Offspring C₂ = P₂[1 : k]  concatenated with  P₁[k+1 : L]
4. With probability **(1 − p_c)**, offspring C₁ = P₁, C₂ = P₂ (parents cloned unchanged).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Uniform Crossover

- For each gene position *i* ∈ {1, 2, …, L}, a coin-flip **u_i ~ U(0,1)** is drawn.
- If **u_i < 0.5**, then C₁[i] ← P₁[i] and C₂[i] ← P₂[i].
- Otherwise, C₁[i] ← P₂[i] and C₂[i] ← P₁[i].
- **Mask** M = (u_1, u_2, …, u_L) governs the swap.

### 2.4 Mutation — Operational Logic

**(A) Bit-Flip Mutation (Binary Encoding)**
- For each gene *i* in a chromosome of length L, sample *r_i ~ U(0,1)*.
- If **r_i < p_m**, flip the bit (0 → 1, or 1 → 0).

**(B) Gaussian Mutation (Real-Valued Encoding)**
- For each real-valued gene *x_i*, perturb it by sampling a small Gaussian noise:
  $$x_i^{\text{new}} = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$
  where **σ** is the *mutation step-size* (often adapted by self-adaptive strategies).

**(C) Swap / Inversion / Scramble Mutation (Permutation Encoding)**
- **Swap**: exchange two randomly chosen positions.
- **Inversion**: reverse a randomly chosen sub-segment.
- **Scramble**: randomly shuffle a randomly chosen sub-segment.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every key equation, parameter, and constraint** required to answer Module 1 questions on Crossover & Mutation in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Parameters & Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crossover Rate | $p_c \in [0.6,\ 0.95]$ | Higher in early generations |
| 2 | Mutation Rate | $p_m \in [0.001,\ 0.1]$ | Inversely related to chromosome length L |
| 3 | Expected # of mutated genes per chromosome | $E[m] = L \cdot p_m$ | For L = 100, p_m = 0.01 ⇒ E[m] = 1 gene |
| 4 | Bit-Flip Mutation rule | $x_i^{\text{new}} = 1 - x_i$ if $r_i < p_m$ | $r_i \sim U(0,1)$ |
| 5 | Gaussian Mutation | $x_i^{\text{new}} = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Bounded by domain $x_i \in [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ |
| 6 | Single-Point Crossover cut | $k \sim U\{1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$ | Uniform on integer lattice |
| 7 | Uniform Crossover mask | $u_i \sim U(0,1)$ per gene | Swap when $u_i \ge 0.5$ |
| 8 | Arithmetic Crossover | $C_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$ | $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, often $\alpha = 0.5$ |
| 9 | Two-Point Crossover | cuts at $k_1, k_2$ with $k_1 < k_2$ | $k_1, k_2 \sim U\{1, L-1\}$ |
| 10 | Schema Theorem (building-block growth) | $E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$ | Goldberg (1989) — foundation of GA theory |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Crossover + Mutation are deployed in industry & research:**
> - **Aerospace**: NASA evolved antenna shapes for the ST5 mission using a GA — crossover combined good segments of antenna designs, mutation introduced novel bends.
> - **VLSI Floor-Planning**: crossover recombines successful sub-block layouts; mutation perturbs block orientations.
> - **Financial Portfolio Optimization**: real-coded GA with arithmetic crossover + Gaussian mutation.
> - **Job-Shop / TSP Scheduling**: permutation GA with **Order Crossover (OX)** + **Swap Mutation**.
> - **Hyper-parameter tuning of Deep Learning models**: population-based training uses crossover-style recombination of hyper-parameter vectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single-Point Crossover + Bit-Flip Mutation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **fully worked out binary GA step** that has appeared in similar form in past KTU ESE papers. Practice this example until you can reproduce it on the answer script in under 6 minutes.

**Given:**
- Two parent chromosomes, each of length **L = 8** (binary encoding):
  - P₁ = `1 0 1 1 | 0 0 1 0`
  - P₂ = `0 1 0 0 | 1 1 0 1`
- Random crossover cut-point **k = 4** (after the 4th gene).
- Crossover rate **p_c = 0.85** ⇒ crossover *does* occur.
- Random mutation mask (positions flagged where r < p_m) ⇒ mutation at **position 6** of C₁ and **position 2** of C₂.
- Mutation rate **p_m = 0.05** (satisfied for the two flagged positions).

**Step 1 — Apply Single-Point Crossover at k = 4**

The vertical bar `|` marks the cut. Offspring inherit the **left segment** from one parent and the **right segment** from the other.

- C₁ = (P₁ left of cut) ∥ (P₂ right of cut)
- C₂ = (P₂ left of cut) ∥ (P₁ right of cut)

Written out explicitly:

$$C_1 = \underbrace{1\ 0\ 1\ 1}_{\text{from } P_1,\ \text{genes } 1\text{–}4} \ \underbrace{1\ 1\ 0\ 1}_{\text{from } P_2,\ \text{genes } 5\text{–}8}$$

$$C_2 = \underbrace{0\ 1\ 0\ 0}_{\text{from } P_2,\ \text{genes } 1\text{–}4} \ \underbrace{0\ 0\ 1\ 0}_{\text{from } P_1,\ \text{genes } 5\text{–}8}$$

So before mutation:

- C₁ = `1 0 1 1 1 1 0 1`
- C₂ = `0 1 0 0 0 0 1 0`

**Step 2 — Apply Bit-Flip Mutation**

Flip the bit at the flagged positions (gene 6 of C₁ and gene 2 of C₂):

- At position 6 of C₁: bit = 1, flip ⇒ 0
- At position 2 of C₂: bit = 1, flip ⇒ 0

$$C_1^{\text{mut}} = 1\ 0\ 1\ 1\ 1\ \mathbf{0}\ 0\ 1$$

$$C_2^{\text{mut}} = 0\ \mathbf{0}\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1\ 0$$

**Step 3 — Verify Hamming Distances (Analytical Sanity Check)**

$$\text{Hamming}(P_1, P_2) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert P_1[i] - P_2[i] \vert = 8\ \text{(all bits differ)}$$

$$\text{Hamming}(C_1, P_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert C_1^{\text{mut}}[i] - P_1[i] \vert = 4$$

$$\text{Hamming}(C_2, P_2) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert C_2^{\text{mut}}[i] - P_2[i] \vert = 4$$

**Interpretation:** Crossover reduced the distance of each child to its *corresponding* parent from 8 to 4 — the children genuinely inherit half of each parent's building blocks, exactly as the Schema Theorem predicts. Mutation perturbed 2 of 16 total bits (12.5%), within the expected $L \cdot p_m = 0.4$ per-chromosome average.

### 3.2 Derivations of the Schema Theorem (Holland's Fundamental Theorem of GAs)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Schema Theorem** is the *theoretical justification* for why crossover works, and is regularly asked as a 7-mark derivation in the KTU 2024 ESE Part B.

A *schema* **H** is a template over the alphabet {0, 1, *}, where * is a "don't care" symbol. Two key schema properties:

- **Order o(H)** = number of fixed (non-`*`) positions in H.
- **Defining length δ(H)** = distance between the first and last fixed positions in H.

**Step 1 — Effect of Selection on Schema H**

Under fitness-proportional (Roulette-Wheel) selection, the expected number of copies of schema H at generation t+1 is:

$$E[m(H, t+1)] = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$$

where **f(H)** is the *average fitness* of chromosomes matching H, and **f̄** is the *population average fitness*.

**Step 2 — Effect of Single-Point Crossover on Schema H**

A schema H is *destroyed* by single-point crossover if the cut-point k falls strictly between its first and last fixed positions. The probability of survival through crossover is:

$$P_s^{\text{co}} = 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of Bit-Flip Mutation on Schema H**

A schema H is destroyed by mutation if *any* of its o(H) fixed bits is flipped. Survival probability is:

$$P_s^{\text{mut}} = (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \approx 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)\quad \text{(for small } p_m\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine Selection × Crossover × Mutation (Independence Assumption)**

$$E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[\, 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\,\right]$$

This is the **Schema Theorem**. It states: *short, low-order, above-average-fitness schemata grow exponentially across generations*. These are called **building blocks** — crossover's exploitation target.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Crossover When?

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often ask "compare any two crossover operators." The matrix below is your ready-made answer scaffold.

| Property | Single-Point | Two-Point | Uniform | Arithmetic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **# of cut-points** | 1 | 2 (or k) | 0 (per-gene coin flip) | 0 (weighted blend) |
| **Positional Bias** | **High** (endpoints co-inherited) | Medium | **Low** | None |
| **Distributional Bias** | Low | Low | **High** (50-50 mix) | None |
| **Best for** | Tight linkage of bits | Moderate linkage | Independent bits | Real-coded continuous |
| **Risk** | Hitch-hiking | Moderate disruption | High disruption | Loss of diversity if α static |
| **Time complexity** | $O(L)$ | $O(L)$ | $O(L)$ | $O(L)$ |

### 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis — How p_c and p_m Drive Search Behavior

A standard result (De Jong, 1975) is the trade-off surface:

- **High p_c, Low p_m** → fast convergence, high risk of *premature convergence*.
- **Low p_c, High p_m** → slow convergence, behaves more like a random walk.
- **Balanced (p_c ≈ 0.8, p_m ≈ 0.01)** → empirically optimal across many benchmarks (De Jong's 5-function test suite).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU 14-marker is: *"Discuss the role of crossover and mutation rates on GA convergence with a labeled graph."* Use the trade-off description above as your answer backbone, and pair it with a typical convergence curve in SECTION_4.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The GA Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows **exactly where** crossover and mutation fit within the full Genetic Algorithm loop, as expected in KTU 2024 Module 1 descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize Population P of N chromosomes]
    B --> C[Evaluate Fitness f of every chromosome]
    C --> D{Stop Criterion Met?}
    D -- Yes --> Z([Return Best Solution])
    D -- No --> E[Selection: Roulette or Tournament]
    E --> F[Apply Crossover with probability pc]
    F --> G[Apply Mutation with probability pm]
    G --> H[Form New Offspring Population Q]
    H --> I[Replace P with Q Elitism Optional]
    I --> C
    classDef op fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#E6F8E6,stroke:#16A34A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class F,G op
    class C,D,I ctrl
    class A,Z term
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Single-Point Crossover with Cut-Point k = 4

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PARENT 1   1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0"] --> C1["OFFSPRING 1   1 0 1 1 1 1 0 1"]
    P2["PARENT 2   0 1 0 0 1 1 0 1"] --> C1
    P2 --> C2["OFFSPRING 2   0 1 0 0 0 0 1 0"]
    P1 --> C2
    CUT["CUT AT k = 4"] -.-> C1
    CUT -.-> C2
    MUT1["FLIP GENE 6: 1 TO 0"] -.-> C1M["MUTATED C1   1 0 1 1 1 0 0 1"]
    C1 --> C1M
    MUT2["FLIP GENE 2: 1 TO 0"] -.-> C2M["MUTATED C2   0 0 0 0 0 0 1 0"]
    C2 --> C2M
    classDef parent fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#000
    classDef child fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000
    classDef mut fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    class P1,P2 parent
    class C1,C2,C1M,C2M child
    class MUT1,MUT2,CUT mut
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Crossover Operator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["What is the encoding of the chromosome?"] --> Q1{Binary or Real?}
    Q --> Q2{Permutation or Sequence?}
    Q1 -- Binary --> R1["Single-Point or Two-Point Crossover if bits are linked"]
    Q1 -- Binary --> R2["Uniform Crossover if bits are independent"]
    Q1 -- Real --> R3["Arithmetic or BLX-alpha Crossover"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R4["Order Crossover OX or PMX"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R5["Cycle Crossover CX for preserving position info"]
    classDef q fill:#F5F3FF,stroke:#6D28D9,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#ECFDF5,stroke:#047857,color:#000
    class Q,Q1,Q2 q
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 r
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Trade-off Surface — Convergence Behavior vs (p_c, p_m)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZONE_A["HIGH pc LOW pm Zone"]
        A1["Fast initial gain"]
        A2["Premature convergence risk"]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_B["BALANCED Zone Recommended"]
        B1["Steady improvement"]
        B2["Diversity maintained"]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_C["LOW pc HIGH pm Zone"]
        C1["Slow convergence"]
        C2["Random walk behavior"]
    end
    ZONE_A --> ZONE_B
    ZONE_B --> ZONE_C
    classDef bad fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,color:#000
    class A1,A2 bad
    class B1,B2 good
    class C1,C2 neutral
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams in your answer script:** Examiners reward a *labeled* diagram. Always write a one-line caption beneath the figure (e.g., *"Figure 1: Block diagram showing the placement of crossover and mutation within the GA main loop."*). This alone is worth **1–2 easy marks** in descriptive Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between crossover and mutation in a Genetic Algorithm. Why is mutation rate typically kept much lower than crossover rate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Crossover | Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| Operates on | Two or more parents (binary/binary → binary) | A single chromosome |
| Role | **Exploitation** — combines existing building blocks | **Exploration** — introduces new genetic material |
| Typical probability | **p_c ≈ 0.6 – 0.95** (high) | **p_m ≈ 0.001 – 0.1** (low) |
| Biological analogy | Sexual reproduction | Copying error during DNA replication |
| Effect on diversity | Tends to reduce diversity (converges) | Tends to increase diversity (diverges) |

Crossover is the **primary search driver** in GAs — without it, the algorithm degenerates into a parallel random walk. Hence p_c is set high. Mutation is a *background* operator that only needs to occasionally refresh lost alleles, so a low p_m is sufficient. (3 marks — full credit only if all four rows plus the p_c / p_m justification are present.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is a schema in GA terminology? Define the **order** and **defining length** of a schema, with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **schema** is a similarity template describing a subset of chromosomes with identical bits at certain positions, defined over the alphabet {0, 1, *}, where * is a *don't-care* symbol.

- **Order o(H)** = number of *fixed* (non-`*`) positions in H.
- **Defining length δ(H)** = distance between the *first* and *last* fixed positions of H.

**Example:** H = `* 1 0 * 1 * * 0`
- Fixed positions = {2, 3, 5, 8} ⇒ **o(H) = 4**
- First fixed = position 2, Last fixed = position 8 ⇒ **δ(H) = 8 − 2 = 6**

(3 marks — 1 for the schema definition, 1 for o(H), 1 for δ(H) with the example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question in a module test has an **internal choice**. Both Question A and Question B below are full 14-mark, 7+7 sub-part structures — answer **only one** as per the choice instruction printed on your question paper.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the operation of a **Genetic Algorithm** with a neat block diagram. Explain in detail the role of the **crossover operator**, with a worked example of **single-point crossover** on two 8-bit chromosomes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mutation operator** in GAs. Discuss **bit-flip** and **Gaussian mutation** with their mathematical formulations. State the typical range of mutation probability and justify why a low value is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

A Genetic Algorithm is a population-based metaheuristic that mimics natural selection. The main loop is:

1. **Initialize** a population of *N* random chromosomes.
2. **Evaluate** fitness *f(x)* of every chromosome.
3. **Repeat** until termination:
   a. **Select** parents (Roulette-Wheel / Tournament / Rank).
   b. **Crossover** (with probability p_c) → offspring.
   c. **Mutate** (with probability p_m) → perturbed offspring.
   d. **Evaluate** offspring and **replace** the population (optionally with elitism).
4. **Return** the best chromosome found.

**Crossover** combines two parents to exploit good "building blocks." **Worked Example** (already derived in §3.1):

- P₁ = `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0`, P₂ = `0 1 0 0 1 1 0 1`, cut at k = 4.
- C₁ = `1 0 1 1 1 1 0 1`, C₂ = `0 1 0 0 0 0 1 0`.

Crossover exploits the schema theorem — short, high-fitness schemata from each parent are concatenated in the offspring.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Mutation** is a unary operator that perturbs a single chromosome.

- **Bit-flip mutation (binary encoding):**
  $$x_i^{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} 1 - x_i, & \text{if } r_i < p_m \\ x_i, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\quad r_i \sim U(0,1)$$

- **Gaussian mutation (real encoding):**
  $$x_i^{\text{new}} = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2), \quad \text{clipped to } [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$$

**Typical range:** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ (De Jong, 1975).

**Why low?** A high p_m turns the GA into a random search, destroying the building blocks assembled by crossover. A low p_m keeps most genes intact while occasionally refreshing lost alleles — this is the **exploration–exploitation balance** that makes GAs work. For a chromosome of length L, the expected number of mutated genes is $L \cdot p_m$, which should typically be **less than 1**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Schema Theorem** of Genetic Algorithms. Clearly define the concepts of **order** *o(H)* and **defining length** *δ(H)*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **single-point, two-point, and uniform crossover** using the dimensions of positional bias, disruption, and best-use case. Also, list any **three** variants of mutation used in real-coded GAs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The **Schema Theorem** (Holland, 1975; formalized by Goldberg, 1989) describes how short, low-order, above-average-fitness schemata (called *building blocks*) propagate exponentially across generations under the combined effect of selection, crossover, and mutation:

$$E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[\, 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\,\right]$$

Definitions:
- **Schema H**: a similarity template over {0, 1, *}.
- **Order o(H)**: number of *fixed* positions.
- **Defining length δ(H)**: distance between the first and last fixed positions.

**Interpretation:**
- The factor $f(H)/\bar{f}$ is the **selection pressure** (amplifies above-average schemata).
- The bracket term is the **disruption probability** from crossover and mutation.
- Low o(H) and low δ(H) make the bracket term close to 1, so building blocks survive.

**(b) [7 marks]**

| Property | Single-Point | Two-Point | Uniform |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positional bias** | High | Medium | None |
| **Distributional bias** | Low | Low | High (50-50) |
| **Disruption** | Low (cut only) | Medium | High |
| **Best for** | Tight linkage | Moderate linkage | Independent bits |

**Three mutation variants for real-coded GAs:**
1. **Uniform mutation:** $x_i^{\text{new}} \sim U(x_{\min}, x_{\max})$ with probability p_m.
2. **Gaussian mutation:** $x_i^{\text{new}} = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ clipped to domain.
3. **Polynomial / Power mutation** (Deb & Goyal, 1996): a self-adaptive perturbation that respects the search space boundaries.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners routinely deduct marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing exploitation vs. exploration.** Many students write "crossover introduces diversity" — this is WRONG. Crossover *exploits* existing diversity; mutation *introduces* it. −1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the Schema Theorem's inequality direction.** The theorem is a *lower bound* (≥), not an equality. Examiners check this. −1 mark.
> 3. **Using p_m too high in numerical examples.** If you solve a problem and choose p_m = 0.5, the examiner will mark the result as "unrealistic — violates De Jong's tuning." −1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the worked example for crossover.** A 7-mark question that *says* "with an example" will lose 2 marks if you only describe the operator in words.
> 5. **Wrong formula for Hamming distance.** Use $H(P_1, P_2) = \sum_i \vert P_1[i] - P_2[i] \vert$, *not* $\sum_i (P_1[i] + P_2[i])$.
> 6. **Mixing up order o(H) and defining length δ(H).** A common slip. Mnemonic: **o**rder = **c**ount, **d**efining length = **d**istance.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (read once before entering the exam hall):**
>
> - [ ] Crossover is an **exploitation** operator; mutation is an **exploration** operator. (Most-tested single fact.)
> - [ ] Typical **p_c ≈ 0.6 – 0.95**; typical **p_m ≈ 0.001 – 0.1**.
> - [ ] Single-point crossover uses one cut-point *k*; offspring = left-parent₁ + right-parent₂ and vice-versa.
> - [ ] Uniform crossover uses a per-gene coin flip; **no positional bias** but **high disruption**.
> - [ ] Bit-flip mutation: invert gene if *r < p_m*, where $r \sim U(0,1)$.
> - [ ] Gaussian mutation: $x_i^{\text{new}} = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, then clip to domain.
> - [ ] **Schema Theorem** (memorize the equation and the inequality sign):
>
>       $$E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \, o(H)\right]$$
>
> - [ ] **Order o(H)** = number of fixed bits; **defining length δ(H)** = last-fixed − first-fixed.
> - [ ] Building blocks are *short, low-order, above-average-fitness* schemata.
> - [ ] De Jong's tuning: high p_c, low p_m, small population, elitism ⇒ best all-round GA configuration.
> - [ ] In a worked example, always show the **cut-point** as a vertical bar `|` in the parent chromosomes.
> - [ ] Crossover + Mutation is **complementary** — never one without the other for a robust GA.
> - [ ] Arithmetic crossover (real-coded): $C_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$, $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
> - [ ] For permutation problems, use **Order Crossover (OX)** or **PMX** — *not* bit-flip.
> - [ ] Premature convergence is a consequence of too-high p_c and too-low p_m — a classic ESE essay hook.
> - [ ] Schema Theorem is the **theoretical justification** for why GAs work; Holland (1975), Goldberg (1989).
> - [ ] Remember to **draw a labeled block diagram** in any 7-mark question that says "with a neat sketch" — easy 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
