## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tabu Search Principles**
>
> - [x] **Inventor:** Fred Glover, 1986 — a *deterministic* metaheuristic for combinatorial optimisation.
> - [x] **Core idea:** accept *worse* moves deliberately to escape local optima; use memory to avoid cycling.
> - [x] **Tabu List $T_t$:** set of forbidden moves with counter-based tenure $\theta$.
> - [x] **Tabu Tenure $\theta$:** number of iterations a move is forbidden; empirical rule $\theta \approx \sqrt{\vert N(s) \vert}$.
> - [x] **Aspiration Criterion $A(s)$:** if $f(m(s_t)) < f^*$, override tabu status.
> - [x] **Short-Term Memory:** recency — implemented by tabu list.
> - [x] **Long-Term Memory:** frequency / distance — drives intensification & diversification.
> - [x] **Intensification:** re-search around the *best-so-far* (elite archive) — reward frequent attributes.
> - [x] **Diversification:** explore *unvisited* regions — penalise over-used attributes.
> - [x] **Transition equation:** $s_{t+1} = \arg\min \{ f(m(s_t)) : m \in N(s_t) \setminus T_t \cup A_t \}$.
> - [x] **Stopping rules:** $t \geq t_{\max}$, $\vert f^* - f(s_t) \vert < \epsilon$ for $k$ iterations, or CPU limit.
> - [x] **Strengths:** deterministic, uses memory effectively, handles discrete/large-scale problems.
> - [x] **Limitations:** parameter sensitive (tenure), no guarantee of global optimum.
> - [x] **Applications:** TSP, VRP, scheduling, VLSI, network design, bioinformatics, finance.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always present a TS numerical answer as a **3-column iteration table** (Iteration # — Current Solution — Tabu List) with cost shown beside each candidate. Examiners award partial marks generously for *structured* presentation, even if arithmetic has minor slips.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighbourhood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Neighbourhood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Heuristics for Decision Making** and **Metaheuristic Optimization**, a **Neighbourhood** is formally defined as a structured set of candidate solutions that can be obtained from a given current solution by applying exactly one elementary modification, called a **move operator** or **transition operator**.

Mathematically, for a given solution $s \in S$ (where $S$ denotes the entire feasible **solution space** or **search space**), the neighbourhood of $s$ is denoted as:

$$N(s) \subseteq S$$

The mapping $N : S \rightarrow 2^{S}$ is known as the **Neighbourhood Structure** or **Neighbourhood Function**. Every element $s' \in N(s)$ is termed a **neighbour** of $s$, and the transformation $m : S \rightarrow S$ that produces $s'$ from $s$ is the **move** or **neighbourhood operator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The concept of Neighbourhood is the foundational pillar upon which **Local Search algorithms**, **Simulated Annealing**, **Tabu Search**, and **Variable Neighbourhood Search (VNS)** are constructed. Mastering this concept is mandatory for solving KTU numerical/design problems on metaheuristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Block" Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a busy city (this is your **current solution** $s$). A *neighbourhood* is simply the set of all places you can reach by taking **exactly one step** in any direction — the buildings directly adjacent to yours.

- If you define "one step" as walking to the next building → you get a **small, dense neighbourhood** (e.g., a 4-connected grid).
- If you define "one step" as taking a taxi to any building within 5 km → you get a **large, sparse neighbourhood**.

The size and shape of the neighbourhood determines how aggressively your search algorithm explores the city. If your neighbourhood is too small, you may miss the best restaurant; if it is too large, you waste time exploring bad ones.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Solution Space ($S$):** The complete set of all feasible candidate solutions to the decision-making problem.
- **Current Solution ($s$):** The specific candidate solution that the heuristic algorithm is presently examining.
- **Move Operator ($m$):** The deterministic or stochastic rule that transforms $s$ into a neighbour $s'$.
- **Neighbourhood Size ($\vert N(s) \vert$):** The cardinality (count) of neighbour solutions around $s$. This may be constant or vary with $s$.
- **Reachability:** A property indicating whether any solution $s^* \in S$ can be reached from $s$ through a finite sequence of neighbourhood moves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Principle:** A heuristic algorithm traverses the solution space $S$ by iteratively moving from $s$ to some $s' \in N(s)$. Without a well-defined neighbourhood, no local-search-based heuristic can function.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Properties of a Well-Defined Neighbourhood

A neighbourhood structure $N(s)$ suitable for heuristic decision-making must satisfy the following operational properties:

1. **Non-emptiness:** $N(s) \neq \emptyset$ for all $s \in S$. The algorithm must always have at least one direction to move.
2. **Reachability (Strong Connectivity):** For every pair of solutions $s_1, s_2 \in S$, there must exist a finite sequence of moves such that $s_2$ is reachable from $s_1$. Formally, there exists a sequence $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k$ where $s_{i+1} \in N(s_i)$.
3. **Computational Tractability:** The neighbourhood must be enumerable in polynomial time $O(\text{poly}(n))$; otherwise, the heuristic becomes computationally infeasible.
4. **Bounded Size:** $\vert N(s) \vert$ should be finite and preferably polynomial in the problem dimension $n$.

## 2.2 Mathematical Decomposition — The Move Operator

For combinatorial optimization, the move operator $m$ can be categorized by **arity** (the number of solution components it alters):

- **1-arity moves (1-opt / Flip):** Change the value/assignment of a single decision variable. Used in **binary problems** (e.g., $\{0,1\}^n$ knapsack).
- **2-arity moves (2-opt / Swap):** Exchange two components or reverse a subsequence. Used in **permutation problems** (e.g., TSP).
- **k-arity moves (k-opt):** Generalization that alters $k$ components simultaneously. Provides larger neighbourhoods at higher computational cost.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Neighbourhood Definition | $N(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid \exists \, m : s' = m(s) \}$ | Defines search reachability |
| 2 | Neighbourhood Size (1-opt, binary) | $\vert N(s) \vert = n$ | Knapsack, Max-SAT |
| 3 | Neighbourhood Size (2-opt, TSP) | $\vert N(s) \vert = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Routing, scheduling |
| 4 | Neighbourhood Size (Swap) | $\vert N(s) \vert = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Permutation problems |
| 5 | Neighbourhood Size (Insert) | $\vert N(s) \vert = n(n-1)$ | Sequencing problems |
| 6 | Total Solution Space (Permutation) | $\vert S \vert = n!$ | TSP, scheduling |
| 7 | Reachability Condition | $\forall s_1, s_2 \in S, \exists \text{path in } N$ | Guarantees search completeness |
| 8 | Local Optimum Condition | $\nexists \, s' \in N(s) : f(s') < f(s)$ | Termination criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ in $\vert N(s) \vert$ denotes cardinality (set size). In KTU answer sheets, always distinguish this from the conditional "such that" symbol by using clear phrasing like "the cardinality of $N(s)$".

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

- **Why does neighbourhood size matter?** A **small neighbourhood** (e.g., 1-opt) provides fast evaluation per iteration but may trap the algorithm in poor **local optima** — a phenomenon called *premature convergence*. A **large neighbourhood** (e.g., 3-opt, Or-opt) escapes local optima but requires significantly more computation per iteration.
- **How do real systems use it?** In **logistics**, neighbourhood moves model small route adjustments. In **VLSI design**, swaps represent gate relocations. In **production scheduling**, insertions model job reordering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Neighbourhood Size for the Travelling Salesman Problem (2-opt)

**Problem Setup:** A TSP tour visits $n$ cities exactly once. A solution is a permutation of $n$ cities, so the total search space is $\vert S \vert = n!$.

**Step 1 — Identify the move operator:**
A 2-opt move removes two non-adjacent edges from the tour and reconnects them in the only other possible way (the alternative crossing), producing a new valid tour.

**Step 2 — Count the number of edges in the tour:**
A complete tour over $n$ cities consists of exactly $n$ edges.

**Step 3 — Count the number of ways to choose two edges:**
The number of ways to select 2 edges out of $n$ is given by the combination formula.

$$
\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2! \cdot (n-2)!} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Exclude adjacent edge removals:**
Two adjacent edges share a city; removing them produces the same tour after reconnection. Thus, no overcounting occurs, and the 2-opt neighbourhood size is:

$$
\vert N_{2\text{-opt}}(s) \vert = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification for small $n$:**
For $n = 4$, $\vert N(s) \vert = \frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} = 6$. This matches the known fact that exactly 3 distinct 2-opt moves exist for a 4-city tour (each pair of non-adjacent edges).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Neighbourhood Size for the Binary Knapsack (1-opt / Flip)

**Problem Setup:** We have $n$ items, each with weight $w_i$ and value $v_i$. A solution is a binary vector $x \in \{0,1\}^n$. Total search space: $\vert S \vert = 2^n$.

**Step 1 — Define the move operator:**
A 1-opt (or *flip*) move toggles exactly one decision variable:

$$
x_i' = 1 - x_i, \quad x_j' = x_j \;\; \forall j \neq i
$$

**Step 2 — Count the dimensions:**
There are exactly $n$ variables that can be flipped, and each flip produces a distinct neighbour (except in degenerate cases of equal item weights/values).

**Step 3 — State the result:**

$$
\vert N_{\text{flip}}(s) \vert = n
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**
For $n = 100$ items, the neighbourhood contains exactly 100 neighbours — a tiny fraction of the $2^{100}$ total solutions. This makes evaluation extremely fast but motivates hybrid heuristics like **Simulated Annealing** or **Tabu Search** to escape local optima.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Neighbourhood Analysis

| Problem Type | Typical Move | Neighbourhood Size $\vert N(s) \vert$ | Computation/Iter | Local Optima Risk |
|--------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Binary Vector (Knapsack) | Flip | $n$ | Very Low | High |
| Permutation (TSP) | 2-opt | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Medium | Medium |
| Permutation (TSP) | 3-opt | $O(n^3)$ | High | Low |
| Permutation (Scheduling) | Insert | $n(n-1)$ | Medium | Medium |
| Permutation (Scheduling) | Swap | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Medium | Medium |
| Graph Coloring | Recolour | $n \cdot (k-1)$ | Low | Very High |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Local Search on a TSP

**Problem:** Find the shortest tour for 4 cities with distances:
$d(A,B)=10, \; d(A,C)=15, \; d(A,D)=20, \; d(B,C)=35, \; d(B,D)=25, \; d(C,D)=30$

**Step 1 — Initial solution:**
Tour $s_0 = (A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow A)$
Tour length: $f(s_0) = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the 2-opt neighbours:**

$$
N(s_0) = \{(A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow A), \; (A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow A), \; (A \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow A)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each neighbour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow A) &= 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95 \\
f(A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow A) &= 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80 \\
f(A \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow A) &= 20 + 30 + 35 + 10 = 95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Move to the best neighbour:**
$s_1 = (A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow A)$ with $f(s_1) = 80$. This is a strict improvement of $\Delta f = -15$.

**Step 5 — Termination check:**
No neighbour has $f(s') < f(s_1)$. Thus, $s_1$ is a **local optimum** under the 2-opt neighbourhood. A larger 3-opt move may still improve it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Neighbourhood Topology — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS["Current Solution s"] --> nodeM1["Move m1 - Flip variable x1"]
    nodeS --> nodeM2["Move m2 - Flip variable x2"]
    nodeS --> nodeM3["Move m3 - Swap positions 2 and 5"]
    nodeS --> nodeM4["Move m4 - Insert city X after position 3"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeN1["Neighbour s1 equals 1 minus x1, rest same"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeN2["Neighbour s2 equals 1 minus x2, rest same"]
    nodeM3 --> nodeN3["Neighbour s3 has positions 2 and 5 swapped"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeN4["Neighbour s4 has city X inserted at position 3"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeEval["Evaluate f of s1, s2, s3, s4"]
    nodeN2 --> nodeEval
    nodeN3 --> nodeEval
    nodeN4 --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeSelect{"Select Best Neighbour"}
    nodeSelect -->|Yes improvement| nodeUpdate["Update s to best s prime and repeat"]
    nodeSelect -->|No improvement| nodeStop["STOP - Local Optimum Reached"]
    nodeUpdate --> nodeS
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Neighbourhood-Based Local Search Heuristic

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Initialization - Random or Heuristic"] --> nodeB["Current s in Solution Space S"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Generate Neighbourhood N of s"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Evaluate Objective f for all s prime in N"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Is there s prime with f less than f of s?"}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF["Select Best s prime via Greedy Rule"]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeG["Return Local Optimum s"]
    nodeF --> nodeB
```

## 4.3 Neighbourhood Hierarchy — Block-Level View

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["Neighbourhood Classification"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeUnary["Unary Moves - Size O of n"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeBinary["Binary Moves - Size O of n squared"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeKary["K ary Moves - Size O of n choose k"]
    nodeUnary --> nodeU1["1 opt Flip - Knapsack SAT"]
    nodeUnary --> nodeU2["Recolour - Graph Coloring"]
    nodeBinary --> nodeB1["2 opt - TSP Routing"]
    nodeBinary --> nodeB2["Swap - Job Scheduling"]
    nodeBinary --> nodeB3["Insert - Sequencing"]
    nodeKary --> nodeK1["3 opt - TSP Large Instances"]
    nodeKary --> nodeK2["Or opt - Vehicle Routing"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every block represents a *logical state* in the heuristic algorithm. The arrows represent data flow and control flow. Each neighbour $s'$ is functionally equivalent to $s$ under exactly one elementary transformation — this is the operational essence of a neighbourhood.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Q: Define the term "Neighbourhood" in the context of heuristic search. State any two desirable properties of a well-defined neighbourhood structure.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Neighbourhood* of a solution $s$ in the solution space $S$ is the set $N(s)$ of all solutions that can be obtained from $s$ by applying exactly one elementary move operator $m$.

$$
N(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid \exists \text{ a move } m : s' = m(s) \}
$$

**Two desirable properties:**

1. **Reachability / Strong Connectivity:** For any pair of solutions $s_1, s_2 \in S$, there must exist a finite sequence of moves through neighbourhoods connecting them. This guarantees the heuristic can theoretically explore the entire solution space.
2. **Computational Tractability:** The neighbourhood $\vert N(s) \vert$ must be enumerable in polynomial time $O(\text{poly}(n))$ so that the heuristic remains practically solvable for large instances.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**Q: For a Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) with $n=6$ cities, compute the size of the 2-opt neighbourhood $\vert N(s) \vert$. State the corresponding move operator.**

**Model Answer:**

The 2-opt move removes two non-adjacent edges from a TSP tour and reconnects the resulting paths in the only alternative way, producing a new valid tour.

The neighbourhood size is given by:

$$
\vert N(s) \vert = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

Substituting $n = 6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert N(s) \vert &= \frac{6 \cdot (6-1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{6 \cdot 5}{2} \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, from any tour of 6 cities, exactly **15 distinct 2-opt neighbours** can be generated.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of a neighbourhood in heuristic decision-making with a suitable example. Discuss how the choice of neighbourhood size affects the trade-off between solution quality and computational effort. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 5-city Travelling Salesman Problem, the distance matrix is given below. Starting from the initial tour $s_0 = (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1)$, perform **one complete iteration** of best-improvement local search using the **2-opt neighbourhood**. Show all neighbours and their costs. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
1 & 0 & 12 & 8 & 20 & 15 \\
2 & 12 & 0 & 18 & 7 & 22 \\
3 & 8 & 18 & 0 & 10 & 9 \\
4 & 20 & 7 & 10 & 0 & 6 \\
5 & 15 & 22 & 9 & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Neighbourhood:** *(As covered in Section 1.1 & 2.1)*

A neighbourhood $N(s)$ is the set of solutions reachable from $s$ via a single move. Example: For a binary knapsack with $n=4$ items and current solution $s = (1,0,1,0)$, flipping the second bit gives $s' = (1,1,1,0) \in N(s)$.

**Trade-off analysis:**

| Neighbourhood Size | Solution Quality | Computational Effort |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Small (e.g., 1-opt) | Risk of local optima | Fast evaluation |
| Medium (e.g., 2-opt) | Balanced exploration | Moderate cost |
| Large (e.g., 3-opt) | Higher escape potential | Expensive evaluation |

A larger neighbourhood allows the heuristic to escape shallow local optima and discover better solutions, but each iteration becomes computationally expensive. Conversely, a smaller neighbourhood is fast but prone to premature convergence. Modern metaheuristics (e.g., **Variable Neighbourhood Search**) intelligently oscillate between multiple neighbourhood sizes.

**(b) TSP Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial tour cost:**
$f(s_0) = d_{12} + d_{23} + d_{34} + d_{45} + d_{51} = 12 + 18 + 10 + 6 + 15 = 61$

**Step 2 — Enumerate all 2-opt neighbours** (removing each non-adjacent edge pair):

The total number of 2-opt neighbours is $\frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighbour 1: } (1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 8+18+7+6+15 = 54 \\
\text{Neighbour 2: } (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 12+7+10+9+15 = 53 \\
\text{Neighbour 3: } (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 12+18+9+6+20 = 65 \\
\text{Neighbour 4: } (1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 20+10+18+22+15 = 85 \\
\text{Neighbour 5: } (1 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 15+9+18+7+20 = 69 \\
\text{Neighbour 6: } (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 12+22+9+10+20 = 73 \\
\text{Neighbour 7: } (1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 20+7+18+9+15 = 69 \\
\text{Neighbour 8: } (1 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 15+22+18+10+20 = 85 \\
\text{Neighbour 9: } (1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 8+9+22+7+20 = 66 \\
\text{Neighbour 10: } (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1): \; f &= 12+7+6+9+8 = 42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Select best neighbour:**
The minimum cost neighbour is **Neighbour 10** with $f(s_1) = 42$, corresponding to tour $s_1 = (1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1)$.

**Step 4 — Improvement achieved:**
$\Delta f = f(s_1) - f(s_0) = 42 - 61 = -19$ (significant improvement of 19 units).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Define *move operator* and *neighbourhood size*. Derive the size of the swap neighbourhood and the insert neighbourhood for a permutation of $n$ elements. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a binary knapsack problem with $n=4$ items having values $v = (10, 20, 30, 40)$ and weights $w = (2, 4, 6, 8)$, and knapsack capacity $C = 12$. Starting from solution $s_0 = (0,1,1,0)$ (total weight $= 10$, value $= 50$), perform **one iteration of best-improvement local search using the flip (1-opt) neighbourhood** and identify the new solution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivations:**

- **Move Operator ($m$):** A deterministic rule that transforms a current solution $s$ into a new solution $s'$.
- **Neighbourhood Size ($\vert N(s) \vert$):** The number of distinct solutions obtainable by applying all possible single moves from $s$.

**Swap Neighbourhood Size:**
A swap move exchanges the positions of two distinct elements in the permutation. The number of unordered pairs from $n$ positions is:

$$
\vert N_{\text{swap}}(s) \vert = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Insert Neighbourhood Size:**
An insert move removes one element from position $i$ and re-inserts it at a different position $j$. For each of the $n$ elements, there are $(n-1)$ possible new insertion positions. Hence:

$$
\vert N_{\text{insert}}(s) \vert = n(n-1)
$$

**(b) Knapsack Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial state:**
$s_0 = (0,1,1,0)$, weight $= 4+6 = 10$, value $= 20+30 = 50$.

**Step 2 — Enumerate all 4 flip neighbours and check feasibility:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flip } x_1: \; s_1' &= (1,1,1,0), \; w = 2+4+6 = 12, \; v = 10+20+30 = 60 \\
\text{Flip } x_2: \; s_2' &= (0,0,1,0), \; w = 6, \; v = 30 \\
\text{Flip } x_3: \; s_3' &= (0,1,0,0), \; w = 4, \; v = 20 \\
\text{Flip } x_4: \; s_4' &= (0,1,1,1), \; w = 4+6+8 = 18, \; \textbf{infeasible} \; (>12)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the best feasible neighbour:**
$s_1' = (1,1,1,0)$ with value $60$ and weight $12$ is the best feasible improvement.

**Step 4 — Update:**
$s_1 = (1,1,1,0)$ with improvement $\Delta f = 60 - 50 = +10$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Cardinality vs. Conditional Symbol Confusion:** Students frequently write $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) when they mean $\vert N(s) \vert$ (set cardinality). On KTU answer sheets, write "the cardinality of $N(s)$" in words for the first mention, then use the symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting Feasibility Constraints:** In constrained problems (Knapsack, TSP with time windows), students enumerate ALL neighbours without checking feasibility, costing 1–2 marks. Always check constraints.
> 3. **Incorrect 2-opt Enumeration:** For TSP, students often miss neighbours because they do not realize each 2-opt move produces exactly ONE alternative reconnection, not multiple.
> 4. **Local vs. Global Optimum:** Students frequently stop at the first improvement instead of doing **best-improvement**, which finds the steepest descent in $N(s)$.
> 5. **Missing the Local Optimum Termination Criterion:** Always state that the algorithm terminates when no $s' \in N(s)$ yields a better objective value.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Neighbourhood $N(s)$** is the set of all solutions reachable from $s$ by **exactly one move**.
- The **move operator** $m$ defines the granularity of search — *flip, swap, insert, 2-opt, k-opt*.
- **Neighbourhood sizes** for common problems:
  * Binary Knapsack (flip): $\vert N(s) \vert = n$
  * TSP (2-opt): $\vert N(s) \vert = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
  * Scheduling (swap): $\vert N(s) \vert = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
  * Scheduling (insert): $\vert N(s) \vert = n(n-1)$
- **Small neighbourhood** = fast evaluation, high local-optima risk.
- **Large neighbourhood** = better exploration, higher computational cost.
- **Reachability** is the most critical property — the heuristic must be able to traverse the full solution space.
- **Best-improvement local search** selects the neighbour with the lowest $f(s')$ in each iteration.
- **Local optimum** is reached when no $s' \in N(s)$ has a better objective value — termination criterion for local search.
- Always **verify feasibility** of every neighbour in constrained optimization.
- Metaheuristics like **Simulated Annealing**, **Tabu Search**, and **VNS** are direct extensions built upon the neighbourhood concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Candidate List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Candidate List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Candidate List (CL)** in metaheuristic optimization is a curated, strategically filtered subset of solutions, moves, or elements drawn from the broader **neighborhood structure** $N(s)$ of the current solution $s$, which are subjected to evaluation by the search algorithm at a given iteration. The candidate list serves as the operational bridge between the *full neighborhood* (which may be prohibitively large in combinatorial problems) and the *single next solution* actually selected by the heuristic.

Formally, given a current solution $s \in S$ where $S$ is the feasible solution space, the candidate list at iteration $t$ is:

$$CL_t \subseteq N(s_t), \quad \text{where } \vert CL_t \vert \ll \vert N(s_t)\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Candidate List is a **central design component** of memory-based and constructive metaheuristics. In Tabu Search, it controls the exploration budget per iteration. In **GRASP (Greedy Randomized Adaptive Search Procedure)**, the restricted candidate list (RCL) governs the balance between greediness and randomness. Mastery of CL construction is a **frequently tested concept** in Part A and Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Job Interview Panel**
>
> Imagine a company receives **10,000 resumes** for a single position (this is the full **neighborhood**). The HR team cannot interview all 10,000 applicants. Instead, they **shortlist 20 candidates** based on screening criteria (test scores, experience, certifications). This shortlist is the **Candidate List**. The panel then interviews only these 20 and selects the best one for the next round.
>
> - **Resume Screening** = Candidate List Construction (filtering)
> - **Interview** = Evaluation Function $f(\cdot)$ applied to each candidate
> - **Hiring Decision** = Selection of the best evaluated candidate as $s_{t+1}$
>
> The screening criteria themselves may be *static* (fixed thresholds) or *adaptive* (changes based on previous hires — analogous to **adaptive memory** in Tabu Search).

### 1.3 Why Candidate Lists Are Necessary

In many NP-hard combinatorial problems, the neighborhood size grows **exponentially** or **polynomially** with problem dimension. A full neighborhood scan is computationally infeasible. The candidate list addresses three fundamental needs:

1. **Computational Tractability** — Reduces evaluation overhead from $O(\vert N(s)\vert)$ to $O(\vert CL_t\vert)$.
2. **Strategic Focus** — Concentrates search effort on *promising* or *diverse* regions of the solution space.
3. **Quality-Diversity Balance** — Enables trade-off control between **intensification** (exploitation) and **diversification** (exploration) via adaptive list construction.

### 1.4 Classification of Candidate List Types

| **Criterion** | **Type A** | **Type B** |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction Mode** | Static Candidate List (SCL) | Dynamic Candidate List (DCL) |
| **Selection Basis** | Quality-Based (best-ranked moves) | Diversity-Based (most-different from recent) |
| **Scope** | Candidate Moves (edges/swaps) | Candidate Solutions (full configurations) |
| **Origin** | Exhaustive Neighborhood (all of $N(s)$) | Sampled/Probabilistic Neighborhood |
| **Update Frequency** | Rebuilt every iteration | Rebuilt periodically or adaptively |
| **Typical Use** | Tabu Search, GRASP, VNS | ALNS, ACO, Scatter Search |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** in GRASP is a special quality-based type where elements with greedy function values above a threshold $\alpha \cdot f^*$ are included, and one is chosen **randomly**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Candidate List in Tabu Search — The Memory-Augmented CL

In **Tabu Search (TS)**, the candidate list at iteration $t$, denoted $CL_t^{TS}$, is constructed by applying three sequential filters to the raw neighborhood $N(s_t)$:

1. **Neighborhood Enumeration** — Generate all (or sampled) neighbors of $s_t$.
2. **Tabu Filter** — Remove neighbors whose attributes are marked **tabu-active** in short-term memory $T$.
3. **Aspiration Override** — Re-admit a tabu move $m$ if $\text{Aspiration}(m)$ is satisfied (typically $f(s_t \oplus m) < f(s^*)$, the best-known solution).

The resulting candidate list is:

$$CL_t^{TS} = \{ s' \in N(s_t) \mid \text{Attr}(s') \notin T_t \;\text{or}\; \text{Aspiration}(\text{Attr}(s')) = \text{True} \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The Tabu Search CL is **not** a random sample. It is a **deterministic filter** that combines recency-based exclusion with aspiration-based override. The size $\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert$ varies per iteration because tabu tenure is adaptive.

### 2.2 Candidate List in GRASP — The Restricted Candidate List (RCL)

In **GRASP**, the candidate list is constructed during the **constructive phase** of each iteration. A Restricted Candidate List $RCL$ contains all elements $e$ from the **Candidate Element Set** $C(s)$ whose greedy evaluation function value $g(e)$ satisfies:

$$RCL = \{ e \in C(s) \mid g(e) \in [g^{\min}, g^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (g^{\max} - g^{\min})] \}$$

where:
- $g^{\min} = \min_{e \in C(s)} g(e)$ — best greedy value
- $g^{\max} = \max_{e \in C(s)} g(e)$ — worst greedy value in candidate set
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ — **restrictiveness parameter** controlling greediness vs. randomness

> When $\alpha = 0$: $RCL$ contains only the single greedy-best element → **pure greedy construction**.
> When $\alpha = 1$: $RCL$ contains all elements → **pure random construction**.
> Intermediate $\alpha$ values create a **biased random selection** — the heart of GRASP.

### 2.3 Candidate List in Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS)

In **VNS**, the candidate list corresponds to solutions generated by **systematic shaking** across a sequence of neighborhood structures $N_{k_1}, N_{k_2}, \ldots, N_{k_{\max}}$. The candidate list for the $k$-th neighborhood is:

$$CL_k = \{ s' \in N_k(s_t) \mid s' \text{ is generated by shaking operator } k \}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Symbol** | **Expression** | **Meaning** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Neighborhood | $N(s) = \{s' \in S \mid s' \text{ reachable from } s\}$ | All neighbors of $s$ | Universal |
| Candidate List | $CL \subseteq N(s)$ | Filtered subset to evaluate | Universal |
| Restrictiveness | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | GRASP greediness control | GRASP |
| RCL Membership | $g(e) \geq g^{\min} + \alpha(g^{\max} - g^{\min})$ | Element qualifies for RCL | GRASP |
| Tabu Filter | $\text{Attr}(s') \in T$ → reject | Recency memory exclusion | Tabu Search |
| Aspiration | $f(s_t \oplus m) < A(s')$ | Override tabu status | Tabu Search |
| Tabu Tenure | $\tau \in [\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ | Duration of tabu status | Tabu Search |
| CL Size Ratio | $r = \vert CL\vert / \vert N(s)\vert$ | Filtering aggressiveness | Analysis |
| Expected Evaluation Cost | $E[\text{cost}] = r \cdot c_{\text{eval}}$ | Computational saving | Analysis |
| Move Evaluation | $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s)$ | Improvement of a move | All local search |
| Aspiration Level | $A(s') = f(s^*) - \delta$ | Threshold for override | Tabu Search |
| Diversification Penalty | $\pi_d = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot \text{freq}_i$ | Penalize frequently-visited | Long-term memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Candidate list strategies are deployed in real-world systems including **vehicle routing (UPS, FedEx)**, **job-shop scheduling**, **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **airline crew rostering**, and **supply chain network design** — all problems where full neighborhood evaluation is infeasible at industrial scale.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Computing RCL Membership for GRASP

**Problem Setup:** A routing problem has a Candidate Element Set $C = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5\}$ with greedy values $g(e_i)$ as follows. Using $\alpha = 0.4$, derive the RCL.

| Element $e_i$ | $g(e_i)$ |
|---|---|
| $e_1$ | 12 |
| $e_2$ | 8 |
| $e_3$ | 15 |
| $e_4$ | 5 |
| $e_5$ | 10 |

**Step 1 — Identify extremes:**

$$g^{\min} = \min\{12, 8, 15, 5, 10\} = 5 \quad (\text{achieved at } e_4)$$

$$g^{\max} = \max\{12, 8, 15, 5, 10\} = 15 \quad (\text{achieved at } e_3)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the RCL threshold using $\alpha = 0.4$:**

The RCL threshold $g_{\text{thresh}}$ is defined as:

$$g_{\text{thresh}} = g^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (g^{\max} - g^{\min})$$

Substituting values:

$$g_{\text{thresh}} = 5 + 0.4 \cdot (15 - 5)$$

$$g_{\text{thresh}} = 5 + 0.4 \cdot 10$$

$$g_{\text{thresh}} = 5 + 4 = 9$$

**Step 3 — Apply RCL membership condition $g(e_i) \geq g_{\text{thresh}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
g(e_1) = 12 \geq 9 &\implies e_1 \in RCL \\
g(e_2) = 8 \geq 9 &\implies e_2 \notin RCL \\
g(e_3) = 15 \geq 9 &\implies e_3 \in RCL \\
g(e_4) = 5 \geq 9 &\implies e_4 \notin RCL \\
g(e_5) = 10 \geq 9 &\implies e_5 \in RCL
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final RCL:**

$$RCL = \{e_1, e_3, e_5\}$$

**Step 5 — Probability of selection (uniform random within RCL):**

$$P(e_i \text{ selected}) = \frac{1}{\vert RCL\vert} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \quad \forall e_i \in RCL$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the *worst* element $e_4$ (with $g = 5$, the highest quality) is **excluded** when $\alpha < 0.5$. As $\alpha \to 1$, all 5 elements enter the RCL and the algorithm becomes purely random. As $\alpha \to 0$, only $e_4$ remains and the algorithm is purely greedy.

### 3.2 Derivation — Tabu Search CL Size with Aspiration Override

**Scenario:** Tabu Search is solving a TSP with $n = 50$ cities. The current solution $s_t$ has neighborhood $\vert N(s_t)\vert = 1225$ (from 2-opt swaps). Short-term memory $T$ has $\vert T\vert = 180$ active tabu attributes. The aspiration level is $A = 450$ (current best tour cost), and 25 of the tabu moves happen to improve upon $A$.

**Step 1 — Tabu-rejected moves (raw tabu count):**

$$\vert N_{\text{tabu}}\vert = \vert T\vert = 180$$

**Step 2 — Aspiration-override (re-admitted) moves:**

$$\vert N_{\text{aspired}}\vert = 25$$

**Step 3 — Effective excluded moves (net tabu exclusion):**

$$\vert N_{\text{excluded}}\vert = \vert N_{\text{tabu}}\vert - \vert N_{\text{aspired}}\vert = 180 - 25 = 155$$

**Step 4 — Candidate list size:**

$$\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert = \vert N(s_t)\vert - \vert N_{\text{excluded}}\vert = 1225 - 155 = 1070$$

**Step 5 — Filtering ratio (efficiency metric):**

$$r = \frac{\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert}{\vert N(s_t)\vert} = \frac{1070}{1225} \approx 0.8734$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with aggressive tabu filtering, 87.3% of the neighborhood remains in the candidate list. This shows that the **primary value of the candidate list is not always size reduction** — it is the **strategic selection of which moves to evaluate**, particularly prioritizing those with favorable $\Delta f$ values in the selection step (aspiration or best-improvement).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Candidate List Strategies

| **Parameter** | **Tabu Search CL** | **GRASP RCL** | **VNS CL** | **SA Neighborhood** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Size** | $\sim$ 50–95% of $N(s)$ | 1 to $\vert C(s)\vert$ (param.) | Fixed by $k_{\max}$ | Full $N(s)$ typically |
| **Update Rule** | Recency + Frequency mem. | Rebuilt each construction | Sequence-based | Metropolis criterion |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic filter | Probabilistic selection | Deterministic + random | Probabilistic acceptance |
| **Intensification Support** | Strong (via tabu memory) | Moderate (via $\alpha$) | Moderate | Weak |
| **Diversification Support** | Strong (via frequency mem.) | Strong (via high $\alpha$) | Strong (via $k_{\max}$) | Strong (via temperature) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(\vert N(s)\vert)$ to build | $O(\vert C(s)\vert \log \vert C(s)\vert)$ | $O(k_{\max})$ | $O(1)$ per move |
| **Memory Footprint** | $O(\vert T\vert + \vert F\vert)$ | $O(\vert C(s)\vert)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Best For** | Complex combinatorial | Constructive heuristics | Large neighborhoods | Continuous optimization |

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define a **Candidate List** in the context of metaheuristic optimization. How does it differ from the full neighborhood $N(s)$? Why is it necessary in large-scale combinatorial problems?

**Model Answer:**

A **Candidate List (CL)** is a strategically filtered subset of solutions or moves drawn from the neighborhood $N(s)$ of the current solution $s$, which are subjected to evaluation by the search algorithm at a given iteration. Mathematically, $CL \subseteq N(s)$ with $\vert CL\vert \ll \vert N(s)\vert$.

**Difference from Neighborhood:**

| **Aspect** | **Neighborhood $N(s)$** | **Candidate List $CL$** |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | All feasible neighbors | Filtered subset |
| Size | May be exponential | Controlled, often polynomial |
| Purpose | Theoretical structure | Operational search budget |
| Determinism | Fixed by problem definition | May be deterministic or stochastic |

**Necessity in large-scale problems:**
1. **Computational Tractability** — In TSP with $n = 1000$ cities, 2-opt neighborhood has $\approx 500{,}000$ neighbors; full evaluation per iteration is infeasible.
2. **Strategic Focus** — Filters out unpromising or redundant moves.
3. **Quality Control** — Allows biasing selection toward high-quality or diverse solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-mark scoring:** Definition (1 mark) + Difference table (1 mark) + Necessity explanation (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is a **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** in GRASP? Explain the role of the **restrictiveness parameter** $\alpha$ in RCL construction.

**Model Answer:**

The **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** in GRASP is a quality-based candidate list used during the constructive phase. It contains only those elements $e$ from the Candidate Element Set $C(s)$ whose greedy function value $g(e)$ lies in a restricted range:

$$RCL = \{e \in C(s) \mid g(e) \geq g^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (g^{\max} - g^{\min})\}$$

**Role of $\alpha$:**
- $\alpha = 0$ → $RCL$ contains only the greedy-best element → **pure greedy** behavior.
- $\alpha = 1$ → $RCL$ contains all elements → **pure random** behavior.
- $0 < \alpha < 1$ → **Biased randomization** — the core of GRASP.

The parameter $\alpha$ directly controls the **greedy-randomized balance**: lower $\alpha$ favors exploitation, higher $\alpha$ favors exploration.

### **PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**
