## **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Describe the construction of the **candidate list in Tabu Search** with reference to short-term memory, aspiration criteria, and the role of move attributes. Discuss how the candidate list size affects the balance between intensification and diversification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a Tabu Search solving a graph coloring problem. The current solution $s_t$ has 320 neighbors in $N(s_t)$. The short-term memory $T$ currently marks 85 attributes as tabu-active. The aspiration level $A = 42$ (current best number of colors used), and exactly 12 of the tabu moves would produce a solution with fewer than 42 colors. Compute:
- (i) The number of tabu-rejected moves after aspiration override.
- (ii) The final candidate list size $\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert$.
- (iii) The filtering efficiency ratio $r$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

The **Tabu Search Candidate List** is constructed through a three-stage pipeline:

**Stage 1 — Neighborhood Enumeration:**
Generate the full neighborhood $N(s_t)$ of the current solution $s_t$ using the defined move structure (e.g., 2-opt for TSP, swap for scheduling, recoloring for graph coloring).

**Stage 2 — Tabu Filtering using Short-Term Memory:**
Short-term memory $T$ stores recently used move attributes (e.g., swapped edges, recolored vertices). Any neighbor $s' \in N(s_t)$ whose generating move attribute is present in $T$ is **temporarily forbidden** (tabu):

$$\text{Reject } s' \text{ if } \text{Attr}(s' \in N(s_t)) \in T$$

The tabu tenure $\tau$ (typically in range $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$) determines how long an attribute remains tabu.

**Stage 3 — Aspiration Criterion Override:**
A tabu move may be re-admitted to the candidate list if it satisfies the aspiration condition. The standard **aspiration-by-objective** criterion is:

$$\text{Admit } s' \text{ if } f(s') < A(\text{best known})$$

Aspiration prevents the search from being trapped when a tabu move would lead to a new global best.

**Final Candidate List:**

$$CL_t^{TS} = \{s' \in N(s_t) \mid \text{Attr}(s') \notin T \;\text{or}\; f(s') < A(\text{best})\}$$

**Effect of CL Size on Intensification vs. Diversification:**

| **CL Size** | **Effect** | **Search Behavior** |
|---|---|---|
| Large ($\vert CL\vert \approx \vert N(s)\vert$) | Weak filtering | More intensification (exploitation) |
| Small ($\vert CL\vert \ll \vert N(s)\vert$) | Strong filtering | More diversification (exploration) |
| Dynamic (adaptive) | Balanced | Oscillates between focus and spread |

A *small* candidate list forces the algorithm to accept whatever passes the filter, often leading to surprising, diverse moves. A *large* candidate list allows best-improvement selection, deepening exploitation around the current basin.

> [!NOTE]
> **7-mark scoring:** Pipeline description (3 marks) + Memory & aspiration (2 marks) + CL size analysis (2 marks).

### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Given:** $\vert N(s_t)\vert = 320$, $\vert T\vert = 85$ tabu attributes, $\vert N_{\text{aspired}}\vert = 12$ moves that override tabu status via aspiration.

**(i) Tabu-rejected moves after aspiration override:**

The net tabu exclusion is the raw tabu count minus those re-admitted by aspiration:

$$\vert N_{\text{excluded}}\vert = \vert T\vert - \vert N_{\text{aspired}}\vert$$

$$\vert N_{\text{excluded}}\vert = 85 - 12 = 73$$

**(ii) Final candidate list size:**

$$\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert = \vert N(s_t)\vert - \vert N_{\text{excluded}}\vert$$

$$\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert = 320 - 73 = 247$$

**(iii) Filtering efficiency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\vert CL_t^{TS}\vert}{\vert N(s_t)\vert} = \frac{247}{320} = 0.7719$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 77.2% of the neighborhood is retained in the candidate list. The tabu filter removed 22.8% of moves, with 12 of those 85 (14.1%) being rescued by aspiration.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** in GRASP with a suitable mathematical formulation. Discuss how the choice of $\alpha$ affects solution diversity and convergence speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GRASP heuristic is being used to construct a solution for a set covering problem. At a particular construction step, the Candidate Element Set $C(s) = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5, e_6\}$ has the following incremental cost values $c(e_i)$ (lower is better):

| $e_i$ | $e_1$ | $e_2$ | $e_3$ | $e_4$ | $e_5$ | $e_6$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $c(e_i)$ | 14 | 9 | 22 | 6 | 18 | 11 |

Using a restrictiveness parameter $\alpha = 0.5$, determine the RCL. If a uniform random selection picks $e_3$, what is the probability that this selection was made *given* that GRASP was operating in a "high-diversity" mode? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

The **RCL in GRASP** is a quality-based candidate list that enables biased randomization during the constructive phase. Given the Candidate Element Set $C(s)$ at construction step $k$ with greedy evaluation function $g(e)$ (or incremental cost $c(e)$):

**RCL Membership Condition:**

$$RCL = \{e \in C(s) \mid g(e) \in [g^{\min}, g^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (g^{\max} - g^{\min})]\}$$

For minimization problems (where lower $g$ is better), the condition is reformulated as:

$$RCL = \{e \in C(s) \mid g(e) \leq g^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (g^{\max} - g^{\min})\}$$

**Effect of $\alpha$ on Diversity and Convergence:**

| **$\alpha$ Value** | **RCL Size** | **Diversity** | **Convergence Speed** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha \to 0$ | 1 (greedy-best only) | Very Low | Very Fast (may stagnate) |
| $\alpha \in [0.2, 0.5]$ | Small-to-moderate | Moderate | Balanced |
| $\alpha \in [0.6, 0.8]$ | Large | High | Slower |
| $\alpha \to 1$ | All elements | Maximum | Slowest (random walk) |

- **Low $\alpha$** → High convergence speed but low diversity → risk of premature convergence to local optima.
- **High $\alpha$** → High diversity but slow convergence → broad exploration, may miss fine-tuning.
- **Adaptive $\alpha$** (e.g., Reactive GRASP) dynamically adjusts $\alpha$ based on solution quality history to balance both objectives.

> [!NOTE]
> **7-mark scoring:** RCL formulation (2 marks) + Role of $\alpha$ in diversity (3 marks) + Convergence discussion (2 marks).

### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**Given:** $C(s) = \{e_1, \ldots, e_6\}$ with $c(e_i) = \{14, 9, 22, 6, 18, 11\}$. Since lower cost is better (minimization), use:

$$RCL = \{e \in C(s) \mid c(e) \leq c^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (c^{\max} - c^{\min})\}$$

**Step 1 — Identify extremes:**

$$c^{\min} = \min\{14, 9, 22, 6, 18, 11\} = 6 \quad (\text{at } e_4)$$

$$c^{\max} = \max\{14, 9, 22, 6, 18, 11\} = 22 \quad (\text{at } e_3)$$

**Step 2 — Compute RCL threshold with $\alpha = 0.5$:**

$$c_{\text{thresh}} = c^{\min} + \alpha \cdot (c^{\max} - c^{\min})$$

$$c_{\text{thresh}} = 6 + 0.5 \cdot (22 - 6)$$

$$c_{\text{thresh}} = 6 + 0.5 \cdot 16$$

$$c_{\text{thresh}} = 6 + 8 = 14$$

**Step 3 — Apply RCL membership $c(e_i) \leq 14$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c(e_1) = 14 \leq 14 &\implies e_1 \in RCL \\
c(e_2) = 9 \leq 14 &\implies e_2 \in RCL \\
c(e_3) = 22 \leq 14 &\implies e_3 \notin RCL \\
c(e_4) = 6 \leq 14 &\implies e_4 \in RCL \\
c(e_5) = 18 \leq 14 &\implies e_5 \notin RCL \\
c(e_6) = 11 \leq 14 &\implies e_6 \in RCL
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final RCL:**

$$RCL = \{e_1, e_2, e_4, e_6\}$$

**Step 5 — Probability that $e_3$ was selected in "high-diversity" mode:**

Since $e_3 \notin RCL$ under $\alpha = 0.5$, selecting $e_3$ is **impossible** in standard GRASP. For $e_3$ to be in the RCL, $\alpha$ must satisfy:

$$22 \leq 6 + \alpha \cdot 16 \implies 16 \leq 16\alpha \implies \alpha \geq 1.0$$

So $e_3$ can only be selected when $\alpha = 1$ (pure random). In a "high-diversity" mode where $\alpha \geq 0.9$:

$$P(\text{select } e_3 \mid \text{high-diversity}) = \frac{1}{\vert RCL_{\alpha \geq 0.9}\vert}$$

At $\alpha = 0.9$: $c_{\text{thresh}} = 6 + 0.9 \cdot 16 = 20.4$, so $RCL = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4, e_6\}$ (5 elements), giving:

$$P(e_3 \text{ selected}) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $g^{\min}$ with $g^{\max}$** — Always verify whether the problem is a minimization or maximization. For minimization, the RCL includes *low-cost* elements up to a threshold.
> 2. **Using strict inequality vs. non-strict** — The condition $g(e) \leq g^{\min} + \alpha(g^{\max} - g^{\min})$ is typically non-strict ($\leq$), so $e_1$ (with $c = 14$ exactly at the threshold) is included.
> 3. **Forgetting aspiration in Tabu CL computation** — Students often compute $\vert CL\vert = \vert N\vert - \vert T\vert$ without subtracting aspiration-override moves. Always account for re-admission.
> 4. **Confusing CL with Neighborhood** — The CL is a *filtered subset*, not the full neighborhood. Writing $CL = N(s)$ loses marks.
> 5. **Mixing up $\alpha$ semantics** — $\alpha$ is the *restrictiveness* parameter, not the *selection probability*. Probability within RCL is $1/\vert RCL\vert$ (uniform) or weighted by quality.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Candidate List**

- **Definition:** A Candidate List (CL) is a filtered subset of the neighborhood: $CL \subseteq N(s)$.
- **Primary Purpose:** Reduce computational cost and enable strategic search focusing.
- **Tabu Search CL:** Built by removing tabu-active attributes from $N(s)$, then re-admitting via aspiration criterion. *Not* a size-reduction tool — it is a *quality-direction* tool.
- **GRASP RCL:** Defined by the restrictiveness parameter $\alpha \in [0,1]$. $\alpha = 0$ → pure greedy; $\alpha = 1$ → pure random.
- **RCL Formula (Minimization):** $RCL = \{e \mid c(e) \leq c^{\min} + \alpha(c^{\max} - c^{\min})\}$.
- **RCL Formula (Maximization):** $RCL = \{e \mid g(e) \geq g^{\min} + \alpha(g^{\max} - g^{\min})\}$.
- **Aspiration Criterion:** $A(s') = f(s^*) - \delta$; satisfied when $f(s') < f(s^*)$ (for minimization).
- **Tabu Tenure:** $\tau \in [\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$; typical values 5–20 for moderate-size problems.
- **CL Size vs. Strategy:** Small CL → more diversification; Large CL → more intensification.
- **Computational Saving:** $E[\text{cost}] \propto \vert CL\vert$ — smaller CL = faster iteration.
- **Filtering Ratio:** $r = \vert CL\vert / \vert N(s)\vert$ — measure of how aggressively the search is filtered.
- **Real-World Analogy:** Shortlisting job applicants from a large applicant pool.
- **Exam Formula to Memorize:** $g_{\text{thresh}} = g^{\min} + \alpha(g^{\max} - g^{\min})$ — *most-tested formula in this module.*
- **Pitfall:** Do not confuse the CL with the full neighborhood — always state the subset relationship.
- **Diversity Control:** GRASP uses $\alpha$; Tabu Search uses frequency-based long-term memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Term and Long Term Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Short Term and Long Term Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory**, in the context of cognitive psychology and decision-making heuristics, is defined as the cognitive faculty that enables an organism to **encode**, **store**, **retain**, and subsequently **retrieve** information and past experiences. In KTU 2024 Scheme cognitive models, human memory is operationally classified into three hierarchical stages based on the **Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model (1968)**:

1. **Sensory Memory (SM)** — Ultra-brief (≤ 1–3 seconds) retention of raw sensory stimuli.
2. **Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory (WM)** — A limited-capacity, conscious workspace for active processing.
3. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** — A relatively permanent, high-capacity repository of knowledge, skills, and episodic events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** In "Heuristics for Decision Making," STM and LTM are *not* studied as isolated psychological constructs — they are studied because **memory availability is the substrate of every cognitive heuristic** (availability, recognition, frequency, affect, etc.). A decision-maker cannot apply a heuristic on information that cannot be retrieved.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library of the Mind"

Imagine the human memory system as a **two-tier university library**:

- **Short-Term Memory = The Front Desk Reading Room.** You can carry only a few books (≈ 7) at a time, you read them for a short while (15–30 seconds), and if nobody is using them, they are returned to the stacks. This is where active *thinking* and *calculation* occur.
- **Long-Term Memory = The Underground Archive.** A vast, almost limitless repository containing every book you have ever read, every lecture you have ever attended, and every face you have ever seen. Information gets there only if you "register" it consciously (rehearsal) or if it was emotionally significant.

When a student makes a decision — say, "Should I invest in this startup?" — they do not run a Bayesian computation. They **search LTM for analogous cases** and apply a **retrieval-based heuristic** (often the *availability* or *recognition* heuristic). The accuracy of the decision is therefore bound by the **retrievability** and **accessibility** of stored memories.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics

| Metric | Short-Term Memory (STM) | Long-Term Memory (LTM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956) | Essentially **unlimited** (10¹⁵ bits, per estimates) |
| **Duration** | **15 – 30 seconds** (without rehearsal) | **Minutes to a lifetime** |
| **Code** | Primarily **acoustic/phonological** | Primarily **semantic** (meaning-based) |
| **Conscious Access** | Full | Retrieval-based; partial |
| **Forgetting Cause** | Decay + Interference | Interference + Retrieval failure (decay minimal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Empirical Anchors (Bold for KTU High-Yield):**
> - Miller's Magic Number: **7 ± 2** items.
> - STM Duration: **15–30 seconds** (Peterson & Peterson, 1959).
> - LTM Capacity: Theoretically **unbounded**.
> - Encoding Locus: **Acoustic → STM**, **Semantic → LTM** (Baddeley, 1966).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Multi-Store Model

The cognitive flow of information follows a precise pipeline. Each stage has a *gate* that determines whether information advances to the next store.

### Stage 1 — Sensory Memory
- Raw stimulus enters through **sensory registers** (iconic for vision, echoic for audition).
- Capacity: Large, parallel.
- Duration: **~250 ms (visual)**, **~2–4 s (auditory)**.
- Most data is **filtered out via selective attention**; only attended information proceeds.

### Stage 2 — Short-Term Memory (STM)
- Operates as a **temporary buffer** holding the currently attended stimuli.
- **Maintenance Rehearsal** (rote repetition) sustains contents; without it, traces decay within 15–30 s.
- Capacity is measured in **chunks** (Miller, 1956) — a chunk may be a digit, a word, or a meaningful unit.
- Operates primarily on a **phonological loop** (Baddeley & Hitch, 1974).

### Stage 3 — Long-Term Memory (LTM)
- Information that undergoes **Elaborative Rehearsal** (deep, semantic processing) is transferred from STM to LTM.
- Storage is organized via **semantic networks**; retrieval follows **spreading activation** (Collins & Loftus, 1975).
- LTM is sub-divided (see §2.2 below).

## 2.2 The Working Memory Model (Baddeley & Hitch) — KTU High-Yield

Modern KTU 2024 Scheme treatment of STM emphasizes the **Working Memory Model**, which decomposes the older "STM" into specialized sub-components:

1. **Phonological Loop** — Handles verbal/auditory information. Sub-divided into:
   - *Phonological Store* (inner ear) — Brief echoic trace.
   - *Articulatory Rehearsal Process* (inner voice) — Subvocal repetition.
2. **Visuospatial Sketchpad** — Handles visual and spatial information ("inner eye").
3. **Central Executive** — An attentional controller that coordinates the two slaves, inhibits irrelevant information, and shifts task sets. *Corresponds loosely to Kahneman's "System 2."*
4. **Episodic Buffer** (added 2000) — Integrates information from the phonological loop, sketchpad, and LTM into a coherent episodic representation.

## 2.3 The Two Sub-Types of Long-Term Memory

| Sub-Type | Sub-Sub-Type | Nature | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Explicit / Declarative** | **Episodic** | Autobiographical, time-stamped events | "My first day at KTU College" |
| | **Semantic** | General world knowledge, facts | "Kerala's capital is Thiruvananthapuram" |
| **Implicit / Non-Declarative** | **Procedural** | Motor skills, habits | Riding a bicycle, typing |
| | **Priming** | Facilitation of identification by prior exposure | Faster word recognition after exposure |
| | **Classical Conditioning** | Learned associations | Pavlovian salivation |
| | **Non-Associative** (habituation/sensitization) | Response modulation | Ignoring background noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision-Making Link:** *Semantic* memory underpins **availability** and **familiarity-based heuristics**; *episodic* memory underpins **recency** and **frequency** heuristics. Procedural memory silently runs **System 1** intuitions.

## 2.4 Processes of Memory (The E-S-R Triad)

For any memory system to function, three processes must operate:

1. **Encoding** — Conversion of sensory input into a storable form.
   - *STM:* Acoustic encoding dominates.
   - *LTM:* Semantic / elaborative encoding dominates (Craik & Lockhart, 1972, *Levels of Processing*).
2. **Storage** — Retention of encoded information over time.
3. **Retrieval** — Accessing stored information when needed.
   - *Recall* (free / cued / serial) — generates information from memory with no cues.
   - *Recognition* — identifies information as previously encountered. **Easier than recall**; forms the cognitive basis of the **Recognition Heuristic** (Goldberg & Gigerenzer).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Application in Memory / Heuristics |
|---|---|---|
| **C** (Capacity of STM) | $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | Miller's Law; **base-rate of cognitive load** |
| **R(t)** (Retention) | $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$ | Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve; **S** = relative strength of memory |
| **P(recall)** | $P \propto (\text{rehearsals})^{0.5}$ | Power-law of practice (Newell & Rosenbloom) |
| **Spreading Activation** | $A_{i}(t) = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ji} \cdot A_{j}(t-\Delta t) \cdot d_{ji}^{-1}$ | Semantic priming in LTM retrieval |
| **Levels of Processing** | Depth = f(encoding elaboration) | Deeper semantic processing $\Rightarrow$ stronger LTM trace |
| **Serial Position Effect** | $P_{recall}(i) = f(\text{primacy}, \text{recency})$ | First & last items best remembered |
| **Recognition vs Recall Ratio** | $P_{recog} \approx 2 \cdot P_{recall}$ | Justifies the **Recognition Heuristic** |
| **Encoding Specificity** | Retrieval success = $f(\text{match of cues at encode and retrieve})$ | Context-dependent memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Memory Models:** Memory models are foundational in **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **UX design** (e.g., the 7±2 rule dictates menu-item limits), **AI cognitive architectures** (e.g., SOAR, ACT-R), **educational technology** (spacing effect drives flashcard algorithms), and **forensic decision support systems**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Function

The classical quantification of memory decay comes from Hermann Ebbinghaus (1885). We derive the standard exponential form.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Ebbinghaus measured savings (the reduction in trials required to re-learn a list) at increasing time intervals. The data suggested that **the rate of forgetting is proportional to the current strength of the memory trace**.

### Step 2 — Set up the Differential Equation
Let $S(t)$ be the *strength* of the memory trace at time $t$. Empirical observation gives:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -k \cdot S(t)
$$

where $k > 0$ is the *decay constant*, representing how rapidly a given trace fades.

### Step 3 — Solve the ODE
This is a first-order linear homogeneous ODE. Separating variables:

$$
\int \frac{dS}{S} = -\int k \, dt
$$

$$
\ln \vert S \vert = -k t + C_1
$$

$$
S(t) = S_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Strength to Retention Proportion
Define *retention* $R(t) = S(t)/S_0$. Then:

$$
R(t) = e^{-k t}
$$

It is conventional to express decay in terms of **time-constant** $S = 1/k$ (analogous to RC time-constant in electrical circuits):

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-t/S}}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- A *stronger* memory (larger $S$, e.g., through **elaborative rehearsal** or **spacing**) decays more slowly.
- The trace never reaches zero — it asymptotes — explaining why "forgotten" material can sometimes be retrieved with appropriate cues (a key premise of the **encoding-specificity principle**).

> [!NOTE]
> This exponential form is the same mathematical structure as **capacitor discharge** in an RC circuit, **Newton's law of cooling**, and **radioactive decay** — a beautiful example of universality in quantitative models.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing STM and LTM

We now formalize the comparison with an analytical matrix that KTU examiners frequently test.

$$
\Delta_{\text{STM vs LTM}} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\text{Dimension} & \text{STM} & \text{LTM} \\
\text{Capacity (chunks)} & 7 \pm 2 & \approx 10^{15} \text{ bits} \\
\text{Duration} & 15\text{–}30 \text{ s} & \text{Years to lifetime} \\
\text{Primary Code} & \text{Acoustic} & \text{Semantic} \\
\text{Forgetting Driver} & \text{Decay + Displacement} & \text{Interference + Retrieval failure} \\
\text{Rehearsal Need} & \text{Maintenance (rote)} & \text{Elaborative (semantic)} \\
\text{Consciousness} & \text{Full} & \text{Retrieval-dependent} \\
\text{Neural Substrate} & \text{DLPFC, parietal} & \text{Hippocampus, neocortex} \\
\text{Output to Decision} & \text{Cent. Exec. control} & \text{Intuition (System 1)} \\
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.3 Levels-of-Processing Derivation (Craik & Lockhart, 1972)

The LOP framework replaces the discrete "stores" with a continuum of processing depth.

### Step 1 — Define Processing Depth
Let $D$ denote the depth of encoding. Encoding may be:
- **Shallow:** $D_1$ — Structural (visual features of letters).
- **Intermediate:** $D_2$ — Phonological (sound of the word).
- **Deep:** $D_3$ — Semantic (meaning of the word in context).

### Step 2 — Hypothesis
The probability of a successful recall is monotonically increasing with $D$:

$$
P(\text{recall}) = f(D), \quad f'(D) > 0
$$

Empirically, a logistic form fits well:

$$
P(\text{recall}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\alpha D + \beta)}}
$$

### Step 3 — KTU Exam Implication
For a decision-maker, **deep semantic encoding** of consequences (e.g., "If I drop out, my parents' retirement plan will collapse") produces a stronger LTM trace than shallow structural encoding (e.g., "The form has 4 boxes to tick"). Therefore, **deeper-processed risks are more *available* in memory and disproportionately influence judgment** — explaining the *availability* heuristic in its full form.

## 3.4 Spreading Activation Derivation (Collins & Loftus, 1975)

### Step 1 — Graph Model
Represent LTM as a directed graph where **nodes** = concepts and **edges** = semantic relations. Let $w_{ji}$ be the edge weight from concept $j$ to concept $i$, and $d_{ji}$ be the shortest path length.

### Step 2 — Activation Update Rule
When a concept is cued, activation propagates outward. For a discrete time step $\Delta t$:

$$
A_i(t) = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ji} \cdot \frac{A_j(t - \Delta t)}{d_{ji}}
$$

### Step 3 — Implication for Heuristic Decision-Making
A *prime* (e.g., a recent news story about plane crashes) injects activation at the "plane crash" node. This activation spreads to neighbouring nodes ("air travel", "risk", "danger"), inflating their *availability*. The decision-maker then overestimates the probability of plane crashes, manifesting the **availability heuristic**. The **availability heuristic is therefore a direct consequence of spreading activation in LTM retrieval.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Synthesis:** Every cognitive bias discussed in this course can be reduced to a property of memory encoding, storage, or retrieval. The list of "heuristics" is really a list of *memory short-cuts*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Multi-Store Model of Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env["Environment Stimulus"] --> sm["Sensory Memory\nDuration: 0.25 to 4 s\nCapacity: Large"]
    sm -- "Selective Attention" --> stm["Short Term Memory\nCapacity: 7 plus minus 2 chunks\nDuration: 15 to 30 s\nCode: Acoustic"]
    stm -- "Maintenance Rehearsal" --> stm
    stm -- "Elaborative Rehearsal" --> ltm["Long Term Memory\nCapacity: Unlimited\nDuration: Years\nCode: Semantic"]
    ltm -- "Retrieval via Spreading Activation" --> wm["Working Memory Workspace"]
    wm -- "Recall" --> out1["Decision Output"]
    wm -- "Recognition" --> out2["Decision Output"]
    wm -- "Procedural Trigger" --> out3["Automatic Response System 1"]
    ltm -.->|"Context-dependent cue"| stm
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Baddeley's Working Memory Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["Auditory Input"] --> phs["Phonological Store\nInner Ear"]
    input2["Visual Input"] --> vsp["Visuospatial Sketchpad\nInner Eye"]
    phs --> arp["Articulatory Rehearsal Process\nInner Voice"]
    arp --> phs
    ce["Central Executive\nAttention Controller"] --> phs
    ce --> vsp
    ce --> eb["Episodic Buffer\nIntegrator"]
    phs --> eb
    vsp --> eb
    ltm2["Long Term Memory\nSemantic Network"] --> eb
    eb --> ltm2
    eb --> dec["Decision Output\nHeuristic Selection"]
    ce --> dec
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory-Forgetting Dynamics (Ebbinghaus)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    enc["Encoding Phase\nRehearsal Strength S"] --> store["Memory Trace\nR of t"]
    store --> decay["Decay Function\nR of t = exp minus t over S"]
    decay --> ret["Retained Knowledge\nAvailable for Heuristic Use"]
    cue["Retrieval Cue"] --> ret
    ret --> output["Decision via Availability"]
    enc -.->|"High elaboration"| strongS["Large S slow decay"]
    enc -.->|"Low elaboration"| weakS["Small S fast decay"]
    strongS --> store
    weakS --> store
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Sub-Types and Decision-Making Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ltm3["Long Term Memory"]
    ltm3 --> exp["Explicit Declarative"]
    ltm3 --> imp["Implicit Non-Declarative"]
    exp --> epi["Episodic\nTime-stamped events"]
    exp --> sem["Semantic\nGeneral knowledge"]
    imp --> pro["Procedural\nSkills and habits"]
    imp --> pri["Priming\nFaster recognition"]
    imp --> cond["Classical Conditioning\nLearned responses"]
    epi --> av["Availability Heuristic\nUsed for frequency judgement"]
    sem --> fam["Familiarity Recognition Heuristic\nRecognise vs recall"]
    pro --> sys1["System 1 Intuition\nFast automatic"]
    pri --> sys1
    cond --> aff["Affect Heuristic\nEmotional tagging"]
```

## 4.5 ASCII Block Diagram — Information Processing Pipeline

```
+-------------+     +-----------+     +-----------------+     +-----------+
|  STIMULUS   | --> | SENSORY   | --> | SHORT-TERM      | --> | LONG-TERM |
|  (External) |     | REGISTER  |     | MEMORY (STM)    |     | MEMORY    |
|             |     | (≤ 4 s)   |     | (7±2 chunks)    |     | (unlimit.)|
+-------------+     +-----------+     +-----------------+     +-----------+
                          |                  |                      |
                          v                  v                      v
                   [Selective         [Maintenance          [Retrieval via
                    Attention]         Rehearsal]            Spreading
                                        |                    Activation]
                                        v                      |
                                  [Elaborative <---------------+
                                    Rehearsal]
                                        |
                                        v
                              +-------------------+
                              |  WORKING MEMORY   |
                              |  (Central Exec.)  |
                              +-------------------+
                                        |
                                        v
                              [ HEURISTIC DECISION ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams are deliberately drawn in a layered **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than anatomical drawings, per the KTU schematic convention. Each block represents a *functional* memory stage with measurable properties.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Short-Term Memory (STM) and Long-Term Memory (LTM) along the dimensions of capacity, duration, and primary encoding code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | STM | LTM |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 7 ± 2 chunks (Miller, 1956) | Virtually unlimited |
| **Duration** | 15–30 s without rehearsal | Minutes to a lifetime |
| **Primary Code** | Acoustic / phonological | Semantic (meaning-based) |

A neat tabular distinction covering all three dimensions fetches full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State Miller's Magic Number and explain the concept of "chunking" in short-term memory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
George A. Miller (1956) demonstrated in his landmark paper *"The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two"* that the capacity of STM is approximately **7 ± 2 chunks**. A *chunk* is a meaningful unit formed by grouping individual items (e.g., digits, letters) based on prior knowledge — for example, the 10-digit number "2024123456" can be recoded as three chunks: "2024 / 123 / 456", expanding effective STM capacity.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model of Memory**. Describe the role of attention and rehearsal in transferring information between stores. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Baddeley–Hitch Working Memory Model** in detail. How does the Central Executive contribute to decision-making under bounded rationality? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **Atkinson–Shiffrin Model (1968)** posits three distinct memory stores connected by information-flow pathways:

1. **Sensory Memory (SM)** — Receives raw input from environmental stimuli.
   - *Iconic* store (visual, ~250 ms).
   - *Echoic* store (auditory, ~2–4 s).
   - Most information decays; only attended stimuli are passed forward.

2. **Short-Term Memory (STM)** — A limited-capacity buffer of **7 ± 2 chunks**, **15–30 s** in duration.
   - Operates on a **phonological code** (Conrad, 1964).
   - **Maintenance rehearsal** (rote repetition) keeps items alive.
   - Loss occurs via **decay** and **displacement** by new inputs.

3. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** — A relatively permanent store with **unlimited capacity** organized as a **semantic network**.
   - Items reach LTM only via **elaborative rehearsal** (deep, meaning-based processing).
   - **Spreading activation** (Collins & Loftus, 1975) governs retrieval.

4. **Role of Attention** — Functions as the *gatekeeper* between SM and STM. Without selective attention, sensory traces decay before reaching STM.

5. **Role of Rehearsal** — *Maintenance rehearsal* sustains STM contents; *elaborative rehearsal* transfers them into LTM. The two types of rehearsal are conceptually distinct: maintenance is *shallow*; elaborative is *deep*.

A clean block diagram with three labelled boxes and arrows showing attention and rehearsal pathways fetches full marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The **Baddeley–Hitch Working Memory Model (1974, revised 2000)** decomposes STM into four functional components:

1. **Phonological Loop** — Comprises:
   - *Phonological Store*: brief echoic trace of auditory stimuli (~2 s).
   - *Articulatory Rehearsal Process*: subvocal repetition that refreshes the store.
   - Handles **verbal and acoustic** information. Evidence: phonological similarity effect, word-length effect.

2. **Visuospatial Sketchpad** — Manipulates **visual and spatial** information; supports mental imagery and spatial navigation.

3. **Central Executive** — A domain-general **attentional controller** that:
   - Allocates cognitive resources between the phonological loop and sketchpad.
   - Suppresses irrelevant stimuli (inhibition).
   - Switches between tasks (task-shifting).
   - Is closely linked to **Kahneman's System 2** and to **bounded rationality** in Herbert Simon's sense.

4. **Episodic Buffer** (Baddeley, 2000) — A limited-capacity store that **integrates** information from the two slaves, LTM, and perception into a coherent episodic representation.

**Connection to Bounded Rationality & Decision-Making:** Simon's *bounded rationality* holds that decision-makers cannot optimize; they *satisfice*. The **Central Executive** is the neural instantiation of this bound: it has a **finite attentional resource** (Kahneman's "effort" or "mental effort"). When the Central Executive is overloaded (e.g., by cognitive load, time pressure, or competing stimuli), decision-making reverts to **System 1 heuristics** — fast, frugal, but biased. Hence, *working memory capacity is a direct predictor of susceptibility to cognitive biases and heuristic errors*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse **STM** and **Working Memory**. STM is a *store*; WM is a *system* (with multiple components). Use the correct term per the sub-question. (Lose 1 mark for this conflation.)
> - In part (a), drawing the diagram without labels for *attention* and *rehearsal* arrows costs 2 marks.
> - In part (b), writing only "central executive controls attention" without linking to **bounded rationality** and **heuristic choice** will fetch only 3 of 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **Levels-of-Processing Framework** (Craik & Lockhart, 1972). How does depth of processing affect the formation of long-term memories and the application of the availability heuristic? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Ebbinghaus Forgetting Function** $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$ and explain how the **spacing effect** and **elaborative rehearsal** influence the time-constant $S$. Connect this to the **availability heuristic** in real-world decisions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Craik & Lockhart's Levels-of-Processing (LOP) Framework (1972)** rejected the discrete-store view and proposed a **continuum of processing depth**:

1. **Shallow Processing (Structural)** — Analysis of perceptual features (e.g., is the word in capital letters?).
2. **Intermediate Processing (Phonological)** — Analysis of sound (e.g., does the word rhyme with "table"?).
3. **Deep Processing (Semantic)** — Analysis of meaning in context (e.g., does the sentence make sense?).

**Key Hypothesis:** *Deeper processing leads to stronger, more durable, more easily retrieved memory traces.* This is the **"depth = durability"** principle.

**Empirical Evidence (Craik & Tulving, 1975):** Semantic-encoding conditions produced ~ 2× the recall of structural-encoding conditions.

**Mathematical Formulation (introduced in §3.3):**

$$
P(\text{recall}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\alpha D + \beta)}}
$$

where $D$ = depth, $\alpha, \beta$ are positive constants fit empirically.

**Connection to the Availability Heuristic:**
- The **availability heuristic** (Tversky & Kahneman, 1973) judges frequency/probability by *ease of retrieval*.
- **Deeper-processed** events are more retrievable, hence more *available*.
- Therefore, events that have been **semantically elaborated** during encoding (e.g., vivid, personally relevant, emotionally tagged events) are systematically **over-weighted** in judgment, distorting the perceived probability distribution of the world.
- This explains why vivid media coverage (deep, emotional, narrative encoding) of, e.g., plane crashes leads to **systematic overestimation of flying risk** — a textbook availability bias.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation** (recapitulated from §3.1 with parameter interpretation):

**Step 1.** Let $S(t)$ = memory strength at time $t$. Empirically, $\frac{dS}{dt} = -k S(t)$.

**Step 2.** Solve the ODE:

$$
S(t) = S_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 3.** Define retention $R(t) = S(t)/S_0$ and substitute $S = 1/k$:

$$
R(t) = e^{-t/S}
$$

**Step 4. Effect of Spacing Effect on $S$:**

The **spacing effect** (Ebbinghaus, 1885; Cepeda et al., 2006) shows that *distributed practice* yields stronger long-term retention than *massed practice*. Mathematically:

$$
S_{\text{spaced}} > S_{\text{massed}}
$$

because each spaced retrieval event triggers **reconsolidation**, strengthening the synaptic trace. A larger $S$ means a **slower decay** and a **flatter retention curve**.

**Step 5. Effect of Elaborative Rehearsal on $S$:**

Per Craik & Lockhart, semantic elaboration produces deeper encoding. We model this as a multiplicative boost to the time-constant:

$$
S_{\text{deep}} = \gamma \cdot S_{\text{shallow}}, \quad \gamma > 1
$$

For example, a medical student elaborating "metformin activates AMPK to suppress hepatic gluconeogenesis" will have $S \gg$ a student who merely memorizes "metformin treats diabetes."

**Step 6. Real-World Connection — Availability Heuristic:**

- High-$S$ memories (deep, spaced, elaborated) decay slowly and remain *highly available* for years.
- Low-$S$ memories (shallow, massed) decay rapidly and are *unavailable* within days.
- The **availability heuristic** exploits this asymmetry: retrievable items feel *more frequent* and *more probable*.
- **Engineering implication:** UX designers, public-health communicators, and risk analysts must recognize that *availability ≠ base-rate frequency*. A single viral news story with deep emotional encoding may dominate a citizen's risk perception, displacing a thousand low-$S$ statistical facts — leading to systematic misjudgement of real-world probabilities.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - Forgetting to *define* $S$ and $k$ before writing the ODE loses 1 mark.
> - Skipping the separation-of-variables step costs 1 mark.
> - Writing the spacing effect as merely "studying a little each day" without linking it to **reconsolidation** and the boost in $S$ will fetch only 2 of 3 marks for that sub-section.
> - The final connection to the *availability heuristic* in real-world decisions is **compulsory** for full marks; omitting it costs 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):**

- **Three Memory Stores** — Sensory → STM → LTM; gates are *attention* and *rehearsal*.
- **Miller's Law** — STM capacity is **7 ± 2 chunks**; chunking boosts effective capacity.
- **STM Duration** — **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal.
- **LTM Capacity** — Theoretically **unbounded**; code is **semantic**.
- **Acoustic vs Semantic Encoding** — Acoustic dominates STM; semantic dominates LTM.
- **Maintenance vs Elaborative Rehearsal** — Rote repetition sustains STM; deep semantic processing transfers to LTM.
- **Working Memory (Baddeley–Hitch)** — Phonological loop + Visuospatial sketchpad + Central Executive + Episodic buffer.
- **Central Executive** = Attentional controller; corresponds to **System 2** and **bounded rationality**.
- **LTM Sub-Types** — Explicit (Episodic, Semantic) and Implicit (Procedural, Priming, Conditioning).
- **Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve** — $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$; the time-constant $S$ is boosted by **elaboration** and **spacing**.
- **Levels of Processing** — Deeper semantic encoding $\Rightarrow$ stronger LTM traces and greater *availability* in heuristic use.
- **Spreading Activation (Collins & Loftus)** — Activation spreads along semantic-network edges; the activation level of a node determines its *availability* and its influence on judgement.
- **Recall vs Recognition** — Recognition is easier; underpins the **Recognition Heuristic** (Goldberg & Gigerenzer).
- **Encoding Specificity** — Retrieval success depends on cue-match between encoding and retrieval contexts.
- **Decision-Making Link** — Every heuristic in the course is a *memory short-cut*. Heuristics are fast, frugal, and *accurate on average* in the *adaptive toolbox*, but produce systematic **biases** (availability, frequency, affect, anchoring) when LTM is biased or STM is overloaded.
- **Engineering Applications** — UX design (7±2 menu items), AI cognitive architectures (ACT-R, SOAR), spaced-repetition learning algorithms (Anki, SuperMemo), and forensic decision-support systems all exploit these memory principles.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Short Term and Long Term Memory**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold Accepting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threshold Accepting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Accepting (TA)** is a deterministic trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm introduced by **Gunter Dueck and Tobias Scheuer (1990)** as a deterministic counterpart to Simulated Annealing. It iteratively improves a candidate solution by accepting a worse neighboring solution **only if the deterioration in the objective function value does not exceed a predefined, gradually decreasing threshold**.

In rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, TA belongs to the family of **Local Search Metaheuristics with Modified Acceptance Criterion**, classified under **Stochastic-Deterministic Hybrid Heuristics** within the syllabus taxonomy of Heuristics for Decision Making.

The mathematical acceptance rule is stated as:

$$
x_{new} \text{ is accepted if } \Delta f = f(x_{new}) - f(x_{current}) \leq T_k
$$

where $T_k$ is the threshold at iteration $k$, which is monotonically decreased according to a **threshold (cooling) schedule**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Hiking Down a Foggy Mountain at Dusk:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded (or hiking in dense fog) and trying to reach the lowest valley. You can only feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet with each step. In pure greedy local search, you would only step *downhill* — you would get stuck on the first small hilltop. In **Threshold Accepting**, you allow yourself to take a *small uphill* step, **provided the step is not too steep**. As the evening deepens (the threshold shrinks), you become more choosy — eventually refusing even tiny uphill moves. By sunrise (algorithm termination), you will be deep in the valley with very high probability.

This analogy captures the three pillars of TA:
1. **Exploration** early (large threshold → allows large uphill moves)
2. **Exploitation** later (small threshold → restricts uphill moves)
3. **Determinism** (no coin flip — the decision is fully rule-based)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Salient Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Algorithmic Parameters (must be committed to memory for KTU exams):**
> - **T₀ (Initial Threshold)**: usually set to a small positive fraction (e.g., 1 % to 5 %) of the estimated maximum objective-function deterioration between neighbouring solutions.
> - **α (Cooling Factor)**: typically in the range $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$.
> - **N_iter (Iterations per threshold level)**: a multiple of neighbourhood size.
> - **Stopping criterion**: $T_k \leq T_{min}$ or fixed total iterations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Threshold Accepting algorithm executes the following deterministic logic at every step:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Generate an initial feasible solution $x_0$ (often via greedy heuristic or random seeding).
   - Set the current solution $x_{current} \leftarrow x_0$.
   - Initialise the threshold $T \leftarrow T_0$ and iteration counter $k \leftarrow 0$.

2. **Neighbourhood Sampling**
   - At each iteration, generate a candidate $x_{new} \in N(x_{current})$ (the pre-defined neighbourhood of the current solution).

3. **Deterministic Acceptance Test** — *the heart of TA*
   - Compute the objective deterioration $\Delta f = f(x_{new}) - f(x_{current})$.
   - If $\Delta f \leq 0$ : **accept unconditionally** (improving move).
   - Else if $0 < \Delta f \leq T_k$ : **accept the deterioration** (escape local optimum).
   - Else : **reject** the candidate and retain $x_{current}$.

4. **Threshold Update**
   - After a fixed number of accepted/rejected trials, reduce the threshold deterministically:
   $T_{k+1} \leftarrow \alpha \cdot T_k$, with $0 < \alpha < 1$.

5. **Termination**
   - Stop when $T_k \leq T_{min}$ OR when a maximum iteration budget is exhausted.
   - Return the best-encountered solution $x_{best}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the equations below — they form the spine of any TA-related KTU question.**

| Quantity | Equation / Definition | Boundary Condition / Range | Practical Use |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|--------------|
| Objective Deterioration | $\Delta f = f(x_{new}) - f(x_{current})$ | Real-valued, can be $\leq 0$ or $> 0$ | Core decision input |
| **TA Acceptance Rule** | Accept if $\Delta f \leq T_k$ | $T_k \geq 0$ | Deterministic gate |
| Threshold Schedule (Geometric) | $T_{k} = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Most widely used cooling |
| Threshold Schedule (Linear) | $T_k = T_0 - k \cdot \beta$ | $\beta > 0$ | Used for small problems |
| Threshold Schedule (Logarithmic) | $T_k = T_0 \cdot \dfrac{c}{\log(k+c)}$ | $c > 0$ | Theoretically convergent |
| Convergence to Greedy Search | As $T_k \rightarrow 0$ | $T_{min} \approx 0$ | Reduces to steepest descent |
| Comparison: SA Probability | $P_{SA} = \exp(-\Delta f / T_k)$ | $0 < P_{SA} \leq 1$ | Probabilistic alternative |
| TA Acceptance Region | $\Delta f \in [0,\ T_k]$ | Deterministic band | Replaces $P_{SA}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall (often asked):** TA and SA are *not* identical. TA uses a **hard threshold** (binary decision), whereas SA uses a **probabilistic Boltzmann distribution**. In the limit $T \to 0$, TA → pure greedy descent; SA → greedy descent as well, but via probability collapse.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Threshold Accepting is extensively deployed in:
- **Vehicle Routing & TSP** (travel logistics)
- **VLSI circuit partitioning** (chip design)
- **Job-shop scheduling** (manufacturing)
- **Continuous black-box optimization** (engineering design)
- **Bio-informatics** (protein folding heuristic layers)

Its deterministic acceptance makes TA **easier to tune, more reproducible, and faster per iteration** than SA — a critical advantage in industrial real-time systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Threshold Schedule

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the geometric cooling $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$ is equivalent to a controlled exponential decay and derive its convergence characteristics.

**Step 1 — Starting Point.** Let $T_0$ be the initial threshold chosen empirically. Define the multiplicative decay:

$$
T_1 = \alpha \cdot T_0, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1
$$

**Step 2 — Induction Hypothesis.** Assume at step $k$, the threshold is given by:

$$
T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Iterative Update.** Applying the update rule $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$:

$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k
$$

Substituting the induction hypothesis:

$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot (T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k})
$$

$$
T_{k+1} = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion by Induction.** The closed-form expression for the threshold at iteration $k$ is therefore:

$$
T_{k} = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Analysis.** Taking the limit as $k \rightarrow \infty$:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} T_k = \lim_{k \to \infty} T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k} = T_0 \cdot 0 = 0
$$

Hence the threshold converges monotonically to zero, ensuring that the algorithm asymptotically behaves like a **greedy local search**.

**Step 6 — Time to Reach Threshold Level $T_{min}$.** Solving $T_0 \alpha^k = T_{min}$ for $k$:

$$
\alpha^{k} = \frac{T_{min}}{T_0}
$$

$$
k \cdot \ln \alpha = \ln\!\left(\frac{T_{min}}{T_0}\right)
$$

$$
k = \frac{\ln(T_{min}/T_0)}{\ln \alpha}
$$

Since $\ln \alpha < 0$ and $\ln(T_{min}/T_0) < 0$, the ratio is positive — confirming feasibility.

## 3.2 Comparison Derivation — TA vs. SA Acceptance Region

For **Simulated Annealing**, the acceptance probability of a worsening move $\Delta f > 0$ is:

$$
P_{SA}(\Delta f) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T_k}\right)
$$

For **Threshold Accepting**, the acceptance indicator is:

$$
\mathbb{1}_{TA}(\Delta f) = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta f \leq T_k \\ 0, & \Delta f > T_k \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algebraic Equivalence at Critical $\Delta f$:** Setting $\exp(-\Delta f / T_k) = 0.5$ yields the SA 50 % acceptance boundary:
>
> $$
> \Delta f_{SA, 50\%} = T_k \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \, T_k
> $$
>
> This means TA with threshold $T_k$ is roughly **as permissive as SA with temperature $T_k \cdot 1.44$**. This equivalence is a frequent KTU question.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When TA Outperforms SA

| Criterion | Threshold Accepting | Simulated Annealing |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Acceptance Basis | Deterministic | Probabilistic (Boltzmann) |
| Tuning Complexity | **Low (one main parameter)** | Medium (T + cooling + probability function) |
| Reproducibility | **Excellent (no RNG inside loop)** | Stochastic — different runs vary |
| Computational Cost per Iteration | **Lower** | Higher (exp + RNG) |
| Convergence Speed | Comparable | Comparable |
| Suitability for Noisy Landscapes | Moderate | **Slightly better** (probabilistic smoothing) |
| Ease of Implementation | **High** | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Initialize x_current, T = T_0"]
    nodeB["Set k = 0, best = x_current"]
    nodeC["Generate neighbor x_new in N(x_current)"]
    nodeD{"Compute Delta f = f(x_new) minus f(x_current)"}
    nodeE{"Delta f less than or equal to T_k ?"}
    nodeF["x_current = x_new"]
    nodeG["Reject x_new, keep x_current"]
    nodeH["Update best if f(x_current) better than f(best)"]
    nodeI{"k equals N_per_level ?"}
    nodeJ["T = alpha times T, k = 0"]
    nodeK{"T less than or equal to T_min ?"}
    nodeL["RETURN best solution"]
    nodeM["Continue outer loop"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeM --> nodeC
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeL
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["Input Module: Problem Instance and Parameters"]
    blockB["Initial Solution Generator"]
    blockC["Neighbourhood Operator Module"]
    blockD["Objective Function Evaluator"]
    blockE["Acceptance Decision Unit using T_k"]
    blockF["Threshold Schedule Controller"]
    blockG["Best Solution Memory"]
    blockH["Termination Checker"]
    blockI["Output Module: Best Solution Found"]

    blockA --> blockB --> blockE
    blockA --> blockF
    blockF --> blockE
    blockC --> blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockG
    blockE --> blockH
    blockF --> blockH
    blockG --> blockI
    blockH --> blockI
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Plot — Threshold Decay vs. Acceptance Region

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    plotA["Y axis: Objective Deterioration Delta f"]
    plotB["X axis: Iteration k"]
    plotC["Curve T_k = T_0 alpha to the k, decays geometrically"]
    plotD["Shaded Accept Region below curve"]
    plotE["Reject Region above curve"]
    plotF["As k grows, accept region shrinks toward zero"]

    plotA --> plotC
    plotB --> plotC
    plotC --> plotD
    plotC --> plotE
    plotC --> plotF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Threshold Accepting algorithm. How does it differ from Simulated Annealing in its acceptance criterion?**

**Model Answer:**
Threshold Accepting (TA), proposed by Dueck and Scheuer (1990), is a deterministic metaheuristic in which a worsening neighbouring solution $x_{new}$ is accepted if the objective deterioration $\Delta f = f(x_{new}) - f(x_{current})$ does not exceed a current threshold $T_k$.

The **key difference from Simulated Annealing** lies in the acceptance rule. While SA accepts a worse move with **probability** $\exp(-\Delta f / T_k)$, TA accepts it **deterministically** if $\Delta f \leq T_k$, i.e., the move lies within a hard threshold band. This makes TA computationally cheaper per iteration and fully reproducible across runs.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the threshold schedule $T_k$ in Threshold Accepting? Mention any two commonly used schedules.**

**Model Answer:**
The threshold schedule $T_k$ controls the trade-off between **exploration** (early phase) and **exploitation** (later phase). A large initial $T_0$ allows broad exploration of the search space; as $k$ increases, $T_k$ shrinks, forcing the algorithm to behave like a greedy local search.

Two commonly used schedules are:

$$
T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k} \quad \text{(Geometric cooling, } 0 < \alpha < 1\text{)}
$$

$$
T_k = \frac{T_0}{1 + k} \quad \text{(Linear-Logistic cooling)}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **forgetting the deterministic nature** of TA and confusing it with Simulated Annealing.
> - Always state the **exact acceptance rule** $\Delta f \leq T_k$ — vague phrasing will cost you at least 2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, **show the threshold update step-by-step** with actual numeric values; examiners reward trace tables.
> - Mentioning **Dueck and Scheuer (1990)** as originators earns a content-credit mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Threshold Accepting algorithm in detail with a neat flowchart. Discuss the significance of the threshold schedule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Threshold Accepting is a deterministic metaheuristic proposed by Dueck and Scheuer (1990) for solving combinatorial and continuous optimization problems. It improves upon local search by accepting controlled worsening moves.

**2. Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
Step 1: Choose initial solution x_0, set x_current = x_0, best = x_0.
Step 2: Initialize threshold T = T_0, k = 0.
Step 3: Repeat until stopping criterion met:
   a. Generate x_new from neighbourhood N(x_current).
   b. Compute Delta f = f(x_new) - f(x_current).
   c. If Delta f ≤ T_k, then x_current = x_new.
   d. If f(x_current) < f(best), best = x_current.
   e. Increment k; if k = N_per_level, update T = alpha * T, k = 0.
Step 4: Return best.
```

**3. Significance of Threshold Schedule (3 marks):**
- Controls the exploration–exploitation balance.
- Ensures asymptotic convergence to a local optimum.
- Geometric schedule $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$ is preferred for simplicity and monotonic decay.

**(b)** A minimization problem is solved by TA with $T_0 = 100$ and $\alpha = 0.8$. Compute the thresholds at iterations $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. If at $k = 2$ a candidate solution gives $\Delta f = 25$, will it be accepted? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Threshold values (2 marks):**

$$
T_0 = 100
$$

$$
T_1 = 100 \cdot 0.8 = 80
$$

$$
T_2 = 100 \cdot 0.8^{2} = 100 \cdot 0.64 = 64
$$

$$
T_3 = 100 \cdot 0.8^{3} = 100 \cdot 0.512 = 51.2
$$

$$
T_4 = 100 \cdot 0.8^{4} = 100 \cdot 0.4096 = 40.96
$$

**Acceptance Test (5 marks):**

At $k = 2$, threshold is $T_2 = 64$.

Given $\Delta f = 25$.

Since $25 \leq 64$, the acceptance condition $\Delta f \leq T_k$ is **satisfied**.

**Therefore, the candidate solution will be accepted.**

**Verification (1 mark):** Since $\Delta f = 25 > 0$, this is a worsening move. In a pure greedy local search it would be rejected, but TA's deterministic acceptance band allows it — illustrating TA's ability to escape local optima.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]`

**(a)** Compare Threshold Accepting and Simulated Annealing with respect to acceptance criterion, parameter sensitivity, and convergence behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Threshold Accepting | Simulated Annealing |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Acceptance Criterion (2 marks) | Deterministic: accepts if $\Delta f \leq T_k$ | Probabilistic: accepts with probability $\exp(-\Delta f / T_k)$ |
| Reproducibility (1 mark) | Fully reproducible across runs | Stochastic — runs may differ |
| Parameter Sensitivity (2 marks) | Mainly depends on $T_0$ and $\alpha$ | Depends on $T_0$, $\alpha$, plus randomness seeding |
| Computational Cost (1 mark) | Lower (no exp/RNG per step) | Higher (exponential evaluation + RNG) |
| Convergence (1 mark) | As $T_k \to 0$, reduces to greedy descent | Probabilistic asymptotic convergence guaranteed under slow cooling |

**(b)** Derive the closed-form geometric threshold schedule $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$ using induction, and determine the number of iterations required for $T_k$ to fall below $T_{min} = 0.5$, given $T_0 = 100$ and $\alpha = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Base Case (1 mark):** $T_0 = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{0} = T_0$ ✓

**Inductive Step (4 marks):** Assume $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$. Then:

$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k = \alpha \cdot T_0 \alpha^{k} = T_0 \alpha^{k+1}
$$

Hence by induction, $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$ for all $k \geq 0$.

**Iteration Count Calculation (2 marks):** Solve $T_0 \alpha^k < T_{min}$:

$$
100 \cdot 0.9^{k} < 0.5
$$

$$
0.9^{k} < 0.005
$$

$$
k \cdot \ln(0.9) < \ln(0.005)
$$

$$
k > \frac{\ln(0.005)}{\ln(0.9)} = \frac{-5.298}{-0.1054} \approx 50.27
$$

**Therefore, a minimum of $k = 51$ iterations** is required for the threshold to drop below $0.5$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must know before entering the exam hall):**

- **Origin:** Threshold Accepting was introduced by **Dueck and Scheuer (1990)** as a deterministic counterpart to Simulated Annealing.
- **Acceptance Rule:** A worsening move with $\Delta f = f(x_{new}) - f(x_{current}) > 0$ is accepted **iff** $\Delta f \leq T_k$.
- **Deterministic Nature:** Unlike SA, TA has no probabilistic component in its acceptance step — making it **reproducible**.
- **Threshold Schedule:** Governs exploration vs. exploitation; the **geometric schedule** $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$ is the most common.
- **Cooling Factor Range:** Typically $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$; lower values = faster convergence but risk of premature convergence.
- **Asymptotic Behaviour:** As $k \to \infty$, $T_k \to 0$, and TA degenerates to **greedy local search**.
- **Iteration Count Formula:** $k = \dfrac{\ln(T_{min}/T_0)}{\ln \alpha}$ — frequently asked.
- **Comparison with SA:** SA uses $\exp(-\Delta f / T_k)$; TA uses a hard threshold band; at the **50 % acceptance point**, $\Delta f_{SA} = T_k \ln 2 \approx 0.693 T_k$.
- **Engineering Applications:** TSP, VLSI partitioning, job-shop scheduling, continuous black-box optimization.
- **Examiner Hot-Spots:** Threshold schedule derivations, numeric acceptance tests, TA-vs-SA comparison tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of TS in Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of TS in Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tabu Search (TS)** is a *higher-level, memory-based metaheuristic* introduced by **Fred Glover (1986)** for solving combinatorial and discrete optimization problems that arise in **decision-making environments**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the *Application of TS in Decision Making* refers to the systematic deployment of Tabu Search as an adaptive neighbourhood-search procedure to guide a decision-maker (or an automated decision system) toward *high-quality, near-optimal decisions* in problems where exhaustive enumeration is computationally prohibitive.

A decision-making instance under TS is formally stated as the tuple:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi \;=\;& \{\mathcal{S},\; \mathcal{N}(\cdot),\; f(\cdot),\; \mathcal{T},\; A,\; \text{Stop}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = finite/infinite solution space of feasible decisions.
- $\mathcal{N}(x)$ = neighbourhood structure associated with each decision $x \in \mathcal{S}$.
- $f:\mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = objective function measuring *decision quality* (cost, profit, makespan, risk, etc.).
- $\mathcal{T}$ = **tabu list** (short-term memory of forbidden moves).
- $A$ = **aspiration criterion** that may override tabu status.
- $\text{Stop}$ = termination rule (max iterations, time budget, convergence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Tabu Search is classified as an *iterative, trajectory-based metaheuristic* with explicit memory, distinguishing it from memory-less heuristics such as Simulated Annealing (SA) or Genetic Algorithms (GA) which use probabilistic population/stochastic mechanisms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mountain Climber with a Diary Analogy**
> Imagine a mountain climber searching for the **lowest valley** (best decision) in a fog-covered landscape. Without memory, the climber keeps slipping back into valleys already visited. Now, give the climber a **diary** (the tabu list) where she records the valleys she has recently visited. She *forbids* herself from re-entering these valleys for a fixed period. However, if a forbidden valley appears *exceptionally deep* (aspiration), she is allowed to enter it. The diary length (tabu tenure) and the depth criterion (aspiration) jointly steer the climber toward undiscovered regions — exactly how Tabu Search steers a **decision-making process**.

In a managerial/engineering decision context:

- The **landscape** = the objective-function surface of business/engineering choices (cost vs quality, time vs risk).
- The **climber** = the decision-support engine running TS.
- The **diary entries** = recently *selected* decisions now marked *tabu* to prevent cycling.
- The **aspiration rule** = override when a tabu move yields the **global best-so-far** solution.

This memory-driven, deterministic-with-controlled-randomness philosophy is what makes TS exceptionally effective for **combinatorial decision problems** such as scheduling, routing, portfolio selection, and resource allocation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Tabu Search Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Tabu Tenure ($tt$)** — number of iterations a move remains *forbidden*; typically $\mathbf{tt \in [0.1 \cdot n,\; 0.5 \cdot n]}$ where $n$ = problem dimension.
- **Aspiration Level ($A(x)$)** — quality threshold that, if beaten, permits a tabu move; default $\mathbf{A(x) = f(x^*_{\text{best}})}$.
- **Neighbourhood Size ($\vert\mathcal{N}(x)\vert$)** — number of candidate moves generated per iteration; typically $\mathbf{\vert\mathcal{N}\vert = \mathcal{O}(n)}$ to $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(n^2)}$.
- **Diversification Intensity ($D$)** — long-term memory frequency penalty; usually $\mathbf{D \in [0,\, 1]}$ as a penalty coefficient.
- **Intensification Trigger** — frequency-based recovery of elite solutions; standard threshold $\mathbf{\geq 2 \cdot n}$ visits to a feature.
- **Stopping Rule** — $\mathbf{I_{\max} \in [100,\; 10\,000]}$ iterations OR $\mathbf{\Delta f < 10^{-6}}$ for $\mathbf{50}$ consecutive iterations.

---

## 1.4 Why Tabu Search for Decision Making?

| Decision-Making Need | Tabu Search Response |
|---|---|
| Avoid cycling between sub-optimal decisions | **Short-term memory** ($\mathcal{T}$) forbids recent moves. |
| Escape local optima (bad decisions that "look good") | **Diversification** via long-term memory + strategic oscillation. |
| Handle *NP-hard* combinatorial choices (routing, scheduling) | **Neighbourhood search** with polynomial per-iteration cost. |
| Incorporate decision-maker's preferences | **Aspiration criterion** + customizable objective $f(\cdot)$. |
| Re-optimize as data changes (dynamic decisions) | **Warm-start** from previous solution + adaptive $tt$. |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

The application of Tabu Search in a **decision-making pipeline** unfolds as a sequence of well-defined operational stages. Below is the canonical 6-stage logical breakdown expected in KTU 2024 evaluations:

### Stage A — *Decision Encoding*
The real-world decision variables are encoded into a **solution representation** $x$ that TS can manipulate.
- **Permutation encoding** for sequencing decisions (e.g., job order, vehicle route).
- **Binary encoding** for inclusion/exclusion decisions (e.g., project selection, knapsack).
- **Real-valued encoding** for continuous portfolio weights (with discretization).

### Stage B — *Neighbourhood Definition*
A **move operator** $m \in \mathcal{M}$ defines how one decision $x$ transforms into another $x'$.
- **Swap move**: $x' = x$ with two components exchanged.
- **Insert/Remove move**: shift a component across positions.
- **2-opt move**: reverse a sub-sequence (routing decisions).
- **Flip move**: toggle a binary variable (project portfolio decisions).

### Stage C — *Tabu Classification*
Each candidate move $m$ is tested against the **tabu list** $\mathcal{T}$. A move is *tabu* if it would *undo* a recently performed move within tenure $tt$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tabu}(m) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{True}, & \text{if } m \in \mathcal{T} \;\text{AND}\; f(x') < A \\ \text{False}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stage D — *Aspiration Override*
If a tabu move produces a solution better than the **best-known decision** $x^*$, the tabu restriction is waived.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \;=\; f(x^*_{\text{best}})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stage E — *Selection of Next Decision*
TS adopts a **best-improvement** (greedy) strategy among admissible (non-tabu or aspirationally overridden) neighbours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' \;=\; \arg\min_{x' \in \mathcal{N}^*(x)} \; f(x')
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathcal{N}^*(x)$ is the *admissible neighbourhood* after tabu filtering.

### Stage F — *Memory Update*
After committing to $x'$, the move $m$ is appended to $\mathcal{T}$ with a countdown timer $tt$. If $tt = 0$, the move is removed (FIFO discipline).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Decision Makers:** The deterministic *best-improvement* step guarantees **repeatable** decisions — a regulatory and auditability benefit that stochastic heuristics (SA, GA) cannot offer natively.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Tabu Search Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations**
> (Use $\vert$ for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown tables.)

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective minimization | $\min\limits_{x \in \mathcal{S}} f(x)$ | Core decision criterion | Cost / Time / Risk |
| 2 | Neighbourhood | $\mathcal{N}(x) = \{x' \;:\; d(x,x') \leq k\}$ | Defines decision-move distance | $k$-swap / $k$-opt |
| 3 | Tabu activation | $\text{Tabu}(m) = \mathbb{1}[m \in \mathcal{T}_{tt}]$ | Binary classification | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| 4 | Aspiration rule | $A = f(x^*_{\text{best}})$ | Override quality threshold | Same as $f$ |
| 5 | Tabu tenure | $tt = \lceil \alpha \cdot n \rceil$ | Memory horizon | $\alpha \in [0.1,\, 0.5]$ |
| 6 | Frequency penalty | $\phi(x) = -\lambda \cdot \text{freq}(x)$ | Long-term diversification | $\lambda > 0$ |
| 7 | Updated objective | $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x) + \phi(x)$ | Diversified decision quality | Same as $f$ |
| 8 | Convergence gap | $\Delta_k = \vert f(x^*_k) - f(x^*_{k-1}) \vert$ | Termination metric | $\Delta_k < \varepsilon$ |
| 9 | Stop — iteration rule | $k \geq I_{\max}$ | Time / budget bound | Iterations |
| 10 | Stop — quality rule | $\Delta_k < \varepsilon$ for $W$ consecutive $k$ | Convergence-based halt | $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$, $W = 50$ |
| 11 | Diversification rate | $\rho = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{N}_{\text{div}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{N} \vert}$ | Fraction of diversifying moves | $\rho \in [0,\, 1]$ |
| 12 | Elite reactivation | $\text{Reactivate}(x_e)$ if $\text{freq}(x_e) \geq \theta$ | Intensification trigger | $\theta = 2n$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Decision-Making Applications

> [!NOTE]
> These six application domains are KTU-favourite essay territory for 14-mark Part B questions.

1. **Production Scheduling** — minimize makespan $\sum_j C_j$ across machines; TS outperforms dispatch rules.
2. **Vehicle Routing (VRP)** — minimize total route distance $\sum_{r \in R} \sum_{e \in r} d_e$; 2-opt neighbourhood is canonical.
3. **Portfolio Selection** — maximize risk-adjusted return $\dfrac{E[R_p] - r_f}{\sigma_p}$ under cardinality constraints.
4. **Project / Investment Selection (Knapsack)** — maximize $\sum v_i x_i$ subject to $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$.
5. **Network / Hub Design** — minimize $\sum_{i,j} c_{ij} y_{ij}$ for hub-and-spoke topologies.
6. **Dynamic Re-Decision Making** — re-optimize as new data arrives (real-time inventory, dispatching).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Tabu Search on a 4-City TSP (Decision-Making Setting)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical purpose:** Demonstrate how a routing *decision* (which city-order is chosen) evolves under TS, with explicit tabu bookkeeping, aspiration override, and convergence arithmetic.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A logistics manager must plan the visit sequence of **4 delivery cities** $\mathcal{C} = \{A, B, C, D\}$ starting and ending at depot $A$. The pairwise distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$ (in km) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 12 & 28 & 19 \\ 12 & 0 & 15 & 24 \\ 28 & 15 & 0 & 10 \\ 19 & 24 & 10 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Objective:** Find the permutation $x = (A, i, j, k, A)$ minimizing total tour length $f(x) = \sum_{e \in x} d_e$.

### 3.1.2 Tabu Search Parameters

- **Neighbourhood operator:** 2-opt (reverse a sub-segment).
- **Tabu tenure:** $tt = 2$ iterations.
- **Aspiration:** any tabu move producing $f(x') < f(x^*_{\text{best}})$.
- **Initialization:** $x_0 = (A, B, C, D, A)$ with $f(x_0) = 12 + 15 + 10 + 19 = 56$ km.
- **$x^*_{\text{best}}$** initialised to $x_0$.

### 3.1.3 Iteration-by-Iteration Walkthrough

#### **Iteration $k = 1$**

Generate the 2-opt neighbourhood of $x_0 = (A,B,C,D,A)$ by reversing sub-segments of length 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &: (A,C,B,D,A) \rightarrow f = 28 + 15 + 24 + 19 = 86 \\
N_2 &: (A,B,D,C,A) \rightarrow f = 12 + 24 + 10 + 28 = 74 \\
N_3 &: (A,D,C,B,A) \rightarrow f = 19 + 10 + 15 + 12 = 56 \\
N_4 &: (A,D,B,C,A) \rightarrow f = 19 + 24 + 15 + 28 = 86 \\
N_5 &: (A,C,D,B,A) \rightarrow f = 28 + 10 + 24 + 12 = 74 \\
N_6 &: (A,B,C,D,A) \text{ (identity, excluded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the tabu list $\mathcal{T} = \emptyset$, all candidates are admissible. **Best-improvement selection**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 \;=\; \arg\min_i f(N_i) \;=\; N_1 \text{ or } N_3 \text{ (tied at } 56)
\end{aligned}
$$

Tie broken by lexicographic order ⇒ $x_1 = (A, C, B, D, A)$. **Tabu list updated** with the reverse-move $(B,C) \rightarrow (C,B)$ for $tt = 2$.

$f(x^*_{\text{best}}) \leftarrow \min(56, 86) = 56$ ⇒ no improvement yet.

#### **Iteration $k = 2$**

Evaluate 2-opt neighbours of $x_1 = (A, C, B, D, A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &: (A,B,C,D,A) \rightarrow f = 56 \\
M_2 &: (A,C,D,B,A) \rightarrow f = 28 + 10 + 24 + 12 = 74 \\
M_3 &: (A,D,B,C,A) \rightarrow f = 86 \\
M_4 &: (A,C,B,A,D,A) \text{ (infeasible, length mismatch — discarded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabu filter:** $M_1$ would undo the move $(B,C) \rightarrow (C,B)$ which is in $\mathcal{T}$ with one iteration remaining. Aspiration test: $f(M_1) = 56 = f(x^*_{\text{best}})$ — **does NOT beat** $x^*_{\text{best}}$; aspiration denied.

Hence $M_1$ remains **tabu**. Among admissible $M_2, M_3$: best is $M_2$ with $f = 74$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 \;=\; (A, C, D, B, A), \quad f(x_2) = 74
\end{aligned}
$$

$\mathcal{T}$: previous entry expires (now $tt = 0$); append the move $(B,D) \rightarrow (D,B)$ with $tt = 2$.

#### **Iteration $k = 3$**

2-opt neighbours of $x_2 = (A, C, D, B, A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: (A,D,C,B,A) \rightarrow f = 19 + 10 + 15 + 12 = 56 \\
P_2 &: (A,C,B,D,A) \rightarrow f = 86 \\
P_3 &: (A,B,D,C,A) \rightarrow f = 74
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabu filter:** $P_2$ would reverse $(D,B) \rightarrow (B,D)$, in $\mathcal{T}$. Aspiration: $f(P_2) = 86 > 56$ ⇒ denied.
$P_3$ would reverse $(C,D) \rightarrow (D,C)$; not in $\mathcal{T}$ ⇒ admissible.
$P_1$ admissible.

**Best admissible:** $P_1$ with $f = 56$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_3 \;=\; (A, D, C, B, A), \quad f(x_3) = 56
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(x_3) = 56 = f(x^*_{\text{best}})$ (not strictly better), no aspiration needed.

$\mathcal{T}$: previous entry expires; append move $(C,D) \rightarrow (D,C)$ with $tt = 2$.

$f(x^*_{\text{best}})$ remains $\mathbf{56}$ km.

#### **Iteration $k = 4$**

Neighbourhood of $x_3 = (A, D, C, B, A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &: (A,C,D,B,A) \rightarrow f = 74 \\
Q_2 &: (A,D,B,C,A) \rightarrow f = 86 \\
Q_3 &: (A,B,C,D,A) \rightarrow f = 56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabu filter:** $Q_3$ would reverse $(D,C) \rightarrow (C,D)$ which is in $\mathcal{T}$ with $tt = 1$. Aspiration test: $f(Q_3) = 56 = f(x^*_{\text{best}})$ — not strictly better ⇒ aspiration denied, $Q_3$ stays tabu.

**Best admissible:** $Q_1$ with $f = 74$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_4 \;=\; (A, C, D, B, A), \quad f(x_4) = 74
\end{aligned}
$$

This revisits $x_2$ — **cycling detected**! Long-term frequency memory now triggers **diversification**: penalize $f(x)$ with $\phi(x) = -\lambda \cdot \text{freq}(x)$ where $\text{freq}(x_2) = 2$, $\lambda = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}(x_4) \;=\; 74 - 5 \cdot 2 \;=\; 64
\end{aligned}
$$

Effective ranking now forces exploration of an under-visited region.

### 3.1.4 Convergence Statement

After $\mathbf{4}$ iterations, the best-known decision is $x^* = (A, B, C, D, A)$ or $(A, D, C, B, A)$ with $f(x^*) = 56$ km.

**Stopping check:** $\Delta_4 = \vert f(x^*_4) - f(x^*_3) \vert = \vert 56 - 56 \vert = 0$. With $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$, the algorithm halts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision-maker's takeaway:** Both $(A,B,C,D,A)$ and $(A,D,C,B,A)$ deliver identical 56 km tours — these are *symmetric reverse tours* of the same loop. TS correctly identifies multiple *equally optimal decisions*, enabling managerial flexibility (e.g., prioritizing customer A first if service contracts dictate).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Tabu Search

| Problem Characteristic | Tabu Search Suitability | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| NP-hard combinatorial optimization | **Excellent** | Polynomial per-iteration cost. |
| Need for *repeatable* decisions | **Excellent** | Deterministic best-improvement. |
| Solution requires memory of past decisions | **Excellent** | Native tabu + frequency memory. |
| Continuous non-convex objective | **Moderate** | Needs discretization or hybrid. |
| Pure unconstrained continuous optimization | **Poor** | Use Simulated Annealing or CMA-ES. |
| Real-time strict $\mathcal{O}(1\text{ms})$ decisions | **Moderate** | Tune $\vert\mathcal{N}\vert$ for latency. |
| Multi-objective (3+ objectives) | **Moderate** | Requires Pareto-aspiration variants. |
