# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tabu Search Decision-Making Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Decision Problem Definition]) --> initB[Encode decisions as solution x0]
    initB --> initC[Compute initial objective f x0]
    initC --> evalD{Convergence reached?}
    evalD -- Yes --> stopE([Output best decision x-star])
    evalD -- No --> neighF[Generate neighbourhood N x using move operator]
    neighF --> filtG[Apply tabu list filter T]
    filtG --> aspnH{Aspiration override triggered?}
    aspnH -- Yes --> allowI[Admit move, mark as aspirational]
    aspnH -- No --> allowI
    allowI --> selJ[Best-improvement selection of x-prime]
    selJ --> updK[Update tabu list T with new move for tt iterations]
    updK --> divL{Frequency penalty active?}
    divL -- Yes --> penM[Penalize f using phi x]
    divL -- No --> recN[Record new x and f x]
    penM --> recN
    recN --> evalD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node-ID Compliance:** All identifiers (startA, initB, …, recN) are alphanumeric and free of reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Application Layer View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Decision Data Source: ERP CRM Sensors] --> encB[Encoder: Map decisions to solution space S]
    encB --> tsC[Tabu Search Engine]
    tsC --> tabuD[Tabu Memory Bank]
    tsC --> aspE[Aspiration Module]
    tsC --> divF[Diversification Module]
    tsC --> outG[Decision Output: x-star and f x-star]
    outG --> actH[Decision Support Dashboard]
    actH --> execI[Execution Layer: Operations / Allocation]
    execI --> fbJ[Feedback Channel: Outcomes and New Data]
    fbJ --> encB
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Decision Data Source** feeds raw managerial / sensor inputs.
- The **Encoder** maps them into the formal solution space $\mathcal{S}$.
- The **TS Engine** orchestrates neighbourhoods, tabu filtering, aspiration, and diversification.
- The **Decision Support Dashboard** visualizes $x^*$ (recommended decision) and $f(x^*)$ (expected quality).
- The **Feedback Channel** closes the loop — TS adapts to dynamic environments (e.g., updated demand, costs).

---

## 4.3 Tabu Tenure vs Decision Quality — Conceptual Trade-off

```
Decision Quality f(x*)
      ▲
      │            ● Plateau region
      │         ●     ●
      │      ●            ●
      │   ●                  ●
      │  ●                     ●
      │ ●                        ●
      │●                            ●
      └──────────────────────────────────► Tabu Tenure tt
       0   0.1n  0.2n  0.3n  0.4n  0.5n

     Region A         Region B         Region C
     (too short:      (balanced:       (too long:
      cycling)         best decisions)  over-forbidden)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Operating in Region A causes *cycling* (decisions repeat). Region C causes *starvation* (good moves permanently forbidden). Region B — typically $tt \approx 0.2n$ — is the KTU-recommended default.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Tabu Search and state any two characteristics that distinguish it from Simulated Annealing.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Tabu Search (TS), proposed by **Fred Glover in 1986**, is a *memory-based metaheuristic* that iteratively improves a candidate decision $x$ by exploring its neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$ while forbidding recently visited moves through a **tabu list** $\mathcal{T}$.
>
> Two distinguishing characteristics over Simulated Annealing (SA):
> 1. **Explicit memory:** TS maintains a tabu list that records recently made moves, whereas SA is *memoryless*.
> 2. **Deterministic best-improvement:** TS selects the admissible neighbour with the lowest $f(\cdot)$; SA accepts worse moves with a *probabilistic* Metropolis criterion.
> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per characteristic = 3 marks.)*

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is the role of the *aspiration criterion* in Tabu Search?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The aspiration criterion overrides the tabu status of a move $m$ if applying $m$ would yield a solution $x'$ with objective value strictly better than the current best-known decision $f(x^*_{\text{best}})$.
> Mathematically: admit $m$ even if $m \in \mathcal{T}$, provided $f(x') < f(x^*_{\text{best}})$.
> **Role:** Prevents the loss of *exceptionally good* decisions that happen to be tabu, ensuring TS does not discard the global optimum due to memory restrictions. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark role = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice — Question A (14 marks)**

> **Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Choice A]`
>
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **application of Tabu Search for solving the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** in a logistics decision-making context. Describe the solution encoding, neighbourhood structure, tabu list design, and aspiration criterion. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A courier company must serve **5 customers** from a central depot. The distance matrix (km) is:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> D \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
> 0 & 8 & 14 & 6 & 11 \\
> 8 & 0 & 9 & 12 & 7 \\
> 14 & 9 & 0 & 5 & 10 \\
> 6 & 12 & 5 & 0 & 8 \\
> 11 & 7 & 10 & 8 & 0
> \end{bmatrix}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Two candidate routes are evaluated:
> - $R_1 = (0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 0)$
> - $R_2 = (0 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 0)$
>
> Compute the total distance for each route. Identify which is the better routing *decision* and explain how a single 2-opt swap move would transform the worse route into the better one. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) — 7 marks**
>
> *Solution encoding:* A VRP solution is encoded as a permutation of customers per vehicle, e.g., $x = ((r_1), (r_2), \dots, (r_K))$ where $r_k$ is the ordered customer list assigned to vehicle $k$. Depot 0 anchors each route.
>
> *Neighbourhood structure:* Classical intra-route **2-opt** (reverse a sub-segment of $r_k$) plus inter-route **relocate** or **swap** (move a customer between vehicles).
>
> *Tabu list design:* Each 2-opt reversal $(i,j)$ is recorded as a forbidden *attribute* for $tt$ iterations; with $tt = 0.2 \cdot n$ where $n$ = total customers.
>
> *Aspiration criterion:* A tabu 2-opt move is admitted if its resulting route length is strictly less than the best-known solution $f(x^*_{\text{best}})$.
>
> *Objective:*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> f(x) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{e \in r_k} d_e
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *(Encoding 2 marks + neighbourhood 2 marks + tabu + aspiration 2 marks + objective 1 mark = 7 marks.)*
>
> **(b) — 7 marks**
>
> Compute $R_1$:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> f(R_1) \;=\; d_{0,1} + d_{1,2} + d_{2,3} + d_{3,4} + d_{4,0} \;=\; 8 + 9 + 5 + 8 + 11 \;=\; 41 \text{ km}
> \end{aligned}
>
> $$

> Compute $R_2$:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> f(R_2) \;=\; d_{0,3} + d_{3,2} + d_{2,4} + d_{4,1} + d_{1,0} \;=\; 6 + 5 + 10 + 7 + 8 \;=\; 36 \text{ km}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Decision:** $R_2$ is the better routing decision since $f(R_2) = 36 < 41 = f(R_1)$.
>
> **2-opt transformation:** Reverse sub-segment $[2,4]$ in $R_1$ to obtain $R_1' = (0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 0)$. New length:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> f(R_1') \;=\; 8 + 7 + 8 + 5 + 14 \;=\; 42 \text{ km}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> This particular swap is *not* enough — a further relocate of customer 2 to the front yields $R_2$ structurally. The key 2-opt on $[3,2]$ within $R_1$ produces the reversal needed to align with $R_2$'s ordering.
>
> *(Distance calculations 3 marks + comparison 1 mark + 2-opt logic 3 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

> **Module 3 Internal Choice — Question B (14 marks)**

> **Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Choice B]`
>
> **(a)** Explain with suitable equations how **Tabu Search can be applied to project selection / capital budgeting decisions**. Discuss the binary encoding scheme, swap-based neighbourhood, and the role of diversification via frequency-based penalties. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A firm must choose from **4 projects** with the following net present value (NPV in ₹ lakh) and capital requirement (₹ lakh). Total capital budget $B = 12$.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Project:} \quad & i=1 & i=2 & i=3 & i=4 \\
> \text{NPV } (v_i): \quad & 8 & 6 & 10 & 5 \\
> \text{Cost } (w_i): \quad & 5 & 4 & 7 & 3
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Evaluate the following two binary decisions:
> - $D_1 = (1,1,0,1)$ (select projects 1, 2, 4)
> - $D_2 = (0,1,1,0)$ (select projects 2, 3)
>
> Compute NPV and capital used. Identify the better decision. Show how a single **bit-flip move** transforms the worse decision into a feasible, better one if possible. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) — 7 marks**
>
> *Binary encoding:* A decision $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ with $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$, where $x_i = 1$ means *select project $i$*.
>
> *Mathematical objective (binary knapsack formulation):*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \max_{x \in \{0,1\}^n} \; & \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i \\
> \text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq B
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *Swap-based neighbourhood:* A *bit-flip move* $m_i$ toggles $x_i$ from 0 to 1 (or 1 to 0). The neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$ contains up to $n$ such flips.
>
> *Tabu activation:* If project $i$ was *added* recently, it is marked tabu to *remove* for $tt$ iterations — preventing oscillation between selecting and deselecting the same project.
>
> *Diversification via frequency penalty:*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \tilde{f}(x) \;=\; \sum_{i} v_i x_i - \lambda \cdot \sum_{i} \text{freq}(x_i)
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Projects selected too often get penalized, forcing the search into *underexplored* regions of the project space. *(Encoding 2 marks + objective 2 marks + tabu 1 mark + diversification 2 marks = 7 marks.)*
>
> **(b) — 7 marks**
>
> Compute for $D_1 = (1,1,0,1)$:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{NPV}(D_1) \;=\; 8 + 6 + 0 + 5 \;=\; 19 \text{ lakh} \\
> \text{Cost}(D_1) \;=\; 5 + 4 + 0 + 3 \;=\; 12 \text{ lakh} \leq 12 \;\;\checkmark \text{ feasible}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Compute for $D_2 = (0,1,1,0)$:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{NPV}(D_2) \;=\; 0 + 6 + 10 + 0 \;=\; 16 \text{ lakh} \\
> \text{Cost}(D_2) \;=\; 0 + 4 + 7 + 0 \;=\; 11 \text{ lakh} \leq 12 \;\;\checkmark \text{ feasible}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Decision:** $D_1$ is better since NPV$(D_1) = 19 > 16 = $ NPV$(D_2)$.
>
> **Bit-flip transformation from $D_2 \rightarrow D_1$:**
> - Flip bit 1 from 0 → 1 ⇒ cost increases by 5 (now 16 > 12 ⇒ **infeasible**).
> - Flip bit 4 from 0 → 1 ⇒ cost increases by 3 (now 14 > 12 ⇒ **infeasible**).
> - Combined flip bits 1 and 4: cost $11 + 5 + 3 = 19 > 12$ ⇒ still infeasible.
> - Therefore, $D_2$ cannot reach $D_1$ by a single bit flip alone; a **sequence** of flips (e.g., flip bit 3 off, then bits 1 and 4 on) is required:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> D_2' = (0,1,0,0) \xrightarrow{\text{flip 1}} (1,1,0,0) \xrightarrow{\text{flip 4}} (1,1,0,1) = D_1
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Cost path: $11 \rightarrow 7 \rightarrow 12$ (each step feasible). This illustrates that TS performs *sequential* admissible flips, not isolated ones.
>
> *(NPV + cost computations 2 marks + comparison 1 mark + flip-sequence analysis 4 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers in TS Decision-Making Answers**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the aspiration override.** Students often state "tabu moves are NEVER accepted." Examiners deduct **2 marks** instantly — aspiration is the *safety valve* and must be discussed.
> 2. **Confusing tabu tenure ($tt$) with iteration count ($k$).** Tenure is a *property of a move*, not a global counter. Write "move $m$ is tabu for $tt$ iterations after insertion."
> 3. **Writing the objective as $\min f(x)$ without stating the decision variables' domain.** Always specify $x \in \{0,1\}^n$ (knapsack), $x \in \Pi_n$ (permutation), or $x \in [0,1]^n$ (continuous portfolio).
> 4. **Skipping feasibility checks after a move.** In constrained decision problems (knapsack, VRP), each candidate move MUST be checked against constraints before objective evaluation — examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for this step.
> 5. **Not drawing the Mermaid/flow diagram in Part B (a).** Even a hand-drawn block diagram earns the "neighbourhood structure" sub-mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Application of TS in Decision Making**
>
> ✅ Tabu Search is a **memory-based, deterministic metaheuristic** (Glover, 1986).
> ✅ Decision-making pipeline: **Encode → Define Neighbourhood → Tabu Filter → Aspiration → Best-Improvement → Update Memory**.
> ✅ Key components: **Tabu List $\mathcal{T}$**, **Aspiration $A = f(x^*)$**, **Tabu Tenure $tt$**.
> ✅ Tabu tenure sweet spot: **$tt \approx 0.2n$**.
> ✅ Aspiration condition: admit a tabu move if $f(x') < f(x^*_{\text{best}})$.
> ✅ Two memory types: **Short-term** (recency, tabu list) and **Long-term** (frequency, diversification).
> ✅ Diversification penalty: $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \cdot \text{freq}(x)$.
> ✅ Stopping rules: iteration cap ($I_{\max}$) **OR** convergence gap ($\Delta_k < \varepsilon$ for $W$ steps).
> ✅ Standard application domains: **VRP, Scheduling, Portfolio, Knapsack, Hub Design, Dynamic Re-decision**.
> ✅ Move operators: **2-opt** (routing), **Swap / Bit-flip** (selection), **Insert / Relocate** (hybrid).
> ✅ Always perform **feasibility verification** of candidates before objective evaluation.
> ✅ Compare TS vs SA vs GA: TS uses **memory + determinism**; SA uses **probabilistic acceptance**; GA uses **population-based recombination**.
> ✅ Recommended equation for decision-maker's KPI reporting: $\Delta f_k = \vert f(x^*_k) - f(x^*_{k-1}) \vert$.
> ✅ Avoid these three pitfalls: (i) No aspiration, (ii) Confusing tenure with iteration count, (iii) Ignoring constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulated Annealing: Main Components of Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simulated Annealing: Main Components of Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a **stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic** inspired by the thermodynamic process of **annealing in metallurgy**, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and slowly cooled to produce a low-energy, defect-free crystalline structure. In optimization, SA transforms this physical analogy into a mathematical framework to escape local optima and probabilistically converge toward the **global optimum** of a discrete or continuous objective function.

The algorithm was formally introduced by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, and Vecchi (1983)** based on the **Metropolis-Hastings algorithm** (1953) from statistical mechanics. It belongs to the class of **local search methods** but allows the acceptance of **inferior (worsening) solutions** with a probability that decreases over time, controlled by a **temperature parameter**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mountain Trekker" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a blindfolded hiker lost in a mountainous terrain, trying to reach the **lowest valley** (global optimum) of the landscape.
> - In a pure greedy/hill-descent algorithm, the hiker always steps **downhill** — they will get stuck in the **nearest valley** (local optimum) and never know the deeper valley exists on the other side of a hill.
> - In **Simulated Annealing**, the hiker is allowed to occasionally step **uphill** (accept worse solutions) to cross hills and explore other valleys.
> - At the **start (high temperature)**, the hiker is "agitated" and accepts many uphill moves — exploring widely.
> - As the journey continues (temperature **cools**), the hiker becomes "steadier" and rarely accepts uphill steps — eventually settling into the deepest valley found.

This trade-off between **exploration** (high T) and **exploitation** (low T) is the heart of Simulated Annealing.

## 1.3 The Six Pillars (Main Components) — Quick Overview

The complete SA framework is defined by **six interdependent components**. Mastering these is essential for KTU Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Main Components of Simulated Annealing:**
> 1. **Solution (State) Space Representation** — $S$
> 2. **Objective Function (Cost / Energy Function)** — $f(s)$
> 3. **Neighborhood Structure (Move Operator)** — $N(s)$
> 4. **Acceptance Probability Function (Metropolis Criterion)**
> 5. **Cooling Schedule (Annealing Schedule)** — $T(t)$
> 6. **Markov Chain Length (Inner Loop Iterations)** — $L_k$

Each component is a **design decision** that directly controls the algorithm's ability to find a high-quality solution within computational limits. In the next sections, we will dissect each one with full mathematical rigor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Solution (State) Space Representation ($S$)

The **state space** is the set of all feasible candidate solutions to the optimization problem.

$$S = \{ s \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \mid s \text{ satisfies all constraints} \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - For **TSP** (Traveling Salesman Problem): $S$ = all permutations of cities.
> - For **0/1 Knapsack**: $S$ = all binary vectors $\{0,1\}^{n}$.
> - For **continuous problems**: $S \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ with box constraints.

**Role in SA:** Provides the **search domain** over which neighbor moves are defined.

## 2.2 Component 2 — Objective Function $f(s)$ (Energy/Cost)

A mapping $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a numerical quality score to every state.

> [!NOTE]
> In SA terminology, $f(s)$ is often called the **"energy"** of state $s$ (borrowed from physics), even though it may represent cost, distance, penalty, or any minimization objective.

**Minimization convention:** Lower $f(s)$ is better. (Maximization problems are converted via $f'(s) = -f(s)$.)

**Role in SA:** Acts as the **landscape** the algorithm navigates.

## 2.3 Component 3 — Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$

Defines how a new candidate state $s'$ is generated from a current state $s$. Formally:

$$N(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid s' \text{ is reachable from } s \text{ by one move operator} \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Move Operators:**
> - **Bit-flip** (binary problems): flip one bit of $s$.
> - **2-opt swap** (TSP): reverse a sub-tour segment.
> - **Perturbation** (continuous): $s' = s + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma)$.

**Role in SA:** Determines the **step size** and the **connectivity** of the search graph. A larger neighborhood gives broader exploration but increases computation per iteration.

## 2.4 Component 4 — Acceptance Probability Function (Metropolis Criterion)

This is the **core mathematical innovation** of SA, given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$P(\text{accept } s') = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } f(s') \leq f(s) \text{ (improving move)} \\ \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta f}{T}\right), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where the cost difference is:

$$\Delta f = f(s') - f(s) > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the exponential form?**
> 1. **Always accepts better solutions** → guarantees monotonic progress.
> 2. **Probability depends on $\Delta f / T$** → worse solutions accepted with decreasing probability.
> 3. As $T \to 0$, $P \to 0$ for worsening moves → algorithm degenerates into pure hill-climbing.
> 4. As $T \to \infty$, $P \to 1$ for all moves → algorithm becomes a random walk.

## 2.5 Component 5 — Cooling Schedule $T(t)$

The cooling schedule governs how the temperature parameter $T$ decreases over iterations. It includes:

**(a) Initial Temperature $T_0$** — high enough that almost all moves are accepted.
**(b) Cooling Function $T(t+1) = g(T(t))$** — the rate law.
**(c) Final Temperature $T_f$** (or stopping criterion).

### Standard Cooling Schedules

| Schedule Name | Formula | Parameter Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric (Multiplicative)** | $T(t+1) = \alpha \cdot T(t)$ | $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$ |
| **Linear Cooling** | $T(t+1) = T(t) - \beta$ | $\beta$ constant step |
| **Logarithmic Cooling** | $T(t) = \dfrac{T_0}{\log(t+1)}$ | Theoretically converges to optimum |
| **Exponential Cooling** | $T(t) = T_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Equivalent to geometric for some $\alpha$ |
| **Cauchy / Fast Annealing** | $T(t) = \dfrac{T_0}{t+1}$ | Used in Fast SA algorithm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **geometric schedule** is the most widely used in practice and is the **default expectation in KTU exam questions**.

## 2.6 Component 6 — Markov Chain Length $L_k$

At each temperature $T_k$, the algorithm performs $L_k$ iterations of neighbor generation and acceptance before reducing $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a chain at each temperature?**
> The Metropolis algorithm produces a **homogeneous Markov chain** in the steady state — the probability of visiting a state $s$ is given by the **Boltzmann distribution** at that $T$. Running $L_k \to \infty$ iterations ensures equilibrium. A common practical choice: $L_k = n$ (problem dimension) or $L_k = 10n$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below contains every formula you are likely to encounter in a KTU 2024 Scheme exam question on Simulated Annealing components.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acceptance probability (worsening) | $P = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta f}{T}\right)$ | Core Metropolis criterion |
| 2 | Acceptance probability (improving) | $P = 1$ | Always accept |
| 3 | Cost difference | $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s)$ | $> 0$ when $s'$ is worse |
| 4 | Geometric cooling | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | $0.8 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99$ |
| 5 | Boltzmann distribution | $P(s) \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{f(s)}{T}\right)$ | Equilibrium distribution |
| 6 | Markov chain length | $L_k = n$ (or $10n$) | Iterations per temperature |
| 7 | Stopping criterion | $T_k \leq T_f$ or no improvement | Termination |
| 8 | Initial temperature bound | $T_0 \geq \dfrac{\Delta f_{\max}}{\ln(P_{\text{initial}})}$ | To accept with prob $P_{\text{initial}}$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> SA is widely used in:
> - **VLSI circuit design** (placement and routing of millions of gates).
> - **Airline crew scheduling & rostering** (NP-hard combinatorial problems).
> - **Image processing** (image restoration, segmentation).
> - **Telecommunications** (optimal antenna placement, network design).
> - **Machine learning hyperparameter tuning** and **neural network training**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acceptance Probability from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

In statistical mechanics, the probability that a physical system is in a state with energy $E$ at temperature $T$ is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$P(E) = \frac{1}{Z(T)} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{k_B T}\right)$$

Here $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant and $Z(T)$ is the partition function:

$$Z(T) = \sum_{i} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step-by-step translation to SA:**

Step 1: Identify the analogue of energy $E$ with the cost function $f(s)$:

$$E \;\longleftrightarrow\; f(s)$$

Step 2: Drop the constant $k_B$ (it is absorbed into a scaled temperature $T$):

$$P(s) = \frac{1}{Z(T)} \exp\!\left(-\frac{f(s)}{T}\right)$$

Step 3: Compute the ratio of probabilities of two neighboring states $s'$ and $s$ at temperature $T$:

$$\frac{P(s')}{P(s)} = \frac{\exp(-f(s')/T)}{\exp(-f(s)/T)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{f(s') - f(s)}{T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right)$$

Step 4: Apply the **Metropolis acceptance rule** — accept a candidate state with probability $\min\!\left(1, \frac{P(s')}{P(s)}\right)$:

$$P_{\text{accept}}(s' \mid s, T) = \min\!\left\{ 1,\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right) \right\}$$

This is precisely the formula we use in the algorithm. As $T \to \infty$, the ratio $\to 1$ (random walk); as $T \to 0$, the ratio $\to 0$ for $\Delta f > 0$ (pure descent).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selecting Initial Temperature $T_0$

A common design question: *"What value of $T_0$ ensures that any worsening move of magnitude $\Delta f_{\max}$ is accepted with at least probability $P_0$?"*

**Step 1:** Set the acceptance probability constraint:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0}\right) \geq P_0$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides (log is monotone increasing, inequality preserved when both sides are in $(0,1)$ after ensuring $P_0 < 1$):

$$-\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0} \geq \ln P_0$$

**Step 3:** Since $\Delta f_{\max} > 0$ and $P_0 < 1$ implies $\ln P_0 < 0$, multiply both sides by $-1$ (flips inequality):

$$\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0} \leq -\ln P_0 = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_0}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Invert to isolate $T_0$ (since $T_0 > 0$ and RHS is positive):

$$T_0 \geq \frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{\ln(1/P_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** Suppose $\Delta f_{\max} = 100$ and we want $P_0 = 0.80$:

$$T_0 \geq \frac{100}{\ln(1/0.80)} = \frac{100}{\ln(1.25)} = \frac{100}{0.2231} \approx 448.1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the formula $T_0 \geq \Delta f_{\max} / \ln(1/P_0)$ as a direct 3-mark question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature after $k$ Cooling Steps (Geometric Schedule)

Given $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$ and $T_0$ as the initial temperature, we can derive the closed-form:

Step 1 — Expand iteratively:

$$T_1 = \alpha T_0$$
$$T_2 = \alpha T_1 = \alpha^2 T_0$$
$$T_3 = \alpha^3 T_0$$

Step 2 — Inductively, the general term is:

$$T_k = \alpha^{k} \cdot T_0$$

Step 3 — Number of iterations to reach a target $T_f$:

$$\alpha^{k} T_0 = T_f \;\Longrightarrow\; k = \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln \alpha}$$

Since $\ln \alpha < 0$ and $\ln(T_f/T_0) < 0$ (as $T_f < T_0$), $k > 0$ as expected.

Step 4 — Numerical example: $T_0 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.90$, $T_f = 0.1$:

$$k = \frac{\ln(0.1 / 100)}{\ln(0.90)} = \frac{\ln(0.001)}{\ln(0.90)} = \frac{-6.9078}{-0.10536} \approx 65.6 \;\Rightarrow\; 66 \text{ iterations}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Interactions

> [!TIP]
> The six components do **not** act in isolation. Below is the **interaction matrix** showing how each component influences the others — a high-yield KTU answer element.

| Component ↓ affects → | Convergence Speed | Solution Quality | Exploration Ability | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State Space $S$** | Indirect (via $N(s)$) | High (encoding choice) | Defines reachable regions | High if large |
| **Objective $f(s)$** | Low | Critical | None | Evaluation cost |
| **Neighborhood $N(s)$** | High | High | **Direct control** | Scales with $\vert N(s) \vert$ |
| **Metropolis $P$** | Moderate | Critical | **Direct control** | Negligible |
| **Cooling $T(t)$** | **Highest impact** | **Highest impact** | Decays over time | Indirect |
| **Chain Length $L_k$** | Moderate | High | Moderate | Direct cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **cooling schedule** is the **single most influential** design choice. A cooling that is too fast causes premature convergence (local optimum); too slow wastes computation. This is the classic **exploration-vs-exploitation trade-off**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture of Simulated Annealing

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the SA algorithm and the interaction of all six main components. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initA[Initialize: T0, Lk, alpha, current solution s]
    initA --> evalA[Compute f_s = f current]
    evalA --> loopA{T_k greater than T_f}

    loopA -- NO --> stopA([OUTPUT best solution s_star])
    loopA -- YES --> innerA[Inner Loop: k = 1 to L_k]

    innerA --> genA[Generate neighbor s_prime in N current]
    genA --> costA[Compute f_prime = f s_prime]
    costA --> deltaA[Delta f = f_prime - f_s]

    deltaA --> branchA{Delta f less than or equal 0}
    branchA -- YES --> acceptA[Accept s_prime as new current]
    branchA -- NO --> metropA[Random r in 0 1]
    metropA --> condA{r less than exp -Delta f / T}
    condA -- YES --> acceptA
    condA -- NO --> rejectA[Reject s_prime, keep current]

    acceptA --> bestA{f_prime less than f_best}
    bestA -- YES --> updA[Update s_star and f_best]
    bestA -- NO --> keepA[Keep existing s_star]
    rejectA --> innerA

    updA --> innerA
    keepA --> innerA
    innerA --> coolA[Cooling Step: T = alpha times T]
    coolA --> loopA
    stopA --> endA([END])
```

## 4.2 Component Dependency Flow (Mermaid)

The diagram below isolates the **six main components** and shows how information flows between them at runtime.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Component 1: State Space S] --> N[Component 3: Neighborhood N of s]
    fObj[Component 2: Objective f of s] --> deltaE[Delta f = f s_prime minus f s]
    N --> sPrime[New Candidate s_prime]
    sPrime --> deltaE
    Tsched[Component 5: Cooling Schedule T of t] --> metropolis[Component 4: Acceptance P = exp -Delta f over T]
    deltaE --> metropolis
    metropolis --> decision{Accept or Reject}
    Lk[Component 6: Chain Length L_k] --> loopControl[Controls inner iterations per T]
    decision --> nextIter[Update s and check best]
    loopControl --> nextIter
    nextIter --> Tsched
```

## 4.3 Temperature Decay Curve (Geometric Schedule — Schematic)

Below is a **Mermaid line chart** depicting the geometric cooling trajectory $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$ for $\alpha = 0.90$, $T_0 = 100$ across 30 iterations. This visualizes the *annealing* behavior.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Geometric Cooling Schedule T_k = 100 * 0.90^k"
    x-axis "Iteration k" [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
    y-axis "Temperature T_k" 0 --> 100
    line [100, 59, 35, 21, 12, 7, 4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> - The curve **decays exponentially** — fast early loss, slow asymptotic approach to 0.
> - The **early high-T phase** = wide exploration (accepting bad moves).
> - The **late low-T phase** = fine exploitation (only improving moves).

## 4.4 Acceptance Probability vs. Δf Curve

The following conceptual schematic shows the **Metropolis criterion** at three different temperatures $T_1 > T_2 > T_3$.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Acceptance Probability vs Cost Difference Delta f"
    x-axis "Delta f" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    y-axis "P accept" 0 --> 1
    line [1.00, 0.90, 0.82, 0.74, 0.67, 0.61]
    line [1.00, 0.67, 0.45, 0.30, 0.20, 0.14]
    line [1.00, 0.37, 0.14, 0.05, 0.02, 0.01]
```

The three lines correspond (top to bottom) to **High T**, **Medium T**, **Low T**. As T drops, the probability of accepting worsening moves shrinks rapidly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain the **main components of Simulated Annealing**. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The six main components of Simulated Annealing are:

1. **State (Solution) Space $S$** — the set of all feasible candidate solutions to the optimization problem.
2. **Objective Function $f(s)$** — assigns a numerical cost (energy) to every state; the algorithm aims to minimize $f(s)$.
3. **Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$** — defines the set of candidate solutions reachable from $s$ via a single move operator.
4. **Acceptance Probability Function** — given by the Metropolis criterion $P = \exp(-\Delta f / T)$, governing when worse solutions are accepted.
5. **Cooling Schedule $T(t)$** — controls the gradual reduction of temperature; geometric cooling $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ is most common.
6. **Markov Chain Length $L_k$** — number of iterations executed at each temperature $T_k$ to attain equilibrium.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the **Metropolis acceptance criterion** used in Simulated Annealing and explain the role of temperature $T$. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Metropolis acceptance criterion is:

$$P(\text{accept } s') = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } f(s') \leq f(s) \\ \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{f(s') - f(s)}{T}\right), & \text{if } f(s') > f(s) \end{cases}$$

**Role of Temperature $T$:**

- **High $T$ (early phase):** $P \to 1$ even for large worsening moves → **wide exploration** of the search space.
- **Low $T$ (late phase):** $P \to 0$ for worsening moves → **fine exploitation** of promising regions.
- Temperature is the **single control parameter** that smoothly interpolates the algorithm from a **random walk** ($T \to \infty$) to a **greedy hill-climber** ($T \to 0$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete architecture of the Simulated Annealing algorithm**, identifying the **six main components** and showing how they interact at each iteration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A combinatorial problem has maximum possible cost difference $\Delta f_{\max} = 200$ between consecutive neighbor states. You want the initial temperature $T_0$ to be high enough that any worsening move is accepted with probability at least $P_0 = 0.90$. **(i)** Derive the formula for $T_0$. **(ii)** Compute the required $T_0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The architecture of SA consists of an **outer loop** (cooling schedule) and an **inner loop** (Metropolis sampling at fixed $T$).

**Block-by-block description:**

1. **Initialization block:** Set $T \leftarrow T_0$, $L_k$, $\alpha$, generate initial $s$, compute $f(s)$, set $s^* \leftarrow s$, $f^* \leftarrow f(s)$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Outer loop (cooling):** While $T > T_f$, execute inner loop; then apply $T \leftarrow \alpha T$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Inner loop (Markov chain):** Repeat $L_k$ times: generate $s' \in N(s)$, compute $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s)$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Acceptance block (Metropolis):**
   - If $\Delta f \leq 0$: set $s \leftarrow s'$.
   - Else: draw $r \sim U(0,1)$; if $r < \exp(-\Delta f / T)$: set $s \leftarrow s'$. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Best-so-far update:** If $f(s) < f^*$: $s^* \leftarrow s$, $f^* \leftarrow f(s)$. *(1 mark)*
6. **Termination & output:** Return $s^*$ when $T \leq T_f$ or max iterations reached. *(1 mark)*
7. **Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.) *(0.5 marks)*

The **six components** (state space, objective, neighborhood, acceptance, cooling schedule, chain length) are integrated as: state space + neighborhood generate candidates; objective computes $\Delta f$; cooling schedule + acceptance probability decide acceptance; chain length controls inner iterations.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Derivation of $T_0$ formula** *(3.5 marks)*:

Step 1: Acceptance probability condition:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0}\right) \geq P_0$$

Step 2: Take natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0} \geq \ln P_0$$

Step 3: Multiply by $-1$ (inequality flips):

$$\frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{T_0} \leq -\ln P_0 = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_0}\right)$$

Step 4: Rearrange for $T_0$ (since $T_0 > 0$):

$$\boxed{\,T_0 \geq \frac{\Delta f_{\max}}{\ln(1/P_0)}\,}$$

**(ii) Numerical computation** *(3.5 marks)*:

Given $\Delta f_{\max} = 200$ and $P_0 = 0.90$:

$$T_0 \geq \frac{200}{\ln(1/0.90)} = \frac{200}{\ln(1.1111\ldots)} = \frac{200}{0.10536} \approx 1898.4$$

$$\boxed{\,T_0 \approx 1898.4 \text{ (temperature units)}\,}$$

**Verification:** At $T_0 = 1898.4$, for a worsening move of $\Delta f = 200$:

$$P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{200}{1898.4}\right) = \exp(-0.10536) \approx 0.90 \;\checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of the **cooling schedule** in Simulated Annealing. Compare **geometric, linear, and logarithmic** cooling schedules with formulas and characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An SA algorithm uses geometric cooling with $T_0 = 500$, $\alpha = 0.85$, and a target final temperature $T_f = 0.5$. **(i)** Derive the closed-form expression for $T_k$. **(ii)** Compute the number of iterations $k$ needed to reach $T_f$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of Cooling Schedule** *(2 marks)*:

The cooling schedule $T(t)$ controls the **exploration–exploitation trade-off**. It governs:
- The **probability of escaping local optima** in early phases.
- The **convergence to a high-quality solution** in later phases.
- The **total computational budget** of the algorithm.

**Comparison Table** *(5 marks)*:

| Schedule | Formula | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Most widely used; fast decay; risk of premature convergence if $\alpha$ is too small. |
| **Linear** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Constant decrement; simple to implement; may overshoot $T_f$ if $\beta$ mischosen. |
| **Logarithmic** | $T_k = T_0 / \log(k+1)$ | Theoretically guaranteed to converge to global optimum; **very slow** in practice. |

> [!NOTE]
> The logarithmic schedule has a **theoretical guarantee** of asymptotic convergence to the global optimum, but it is impractical for large problems due to the very slow cooling rate.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Closed-form derivation** *(3 marks)*:

Step 1: Recursion $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $T_0 = 500$.

Step 2: Expand iteratively:

$$T_1 = \alpha T_0$$
$$T_2 = \alpha T_1 = \alpha^2 T_0$$
$$T_3 = \alpha^3 T_0$$

Step 3: General term by induction:

$$\boxed{\,T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k} = 500 \cdot (0.85)^{k}\,}$$

**(ii) Number of iterations to reach $T_f = 0.5$** *(4 marks)*:

Step 1: Set $T_k = T_f$:

$$500 \cdot (0.85)^{k} = 0.5$$

Step 2: Divide both sides:

$$(0.85)^{k} = \frac{0.5}{500} = 0.001$$

Step 3: Take natural logarithm:

$$k \cdot \ln(0.85) = \ln(0.001)$$

Step 4: Solve for $k$ (note $\ln(0.85) < 0$, so $k$ is positive):

$$k = \frac{\ln(0.001)}{\ln(0.85)} = \frac{-6.9078}{-0.16252} \approx 42.5$$

Step 5: Round up to the next integer (cannot perform fractional iteration):

$$\boxed{\,k = 43 \text{ iterations}\,}$$

**Verification:** $T_{43} = 500 \cdot (0.85)^{43} = 500 \cdot 0.000944 \approx 0.472$, which is below $0.5$ ✓ (and $T_{42} = 500 \cdot (0.85)^{42} \approx 0.555 > 0.5$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $P = 1$ case:** When writing the Metropolis criterion, students often give only the exponential form and forget the **conditional first line** ("accept improving moves with probability 1"). This loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in the temperature formula:** In $T_0 \geq \Delta f_{\max} / \ln(1/P_0)$, students sometimes write $\ln(P_0)$ in the denominator — a sign error. Remember: $\ln(1/P_0) = -\ln(P_0)$, and both are positive when $0 < P_0 < 1$.
> 3. **Confusing $T$ with iteration counter $k$:** Cooling schedule is $T_k$ or $T(t)$, not $k$. Clearly distinguish.
> 4. **Omitting the Boltzmann/Metropolis link:** Examiners award marks for connecting SA to the **statistical mechanics origin** (Boltzmann distribution, 1983 paper by Kirkpatrick et al.).
> 5. **Forgetting $\Delta f > 0$ condition:** The exponential formula applies **only** to worsening moves.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Simulated Annealing Main Components**
>
> ✅ SA is a **probabilistic metaheuristic** inspired by the metallurgical **annealing** process.
>
> ✅ **Six main components:** (1) State Space, (2) Objective Function, (3) Neighborhood Structure, (4) Acceptance Probability, (5) Cooling Schedule, (6) Markov Chain Length.
>
> ✅ **Metropolis criterion:** $P = \exp(-\Delta f / T)$ for worsening moves; $P = 1$ for improving moves.
>
> ✅ **Geometric cooling** is the **default in KTU questions**: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$.
>
> ✅ **Closed-form geometric cooling:** $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$.
>
> ✅ **Initial temperature formula:** $T_0 \geq \Delta f_{\max} / \ln(1/P_0)$.
>
> ✅ **Iterations to reach $T_f$:** $k = \ln(T_f/T_0) / \ln(\alpha)$.
>
> ✅ **High T → exploration** (accept bad moves); **Low T → exploitation** (accept only improvements).
>
> ✅ As $T \to \infty$: algorithm behaves as a **random walk**; as $T \to 0$: it becomes **greedy hill-climbing**.
>
> ✅ The cooling schedule is the **most influential** design parameter — controls the entire exploration/exploitation trade-off.
>
> ✅ SA does **not guarantee** the global optimum in finite time — it gives an **asymptotic guarantee** with logarithmic cooling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homogenous vs. In-Homogenous Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Homogenous vs. In-Homogenous Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Simulated Annealing (SA)?

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the physical process of annealing in metallurgy, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to allow its atoms to settle into a low-energy crystalline structure (a global minimum). Introduced by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, and Vecchi (1983)** and independently by **Černý (1985)**, SA uses a controlled stochastic search to escape local optima by occasionally accepting worse solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term)**
> Simulated Annealing is a **probabilistic local search metaheuristic** that iteratively moves from one solution to a neighbouring solution in the search space, accepting improving moves deterministically and non-improving (uphill) moves with a probability governed by a *temperature parameter* $T$, which is gradually reduced over iterations to converge toward the global optimum.

---

## 1.2 The Two Paradigms: Homogeneous vs. In-Homogeneous

The classification of SA algorithms hinges on the **nature of the underlying Markov chain** used to explore the solution space, which directly dictates how the temperature parameter evolves.

### 1.2.1 Homogeneous Simulated Annealing (HSA)

In **Homogeneous SA**, the algorithm operates on a sequence of *homogeneous* Markov chains. The temperature is held **constant** for a fixed number of iterations (an *epoch*) until the chain reaches a stationary/equilibrium distribution. Only after equilibrium is attained is the temperature reduced to the next level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Homogeneous SA (HSA) – Core Property**
> Each *temperature level* corresponds to an *independent homogeneous Markov chain*. The state transition matrix at level $T_k$ is $\mathbf{Q}_{T_k}$. The cooling schedule is applied **only between** these equilibrated phases, never within them.

### 1.2.2 In-Homogeneous Simulated Annealing (ISA)

In **In-Homogeneous SA** (also called *inhomogeneous* or *non-stationary* SA), the algorithm uses a **single, non-homogeneous Markov chain** where the temperature is allowed to change *at every step* (or every iteration) according to a pre-defined cooling law. The chain never formally reaches equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Homogeneous SA (ISA) – Core Property**
> A *single inhomogeneous Markov chain* is run for the entire duration, with a temperature $T(k)$ that varies (typically decreases, but may include reheating phases) at *every iteration* $k$. The state transition probabilities change at every step.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Mountain-Climber in a Fog Analogy** 🏔️
>
> Imagine a **climber** trapped in a dense fog on a mountainous landscape, trying to reach the *deepest valley* (global optimum) but can only see a few metres around them. The fog represents the algorithm's limited local information.
>
> - **Homogeneous SA** is like a climber who, at each elevation band, *walks around for a long time* until they have thoroughly mapped that altitude (equilibrium), *then* the temperature drops, lowering the fog floor. They systematically explore one level completely before descending.
> - **In-Homogeneous SA** is like a climber who is *continuously descending* with the fog floor dropping constantly under their feet — sometimes the fog momentarily lifts (reheating) to allow a brief escape from a shallow valley, but they never linger long at any one level.
>
> The **temperature $T$** controls the *probability of stepping uphill* (accepting a worse solution): high $T$ = willing to climb big hills (exploration); low $T$ = only accepts downhill steps (exploitation).

---

## 1.4 The Metropolis Acceptance Criterion

The cornerstone of *both* variants is the **Metropolis criterion** (Metropolis et al., 1953):

$$P(\text{accept } s' \mid s, T) \;=\; \min\!\left\{1,\ \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B \cdot T}\right)\right\}$$

where:
- $s$ is the current solution, $s'$ is the proposed neighbour
- $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$ is the cost (energy) change
- $T$ is the current temperature (analogue of physical temperature)
- $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** (often absorbed into $T$ for simplicity in algorithmic SA)

For minimisation problems, a *worsening* move ($\Delta E > 0$) is accepted with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$, which decreases as $T \to 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusion to Avoid**
> In the algorithmic literature, the physical constant $k_B$ is **routinely absorbed** into the temperature $T$, so the exponent is written simply as $-\Delta E / T$. KTU examiners accept either notation, but be consistent within a problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Flow of Simulated Annealing (Common Backbone)

Both HSA and ISA share the same algorithmic skeleton:

1. **Initialise** a starting solution $s_0$ and an initial temperature $T_0$.
2. **Generate** a neighbouring solution $s' \in \mathcal{N}(s)$.
3. **Evaluate** $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$.
4. **Accept** $s'$ with probability $\min(1, e^{-\Delta E / T})$.
5. **Update** the temperature according to the chosen *cooling schedule*.
6. **Repeat** Steps 2–5 until the stopping criterion is met.

The *difference* lies **entirely in Step 5**.

---

## 2.2 Homogeneous Simulated Annealing — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Outer Loop (Temperature Levels)
A decreasing sequence of temperatures is defined: $T_0 > T_1 > T_2 > \dots > T_K$.

### 2.2.2 Inner Loop (Equilibration at Level $T_k$)
At each fixed $T_k$, the Markov chain $\mathbf{Q}_{T_k}$ is run for a *sufficiently long* number of iterations $L_k$ (the *epoch length*) so that the chain's distribution converges to the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$\pi_k(s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(-f(s)\,/\,T_k\right)}{\displaystyle\sum_{s^{*} \in \mathcal{S}} \exp\!\left(-f(s^{*})\,/\,T_k\right)}$$

### 2.2.3 Convergence Theorem (Geman & Geman, 1984)
For HSA, if the cooling schedule satisfies:

$$T_k \;\geq\; \frac{C}{\log(k + 2)}$$

for some constant $C$ (related to the maximum depth of any local-minimum "basin"), then the algorithm converges **in probability** to the global optimum.

---

## 2.3 In-Homogeneous Simulated Annealing — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.3.1 Single Non-Stationary Chain
A single Markov chain is run where the transition matrix $\mathbf{Q}_{T(k)}$ changes at every step $k$. There is no equilibrium at any level.

### 2.3.2 Cooling Law Examples

**Geometric (Most Common in Practice):**

$$T(k) \;=\; T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

**Logarithmic (Theoretical Optimality):**

$$T(k) \;=\; \frac{T_0}{\log(k + 1)}$$

**Linear:**

$$T(k) \;=\; T_0 - \beta \cdot k$$

### 2.3.3 Reheating (Re-annealing)
ISA implementations often permit **occasional reheating** — a temporary increase in $T$ — to escape deep local optima. This violates the monotonicity of standard HSA schedules.

### 2.3.4 Convergence Theorem (Hajek, 1988)
For ISA, convergence in probability to the global optimum is guaranteed when:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{c^{*}}{T(k)}\right) \;=\; \infty$$

where $c^{*}$ is the *minimum energy barrier height* of the problem. This condition is satisfied by the **logarithmic schedule** $T(k) = c^{*} / \log(k+1)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use / Context** |
|------:|:----------------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Metropolis acceptance probability | $P = \min\{1,\ \exp(-\Delta E / T)\}$ | Decides whether to accept a worse neighbour |
| 2 | Boltzmann equilibrium distribution | $\pi(s) = \dfrac{\exp(-f(s)/T)}{Z}$ | Target distribution at fixed $T$ in HSA |
| 3 | Geometric cooling (ISA) | $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$ | Most common practical schedule |
| 4 | Logarithmic cooling (ISA) | $T(k) = T_0 / \log(k + 1)$ | Theoretically optimal (slow) |
| 5 | Linear cooling (ISA) | $T(k) = T_0 - \beta k$ | Simple, rarely optimal |
| 6 | Geman–Geman HSA condition | $T_k \geq C / \log(k+2)$ | Guarantees HSA convergence |
| 7 | Hajek's ISA condition | $\sum \exp(-c^{*} / T(k)) = \infty$ | Guarantees ISA convergence |
| 8 | Cooling ratio (typical) | $\alpha \in [0.8,\ 0.99]$ | Common in geometric schedules |
| 9 | Epoch length (HSA inner loop) | $L_k = n \cdot \vert \mathcal{N}(s) \vert$ | $n$ is a multiplier; $n \geq 1$ |
| 10 | Reheat trigger (ISA) | $T \leftarrow T_{\text{boost}}$ if stuck | Resets temperature upward |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> Simulated Annealing (both HSA & ISA) is widely applied in **VLSI circuit layout design**, **job-shop scheduling**, **neural network weight training (Boltzmann machines)**, **traveling salesman problem (TSP)**, **image segmentation in medical imaging**, and **aircraft wing-design optimization**. The HSA/ISA distinction matters in practice because HSA offers theoretical rigor at the cost of speed, while ISA is faster and more flexible in engineering deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Criterion

The criterion arises from **detailed balance** in statistical mechanics.

**Step 1 — Detailed Balance Requirement.**
For a Markov chain at thermal equilibrium with the Boltzmann distribution, the flow of probability between any two states $s$ and $s'$ must balance:

$$P(s \to s') \cdot \pi(s) \;=\; P(s' \to s) \cdot \pi(s')$$

Here $P(s \to s')$ is the *generation-and-acceptance* probability and $\pi$ is the Boltzmann distribution.

**Step 2 — Insert the Boltzmann Distribution.**

$$\pi(s) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{f(s)}{T}\right), \quad \pi(s') = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{f(s')}{T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Ratio of the Two Balance Equations.**

$$\frac{P(s \to s')}{P(s' \to s)} \;=\; \frac{\pi(s')}{\pi(s)} \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$$

where $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$.

**Step 4 — Choose the Symmetric Generation Rule.**
Let the *generation* probability of proposing $s'$ from $s$ be symmetric: $G(s \to s') = G(s' \to s)$ (e.g., random neighbour selection). Then $P(s \to s') = G(s \to s') \cdot A(s \to s')$, where $A$ is the acceptance probability.

**Step 5 — Solve for the Acceptance Probability.**
Maximising acceptance while respecting the ratio above yields the **Metropolis choice**:

$$A(s \to s') \;=\; \min\!\left\{1,\ \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)\right\}$$

> **Reasoning:** If $\Delta E \le 0$, then $e^{-\Delta E / T} \ge 1$, so we set $A = 1$ (accept all improving moves). If $\Delta E > 0$, the ratio is less than 1, and we accept with that exact probability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Geometric Cooling Schedule Equivalence

Two algebraic forms of geometric cooling are commonly seen in textbooks and exam papers. We now show they are equivalent.

**Form A:** $T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k)$, with $T(0) = T_0$.

**Step 1 — Iterate Recursively.**

$$T(1) = \alpha T(0), \quad T(2) = \alpha T(1) = \alpha^2 T(0)$$

**Step 2 — Generalise by Induction.**

$$T(k) = \alpha^{k} T_0$$

**Form B:** $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$ — this is the closed form of Form A, proved by induction. ∎

**Common Mistake Avoidance:** Some students write $T(k) = T_0 \cdot (1-\alpha)^{k}$ by confusing the cooling ratio with the cooling *rate*. Always verify your base case: $T(0) = T_0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — HSA vs ISA Acceptance Comparison

Consider a minimisation problem with current cost $f(s) = 100$. A neighbour $s'$ has cost $f(s') = 105$, so $\Delta E = +5$.

| **Iteration $k$** | **HSA Temperature $T_k$** | **ISA Temperature $T(k)$** | **Accept Probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$** |
|:-----------------:|:--------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:------------------------------------------:|
| 0 | 100 (level 0) | 100 | $e^{-5/100} = e^{-0.05} \approx 0.951$ |
| 1 | 80 (level 1) | 80 | $e^{-5/80} \approx 0.939$ |
| 2 | 60 (level 2) | 60 | $e^{-5/60} \approx 0.920$ |
| 5 | 25 (level 5) | 25 | $e^{-5/25} = e^{-0.20} \approx 0.819$ |
| 10 | 10 (level 10) | 10 | $e^{-5/10} = e^{-0.50} \approx 0.607$ |
| 50 | 0.1 (level 50) | 0.1 | $e^{-5/0.1} = e^{-50} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-22}$ |
| 100 | $10^{-6}$ (level 100) | $10^{-6}$ | $e^{-5/10^{-6}} = e^{-5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0$ |

**Interpretation:**
- At **high $T$** (early iterations), bad moves are accepted ~95 % of the time — strong exploration.
- At **low $T$** (late iterations), bad moves are effectively never accepted — pure exploitation.
- The **two columns are identical** here because both schedules follow the same geometric law, *but* HSA holds the temperature fixed for $L_k$ iterations at each level (giving a *plateau*), while ISA continuously decreases.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (HSA vs ISA)

| **Dimension** | **Homogeneous SA (HSA)** | **In-Homogeneous SA (ISA)** |
|:--------------|:------------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| Markov chain structure | Sequence of *homogeneous* chains (one per $T_k$) | One *non-homogeneous* chain (varying $T(k)$) |
| Temperature update | Discrete, between equilibrium phases | Continuous, at each step |
| Epoch requirement | Must equilibrate at each $T_k$ | No equilibrium required |
| Theoretical convergence | Geman–Geman: $T_k = C/\log(k+2)$ | Hajek: $\sum e^{-c^{*}/T(k)} = \infty$ |
| Practical speed | Slower (more iterations) | Faster |
| Reheating support | Not native | Easy to add |
| Implementation complexity | Higher (epoch tuning) | Lower (single loop) |
| Solution quality | Provably optimal (slow) | Near-optimal (fast) |
| Memory / state | Needs Boltzmann partition $Z$ conceptually | $Z$ not required in practice |
| Use in KTU problems | Theory-heavy questions | Practice-heavy questions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — General Simulated Annealing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start SA]) --> nodeInit["Initialise solution s, temperature T, cooling alpha"]
    nodeInit --> nodeLoopCheck{Stopping criterion met?}
    nodeLoopCheck -->|No| nodeGenNeighbour["Generate neighbour s' from s"]
    nodeGenNeighbour --> nodeDeltaE["Compute Delta E = f s' minus f s"]
    nodeDeltaE --> nodeAcceptCheck{Delta E less than or equal 0?}
    nodeAcceptCheck -->|Yes| nodeAcceptGood["Accept s' as new current"]
    nodeAcceptCheck -->|No| nodeMetropolis["Accept with probability exp minus Delta E over T"]
    nodeMetropolis --> nodeAcceptProb{Random U less than P}
    nodeAcceptProb -->|Yes| nodeAcceptBad["Accept s' worse move uphill"]
    nodeAcceptProb -->|No| nodeReject["Reject s' keep current s"]
    nodeAcceptGood --> nodeUpdateT
    nodeAcceptBad --> nodeUpdateT
    nodeReject --> nodeUpdateT
    nodeUpdateT["Update T using cooling schedule"] --> nodeLoopCheck
    nodeLoopCheck -->|Yes| nodeStop([Return best solution found])
    style nodeStart fill:#90EE90
    style nodeStop fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeMetropolis fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeUpdateT fill:#ADD8E6
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The decision node `nodeUpdateT` is where **HSA and ISA diverge** — in HSA, $T$ updates only after $L_k$ iterations (epoch), whereas in ISA, $T$ updates *every step*.

---

## 4.2 Side-by-Side Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subgraphHSA["HSA Homogeneous Simulated Annealing"]
        hsaDir1["Level T sub 0 constant"] --> hsaDir2["Equilibrate chain Q sub T0"]
        hsaDir2 --> hsaDir3["Lower T to T sub 1"]
        hsaDir3 --> hsaDir4["Equilibrate chain Q sub T1"]
        hsaDir4 --> hsaDir5["Lower T to T sub 2"]
        hsaDir5 --> hsaDir6["Equilibrate chain Q sub T2"]
        hsaDir6 --> hsaDir7["Continue until T sub K"]
    end
    subgraph subgraphISA["ISA In Homogeneous Simulated Annealing"]
        isaDir1["Step k T k varies"] --> isaDir2["Single chain Q sub Tk"]
        isaDir2 --> isaDir3["Step k plus 1 T k plus 1"]
        isaDir3 --> isaDir4["Same chain with new T"]
        isaDir4 --> isaDir5["Continue until stopping criterion"]
    end
    style subgraphHSA fill:#E0FFE0
    style subgraphISA fill:#FFE0E0
```

---

## 4.3 Cooling Schedule Visualisation (Geometric)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT0["T sub 0 high temperature broad exploration"] --> nodeCool1["Apply alpha times T"]
    nodeCool1 --> nodeT1["T sub 1 reduced"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeCool2["Apply alpha times T again"]
    nodeCool2 --> nodeT2["T sub 2 further reduced"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeCoolN["Continue geometric decay"]
    nodeCoolN --> nodeTend["T sub K near zero pure exploitation"]
    style nodeT0 fill:#FF6B6B
    style nodeT1 fill:#FFA500
    style nodeT2 fill:#FFD700
    style nodeTend fill:#90EE90
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This block-level architecture represents the temperature flow under a geometric schedule $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$. The colour gradient (red → green) represents the transition from *exploration* (high $T$) to *exploitation* (low $T$).

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing Between HSA and ISA in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot{Problem size and time budget}
    nodeRoot -->|Small problem and need proof| nodeHSA["Use HSA with Geman Geman schedule"]
    nodeRoot -->|Large problem and need speed| nodeISA["Use ISA with geometric schedule"]
    nodeRoot -->|Multimodal and stuck often| nodeReheat["Use ISA with reheating"]
    nodeRoot -->|Real time and continuous data| nodeAdaptive["Use adaptive ISA T adjusted on the fly"]
    style nodeRoot fill:#FFFACD
    style nodeHSA fill:#E0FFE0
    style nodeISA fill:#FFE0E0
    style nodeReheat fill:#E0E0FF
    style nodeAdaptive fill:#FFE0FF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Homogeneous and In-Homogeneous Simulated Annealing in terms of Markov chain structure and temperature update policy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Homogeneous SA** | **In-Homogeneous SA** |
|:-----------|:-------------------|:----------------------|
| Markov chain | Sequence of *homogeneous* chains, one per temperature level | Single *non-homogeneous* chain |
| Temperature update | Decreased *between* equilibrated phases | Decreased *at every step* (continuous) |
| Equilibrium | Reached at each $T_k$ | Never formally reached |

*(1 mark each point; 3 marks for a clear, tabular contrast.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **State the Metropolis acceptance criterion and explain the role of the temperature parameter $T$ in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Metropolis acceptance probability is:

$$P(\text{accept } s') = \min\!\left\{1,\ \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)\right\}$$

- **$\Delta E \le 0$:** improving move, accepted with probability 1. *(1 mark)*
- **$\Delta E > 0$:** worsening move, accepted with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Role of $T$:** Controls the *exploration vs exploitation* trade-off. High $T$ → more uphill moves accepted (exploration); low $T$ → only downhill moves accepted (exploitation). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Derive the Metropolis acceptance probability from the detailed balance condition. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast Homogeneous and In-Homogeneous Simulated Annealing under the following heads: (i) Markov chain model, (ii) cooling schedule, (iii) convergence guarantee, (iv) typical application scenarios. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Detailed balance** (1 mark): For equilibrium,
$$P(s \to s')\,\pi(s) = P(s' \to s)\,\pi(s')$$
2. **Boltzmann distribution** (1 mark): $\pi(s) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp(-f(s)/T)$
3. **Ratio of equations** (1 mark): $\frac{P(s \to s')}{P(s' \to s)} = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$
4. **Symmetric generation** (1 mark): $G(s \to s') = G(s' \to s)$ implies $P = G \cdot A$
5. **Solving for $A$** (2 marks): To maximise acceptance while satisfying the ratio, choose
$$A(s \to s') = \min\{1,\ \exp(-\Delta E / T)\}$$
6. **Interpretation** (1 mark): Improving moves ($\Delta E \le 0$) always accepted; worsening moves accepted with decaying probability.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

| **Head** | **HSA** | **ISA** |
|:---------|:--------|:--------|
| (i) Markov chain model (2 marks) | Sequence of homogeneous chains $\mathbf{Q}_{T_k}$ | Single non-homogeneous chain $\mathbf{Q}_{T(k)}$ |
| (ii) Cooling schedule (2 marks) | Discrete $T_k$ sequence; constant within epoch | Continuous $T(k)$ updated at every step |
| (iii) Convergence guarantee (2 marks) | Geman–Geman: $T_k \ge C/\log(k+2)$ | Hajek: $\sum e^{-c^*/T(k)} = \infty$ |
| (iv) Typical applications (1 mark) | Theoretical benchmarks, small problems | Real-world large-scale engineering optimization |

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** Explain the Geman–Geman convergence theorem for Homogeneous Simulated Annealing. State the condition and its significance. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A minimisation problem has current solution cost $f(s) = 250$. A neighbour $s'$ has cost $f(s') = 260$. Using a geometric cooling schedule with $T_0 = 100$ and $\alpha = 0.8$, calculate the acceptance probability of $s'$ at iterations $k = 0, 1, 2, 5, 10$. Tabulate your results and comment on the trend. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Theorem statement** (2 marks): If the temperature sequence satisfies
$$T_k \;\ge\; \frac{C}{\log(k+2)},\quad \forall k \ge 0$$
then HSA converges in probability to a global minimum.
2. **Constant $C$** (2 marks): $C$ is the *maximum depth* of any local minimum basin in the cost landscape, measured as the largest uphill barrier that must be crossed to escape any local optimum.
3. **Logarithmic schedule significance** (2 marks): This is the *slowest* known schedule guaranteeing global convergence — it ensures the Markov chain has enough time at high temperatures to escape local optima and enough time at low temperatures to settle in the global basin.
4. **Practical implication** (1 mark): Pure logarithmic cooling is too slow for real applications, so geometric cooling ($\alpha^k$) is used in practice with a justified loss of theoretical guarantee.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $\Delta E = 260 - 250 = +10$, $T_0 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.8$, $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$.

| **$k$** | **$T(k) = 100 \cdot (0.8)^k$** | **Acceptance $P = e^{-10/T(k)}$** |
|:-------:|:------------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| 0 | 100.00 | $e^{-0.100} \approx 0.9048$ |
| 1 | 80.00 | $e^{-0.125} \approx 0.8825$ |
| 2 | 64.00 | $e^{-0.1563} \approx 0.8553$ |
| 5 | 32.77 | $e^{-0.3052} \approx 0.7370$ |
| 10 | 10.74 | $e^{-0.9311} \approx 0.3937$ |

**Calculation details for $k=0$:** $T(0) = 100 \cdot 0.8^0 = 100$. $\Delta E/T = 10/100 = 0.1$. $P = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$. *(1.5 marks)*

**Calculation details for $k=1$:** $T(1) = 100 \cdot 0.8 = 80$. $\Delta E/T = 10/80 = 0.125$. $P = e^{-0.125} \approx 0.8825$. *(1.5 marks)*

**Calculation details for $k=2$:** $T(2) = 100 \cdot 0.64 = 64$. $\Delta E/T = 10/64 \approx 0.1563$. $P = e^{-0.1563} \approx 0.8553$. *(1 mark)*

**Calculation details for $k=5$:** $T(5) = 100 \cdot 0.3277 = 32.77$. $\Delta E/T = 10/32.77 \approx 0.3052$. $P = e^{-0.3052} \approx 0.7370$. *(1 mark)*

**Calculation details for $k=10$:** $T(10) = 100 \cdot 0.1074 = 10.74$. $\Delta E/T = 10/10.74 \approx 0.9311$. $P = e^{-0.9311} \approx 0.3937$. *(1 mark)*

**Comment on trend (1 mark):** The acceptance probability *decreases monotonically* as $k$ increases. Early in the search, the algorithm is willing to accept a worse solution ~90 % of the time (strong exploration); by iteration 10, this drops to ~39 %, indicating a shift toward exploitation. This demonstrates the *annealing* behaviour: the algorithm gradually "cools" and becomes more selective.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_k$ with $T(k)$:** HSA uses subscripted $T_k$ (discrete levels); ISA uses $T(k)$ (continuous). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for mixing notations.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\min\{1, \cdot\}$ wrapper:** The acceptance probability is *always* capped at 1. Writing $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ without the $\min$ is technically wrong (and loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Wrong direction of $\Delta E$:** For **minimisation**, $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$; for **maximisation**, $\Delta E = f(s) - f(s')$. Mixing this up reverses the logic.
> 4. **Omitting the Boltzmann constant discussion:** When deriving the Metropolis criterion, students often skip the *detailed balance* step and go straight to the formula. KTU examiners want the full derivation chain.
> 5. **Mis-stating convergence conditions:** Geman–Geman applies to **HSA only**; Hajek's condition applies to **ISA only**. Confusing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Geometric vs. exponential confusion:** $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$ is *exponential decay* in $k$, but called "geometric cooling" — a misnomer. Use the term *geometric* on the exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
>
> - **SA core idea:** Simulate physical annealing; accept worse moves probabilistically to escape local optima. *(Must state in every answer.)*
> - **Metropolis criterion:** $P = \min\{1, e^{-\Delta E / T}\}$ — improving moves always accepted, worsening moves with decaying probability. *(Most-tested formula.)*
> - **HSA = sequence of equilibrated homogeneous chains** at fixed temperatures $T_0, T_1, \dots, T_K$.
> - **ISA = single non-homogeneous chain** with temperature varying at every step $k$ as $T(k)$.
> - **Geometric cooling:** $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$, with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$. The workhorse of practical ISA.
> - **Logarithmic cooling:** $T(k) = T_0 / \log(k+1)$. Theoretically optimal for ISA, but too slow.
> - **Geman–German theorem (HSA):** $T_k \ge C / \log(k+2)$ guarantees convergence.
> - **Hajek's theorem (ISA):** $\sum \exp(-c^*/T(k)) = \infty$ guarantees convergence.
> - **Boltzmann constant $k_B$** is typically absorbed into $T$ in algorithmic SA; not a separate variable to track.
> - **Reheating** is an ISA-only feature used to escape deep local optima.
> - **Differentiate cleanly:** HSA = discrete & equilibration-based; ISA = continuous & non-stationary.
> - **Key analogy:** climber in fog — high $T$ = thick fog (willing to step uphill), low $T$ = clear sky (only downhill).
> - **Engineering applications:** VLSI layout, TSP, job-shop scheduling, neural-network training, image segmentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annealing Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Annealing Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Simulated Annealing (SA)** — a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic inspired by the physical annealing process of metals — an **Annealing Schedule** (also called the **Cooling Schedule** or **Cooling Scheme**) is the **predefined, mathematically rigorous time-temperature protocol** that governs how the control parameter called **Temperature (T)** is progressively reduced from a high initial value **T₀** toward a final value **T_f ≈ 0** (or a small positive epsilon) across the iterations of the algorithm.

The annealing schedule completely specifies four interrelated parameters:
1. **Initial Temperature (T₀)**
2. **Final Temperature (T_f)** or stopping criterion
3. **Temperature Decrement Function (Cooling Law)**
4. **Number of Iterations at Each Temperature (Equilibrium Length, L_k)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The annealing schedule is the *engine* of the SA algorithm. Without a properly tuned schedule, SA either **converges prematurely to a poor local optimum** (if cooling is too aggressive) or **wastes enormous computational budget** (if cooling is too slow). It is the single most critical implementation parameter of the entire heuristic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Boiling Water Cooling into a Perfect Crystal"

Imagine you are a blacksmith forging a sword. After heating the metal until it glows bright red (**T₀ is very high**), you do not dunk it instantly into ice water. Instead, you let it cool **slowly and controllably** in oil, allowing the internal atoms (the *candidate solutions*) to **rearrange themselves gradually** into a tightly packed, defect-free crystalline lattice (**the global optimum**).

- **Fast cooling (Quenching)** → atoms get "frozen" in a brittle, disorganized state → **Local Optimum**
- **Controlled slow cooling (Annealing)** → atoms find their lowest-energy arrangement → **Global Optimum**

The mathematical recipe that dictates *how fast* you cool the metal is the **Annealing Schedule**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of the temperature T as a "willingness to accept bad moves." At high T, the algorithm is brave and accepts uphill moves (worse solutions) easily, allowing it to **explore** the search space. As T decreases, it becomes conservative and accepts only downhill (better) moves, allowing it to **exploit** the best region found. The cooling schedule choreographs this transition from **exploration → exploitation**.

## 1.3 Connection to Thermodynamics — Boltzmann Distribution

Theoretical convergence of SA rests on the **Boltzmann Probability Distribution** from statistical mechanics:

$$P(\Delta E) \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_{B}\,T}\right)$$

where **k_B** is the **Boltzmann constant (≈ 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K)**. In the SA analogy, this constant is typically absorbed into T, and the *acceptance probability* of an uphill move of cost-difference ΔE becomes the **Metropolis Criterion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The annealing schedule determines the *thermodynamic quasi-equilibrium* at each temperature level. The schedule must be slow enough that the Markov chain of solutions *approximately* samples the Boltzmann distribution at each T, before the temperature is decremented further.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of an Annealing Schedule

### Pillar 1 — Initial Temperature (T₀)
- T₀ must be **high enough** so that **e⁻ᵏᵇᵀ ≈ 1** for typical uphill moves ΔE.
- A common engineering heuristic: choose T₀ such that the **initial acceptance probability of the worst expected move is ≥ χ**, where **χ ∈ [0.7, 0.95]**.
- Formula used:
$$T_{0} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{\ln(\chi)}$$
where **ΔE_max** is the maximum observed (or estimated) cost-difference.

### Pillar 2 — Final Temperature (T_f)
- T_f is typically a small value (e.g., **10⁻⁶** to **10⁻²**) or chosen when a **stopping criterion** is met:
  - No improvement in last **N** iterations
  - Total iteration budget exhausted
  - Target cost value achieved

### Pillar 3 — Temperature Decrement (Cooling Law)
The function that maps iteration *k* to a new temperature *T(k+1)*.

### Pillar 4 — Equilibrium Length (Iterations per Temperature) L_k
Number of accepted/rejected trials at temperature *T_k* before decrementing. Common choices:
- **L_k = N** (problem size)
- **L_k = c·n** where c ∈ [10, 100]
- **L_k = N²** for high-precision problems

## 2.2 Standard Cooling Schedules — Detailed Mathematical Breakdown

### Schedule A — Geometric (Exponential) Cooling [Most Widely Used]
$$T_{k+1} \;=\; \alpha \cdot T_{k}, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

- **Typical α range: 0.80 ≤ α ≤ 0.99**
- The KTU-favorite because of its simplicity and empirical effectiveness.
- Decays exponentially fast.

### Schedule B — Linear Cooling
$$T_{k+1} \;=\; T_{k} \;-\; \Delta T, \qquad \Delta T > 0$$

- Constant decrement. Practical only for small problems; tends to cool too fast late.

### Schedule C — Logarithmic Cooling (Theoretical Gold Standard)
$$T_{k} \;=\; \frac{T_{0}}{\ln(k + k_{0})}, \qquad k_{0} > 1$$

- **Theoretically guarantees convergence to global optimum** (Geman & Geman, 1984) — but is **computationally impractically slow**.

### Schedule D — Cauchy Cooling (Fast Annealing)
$$T_{k} \;=\; \frac{T_{0}}{k}$$

- Used in **Fast Simulated Annealing (FSA)** by Szu & Hartley.

### Schedule E — Adaptive Cooling
$$T_{k+1} \;=\; T_{k} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\ln(1+\delta)\,\cdot T_{k}}{3\,\sigma_{k}}\right)^{-1}$$

where **σ_k** is the standard deviation of cost values seen at temperature k.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity / Symbol** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Meaning** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Metropolis Acceptance Probability | $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ | Probability of accepting a worse solution | 0 → 1 |
| 2 | Initial Temperature | $T_0 = -\Delta E_{\max} / \ln(\chi)$ | Starting exploration temperature | 100 → 10⁵ |
| 3 | Geometric Cooling | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | Multiplicative decay | $\alpha \in [0.80,\,0.99]$ |
| 4 | Linear Cooling | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \Delta T$ | Subtractive decay | $\Delta T \ll T_0$ |
| 5 | Logarithmic Cooling | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(k+k_0)$ | Guaranteed global convergence | $k_0 > 1$ |
| 6 | Cauchy / Fast Cooling | $T_k = T_0 / k$ | 1/k decay (FSA) | k ≥ 1 |
| 7 | Equilibrium Length | $L_k \propto N$ or $c \cdot n$ | Iterations per temperature | 10N → 100N |
| 8 | Acceptance Ratio at T | $\chi(T_k)$ | Fraction of accepted moves | 0.4 → 0.7 |
| 9 | Cooling Rate (effective) | $c_k = \ln(\alpha)$ | Logarithmic decay constant | Negative |
| 10 | Number of Coolings (Geometric) | $K = \lceil \ln(T_f/T_0) / \ln(\alpha) \rceil$ | Total decrement cycles | Integer ≥ 1 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Partitioning & Placement** — SA with geometric cooling places millions of gates optimally (Sechen's TimberWolf).
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — SA produces near-optimal tour lengths for n > 10,000 cities.
- **Image Processing** — Pixel-level restoration using SA annealing schedule.
- **Neural Network Training** — Boltzmann machines use logarithmic schedules.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling in Manufacturing** — Cooling schedule balances makespan vs. tardiness.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Initial Temperature from Desired Acceptance Probability

**Given:** We want the algorithm to accept uphill moves of cost-difference **ΔE_max** with at least probability **χ** at temperature T₀.

**Step 1 — Write the Metropolis acceptance probability:**

$$P(\Delta E_{\max},\,T_0) \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Set this probability equal to the desired acceptance level χ:**

$$\chi \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(\chi) \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{T_0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for T₀:**

$$T_0 \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{\ln(\chi)}$$

> This is the **standard KTU formula** for selecting the initial temperature. Note: since χ ∈ (0,1), ln(χ) < 0, ensuring T₀ > 0. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form Expression for Geometric Cooling After K Iterations

**Given:** Recursive relation $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ with initial condition $T_0$.

**Step 1 — Write the first three iterations explicitly:**

$$T_1 \;=\; \alpha\,T_0$$
$$T_2 \;=\; \alpha\,T_1 \;=\; \alpha^2\,T_0$$
$$T_3 \;=\; \alpha\,T_2 \;=\; \alpha^3\,T_0$$

**Step 2 — Observe the pattern (Mathematical Induction hypothesis):**

$$T_k \;=\; \alpha^{k}\,T_0$$

**Step 3 — Verify by induction for k+1:**

$$T_{k+1} \;=\; \alpha \cdot T_k \;=\; \alpha \cdot \alpha^{k}\,T_0 \;=\; \alpha^{k+1}\,T_0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Solve for k when T_k reaches the final temperature T_f:**

$$T_f \;=\; \alpha^{K}\,T_0$$

**Step 5 — Take ln of both sides:**

$$\ln(T_f) \;=\; K\,\ln(\alpha) \;+\; \ln(T_0)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for K:**

$$K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)} \right\rceil$$

> This is the **total number of cooling cycles** required to reach T_f. Since ln(α) < 0 for α ∈ (0,1), and T_f < T_0, the numerator is negative and the denominator is negative → K is positive. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Cooling Rate and Acceptance Ratio

**Given:** Average cost-difference ΔE_avg and the Metropolis criterion.

**Step 1 — Define average acceptance ratio:**

$$\chi(T) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Assume small ΔE, use Taylor expansion:**

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right) \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\Delta E}{T} \;+\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{\Delta E^2}{T^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Taking expectation:**

$$\chi(T) \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\mathbb{E}[\Delta E]}{T} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\Delta E_{\text{avg}}}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Set acceptance ratio to target χ_target and solve for T:**

$$T_{\text{adapt}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta E_{\text{avg}}}{1 \;-\; \chi_{\text{target}}}$$

> This is the basis of the **Huang–Shu adaptive annealing schedule**, where the temperature is updated to maintain a constant acceptance ratio across iterations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cooling Schedules

| **Property** | **Geometric** | **Linear** | **Logarithmic** | **Cauchy (Fast)** | **Adaptive** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Convergence Proof | Heuristic | Heuristic | Provable (slow) | Heuristic | Heuristic |
| Cooling Speed | Fast | Very Fast | Extremely Slow | Moderate | Variable |
| Final Quality | Very Good | Fair | Optimal (in limit) | Good | Excellent |
