| Implementation Difficulty | Easy | Easiest | Easy | Easy | Hard |
| Parameter Sensitivity | Moderate | High | Low | Low | Low |
| KTU Exam Likelihood | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Typical α / Parameter | α = 0.95 | ΔT = 0.1·T₀ | k₀ = 2 to 10 | — | σ_k tracking |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Solve a small TSP-style optimization using SA with geometric cooling.

**Given:**
- $T_0 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.85$, $T_f = 0.5$
- Worst observed cost-difference $\Delta E_{\max} = 50$

**Step 1 — Compute number of cooling cycles K:**

$$K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(0.5 / 100)}{\ln(0.85)} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$\ln(0.005) \;\approx\; -5.298$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$\ln(0.85) \;\approx\; -0.1625$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$K \;=\; \lceil -5.298 / -0.1625 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 32.6 \rceil \;=\; 33$$

**Step 5 — Verify via direct recursion:**

$$T_{33} \;=\; 100 \times (0.85)^{33} \;\approx\; 100 \times 0.00497 \;\approx\; 0.497 < 0.5 \quad\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute temperature at k = 10:**

$$T_{10} \;=\; 100 \times (0.85)^{10} \;\approx\; 100 \times 0.19687 \;\approx\; 19.69$$

**Step 7 — Acceptance probability for ΔE = 20 at T₁₀:**

$$P \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{20}{19.69}\right) \;\approx\; \exp(-1.0157) \;\approx\; 0.362$$

So at iteration 10, the algorithm will accept ~36.2% of uphill moves of magnitude 20.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Simulated Annealing with Annealing Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["START"] --> initB["Generate Initial Solution S0"]
    initB --> initC["Set T = T0 high"]
    initC --> iterLoop{"Outer Loop: k = 0 to K"}
    iterLoop -->|Yes| innerLoop{"Inner Loop: i = 1 to L_k"}
    innerLoop -->|Yes| genMove["Generate Neighbour S_new from S_current"]
    genMove --> computeDelta["Compute Delta E = Cost S_new - Cost S_current"]
    computeDelta --> checkDelta{"Delta E less than 0"}
    checkDelta -->|Yes| acceptMove["Accept S_new  S_current = S_new"]
    checkDelta -->|No| metropolis["Generate r Uniform 0 1"]
    metropolis --> checkProb{"exp -Delta E over T greater than r"}
    checkProb -->|Yes| acceptMove
    checkProb -->|No| rejectMove["Reject S_new keep S_current"]
    acceptMove --> innerLoop
    rejectMove --> innerLoop
    innerLoop -->|No| coolStep["Apply Cooling Schedule T k+1 equals alpha times T k"]
    coolStep --> updateBest["Update S_best if improved"]
    updateBest --> iterLoop
    iterLoop -->|No| stopCheck{"T less than T_f OR Budget exhausted"}
    stopCheck -->|Yes| outputBest["Output S_best Global Optimum"]
    stopCheck -->|No| iterLoop
    outputBest --> finishA["END"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Annealing Schedule Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SA_CORE["Simulated Annealing Engine"]
        direction TB
        A1["Initial Solution Generator"] --> A2["Neighbourhood Operator"]
        A2 --> A3["Cost Function Evaluator"]
        A3 --> A4["Metropolis Decision Module"]
        A4 --> A5["Solution Updater"]
    end

    subgraph ANNEAL_SCHED["Annealing Schedule Subsystem"]
        direction TB
        B1["T0 Selector Module"] --> B2["Cooling Law Engine"]
        B2 --> B3["L_k Equilibrium Controller"]
        B3 --> B4["Stopping Criterion Checker"]
        B4 --> B5["Parameter Logger"]
    end

    A1 -. reads initial T from .-> B1
    A4 -. reads current T from .-> B2
    A5 -. signals iteration count to .-> B3
    B4 -. sends STOP signal to .-> A1

    SA_CORE <==> ANNEAL_SCHED

    B2 --> C1["Geometric Law T k equals alpha times T k minus 1"]
    B2 --> C2["Linear Law T k equals T0 minus k times delta T"]
    B2 --> C3["Logarithmic Law T k equals T0 over ln k plus k0"]
    B2 --> C4["Adaptive Law chi target feedback"]

    C1 --> B3
    C2 --> B3
    C3 --> B3
    C4 --> B3
```

## 4.3 Temperature Decay Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH TEMPERATURE PHASE — EXPLORATION"]
        H1["T = T0"]
        H2["Acceptance Probability near 1.0"]
        H3["Algorithm explores broadly"]
    end

    subgraph MID["MIDDLE TEMPERATURE PHASE — TRANSITION"]
        M1["T intermediate"]
        M2["Acceptance Probability 0.3 to 0.6"]
        M3["Balances exploration and exploitation"]
    end

    subgraph LOW["LOW TEMPERATURE PHASE — EXPLOITATION"]
        L1["T near T_f"]
        L2["Acceptance Probability near 0"]
        L3["Algorithm refines near best found"]
    end

    HIGH --> MID --> LOW
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define annealing schedule in simulated annealing. List any four parameters that completely specify an annealing schedule.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An annealing schedule is the mathematically defined **cooling protocol** that controls how the temperature parameter T of the simulated annealing algorithm is reduced over iterations from an initial value T₀ to a final value T_f.

The four parameters that completely specify an annealing schedule are:

1. **Initial Temperature (T₀)** — controls the starting exploration breadth.
2. **Final Temperature (T_f)** or stopping criterion.
3. **Temperature Decrement Function / Cooling Law** — e.g., geometric $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$.
4. **Equilibrium Length (L_k)** — number of inner-loop iterations executed at each temperature level before cooling.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the four parameters.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of the cooling rate α in geometric annealing with one practical implication.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In geometric annealing, the cooling rate is governed by the multiplier **α ∈ (0,1)** such that $T_{k+1} = \alpha\,T_k$.

- If **α is close to 1 (e.g., 0.99)** → cooling is *very slow*, requiring many iterations but yielding higher solution quality.
- If **α is close to 0.8** → cooling is *fast*, finishing quickly but at the risk of converging to a poor local optimum.

**Practical Implication:** Choosing α = 0.95 is a common engineering compromise in VLSI placement algorithms — it balances runtime with near-optimal wire-length.

*(Allocation: 1 mark formula + 1 mark interpretation + 1 mark practical implication.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3]

**(a)** Derive the formula for choosing the initial temperature T₀ of a simulated annealing algorithm to ensure a target acceptance probability χ for uphill moves of magnitude ΔE_max. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simulated annealing algorithm is run with geometric cooling $T_{k+1} = 0.9\,T_k$, $T_0 = 500$, and $T_f = 0.1$. Compute:
   - (i) The total number of cooling cycles K required. **(3 Marks)**
   - (ii) The acceptance probability for an uphill move of ΔE = 50 at iteration k = 5. **(4 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3]

**(a)** Compare geometric, linear, and logarithmic cooling schedules across the dimensions of convergence guarantee, computational speed, and practical usability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a job-shop scheduling problem with the following data: $T_0 = 200$, $\alpha = 0.92$, $\Delta E_{\max} = 80$, and desired acceptance χ = 0.85, determine:
   - (i) Whether T₀ = 200 is correctly chosen; if not, compute the correct T₀. **(4 Marks)**
   - (ii) The temperature after 10 cooling cycles using geometric cooling. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of T₀ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 —** The Metropolis criterion for accepting an uphill move of cost-difference ΔE at temperature T is:

$$P(\Delta E, T) \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\,\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$$

**Step 2 —** Impose the requirement that the probability of accepting the *worst* uphill move (magnitude ΔE_max) at the initial temperature T₀ equals the target acceptance level χ:

$$\chi \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{T_{0}}\right)$$

**Step 3 —** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(\chi) \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{T_{0}}$$

**Step 4 —** Solve algebraically for T₀:

$$T_{0} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{\ln(\chi)}$$

**Step 5 —** Verification: Since χ ∈ (0,1), ln(χ) < 0, hence T₀ > 0. ✓

**Engineering Interpretation:** Choosing χ = 0.90 implies accepting 90% of worst uphill moves at the start — this ensures broad exploration.

*(Mark split: 1 mark step 1, 1 mark step 2, 1 mark step 3, 1 mark step 4, 1 mark step 5, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark final boxed equation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Number of cooling cycles K (3 Marks):**

$$K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)} \right\rceil$$

$$K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(0.1 / 500)}{\ln(0.9)} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(0.0002)}{\ln(0.9)} \right\rceil$$

$$K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{-8.517}{-0.10536} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 80.83 \rceil \;=\; 81$$

**Therefore, K = 81 cooling cycles.** (3 marks)

---

**(ii) Acceptance probability at k = 5 (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 —** Compute T₅ using geometric cooling:

$$T_{5} \;=\; T_{0} \cdot \alpha^{5} \;=\; 500 \cdot (0.9)^{5}$$

**Step 2 —** Evaluate (0.9)⁵:

$$(0.9)^5 \;=\; 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 \;=\; 0.59049$$

**Step 3 —** Compute T₅:

$$T_{5} \;=\; 500 \times 0.59049 \;=\; 295.245$$

**Step 4 —** Apply Metropolis criterion with ΔE = 50:

$$P \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{50}{295.245}\right) \;=\; \exp(-0.1693) \;\approx\; 0.8442$$

**Therefore, P ≈ 0.8442 or 84.42%.** (4 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Geometric** | **Linear** | **Logarithmic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \Delta T$ | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(k+k_0)$ |
| Convergence Guarantee | Heuristic only | Heuristic only | Provably converges to global optimum (Geman & Geman, 1984) |
| Computational Speed | Fast | Very Fast | Extremely Slow |
| Practical Usability | ★★★★★ Most widely used | ★★ Rarely used | ★ (Theoretical only) |
| Parameter Sensitivity | Moderate (depends on α) | High (ΔT critical) | Low |
| Typical Use | VLSI, TSP, Scheduling | Toy problems | Theoretical analysis |

*(Mark split: 1 mark for each cell populated meaningfully + 1 mark for overall conclusion that geometric is the practical winner.)*

**Conclusion:** Geometric cooling dominates real-world implementations due to its balance of speed and quality. Logarithmic cooling is the *theoretical benchmark* but is computationally prohibitive.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Verifying T₀ (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 —** Apply the T₀ formula:

$$T_{0,\text{correct}} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta E_{\max}}{\ln(\chi)} \;=\; -\,\frac{80}{\ln(0.85)}$$

**Step 2 —** Evaluate ln(0.85):

$$\ln(0.85) \;\approx\; -0.16252$$

**Step 3 —** Compute:

$$T_{0,\text{correct}} \;=\; -\,\frac{80}{-0.16252} \;\approx\; 492.27$$

**Step 4 —** Compare with given T₀ = 200:

Since 200 < 492.27, **the given T₀ is too low** — it would not provide the desired 85% acceptance of worst uphill moves.

**Therefore, the correct T₀ ≈ 492.27.** (4 marks)

---

**(ii) Temperature after 10 cycles (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 —** Apply geometric formula:

$$T_{10} \;=\; T_{0,\text{correct}} \cdot \alpha^{10} \;=\; 492.27 \cdot (0.92)^{10}$$

**Step 2 —** Evaluate (0.92)¹⁰:

$$(0.92)^{10} \;\approx\; 0.4344$$

**Step 3 —** Compute:

$$T_{10} \;\approx\; 492.27 \times 0.4344 \;\approx\; 213.81$$

**Therefore, T₁₀ ≈ 213.81.** (3 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $T_0 = -\Delta E_{\max} / \ln(\chi)$ — students often write $T_0 = \Delta E_{\max} / \ln(\chi)$, producing a *negative* temperature. **[-2 marks]**
> 2. **Mixing up L_k (equilibrium length)** with N (problem size) — they are conceptually distinct. L_k is a schedule parameter; N is the input size.
> 3. **Stating that "linear cooling is faster than geometric"** without qualifying — *linear is faster in early iterations but geometric is faster in late iterations logarithmically*.
> 4. **Omitting units** when stating T values — KTU evaluators reward T in **arbitrary "cost units"** if the formula derivation is correct.
> 5. **Not using ceiling brackets ⌈ ⌉** for K — the number of cooling cycles must be a positive integer; if you compute 80.83, you must write 81, not 80.83.
> 6. **Confusing Cauchy cooling with logarithmic cooling** — they look similar ($T_0/k$ vs $T_0/\ln k$) but Cauchy is from Szu & Hartley (1987) and is much faster.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Annealing Schedule = the cooling protocol** of Simulated Annealing that decides how T drops from T₀ → T_f over time.
- **Four pillars of any schedule:** T₀, T_f, cooling law, L_k (equilibrium length).
- **Metropolis acceptance criterion** for uphill moves: $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$.
- **Initial temperature formula:** $T_0 = -\Delta E_{\max} / \ln(\chi)$ — used to achieve acceptance probability χ of worst uphill moves.
- **Geometric cooling (most popular):** $T_{k+1} = \alpha\,T_k$, with $\alpha \in [0.80, 0.99]$.
- **Linear cooling:** $T_{k+1} = T_k - \Delta T$ — simpler but inferior in practice.
- **Logarithmic cooling:** $T_k = T_0 / \ln(k + k_0)$ — only schedule with provable global convergence, but impractically slow.
- **Cauchy cooling (Fast SA):** $T_k = T_0 / k$ — used in Fast Simulated Annealing.
- **Adaptive cooling:** temperature is dynamically adjusted to maintain a target acceptance ratio χ_target — modern engineering standard.
- **Total cycles for geometric schedule:** $K = \lceil \ln(T_f / T_0) / \ln(\alpha) \rceil$.
- **KTU focus areas:** derivation of T₀, numerical computation with geometric cooling, comparison tables, identification of cooling schedules from descriptive questions.
- **Exploration → Exploitation metaphor:** high T = exploration, low T = exploitation. Cooling schedule choreographs this transition.
- **Golden Rule for exams:** Always verify T₀ > 0 by checking the sign of ln(χ); always use ceiling for integer cycle counts; always quote units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Sequencing and Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications in Sequencing and Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sequencing** is the process of determining the optimal **order (sequence)** in which a set of jobs must be processed on a limited number of machines/machines to optimize a measurable performance criterion (such as makespan, total flow time, idle time, or tardiness).

**Scheduling** is the broader process of assigning **specific start and finish times** to each job on each machine (i.e., time-allocation), based on a sequence, to meet the optimization objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence = ORDER of jobs.** **Schedule = ORDER + TIMING of jobs.** A schedule inherently depends on a valid sequence. Heuristics are first applied to **find the sequence**, and then the schedule (with times) is derived.

Mathematically, a sequencing problem of $n$ jobs on $m$ machines can be formulated as a permutation of $n$ jobs:
$$
\pi = (j_1, j_2, \ldots, j_n), \quad \pi \in S_n
$$
where $S_n$ denotes the set of all $n!$ possible permutations. Since enumerating all $n!$ permutations becomes computationally intractable (NP-hard) for moderate $n$, **heuristics** are employed to find a *near-optimal* solution in polynomial time.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Laundry-Coffee-Toast Problem (Illustrative Real-World Case)**
>
> Imagine you wake up and have **three tasks**: brew **Coffee** (takes 1 min), make **Toast** (takes 3 min), and do **Laundry** (takes 30 min). You have only **one washing machine (one operator)** for laundry, but can simultaneously brew coffee while toast is in the toaster and laundry is running. **What order minimizes total breakfast-finish time?**
>
> The naive answer is "do laundry first, then coffee, then toast" — taking 34 min. A smarter sequence (Laundry + Coffee in parallel, then Toast) takes only **31 min**. That smart ordering is **sequencing**, and the timetable (Laundry starts at 0, ends at 30; Coffee at 0–1; Toast at 30–33) is the **schedule**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Sequencing & Scheduling

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Makespan** | $C_{\max}$ | Total time to complete **all** jobs (completion time of the last job) |
| **Flow Time** | $F_i$ | Time a job spends in the system: $F_i = C_i - r_i$ |
| **Idle Time** | $I$ | Total machine downtime during the schedule |
| **Tardiness** | $T_i$ | $\max(0, C_i - d_i)$ — lateness beyond due date |
| **Lateness** | $L_i$ | $C_i - d_i$ (may be negative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations under Module 3, the **dominant objective is Makespan minimization** ($C_{\max}$) for the **$n$-job, $m$-machine permutation flow-shop problem**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Sequencing Problems

Sequencing problems in KTU's heuristics module are classified as follows:

1. **Single Machine, $n$ Jobs** — Trivial; sequence doesn't affect makespan.
2. **Two Machines, $n$ Jobs** — Solvable exactly by **Johnson's Algorithm** (1954).
3. **$m$ Machines, $n$ Jobs (Flow Shop)** — NP-hard; solved via heuristics:
   - **CDS (Campbell-Dudek-Smith) Heuristic**
   - **Palmer's Slope Index Heuristic**
   - **NEH (Nawaz-Enscore-Ham) Heuristic**
   - **Gupta's Heuristic**
4. **$m$ Machines, $n$ Jobs (Job Shop)** — More general; routing differs per job.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Shop** = every job visits machines in the **same order** (1 → 2 → … → $m$). **Job Shop** = each job has its own unique routing.

### 2.2 Johnson's Rule (2 Machines, $n$ Jobs) — The Foundation

**Johnson's Algorithm** optimally solves the 2-machine, $n$-job problem in **$O(n \log n)$** time.

**Steps:**
1. List all $n$ jobs with processing times $(A_j, B_j)$ on Machine-1 and Machine-2.
2. Find the **minimum** of all $A_j$ and $B_j$.
3. If the minimum occurs on **Machine-1** → place the job in the **earliest available slot** (left).
4. If the minimum occurs on **Machine-2** → place the job in the **latest available slot** (right).
5. Remove the placed job; repeat until all jobs are sequenced.
6. **Tie-breaking:** If the minimum is equal on both machines, **arbitrarily** place on either side.

### 2.3 CDS Heuristic (Extension of Johnson to $m$ Machines)

CDS generates $(m-1)$ different 2-machine problems by **summing successive pairs** of machine times, then applies Johnson's rule to each, finally selecting the **best makespan**.

For $m$ machines, define $k$ dummy machine times:
$$
A_j^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}, \qquad B_j^{(k)} = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}, \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, (m-1)
$$

Apply Johnson to each $(A^{(k)}, B^{(k)})$ pair → obtain $(m-1)$ sequences → pick the one with **minimum $C_{\max}$**.

### 2.4 Palmer's Slope Index Heuristic

Computes a **slope index** for each job:
$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (2i - m - 1) \cdot p_{ij}
$$
Then sequence jobs in **descending order** of $S_j$. This tends to put jobs with steeply increasing processing times early.

### 2.5 NEH Heuristic (Most Powerful for $m$-machine Flow Shop)

**NEH Algorithm** is widely regarded as the best-performing simple heuristic for permutation flow-shops:

1. Compute **total processing time** for each job: $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}$.
2. Sort jobs in **descending order** of $T_j$.
3. Take the first two jobs; arrange them in the order giving **minimum $C_{\max}$** (try both $2!=2$ permutations).
4. For each remaining job (3rd, 4th, …), insert it in **all possible positions** among the already-placed jobs; retain the position yielding **minimum $C_{\max}$**.
5. Repeat until all $n$ jobs are sequenced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NEH runs in $O(n^2 m)$ time and consistently outperforms CDS and Palmer in empirical studies.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Makespan** | $C_{\max}$ | Completion time of the last job on last machine |
| **Idle time on Machine $k$** | $I_k = C_{\max} - \sum_j p_{kj}$ | Used for verification |
| **Johnson's min-search** | $\min(\{A_j\} \cup \{B_j\})$ | Step 2 of Johnson's |
| **CDS dummy times** | $A_j^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$ | $k=1,\dots,m-1$ |
| **CDS dummy times** | $B_j^{(k)} = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | $k=1,\dots,m-1$ |
| **Palmer slope index** | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (2i - m - 1) p_{ij}$ | Sequence in descending order |
| **NEH total time** | $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | Initial descending sort |
| **Tardiness** | $T_i = \max(0,\ C_i - d_i)$ | $d_i$ = due date |
| **Total flow time** | $\sum F_i = \sum C_i - \sum r_i$ | $r_i$ = release time |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Johnson's Algorithm (2 Machines, 5 Jobs)

**Problem Data:**

| Job $j$ | $A_j$ (Machine 1) | $B_j$ (Machine 2) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 5 |
| 2 | 4 | 6 |
| 3 | 9 | 2 |
| 4 | 8 | 7 |
| 5 | 5 | 3 |

**Step 1 — Find the global minimum across all $A_j$ and $B_j$:**
$$
\min(A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5, B_1, B_2, B_3, B_4, B_5) = \min(7, 4, 9, 8, 5, 5, 6, 2, 7, 3)
$$

The minimum value is **$2$**, occurring at $B_3$. Per Rule 3, place Job 3 in the **rightmost (last)** slot.

**Step 2 — Remove Job 3. Remaining minimum:**
$$
\min(A_1, A_2, A_4, A_5, B_1, B_2, B_4, B_5) = \min(7, 4, 8, 5, 5, 6, 7, 3) = 3
$$
This is $B_5 = 3$ → place Job 5 in the next rightmost slot.

**Step 3 — Remove Job 5. Remaining minimum:**
$$
\min(A_1, A_2, A_4, B_1, B_2, B_4) = \min(7, 4, 8, 5, 6, 7) = 4
$$
This is $A_2 = 4$ → place Job 2 in the **leftmost (first)** slot.

**Step 4 — Remove Job 2. Remaining minimum:**
$$
\min(A_1, A_4, B_1, B_2, B_4) = \min(7, 8, 5, 6, 7) = 5
$$
Tie between $A_1 = 5$ and $B_1 = 5$ → **arbitrarily** choose to process on Machine 1 first. Place Job 1 in the next leftmost slot.

**Step 5 — Only Job 4 remains.** Place it in the middle remaining slot.

**Final Johnson's Optimal Sequence:** $\pi = (2, 1, 4, 5, 3)$

**Compute the Schedule (Gantt Chart construction):**

| Job | M1 Start | M1 End ($A_j$) | M2 Start ($\max(\text{M1 end}, \text{prev M2 end})$) | M2 End ($C_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 10 |
| 1 | 4 | 11 | 11 | 16 |
| 4 | 11 | 19 | 19 | 26 |
| 5 | 19 | 24 | 26 | 29 |
| 3 | 24 | 33 | 33 | 35 |

**Makespan $C_{\max} = 35$.**

**Verification using the closed-form formula for 2-machine Johnson's makespan:**
$$
C_{\max} = \max_j\left(\sum_{k=1}^{j} A_k\right) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} B_j
$$
provided Machine 1 finishes its last job before Machine 2 completes.

Cumulative $A$: $(4,\ 11,\ 19,\ 24,\ 33)$ — max is $33$.
Cumulative $B$: $6+5+7+3+2 = 23$.
Total: $33 + 2 = 35$. ✓ (Note: last $B$ is added separately; we recompute: $\max(4,11,19,24,33)+\min(A,B\text{-overlap})$ = 35, consistent with Gantt).

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CDS Heuristic (3 Machines, 4 Jobs)

**Problem Data:**

| Job $j$ | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 6 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 8 | 5 |
| 3 | 7 | 2 | 9 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

**Step 1 — Build dummy 2-machine problems. For $m=3$, we have $k=1$ and $k=2$:**

For $k=1$:
$$
A_j^{(1)} = p_{1j}, \qquad B_j^{(1)} = p_{3j}
$$

| Job | $A^{(1)}$ | $B^{(1)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 5 |
| 3 | 7 | 9 |
| 4 | 5 | 3 |

Apply Johnson: min is $B_1=2$ → place Job 1 last; remove. Next min is $A_2=3$ → place Job 2 first. Next min is $B_4=3$ → place Job 4 last remaining. Job 3 in middle.
**Sequence 1:** $(2, 3, 4, 1)$.

For $k=2$:
$$
A_j^{(2)} = p_{1j}+p_{2j}, \qquad B_j^{(2)} = p_{2j}+p_{3j}
$$

| Job | $A^{(2)}$ | $B^{(2)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 8 |
| 2 | 11 | 13 |
| 3 | 9 | 11 |
| 4 | 9 | 7 |

Apply Johnson: min is $B_4=7$ → place Job 4 last. Min is $A_3=9=A_4$ but 4 already placed; tie-break with $A_3=9$ alone → place Job 3 first. Min is $A_1=10$ → place Job 1 next. Job 2 last remaining.
**Sequence 2:** $(3, 1, 2, 4)$.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{\max}$ for each sequence:**

**Sequence 1: $(2, 3, 4, 1)$:**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 3 | 10 | 12 | 21 |
| 4 | 15 | 19 | 24 |
| 1 | 19 | 25 | 27 |

$C_{\max}^{(1)} = 27$.

**Sequence 2: $(3, 1, 2, 4)$:**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 7 | 9 | 18 |
| 1 | 11 | 15 | 20 |
| 2 | 14 | 22 | 25 |
| 4 | 19 | 23 | 28 |

$C_{\max}^{(2)} = 28$.

**Best CDS Sequence:** $(2, 3, 4, 1)$ with $C_{\max} = 27$.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — NEH Heuristic (3 Machines, 4 Jobs)

Use the same data as Example 3.2.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_j$ (sum of processing times):**
$$
T_1 = 4+6+2 = 12, \quad T_2 = 3+8+5 = 16, \quad T_3 = 7+2+9 = 18, \quad T_4 = 5+4+3 = 12
$$

**Step 2 — Sort in descending order of $T_j$:** $T_3 > T_2 > T_1 = T_4$. Tie-break by job index.
Sorted list: $(3, 2, 1, 4)$.

**Step 3 — Take first two jobs. Try both orders:**

Order $(3,2)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 7 | 9 | 18 |
| 2 | 10 | 17 | 22 |

$C_{\max} = 22$.

Order $(2,3)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 3 | 10 | 12 | 21 |

$C_{\max} = 21$. ← **Choose $(2,3)$.**

**Step 4 — Insert Job 1 in all positions in $(2,3)$:**

Position 1: $(1,2,3)$ — compute $C_{\max}$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 10 | 12 |
| 2 | 7 | 15 | 20 |
| 3 | 14 | 16 | 25 |

$C_{\max} = 25$.

Position 2: $(2,1,3)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 1 | 7 | 13 | 18 |
| 3 | 14 | 16 | 27 |

$C_{\max} = 27$.

Position 3: $(2,3,1)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 3 | 10 | 12 | 21 |
| 1 | 14 | 20 | 23 |

$C_{\max} = 23$. ← **Choose $(2,3,1)$.**

**Step 5 — Insert Job 4 in all positions in $(2,3,1)$:**

Position 1: $(4,2,3,1)$ → compute $C_{\max}$.

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 5 | 9 | 12 |
| 2 | 8 | 16 | 21 |
| 3 | 15 | 17 | 26 |
| 1 | 19 | 25 | 28 |

$C_{\max} = 28$.

Position 2: $(2,4,3,1)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 4 | 8 | 12 | 19 |
| 3 | 15 | 17 | 26 |
| 1 | 19 | 25 | 28 |

$C_{\max} = 28$.

Position 3: $(2,3,4,1)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 3 | 10 | 12 | 21 |
| 4 | 15 | 19 | 24 |
| 1 | 19 | 25 | 27 |

$C_{\max} = 27$. ← **Best so far.**

Position 4: $(2,3,1,4)$:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 |
| 3 | 10 | 12 | 21 |
| 1 | 14 | 20 | 23 |
| 4 | 19 | 23 | 26 |

$C_{\max} = 26$. ← **New best!**

**Final NEH Sequence:** $(2, 3, 1, 4)$ with $C_{\max} = 26$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this example, NEH ($\mathbf{C_{\max} = 26}$) outperformed CDS ($\mathbf{C_{\max} = 27}$), illustrating NEH's typical empirical superiority.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Sequencing & Scheduling Heuristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: n Jobs, m Machines] --> B{Problem Type}
    B -- m = 1 --> C[Trivial Sequence: Any Order]
    B -- m = 2 --> D["Johnson's Algorithm (Exact, O(n log n))"]
    B -- m greater than or equal to 3 --> E[Heuristics Layer]
    E --> F[CDS Heuristic: m-1 Johnson Runs]
    E --> G[Palmer's Slope Index]
    E --> H[NEH Heuristic: O(n squared m)]
    F --> I[Evaluate Cmax for Each Sequence]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Select Minimum Cmax Sequence]
    J --> K[Construct Gantt Chart Schedule]
    K --> L[Output: Optimal Sequence and Schedule]
```

### 4.2 Johnson's Algorithm Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Input n Jobs with A_j and B_j] --> S2[Initialize Empty Sequence Left and Right Lists]
    S2 --> S3[Find Global Minimum of All A_j and B_j]
    S3 --> S4{Which Machine Hosts the Minimum?}
    S4 -- Machine 1 --> S5[Append Job to Right End of Sequence]
    S4 -- Machine 2 --> S6[Prepend Job to Left End of Sequence]
    S4 -- Tie --> S7[Tie Break: Arbitrary Choice]
    S5 --> S8[Remove Placed Job from Pool]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9{Pool Empty?}
    S9 -- No --> S3
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Build Schedule Using Forward Pass on M1 and Backward Pass on M2]
    S10 --> S11[Compute Cmax]
```

### 4.3 NEH Heuristic Insertion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Start: n Jobs, m Machines] --> N2[Compute Total Time T_j per Job]
    N2 --> N3[Sort Jobs in Descending Order of T_j]
    N3 --> N4[Take First Two Jobs, Test Both Orders]
    N4 --> N5{Which Order Gives Lower Cmax?}
    N5 --> N6[Retain Better Partial Sequence]
    N6 --> N7[Take Next Job from Sorted List]
    N7 --> N8[Insert Job in Every Possible Position of Current Sequence]
    N8 --> N9[Evaluate Cmax for Each Insertion Position]
    N9 --> N10{All Positions Tested?}
    N10 -- Yes --> N11[Select Position with Minimum Cmax]
    N11 --> N12{All Jobs Inserted?}
    N12 -- No --> N7
    N12 -- Yes --> N13[Output Final NEH Sequence]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Schedule Generation After Sequencing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Block1[Job Sequence from Heuristic] --> Block2[Forward Pass on Machine 1]
    Block2 --> Block3[Constraint: M_i Start equals Max of M_i Minus 1 End and Own Job Ready Time]
    Block3 --> Block4[Compute Completion Times C_i,j]
    Block4 --> Block5[Gantt Chart Visualization]
    Block4 --> Block6[Calculate Performance Metrics]
    Block6 --> Block7[Cmax equals Last Job End on Machine m]
    Block6 --> Block8[Total Flow Time equals Sum of C_i]
    Block6 --> Block9[Makespan minus Sum p_ij equals Idle Time]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between **Sequencing** and **Scheduling**.

**Model Answer:**
Sequencing refers to the determination of the optimal **order** in which jobs must be processed on machines to optimize a criterion such as makespan or flow time. Scheduling refers to the **allocation of specific start and finish times** to each job on each machine consistent with the chosen sequence. In essence, sequencing answers *"which job goes next?"* while scheduling answers *"when exactly does each job start and finish?"* A schedule is constructed only after a valid sequence has been decided.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State **Johnson's Rule** for sequencing $n$ jobs on 2 machines. When is Johnson's algorithm guaranteed to give the optimal solution?

**Model Answer:**
Johnson's Rule states: List all jobs with processing times $(A_j, B_j)$ on the two machines. Repeatedly find the job with the smallest processing time across all jobs and both machines. If the minimum occurs on Machine 1, schedule that job **as early as possible**; if on Machine 2, schedule it **as late as possible**. Remove the placed job and repeat.

Johnson's algorithm is **guaranteed to give the optimal solution** when (i) there are exactly 2 machines, (ii) all jobs must be processed on both machines in the **same order** (Machine 1 then Machine 2), and (iii) the objective is to minimize makespan $C_{\max}$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider 6 jobs with processing times (in hours) on 3 machines given below. Apply **Johnson's Algorithm** on the data treated as a 2-machine flow shop (by combining machines suitably) using the **CDS heuristic**. Determine the best sequence and the corresponding makespan.

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 6 | 4 |
| 3 | 7 | 2 | 7 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 8 |
| 5 | 9 | 3 | 5 |
| 6 | 4 | 7 | 3 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same data, apply **NEH Heuristic** and compare the makespan with that obtained from CDS. Comment on which heuristic performs better and why.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Construct dummy machine times.**

For $m=3$, we have $k=1$ and $k=2$:

**For $k=1$:**
$$
A_j^{(1)} = p_{1j}, \quad B_j^{(1)} = p_{3j}
$$

| Job | $A^{(1)}$ | $B^{(1)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | 7 | 7 |
| 4 | 5 | 8 |
| 5 | 9 | 5 |
| 6 | 4 | 3 |

**Apply Johnson on $k=1$:**
- Minimum is $A_2 = 3$ → Job 2 first.
- Next minimum is $B_6 = 3$ → Job 6 last.
- Next minimum is $B_5 = 5$ → Job 5 second-from-last.
- Next minimum is $A_4 = 5$ → Job 4 second-from-start.
- Remaining: Jobs 1, 3 (with $A_1=8, B_1=6$ and $A_3=7, B_3=7$). Minimum is $B_1=6$ → Job 1 in third position from end. Job 3 occupies the remaining position.

**Sequence from $k=1$:** $(2, 4, 3, 1, 5, 6)$.

**For $k=2$:**
$$
A_j^{(2)} = p_{1j}+p_{2j}, \quad B_j^{(2)} = p_{2j}+p_{3j}
$$

| Job | $A^{(2)}$ | $B^{(2)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13 | 11 |
| 2 | 9 | 10 |
| 3 | 9 | 9 |
| 4 | 9 | 12 |
| 5 | 12 | 8 |
| 6 | 11 | 10 |

**Apply Johnson on $k=2$:**
- Minimum is $B_5 = 8$ → Job 5 last.
- Tie at 9: $A_2 = A_3 = A_4 = 9$, also $B_3 = 9$. Pick $B_3 = 9$ → Job 3 first.
- Remaining ties at $A_2 = A_4 = 9$; pick $A_2 = 9$ → Job 2 second. $A_4 = 9$ → Job 4 third.
- Job 1 ($A_1=13$) → fourth position. Job 6 ($A_6=11$) → fifth position.

**Sequence from $k=2$:** $(3, 2, 4, 1, 6, 5)$.

**Step 2: Compute $C_{\max}$ for each sequence.**

**Sequence $(2, 4, 3, 1, 5, 6)$:**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 9 | 13 |
| 4 | 8 | 12 | 20 |
| 3 | 15 | 14 | 21 |
| 1 | 23 | 25 | 27 |
| 5 | 32 | 28 | 32 |
| 6 | 36 | 35 | 35 |

$C_{\max}^{(1)} = 36$.

**Sequence $(3, 2, 4, 1, 6, 5)$:**

| 4 | 15 | 19 | 27 |
| 1 | 23 | 25 | 32 |
| 6 | 27 | 32 | 35 |
| 5 | 36 | 35 | 40 |

$C_{\max}^{(2)} = 40$.

**Best CDS Sequence:** $(2, 4, 3, 1, 5, 6)$ with $\mathbf{C_{\max} = 36}$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute total processing times $T_j$:**
$$
T_1 = 19, \quad T_2 = 13, \quad T_3 = 16, \quad T_4 = 17, \quad T_5 = 17, \quad T_6 = 14

**Step 2: Sort in descending order:** $T_1 > T_4 = T_5 > T_3 > T_6 > T_2$ → Sorted: $(1, 4, 5, 3, 6, 2)$ (tie between 4, 5 → 4 first).

**Step 7: Insert Job 2 into $(4, 3, 5, 1, 6)$:**

Position 1: $(2, 4, 3, 5, 1, 6)$:

$C_{\max} = 36$.

**Final NEH Sequence:** $(4, 3, 5, 1, 6, 2)$ with $\mathbf{C_{\max} = 36}$.

**Comparison:** Both CDS and NEH yield $C_{\max} = 36$ in this instance (CDS sequence was $(2,4,3,1,5,6)$). NEH typically outperforms CDS empirically due to its **insertion-based local search**, but on this dataset they tie.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Palmer's Slope Index Heuristic** for $n$ jobs on $m$ machines. Apply it to the following 5-job, 4-machine problem and find the sequence and makespan.

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| 2 | 3 | 8 | 5 | 9 |
| 3 | 6 | 4 | 7 | 3 |
| 4 | 8 | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| 5 | 4 | 5 | 9 | 6 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Johnson's, CDS, Palmer's, and NEH** heuristics in terms of (i) time complexity, (ii) optimality guarantee, and (iii) ease of implementation.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute Palmer's slope index** $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (2i - m - 1) p_{ij}$ with $m=4$:
$$
S_j = (2(1)-5)p_{1j} + (2(2)-5)p_{2j} + (2(3)-5)p_{3j} + (2(4)-5)p_{4j}
$$
$$
S_j = -3 p_{1j} - p_{2j} + p_{3j} + 3 p_{4j}
$$

Compute for each job:

$$
S_1 = -3(5) - 7 + 6 + 3(4) = -15 - 7 + 6 + 12 = -4
$$
$$
S_2 = -3(3) - 8 + 5 + 3(9) = -9 - 8 + 5 + 27 = 15
$$
$$
S_3 = -3(6) - 4 + 7 + 3(3) = -18 - 4 + 7 + 9 = -6
$$
$$
S_4 = -3(8) - 6 + 4 + 3(5) = -24 - 6 + 4 + 15 = -11
$$
$$
S_5 = -3(4) - 5 + 9 + 3(6) = -12 - 5 + 9 + 18 = 10
$$

**Step 2: Sort in descending order of $S_j$:** $S_2 = 15 > S_5 = 10 > S_1 = -4 > S_3 = -6 > S_4 = -11$.

**Palmer's Sequence:** $(2, 5, 1, 3, 4)$.

**Step 3: Compute schedule and makespan.**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 11 | 16 | 25 |
| 5 | 7 | 16 | 25 | 31 |
| 1 | 12 | 23 | 31 | 35 |
| 3 | 18 | 27 | 38 | 38 |
| 4 | 26 | 33 | 42 | 43 |

$C_{\max} = 43$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Criterion | Johnson's | CDS | Palmer's | NEH |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Time Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(mn \log n)$ — runs Johnson $(m-1)$ times | $O(mn)$ — single sort | $O(n^2 m)$ |
| **(ii) Optimality Guarantee** | **Optimal** for 2 machines | None — heuristic | None — heuristic | None — heuristic; empirically best |
| **(iii) Ease of Implementation** | Easy (greedy) | Moderate | Easiest (single sort) | Moderate (insertion search) |
| **Applicability** | $m=2$ only | $m \geq 2$ | $m \geq 2$ | $m \geq 2$ |
| **Quality** | Exact | Good | Average | Best (in practice) |

**Discussion:** Johnson's algorithm is unique in guaranteeing optimality but is restricted to 2 machines. Among heuristics, NEH consistently produces makespans within 1–5% of the true optimum in empirical flow-shop benchmarks, while CDS is a natural extension of Johnson. Palmer's heuristic is the simplest but typically yields the weakest solutions.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Gantt Chart Construction Errors** (–2 marks): The completion time on Machine $i$ for Job $j$ is **NOT** simply the sum of $p_{ij}$. It is $\max(C_{i,j-1}, C_{i-1,j}) + p_{ij}$. Forgetting the $\max$ (especially when a job has to wait for the previous job on the same machine) is the #1 error.
>
> 2. **Tie-breaking in Johnson's Algorithm** (–1 mark): When $A_j = B_k$ for different jobs, students often arbitrarily pick one side and forget to justify. Always **state the tie-breaking rule explicitly**.
>
> 3. **CDS — forgetting that $k$ runs from 1 to $(m-1)$** (–2 marks): For $m=3$, you need $k=1$ and $k=2$ — that's 2 Johnson runs, not 1.
>
> 4. **NEH — missing insertion positions** (–2 marks): When inserting the $k$-th job, you must try **all $k$ positions**, not just the ends.
>
> 5. **Palmer's — wrong sign convention** (–1 mark): The slope index weights are $(-m+1, -m+3, \ldots, m-3, m-1)$ or equivalently $(2i-m-1)$. Mixing up signs gives an inverted sequence.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sequence vs Schedule:** Sequence = order of jobs; Schedule = sequence + exact timings.
- **Johnson's Rule** is **optimal for 2 machines** and uses the *minimum-search-and-place* logic.
- **CDS** generates $(m-1)$ 2-machine reductions and applies Johnson to each; select the best makespan.
- **Palmer's Slope Index** $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m}(2i-m-1)p_{ij}$; sequence in descending order.
- **NEH** sorts by total processing time, then iteratively inserts each new job at the position minimizing makespan; **most powerful simple heuristic**.
- **Gantt Chart Rule:** $C_{i,j} = \max(C_{i,j-1}, C_{i-1,j}) + p_{ij}$ with $C_{i,0}=C_{0,j}=0$.
- **Makespan** $C_{\max}$ is the completion time of the last job on the last machine.
- **Idle time** on machine $i$ equals $C_{\max} - \sum_j p_{ij}$ (verify by summing across all machines).
- The **flow-shop** problem assumes **same routing** for every job; **job-shop** allows custom routings.
- For $m=2$, Johnson is **exact**; for $m \geq 3$, all known algorithms are heuristics with no optimality guarantee.
- **Time complexities:** Johnson = $O(n \log n)$; CDS = $O(mn \log n)$; Palmer = $O(mn)$; NEH = $O(n^2 m)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Travelling Salesman Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Travelling Salesman Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** is a classic **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem** in operations research, computational mathematics, and theoretical computer science. Given a set of cities and the distances between every pair of cities, the problem asks to find the **shortest possible route** that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city (forming a **Hamiltonian cycle**).

Formally, the TSP can be stated as: Let $G = (V, E)$ be a complete graph with $n = \vert V \vert$ cities, and let $c_{ij}$ be the cost (distance) of traversing the edge from city $i$ to city $j$. The objective is to find a **closed tour** (permutation $\pi$) of all $n$ vertices such that the total tour cost is minimized.

The mathematical formulation is:

$$Z_{TSP} = \min_{\pi} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{\pi(i), \pi(i+1)}$$

where $\pi(n+1) = \pi(1)$, ensuring the tour is closed (cyclic permutation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TSP belongs to the family of NP-hard problems. There is no known polynomial-time algorithm to solve it exactly for large $n$. The brute-force enumeration of all tours requires $(n-1)!/2$ comparisons (since tours are cyclic and reversible), making exact enumeration feasible only for $n \le 15$ to $n \le 20$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Kerala state tour planner** who must design a 3-day itinerary covering Thiruvananthapuram → Kochi → Kozhikode → Thrissur → Alappuzha and return to Thiruvananthapuram. The planner has a map showing road distances between every pair of cities. The challenge is to **sequence the cities in the best order** so that the total kilometres driven is minimum, no city is skipped, and the journey ends back home.

If there are $n$ cities, the number of possible orderings grows **factorially**. For just 10 cities, there are 9!/2 = **181,440** possible tours. For 20 cities, the number balloons to approximately $6 \times 10^{16}$ — a number so large that even the world's fastest supercomputers cannot enumerate all tours.

**Intuitive Insights:**
- TSP captures the essence of **routing, scheduling, and sequencing decisions** that managers face daily.
- It is a **decision-making problem** because it forces the selection of one route from an astronomically large set of possibilities.
- It justifies the use of **heuristics** (rules of thumb) and **metaheuristics** (smart search strategies) to obtain near-optimal solutions in reasonable time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Tour / Hamiltonian Cycle:** A closed path that visits each vertex exactly once.
> - **Cost Matrix ($C$):** An $n \times n$ symmetric (for undirected) or asymmetric (for directed) matrix where $c_{ij}$ denotes travel cost from $i$ to $j$.
> - **Symmetric TSP (STSP):** $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ for all $i,j$.
> - **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP):** $c_{ij} \neq c_{ji}$ in general.
> - **Optimal Tour:** The tour with minimum total cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of TSP

The TSP can be formulated as a **0–1 Integer Linear Programming (ILP)** model as follows:

**Decision Variable:**
$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the tour includes the edge (route) from city } i \text{ to city } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Objective Function:**
$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$

**Subject to Constraints:**

1. **Visit each city exactly once (out-degree constraint):**
$$\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

2. **Leave each city exactly once (in-degree constraint):**
$$\sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

3. **Subtour elimination (DFJ Miller-Tucker-Zemlin constraints):**
$$u_i - u_j + n \, x_{ij} \leq n - 1 \quad \forall \, 2 \leq i \neq j \leq n$$
where $u_i$ are auxiliary variables to prevent disconnected sub-tours.

### 2.2 Classification of TSP Variants

| Variant | Description | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric TSP (STSP)** | $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ | Road networks (distance same both ways) |
| **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP)** | $c_{ij} \neq c_{ji}$ | One-way streets, flight routes with winds |
| **Euclidean TSP** | Cities lie on a 2D plane, $c_{ij}$ is Euclidean distance | VLSI chip design, drilling PCBs |
| **Metric TSP** | Triangle inequality holds: $c_{ij} \leq c_{ik} + c_{kj}$ | Most practical routing scenarios |
| **Multiple Travelling Salesmen (mTSP)** | $m > 1$ salesmen share the cities | Fleet routing, courier services |
| **Time-Windowed TSP** | Each city has a service time window | Delivery scheduling |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Number of possible tours (asymmetric) | $(n-1)!$ | Brute-force count |
| Number of possible tours (symmetric) | $(n-1)!/2$ | Brute-force count (cycle equivalence) |
| Lower bound via MST | $Z_{TSP} \geq Z_{MST}$ | Quick lower bound check |
| Euclidean distance | $c_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Distance for 2D city coordinates |
| Total tours for $n=10$ | $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ | KTU exam quick fact |
| Total tours for $n=15$ | $14!/2 = 4.35 \times 10^{10}$ | Demonstrates explosion |
| Approximation ratio | $\rho = Z_{heuristic} / Z_{optimal}$ | Quality measure of heuristic |
| Held–Karp lower bound | $LB = n \cdot \det(L^*)^{1/n}$ | Best polynomial-time LB |
| Christofides ratio | $1.5 \times Z_{optimal}$ | Guarantee for metric TSP |
| 2-opt swap cost change | $\Delta = (c_{ik} + c_{jl}) - (c_{ij} + c_{kl})$ | Local search improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the formula $(n-1)!/2$ for symmetric TSP. The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for tours being equivalent under reversal (clockwise vs counter-clockwise). The cyclic equivalence removes another $n$, giving $n!/2n = (n-1)!/2$.

### 2.4 Why TSP Demands Heuristics — The Complexity Argument

The decision version of TSP (asking whether a tour with cost $\leq k$ exists) is **NP-complete**. The optimization version is **NP-hard**. This has profound consequences for decision-making under bounded rationality:

- **For $n \le 15$:** Exact methods like **Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp algorithm)** with $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ complexity are practical.
- **For $15 < n \le 50$:** **Branch and Bound** methods work reasonably.
- **For $n > 50$:** Heuristics and metaheuristics are the only viable options.

This is why TSP is the **canonical benchmark problem** in heuristics research — a method that works well on TSP is likely to work well on other combinatorial problems.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Brute-Force Enumeration of a 4-City TSP

Consider 4 cities with the following **symmetric cost matrix** $C$ (distances in km):

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 |
| **B** | 10 | 0 | 35 | 25 |
| **C** | 15 | 35 | 0 | 30 |
| **D** | 20 | 25 | 30 | 0 |

**Step 1:** Calculate the total number of distinct tours.
Starting from city A (we can fix the start to break symmetry), we have $(4-1)!/2 = 3!/2 = 3$ unique tours. Wait — since we fix A as the start, we have $(4-1)! = 6$ permutations, but with the reversal symmetry this is reduced. For explicit enumeration, we list the 3 unique cycles.

**Step 2:** Enumerate the 3 unique tours starting and ending at A.

- **Tour 1:** A → B → C → D → A
- **Tour 2:** A → B → D → C → A
- **Tour 3:** A → C → B → D → A

**Step 3:** Compute the cost of each tour.

For **Tour 1** (A → B → C → D → A):
$$Z_1 = c_{AB} + c_{BC} + c_{CD} + c_{DA} = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$$

For **Tour 2** (A → B → D → C → A):
$$Z_2 = c_{AB} + c_{BD} + c_{DC} + c_{CA} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$$

For **Tour 3** (A → C → B → D → A):
$$Z_3 = c_{AC} + c_{CB} + c_{BD} + c_{DA} = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$$

**Step 4:** Identify the optimal tour.

$$\min(Z_1, Z_2, Z_3) = \min(95, 80, 95) = 80$$

The optimal tour is **A → B → D → C → A** with a minimum cost of **80 km**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The matrix is symmetric, so $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$. For asymmetric instances, we would need to read $c_{ij}$ in the direction of travel, doubling the number of unique tours to $(n-1)! = 6$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Nearest Neighbour Heuristic

Using the same 4-city cost matrix, starting from city A:

**Step 1:** From city A, find the nearest unvisited city.
$$c_{AB} = 10, \quad c_{AC} = 15, \quad c_{AD} = 20$$
Minimum is $c_{AB} = 10$. Move to **B**.

**Step 2:** From city B, find the nearest unvisited city.
$$c_{BC} = 35, \quad c_{BD} = 25$$
Minimum is $c_{BD} = 25$. Move to **D**.

**Step 3:** From city D, find the nearest unvisited city.
$$c_{DC} = 30$$
Only C is unvisited. Move to **C**.

**Step 4:** Return to start A from C.
$$c_{CA} = 15$$

**Total tour cost:**
$$Z_{NN} = c_{AB} + c_{BD} + c_{DC} + c_{CA} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$$

**Approximation ratio:**
$$\rho = \frac{Z_{NN}}{Z_{optimal}} = \frac{80}{80} = 1.0 \quad \text{(optimal in this case!)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: 2-opt Local Search

Starting from an arbitrary initial tour **A → B → C → D → A** (cost = 95), we attempt to improve via 2-opt swaps.

**Swap candidate 1:** Remove edges (A,B) and (C,D); reconnect as (A,C) and (B,D).
New tour: **A → C → B → D → A**.
New cost: $c_{AC} + c_{CB} + c_{BD} + c_{DA} = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$.
Improvement: $95 - 95 = 0$ (no change).

**Swap candidate 2:** Remove edges (B,C) and (D,A); reconnect as (B,A) and (C,D). This reverses the tour, giving the same cycle.

**Swap candidate 3:** Remove edges (A,B) and (D,A); reconnect as (A,D) and (B,A). Not a valid 2-opt (would create a subtour).

**Conclusion:** No 2-opt improvement is possible. The 2-opt algorithm is at a **local optimum** at cost 95. To escape, we would need a different starting tour or a more advanced method (3-opt, simulated annealing, tabu search).

### 3.4 Derived Bound: MST-based Lower Bound

A minimum spanning tree (MST) is a lower bound for metric TSP because any tour, when an edge is removed, becomes a spanning tree. Therefore:

$$Z_{MST} \leq Z_{TSP}$$

**Computation for the example:** Using Kruskal's algorithm, the MST of the graph is: edges (A,B)=10, (A,C)=15, (A,D)=20 with total weight:
$$Z_{MST} = 10 + 15 + 20 = 45$$

Since $Z_{TSP} = 80$ and $Z_{MST} = 45$, the bound $Z_{TSP} \geq 45$ is satisfied. The **gap ratio** $(Z_{TSP} - Z_{MST})/Z_{TSP} = 35/80 = 0.4375$ or **43.75%**, indicating the MST is a loose (but cheap) lower bound.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Heuristic Selection for TSP

| Heuristic | Time Complexity | Approx. Ratio | Best Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | $O(n!)$ | 1.0 (exact) | $n \leq 10$ | Medium |
| Dynamic Programming (Held-Karp) | $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | 1.0 (exact) | $n \leq 20$ | High |
| Nearest Neighbour | $O(n^2)$ | $\leq (\log n + 1)/2$ | Quick baseline | Very High |
| Christofides Algorithm | $O(n^3)$ | $\leq 1.5$ | Metric TSP guarantee | High |
| 2-opt | $O(n^2)$ per pass | Variable | Mid-size problems | Very High |
| Simulated Annealing | $O(n^2 \cdot k)$ | Variable | Large-scale search | High |
| Genetic Algorithm | $O(n \cdot G \cdot P)$ | Variable | Global exploration | Very High |
| Ant Colony Optimization | $O(n^2 \cdot \text{iter})$ | Variable | Dynamic environments | Medium |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: Nearest Neighbour Heuristic Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select starting city] --> B[Mark current city as visited]
    B --> C{All cities visited?}
    C -->|No| D[Examine distances from current city to all unvisited cities]
    D --> E[Select the unvisited city with minimum distance]
    E --> F[Add edge to tour and update total cost]
    F --> G[Mark selected city as current city]
    G --> C
    C -->|Yes| H[Return to starting city to close the tour]
    H --> I[Compute total tour cost and report solution]
    I --> J[End]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: 2-opt Local Search Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize with any valid tour] --> B[Compute current tour cost]
    B --> C[Set improvement = true]
    C --> D{improvement?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Set improvement = false]
    E --> F[For each pair of edges i,j in tour]
    F --> G[Reverse the segment between edges i and j]
    G --> H[Compute new tour cost]
    H --> I{New cost < Current cost?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept new tour and update cost]
    J --> K[Set improvement = true]
    K --> F
    I -->|No| F
    F --> L[All pairs checked]
    L --> D
    D -->|No| M[Return optimal local tour and cost]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: TSP Heuristic Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[TSP Heuristics] --> TourConstruct[Tour Construction Heuristics]
    Root --> TourImprove[Tour Improvement Heuristics]
    Root --> Meta[Metaheuristic Frameworks]
    
    TourConstruct --> NN[Nearest Neighbour]
    TourConstruct --> Greedy[Greedy Edge Inclusion]
    TourConstruct --> Insert[Insertion Heuristics]
    TourConstruct --> Savings[Clarke Wright Savings]
    
    TourImprove --> TwoOpt[2-opt]
    TourImprove --> ThreeOpt[3-opt]
    TourImprove --> LK[Lin Kernighan]
    TourImprove --> OrOpt[Or-opt moves]
    
    Meta --> SA[Simulated Annealing]
    Meta --> TS[Tabu Search]
    Meta --> GA[Genetic Algorithm]
    Meta --> ACO[Ant Colony Optimization]
    Meta --> PSO[Particle Swarm Optimization]
    
    NN --> E1[O of n squared]
    TwoOpt --> E2[Iterative local search]
    GA --> E2
    SA --> E3[Acceptance of worse moves probabilistically]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp) for Exact TSP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define cost matrix C with n cities] --> B[Choose fixed starting city 1]
    B --> C[Initialize dp of size 2 power n times n]
    C --> D[For subsets S of size 1 containing city j]
    D --> E[Set dp S j = C of 1 j]
    E --> F[For subset sizes k from 2 to n]
    F --> G[For each subset S of size k containing city 1]
    G --> H[For each end city j in S minus 1]
    H --> I[Compute dp S j = min over m in S minus j of dp S minus j m plus C of m j]
    I --> J[Store subproblem result]
    J --> K[Continue iterations]
    K --> L[Final answer = min over j of dp full set j plus C of j 1]
    L --> M[End: Output optimal tour cost and backtrack for path]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end
    
    Exact --> Quality1[Guaranteed Optimal]
    Const --> Quality2[Fast but Lower Quality]

    Quality1 --> Time1[Exponential Time]

```

### 4.6 Decision Tree: Choosing a TSP Heuristic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Decision: Which TSP heuristic to use?] --> Q1{Problem size n?}
    Q1 -->|n less than 15| P1[Use Brute Force or DP for exact answer]
    Q1 -->|15 to 50| P2{Exact or approximate?}
    Q1 -->|50 to 500| P3[Use 2-opt or 3-opt with NN initialization]
    Q1 -->|n greater than 500| P4[Use Genetic Algorithm or ACO]
    
    P2 -->|Exact| P2a[Branch and Bound with MST lower bound]
    P2 -->|Approximate| P2b[Christofides algorithm for guaranteed 1.5 approximation]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q1.** Define the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP). State two real-world applications of TSP.

**Model Answer:**

The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) is a combinatorial optimization problem in which a salesman must find the **shortest possible route** that visits a given set of $n$ cities exactly once and returns to the starting city, given the pairwise distances between all cities.

**Mathematical Statement:** Given a complete graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ cities and a cost matrix $C = [c_{ij}]$, find a Hamiltonian cycle of minimum total cost.

**Two Real-World Applications:**
1. **Vehicle routing in logistics:** A courier company in Kerala (e.g., FedEx) needs to deliver packages to 50 customers and return to the depot. TSP helps minimize fuel consumption and time.
2. **VLSI chip design:** In printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing, holes must be drilled on a board. TSP determines the shortest path for the drill to minimize manufacturing time and cost.
3. **Genome sequencing (DNA fragment assembly):** Reconstructing DNA sequences from overlapping fragments.
4. **Tour planning for tourism:** Planning an itinerary covering multiple tourist destinations in the most efficient order.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2.** What is the difference between a symmetric TSP and an asymmetric TSP? Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Symmetric TSP (STSP) | Asymmetric TSP (ATSP) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost relation | $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ for all $i, j$ | $c_{ij} \neq c_{ji}$ in general |
| Tour count | $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours | $(n-1)!$ unique tours |
| Example | Road distances (driving from Kochi to Alappuzha = driving back) | One-way flight fares (outbound vs return differ) |
| Example 2 | Euclidean distances on a flat map | Driving in cities with one-way streets (Kochi MG Road areas) |

In **STSP**, the cost of travelling from city $i$ to $j$ equals the cost from $j$ to $i$. In **ATSP**, these costs differ.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Formulate the Travelling Salesman Problem as a 0-1 Integer Linear Programming (ILP) model. Define all variables and explain each constraint with its purpose.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the 5 cities with the following symmetric cost matrix, find the optimal tour using brute-force enumeration. Show all unique tours and compute their costs.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 2 | 5 | 7 | 1 |
| **B** | 2 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 6 |
| **C** | 5 | 4 | 0 | 2 | 8 |
| **D** | 7 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 5 |
| **E** | 1 | 6 | 8 | 5 | 0 |

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) ILP Formulation [7 Marks]:**

**Decision Variables:**
$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the salesman travels directly from city } i \text{ to city } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
for all $i, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}, i \neq j$.

**Objective Function:**
$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$

**Constraints:**

1. **Each city is entered exactly once (in-degree):**
$$\sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
*Purpose:* Ensures the salesman arrives at each city from exactly one other city.

2. **Each city is exited exactly once (out-degree):**
$$\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
*Purpose:* Ensures the salesman leaves each city to exactly one other city.

3. **Subtour elimination (MTZ formulation):**
$$u_i - u_j + n \cdot x_{ij} \leq n - 1 \quad \forall \, 2 \leq i \neq j \leq n$$
$$2 \leq u_i \leq n \quad \forall \, i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$$
*Purpose:* Prevents the formation of disconnected sub-tours that visit only a subset of cities. The auxiliary variables $u_i$ represent the position of city $i$ in the tour.

4. **Binary constraint:**
$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall \, i \neq j$$

**(b) Brute-Force Enumeration [7 Marks]:**

Number of unique tours (fixing A as start): $(5-1)!/2 = 24/2 = 12$ unique tours.

We list 12 unique tours and compute costs. Each tour has the form A → ? → ? → ? → ? → A.

Let us organize systematically. Let the sequence be A → (B/C/D/E) → ... and enumerate.

1. **A-B-C-D-E-A:** $2+4+2+5+1 = 14$
2. **A-B-C-E-D-A:** $2+4+8+5+7 = 26$
3. **A-B-D-C-E-A:** $2+3+2+8+1 = 16$
4. **A-B-D-E-C-A:** $2+3+5+8+5 = 23$
5. **A-B-E-C-D-A:** $2+6+8+2+7 = 25$
6. **A-B-E-D-C-A:** $2+6+5+2+5 = 20$
7. **A-C-B-D-E-A:** $5+4+3+5+1 = 18$
8. **A-C-D-B-E-A:** $5+2+3+6+1 = 17$
9. **A-C-E-B-D-A:** $5+8+6+3+7 = 29$
10. **A-D-B-C-E-A:** $7+3+4+8+1 = 23$
11. **A-D-C-B-E-A:** $7+2+4+6+1 = 20$
12. **A-E-B-C-D-A:** $1+6+4+2+7 = 20$

**Minimum cost:**
$$Z^* = \min(14, 26, 16, 23, 25, 20, 18, 17, 29, 23, 20, 20) = 14$$

**Optimal tour:** **A → B → C → D → E → A** with cost **14**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the **subtour elimination constraint** in the ILP formulation. Without it, the model produces a *collection of disconnected cycles* rather than a single Hamiltonian tour. The degree constraints alone (in/out) are NOT sufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #2:** In brute-force enumeration, students frequently miss the **reversal symmetry** — Tour A-B-C-D-A is identical to A-D-C-B-A in cost, so they should be counted as one. KTU expects you to enumerate $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours for symmetric TSP, not $(n-1)!$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Nearest Neighbour Heuristic for solving TSP. Apply it to the following 5-city problem starting from city A, and find the resulting tour cost.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 4 | 8 | 2 | 6 |
| **B** | 4 | 0 | 5 | 7 | 3 |
| **C** | 8 | 5 | 0 | 6 | 9 |
| **D** | 2 | 7 | 6 | 0 | 4 |
| **E** | 6 | 3 | 9 | 4 | 0 |

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the 2-opt local search heuristic for TSP improvement. Starting from the tour A-B-C-D-E-A (cost = 33 from the above matrix), perform one complete pass of 2-opt and report the improved tour.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Nearest Neighbour Heuristic [7 Marks]:**

**Algorithm Description:**
The Nearest Neighbour (NN) heuristic is a **greedy construction heuristic** that builds a tour by repeatedly visiting the closest unvisited city to the current city. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. Start at a chosen city (here, A).
2. From the current city, examine all unvisited cities and move to the one with the minimum distance.
3. Mark the current city as visited.
4. Repeat step 2 until all cities have been visited.
5. Return to the starting city to complete the tour.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — at each of the $n-1$ steps, we scan $n$ cities to find the minimum.

**Application to the 5-city instance:**

**Step 1:** From A, distances to unvisited cities: B=4, C=8, D=2, E=6. Minimum is **D** (distance 2). Move to D.

**Step 2:** From D, distances to unvisited cities: B=7, C=6, E=4. Minimum is **E** (distance 4). Move to E.

**Step 3:** From E, distances to unvisited cities: B=3, C=9. Minimum is **B** (distance 3). Move to B.

**Step 4:** From B, distances to unvisited cities: C=5. Only C remains. Move to C.

**Step 5:** Return from C to A. Distance = 8.

**Total tour cost:**
$$Z_{NN} = c_{AD} + c_{DE} + c_{EB} + c_{BC} + c_{CA} = 2 + 4 + 3 + 5 + 8 = 22$$

**Tour:** **A → D → E → B → C → A** with cost **22**.

**(b) 2-opt Local Search [7 Marks]:**

**Algorithm Description:**
The 2-opt heuristic is a **local search improvement** technique. It works by iteratively removing two edges from the current tour and reconnecting them in the only other possible way (the alternative crossing pattern). If the new tour has a lower cost, the swap is accepted. The process repeats until no improving 2-opt swap exists (local optimum).

**Procedure:**
1. Start with an initial tour.
2. For every pair of non-adjacent edges $(i, i+1)$ and $(j, j+1)$ in the tour:
   - Remove both edges, breaking the tour into two paths.
   - Reconnect the four endpoints in the alternative way, reversing one of the segments.
   - If the new tour cost is lower, keep the new tour.
3. Repeat until no improvement is found.

**Initial Tour:** A → B → C → D → E → A, cost = $4+5+6+4+6 = 25$.

(Wait, let me recompute: $c_{AB}=4, c_{BC}=5, c_{CD}=6, c_{DE}=4, c_{EA}=6$. Total = $4+5+6+4+6 = 25$.)

**2-opt Pass — All possible swaps:**

**Swap 1:** Remove (A,B) and (C,D); reverse B-C segment. New tour: A → C → B → D → E → A.
Cost: $c_{AC} + c_{CB} + c_{BD} + c_{DE} + c_{EA} = 8 + 5 + 7 + 4 + 6 = 30$. **Worse.**

**Swap 2:** Remove (A,B) and (D,E); reverse B-C-D segment. New tour: A → D → C → B → E → A.
Cost: $c_{AD} + c_{DC} + c_{CB} + c_{BE} + c_{EA} = 2 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 6 = 22$. **Better!**

**Swap 3:** Remove (A,B) and (E,A); reverse entire tour. Equivalent to A → E → D → C → B → A, same cycle.

**Swap 4:** Remove (B,C) and (D,E); reverse C-D segment. New tour: A → B → D → C → E → A.
Cost: $c_{AB} + c_{BD} + c_{DC} + c_{CE} + c_{EA} = 4 + 7 + 6 + 9 + 6 = 32$. **Worse.**

**Swap 5:** Remove (B,C) and (E,A); reverse C-D-E segment. New tour: A → B → E → D → C → A.
Cost: $c_{AB} + c_{BE} + c_{ED} + c_{DC} + c_{CA} = 4 + 3 + 4 + 6 + 8 = 25$. **Same.**

**Swap 6:** Remove (C,D) and (E,A); reverse D-E segment. New tour: A → B → C → E → D → A.
Cost: $c_{AB} + c_{BC} + c_{CE} + c_{ED} + c_{DA} = 4 + 5 + 9 + 4 + 2 = 24$. **Better!**

**Best 2-opt move:** Swap 2 — Tour A → D → C → B → E → A with cost **22**.

**Result after 2-opt:** Tour **A → D → C → B → E → A** with cost **22**.

This is identical to the Nearest Neighbour solution in this case, suggesting it is likely the optimal tour (and indeed brute-force would confirm cost 22 is the optimum for this matrix).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #3:** In 2-opt, students often forget that after reversing a segment, the resulting tour must be **valid** (still a single cycle). Removing two edges and reconnecting them always preserves the Hamiltonian cycle property as long as the two edges are not adjacent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #4:** A 2-opt swap that gives the **same cost** is a *plateau move* — the convention in KTU exams is to **accept only strictly improving moves** to ensure monotonic decrease. If a move is accepted, the search must continue from the new tour.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist for Travelling Salesman Problems**

- **Definition:** TSP = find shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting all $n$ cities exactly once and returning to start.
- **Decision version of TSP is NP-complete; optimization version is NP-hard.**
- **Symmetric TSP:** $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ → unique tours count = $(n-1)!/2$.
- **Asymmetric TSP:** $c_{ij} \neq c_{ji}$ → unique tours count = $(n-1)!$.
- **ILP Formulation:** Minimize $\sum \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$, subject to in/out degree = 1, subtour elimination (MTZ: $u_i - u_j + n x_{ij} \leq n-1$), and binary $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Held-Karp Dynamic Programming:** $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ — exact, works for $n \leq 20$.
- **Nearest Neighbour (NN):** Greedy, $O(n^2)$, good baseline but greedy, can be arbitrarily bad.
- **2-opt:** Local search, swap two edges if it improves cost, iterate until local optimum.
- **3-opt:** Extension of 2-opt to three edges; more powerful but slower.
- **Lin-Kernighan:** State-of-the-art local search for TSP, variable $k$-opt moves.
- **Christofides Algorithm:** For metric TSP, guarantees tour cost $\leq 1.5 \times$ optimal.
- **MST Lower Bound:** $Z_{MST} \leq Z_{TSP}$ for metric TSP.
- **Heuristics for decision-making:** Used when optimal solution infeasible due to NP-hardness.
- **Real-world domains:** Logistics, VLSI design, DNA sequencing, tourism, drilling, robotics.
- **Key move cost change for 2-opt:** $\Delta = (c_{ik} + c_{jl}) - (c_{ij} + c_{kl})$; accept if $\Delta < 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examination Tip:** Always state the formula $(n-1)!/2$ when enumerating tours for symmetric TSP. Always include all three constraint types in the ILP (degree + subtour elimination + binary). Always verify your brute-force enumeration by checking the total number of unique tours matches $(n-1)!/2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variants of Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variants of Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a probabilistic, single-solution metaheuristic inspired by the physical annealing process in metallurgy, where a material is heated and slowly cooled to reduce defects and reach a low-energy crystalline state. A **variant of Simulated Annealing** refers to any modified or extended version of the canonical SA algorithm designed to overcome specific limitations such as slow convergence, sensitivity to parameter tuning, premature convergence to local optima, or poor performance on specific problem landscapes.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET634 – Heuristics for Decision Making)** classification, the variants of Simulated Annealing are typically categorized based on three design dimensions:
1. **Cooling Schedule** (how the temperature parameter T is reduced over time)
2. **Neighborhood Generation Strategy** (how new candidate solutions are produced)
3. **Acceptance Criterion** (how worse solutions are probabilistically accepted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish between **Boltzmann Annealing**, **Cauchy (Fast) Annealing**, **Very Fast Simulated Annealing (VFSA)**, **Adaptive Simulated Annealing (ASA)**, and **Threshold Accepting** — all of which are considered direct variants of the SA family.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **mountain climber trapped in fog**, trying to find the lowest valley (the global minimum) in a mountain range. A standard **greedy hill-descent** algorithm would only ever walk downhill, meaning if you are stuck in a small valley (local optimum), you will never escape — even if a deeper valley exists just over the next hill.

Now, think of **Simulated Annealing** as giving the climber a special ability: when the air is **hot** (high temperature), he is willing to take **occasional uphill steps** with some probability. As the air **cools down**, he becomes more **conservative** and rarely takes uphill steps. By the end of the journey, the climber behaves like a strict hill-descender and settles in the deepest valley he can find.

**Variants of SA are simply different "weather patterns" for this climber:**
- **Boltzmann Annealing** = Cool air following the original physics formula
- **Cauchy / Fast SA** = Cool air drops rapidly (good for large problems)
- **Adaptive SA** = The climber adjusts his pace **based on the terrain** he is currently walking on
- **Threshold Accepting** = The climber is given a **strict tolerance limit** instead of probabilistic decisions
- **Very Fast SA (VFSA)** = Uses a smarter cooling law derived from statistical mechanics, with **T₀ = 1** always

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics used in SA Variants:**
> - **Acceptance Probability (P)**: A dimensionless value in **[0, 1]**.
> - **Temperature (T)**: A positive control parameter, typically in **T > 0**.
> - **Boltzmann Constant (k_B)**: Often normalized to **k_B = 1** in algorithmic SA.
> - **Cooling Coefficient (α)**: Usually in **(0, 1)**, e.g., **α = 0.95** or **α = 0.99**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Canonical Simulated Annealing — Recap

The canonical SA algorithm (Kirkpatrick et al., 1983; Černý, 1985) operates as follows:

1. Initialize a **starting solution** $s$ and a **high initial temperature** $T_0$.
2. Generate a **neighboring solution** $s'$ from $s$.
3. Compute the **objective change** $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$.
4. If $\Delta E \leq 0$ (better solution), **accept** $s'$.
5. If $\Delta E > 0$ (worse solution), **accept with probability**:
$$P(\text{accept}) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$$
6. **Reduce temperature** using a cooling schedule.
7. Repeat steps 2–6 until **termination condition** is met (final temperature, time budget, or convergence).

The probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$ is known as the **Metropolis Criterion**, derived from statistical mechanics (Metropolis et al., 1953).

## 2.2 Classification of Variants

### 2.2.1 Variants Based on Cooling Schedule

#### A. **Boltzmann Annealing** (Canonical SA)
The temperature is reduced using the Boltzmann distribution-based schedule:
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(1 + k)}$$
where $k$ is the iteration counter. This guarantees asymptotic convergence to the global optimum but is **extremely slow** in practice.

#### B. **Cauchy Annealing (Fast Simulated Annealing - FSA)**
Introduced by Szu and Hartley (1987), the cooling schedule is much faster:
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{k}$$
This produces a **Cauchy distribution** for the perturbation, allowing **larger jumps** at high temperatures.

#### C. **Very Fast Simulated Annealing (VFSA)** (Ingber, 1989)
Uses a temperature that depends on the problem dimension $n$:
$$T_k = T_0 \cdot \exp\left(-c \cdot k^{1/n}\right)$$
where $c > 0$ is a tuning parameter and $n$ is the number of decision variables. The perturbation follows a **Cauchy-like distribution scaled per dimension**.

#### D. **Exponential Cooling** (Most common in practice)
A geometric reduction of temperature:
$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$
Typically $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$.

#### E. **Linear Cooling**
$$T_k = T_0 - k \cdot \beta$$
where $\beta$ is a small step decrement.

### 2.2.2 Variants Based on Acceptance Criterion

#### F. **Threshold Accepting (TA)** (Dueck & Scheuer, 1990)
Instead of probabilistic acceptance, the algorithm accepts any worse move whose deterioration does not exceed a **threshold** $\tau$ that decreases over time:
$$\text{Accept } s' \text{ if } f(s') - f(s) \leq \tau_k$$
At $\tau = 0$, it degenerates into pure hill-descent. TA is **deterministic** (no random number generator needed for acceptance).

#### G. **Great Deluge Algorithm (GDA)** (Dueck, 1993)
The objective function is constrained to remain below a "water level" that rises monotonically:
$$\text{Accept } s' \text{ if } f(s') \leq W_k$$
where $W_{k+1} = W_k + \Delta W$ and $\Delta W > 0$.

#### H. **Record-to-Record Travel (RRT)** (Dueck, 1993)
Accepts a new solution if it does not deviate more than a fraction $\delta$ from the best-so-far record:
$$\text{Accept } s' \text{ if } f(s') \leq (1 + \delta) \cdot f(s^*)$$

### 2.2.3 Variants Based on Adaptive / Self-Tuning

#### I. **Adaptive Simulated Annealing (ASA)** (Ingber, 1993)
- Adjusts the **step size** of perturbation in each dimension $i$ based on the **sensitivity** of the objective function.
- Re-anneals the temperature periodically (temperature is increased if search stagnates).
- Uses the VFSA temperature law but with **re-annealing intervals** based on acceptance ratios.

#### J. **Parallel Simulated Annealing**
Multiple SA runs executed in parallel on:
- **Multiple processors** (speedup)
- **Multiple starting points** (diversification)
With periodic exchange of best solutions (migration).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Variant Name | Cooling Law / Acceptance Law | Key Constant / Parameter | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boltzmann SA** | $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{\log(1+k)}$ | $T_0$, $k$ | Provable global convergence | Extremely slow |
| 2 | **Cauchy / Fast SA** | $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{k}$ | $T_0$, $k$ | Much faster than Boltzmann | Still slow for large $n$ |
| 3 | **Very Fast SA (VFSA)** | $T_k = T_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot k^{1/n}}$ | $T_0$, $c$, $n$ | Scales with problem dimension | Requires tuning of $c$ |
| 4 | **Exponential Cooling** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | $\alpha \in (0.9, 0.99)$ | Simple, widely used | No convergence guarantee |
| 5 | **Linear Cooling** | $T_k = T_0 - k\beta$ | $\beta$ | Easy to implement | Can reach $T<0$ if not capped |
| 6 | **Threshold Accepting (TA)** | Accept if $\Delta f \leq \tau_k$ | $\tau_0$, $\tau_{\text{final}}$ | Deterministic, no RNG | Cannot escape deep local minima |
| 7 | **Great Deluge (GDA)** | Accept if $f(s') \leq W_k$ | $\Delta W$ | Intuitive, easy to code | Needs careful $\Delta W$ |
| 8 | **Record-to-Record (RRT)** | Accept if $f(s') \leq (1+\delta) f^*$ | $\delta$ | Strong elitism | Less exploration |
| 9 | **Adaptive SA (ASA)** | VFSA + re-annealing | $c$, re-anneal period | Self-tuning | More complex |
| 10 | **Parallel SA** | Same as parent + migration | # processors, migration rate | Faster wall-clock time | Communication overhead |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These SA variants are used in **VLSI circuit placement**, **job-shop scheduling**, **neural network weight optimization (Boltzmann machines)**, **traveling salesman problem (TSP)**, **image segmentation**, **structural design optimization**, and **hyperparameter tuning in machine learning**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Metropolis Acceptance Probability

The acceptance probability in canonical SA is derived from the **Boltzmann factor** in statistical mechanics. Consider a system of particles in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$. The probability of the system being in a state with energy $E$ is:

$$
P(E) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot e^{-E / (k_B T)}
$$

where:
- $E$ = energy of the state
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature
- $Z = \sum_{i} e^{-E_i / (k_B T)}$ = partition function (normalization constant)

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio of probabilities for two energy states $E_1$ and $E_2$:

$$
\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} = \frac{e^{-E_2 / (k_B T)} / Z}{e^{-E_1 / (k_B T)} / Z} = e^{-(E_2 - E_1) / (k_B T)}
$$

**Step 2:** In an optimization context, map "energy" to "objective value" $f(s)$ and normalize $k_B = 1$. For a transition from $s$ (with $f(s)$) to $s'$ (with $f(s')$), the energy change is:

$$
\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)
$$

**Step 3:** If $\Delta E < 0$ (improvement), the exponent becomes positive, making the ratio $> 1$, meaning the new state is **always more probable**. Hence, accept with probability 1.

**Step 4:** If $\Delta E > 0$ (deterioration), the ratio is $< 1$. The algorithm accepts with this probability:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

This is the **Metropolis Criterion**.

**Step 5:** In the algorithmic setting, we generate a uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$ and accept if:

$$
r \leq e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

> **Explanation of conversion logic:** Step 1 uses the canonical Boltzmann distribution; Step 2 establishes the algorithmic mapping; Step 3 handles the improvement case deterministically; Step 4 generalizes to the deterioration case probabilistically; Step 5 translates probability into a Monte Carlo decision rule.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Exponential Cooling Is a Good Compromise

Suppose we want the **expected number of accepted worsening moves** to remain bounded as $k \to \infty$. A worsening move of size $\Delta E$ is accepted with probability $e^{-\Delta E / T_k}$.

**Step 1:** Total acceptance probability at iteration $k$:

$$
P_k(\text{worsening accepted}) = \int_0^{\infty} p(\Delta E) \cdot e^{-\Delta E / T_k} \, d(\Delta E)
$$

**Step 2:** Assume a simplified model where worsening magnitudes are uniformly distributed up to some maximum $M$. Then:

$$
P_k \approx \frac{1}{M} \int_0^M e^{-\Delta E / T_k} \, d(\Delta E) = \frac{T_k}{M} \left( 1 - e^{-M / T_k} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** For **logarithmic (Boltzmann) cooling** $T_k = T_0 / \log(k+1)$, we have $T_k \to 0$ very slowly, ensuring $P_k \to 0$ (no infinite acceptance chain), which is the condition for **theoretical convergence**.

**Step 4:** For **exponential cooling** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, the temperature decays geometrically, and $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$. As $k$ grows, $T_k$ vanishes exponentially fast, giving a **practical** balance between speed and search quality.
