# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a heuristic. How does it differ from an exact method in optimization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **heuristic** is a problem-specific, computationally efficient technique that produces *good-enough* solutions for complex optimization problems without guaranteeing optimality. It typically operates in polynomial time, exploiting problem structure or empirical rules to navigate large search spaces.

An **exact method** (e.g., Branch & Bound, Dynamic Programming) *guarantees* finding the provably optimal solution but generally requires exponential computational time, making it infeasible for large NP-hard problems.

**Key Differences:**
- *Optimality:* Exact methods guarantee it; heuristics do not.
- *Time:* Exact methods are exponential; heuristics are polynomial.
- *Scalability:* Heuristics scale to large real-world problems; exact methods do not.
- *Use case:* Exact methods for small, critical decisions; heuristics for large, real-time decisions.

*(Award full 3 marks for a clear definition + 2-3 distinguishing points.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a metaheuristic? List any two metaheuristic algorithms with one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **metaheuristic** is a high-level, problem-independent algorithmic framework that guides subordinate heuristics to search a solution space efficiently. It orchestrates the *exploration* of new regions and *exploitation* of promising regions to escape local optima and find near-global optima.

**Two examples:**

1. **Simulated Annealing (SA):** Inspired by metallurgical annealing. Used in **VLSI circuit design** to minimize wire length and timing delays.
2. **Genetic Algorithm (GA):** Inspired by natural evolution (selection, crossover, mutation). Used in **job-shop scheduling** to minimize makespan.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two examples with applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify heuristic methods in detail. Discuss the characteristics, advantages, and limitations of each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heuristic algorithm for a 25-city TSP produces a tour of length **1,847 km**. The best-known optimal tour for this instance is **1,792 km**. Compute the **optimality gap** and the **relative percentage deviation (RPD)**. Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

Heuristic methods are classified into **four major classes**:

**1. Constructive Heuristics (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* Build a solution from scratch by adding one component at a time using a greedy rule.
- *Examples:* Nearest Neighbor (TSP), Greedy Knapsack, Clarke-Wright Savings (VRP).
- *Advantages:* Extremely fast, simple to implement, useful for initial solutions.
- *Limitations:* Solution quality often mediocre; greedy choices may be globally suboptimal.

**2. Improvement Heuristics (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* Start from a complete (often greedy) solution and iteratively refine via local moves.
- *Examples:* 2-opt, 3-opt, Lin-Kernighan (TSP); local search for scheduling.
- *Advantages:* Significantly better quality than constructive methods alone.
- *Limitations:* Stuck in *local optima*; sensitive to starting solution and neighborhood definition.

**3. Mathematical / Relaxation-Based Heuristics (1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* Solve a *relaxed* version of the problem (e.g., dropping integrality) and use the relaxed solution to construct a feasible heuristic solution.
- *Examples:* LP relaxation + rounding, Lagrangian relaxation, surrogate relaxation.
- *Advantages:* Provides *bounds* on the optimum; theoretically grounded.
- *Limitations:* Requires expertise; relaxation may be too loose or too tight.

**4. Metaheuristics (1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* High-level strategies that escape local optima using stochastic or memory-based mechanisms.
- *Examples:* Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithm, Ant Colony Optimization, Tabu Search, Particle Swarm Optimization.
- *Advantages:* Problem-independent, near-optimal quality, robust across problem classes.
- *Limitations:* Parameter tuning required; no optimality guarantee; computationally more expensive than simple heuristics.

> *Award 7 marks as: 1 mark per class definition + examples (4 marks) + comparative advantages/limitations (3 marks).*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Heuristic tour length: $f_{\text{best}} = 1847$ km
- Best-known optimum: $f(s^*) = 1792$ km

**Step 1 — Absolute deviation:**

$$\lvert f(s^*) - f_{\text{best}} \rvert = \lvert 1792 - 1847 \rvert = 55 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Optimality Gap calculation:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{\lvert f(s^*) - f_{\text{best}} \rvert}{\lvert f(s^*) \vert} \times 100\% = \frac{55}{1792} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\epsilon = 0.03069 \times 100\% = 3.07\%$$

**Step 4 — RPD calculation:**

$$RPD = \frac{f_{\text{best}} - f_{\text{ref}}}{f_{\text{ref}}} \times 100 = \frac{1847 - 1792}{1792} \times 100 = \frac{55}{1792} \times 100 = 3.07\%$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Significance (3 marks):**

- For a logistics company running 1000 such tours per day, an extra **55 km/day/vehicle** translates to **55,000 km/day fleet-wide**.
- At a fuel cost of ₹80/km (heavy commercial vehicle), the daily extra cost = $55{,}000 \times 80 = ₹44{,}00{,}000$ (₹44 lakhs/day).
- **Annualized:** $\approx ₹ 160$ crores/year in avoidable fuel costs.
- A 3.07% gap is *acceptable* in real-time dispatch systems but *unacceptable* in strategic network design — illustrating the importance of context-dependent heuristic quality thresholds.

> *Award 7 marks: 2 marks formula + 2 marks numerical computation + 3 marks interpretation/significance.*

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **exploration vs. exploitation trade-off** in heuristic search. How does the Simulated Annealing algorithm balance this trade-off mathematically? Derive the acceptance probability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **heuristics, metaheuristics, and hyper-heuristics** with suitable examples. Discuss the **No-Free-Lunch (NFL) theorem** and its implications for heuristic selection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Exploration vs. Exploitation — Conceptual Foundation (3 marks):**

- **Exploration (Diversification):** The search process visits *new, unexplored* regions of the solution space $\mathcal{S}$ to discover potentially better solutions far from the current one. This prevents *premature convergence* to local optima.
- **Exploitation (Intensification):** The search process *refines* the current best region by making small, focused moves to find the best possible solution in the local neighborhood.
- **The Trade-off:** Excessive exploration wastes time in poor regions; excessive exploitation traps the search in local optima. The *art* of heuristic design is dynamically balancing these two.

**Simulated Annealing's Mathematical Balance (4 marks):**

SA uses a *temperature parameter* $T$ that is *high initially* (favoring exploration via random acceptance) and *decays over time* (favoring exploitation via strict acceptance of only improving moves).

The acceptance probability of a *worsening move* with objective increase $\Delta f > 0$ is given by the **Metropolis criterion**:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-\Delta f / T}$$

**Derivation (as in §3.3 above):** Based on the Boltzmann distribution from statistical mechanics, the ratio of occupation probabilities of two energy states is $e^{-\Delta E / (k_B T)}$. Mapping energy to objective value and absorbing $k_B$ into $T$ yields the formula.

**Key properties:**

- As $T \to \infty$: $P_{\text{accept}} \to 1$ (accept all — pure exploration).
- As $T \to 0^+$: $P_{\text{accept}} \to 0$ for $\Delta f > 0$ (accept only improvements — pure exploitation).
- The geometric cooling schedule $T_{t+1} = \alpha T_t$ (with $0 < \alpha < 1$, typically $\alpha = 0.95$) gradually transitions the search from exploration-dominant to exploitation-dominant.

> *Award 7 marks: 3 marks for conceptual explanation + 4 marks for SA mathematical formulation including derivation.*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Heuristic | Metaheuristic | Hyper-Heuristic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Single problem | Problem class | Problem class *or* domain |
| **Operates on** | Solutions | Solutions + solutions | Heuristics themselves |
| **Adaptivity** | Static rule | Adaptive parameters | Adaptive selection/generation |
| **Examples** | 2-opt, NN | SA, GA, ACO, TS | Genetic Programming of LSH, AI-based selector |

**No-Free-Lunch (NFL) Theorem (3 marks):**

The **NFL Theorem** (Wolpert & Macready, 1997) states that *when averaged uniformly over all possible problem instances, every algorithm performs equivalently*. That is, no single heuristic is universally superior across all problems.

**Implications for Heuristic Selection:**

1. **No "best" algorithm exists** in the absolute sense — performance is problem-dependent.
2. **Empirical benchmarking** on representative instances is essential before deployment.
3. **Algorithm portfolios** (combining multiple heuristics) often outperform any single one.
4. Justifies the existence of a *rich ecosystem* of metaheuristics — each tailored to specific problem structures.
5. Encourages **problem-specific customization** rather than blind application of generic methods.

> *Award 7 marks: 4 marks comparison + 3 marks NFL discussion.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing heuristic with metaheuristic:** A heuristic is *problem-specific* (e.g., 2-opt for TSP); a metaheuristic is *problem-independent* (e.g., SA applied to TSP, scheduling, *and* bin packing). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the optimality gap:** Always write "%" — a bare decimal (e.g., 0.0307) is *incomplete*.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions:** When deriving the acceptance probability, explicitly mention that $k_B$ is absorbed into $T$ and the energy maps to the objective function. Omission costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **No examples in classification questions:** A classification without examples scores at most *half* marks. Always pair each class with at least one named algorithm.
> 5. **Skipping engineering interpretation:** A 7-mark numerical question *requires* interpretation. A correct formula with correct numbers but no engineering comment scores at most **4/7**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Basic Concepts:**

- **Heuristic** = Problem-specific, fast, *good-enough* solution strategy. **No optimality guarantee.**
- **Metaheuristic** = Problem-independent, *framework* that uses heuristics as building blocks.
- **Hyper-heuristic** = Operates on *heuristics themselves* (selection or generation).
- **Exploration** = Visit new regions. **Exploitation** = Refine current region. **Balance is critical.**
- **Optimality Gap formula:** $\epsilon = \frac{\lvert f^* - f_{\text{best}} \rvert}{\lvert f^* \rvert} \times 100\%$ — must include **%** sign.
- **RPD formula:** Same as optimality gap when $f_{\text{ref}} = f^*$. Used in benchmark papers.
- **SA acceptance:** $P = e^{-\Delta f / T}$ — derived from **Boltzmann distribution** in statistical mechanics.
- **Cooling schedule:** Geometric decay $T_{t+1} = \alpha T_t$ with $\alpha \approx 0.95$ is *most common*.
- **NFL Theorem:** No algorithm is universally best; *benchmark before you deploy*.
- **Time complexity:** Exact = $O(e^n)$ or $O(n!)$; Heuristics = $O(n^k)$ polynomial.
- **Classification to memorize:** Constructive, Improvement, Relaxation-based, Decomposition, Metaheuristic, Hyper-heuristic.
- **Constructive examples:** Nearest Neighbor, Greedy, Clarke-Wright Savings.
- **Improvement examples:** 2-opt, 3-opt, Lin-Kernighan.
- **Metaheuristic examples:** SA, GA, ACO, PSO, TS — *all five frequently appear in exam answers.*
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** Heuristics exist *because* exact methods fail at $n \geq 50$–$100$.
- **Asymptotic Convergence:** Some metaheuristics (e.g., SA with logarithmic cooling) *converge in probability* to global optimum — but require *infinite* time, hence practical termination is by budget exhaustion.
- **Real-world anchors:** Mentioning Amazon (VRP), VLSI (SA), Job-shop (GA) earns *extra* appreciation marks in descriptive answers.
- **Numerical safety:** Always show formula → substitution → arithmetic → interpretation in numerical answers. Examiners reward *methodical* working.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note:** The term "Generic Algorithm" in the KTU 2024 Scheme module descriptor for *Heuristics for Decision Making* refers to the **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** — a population-based metaheuristic inspired by Charles Darwin's theory of natural selection and genetic evolution. It is one of the foundational topics for KTU university examinations in this course.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a stochastic, population-based search and optimization metaheuristic that mimics the mechanics of natural selection and biological genetics. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals* or *chromosomes*), iteratively applies genetic operators — **selection**, **crossover** (recombination), and **mutation** — and uses a *fitness function* to evolve better solutions over generations until a termination criterion is met.

Mathematically, a GA can be formalized as the tuple:

$$GA = \{P_0, \Phi, \Omega, \Psi, \Lambda, \tau\}$$

where:
- $P_0$ = Initial population (set of candidate solutions)
- $\Phi$ = Fitness evaluation function
- $\Omega$ = Selection operator
- $\Psi$ = Crossover (recombination) operator
- $\Lambda$ = Mutation operator
- $\tau$ = Termination criterion

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary):**
> - **Chromosome / Individual:** A single candidate solution encoded as a string.
> - **Gene:** A single element/position of a chromosome.
> - **Population:** A collection of chromosomes in one generation.
> - **Fitness:** A scalar quality score quantifying how close a chromosome is to the optimum.
> - **Generation:** One iteration of the GA loop.
> - **Locus:** Position index of a gene within the chromosome.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Jungle of Solutions"

Imagine you are standing on a vast mountain range, blindfolded, and your task is to find the **highest peak** (the global optimum). You cannot see the terrain. You have **200 climbers** randomly scattered across the landscape. Each climber reports their **altitude** — this is the *fitness*.

- The **tallest climbers** (highest fitness) are given the most **children** (selection).
- Children are formed by **combining the routes** of two strong climbers (crossover).
- Occasionally, a child takes a **random detour** up a side trail (mutation).
- The weakest climbers are discarded, and the next generation of 200 is formed.
- After many generations, the entire population clusters near the highest peak.

This is exactly what a Genetic Algorithm does — it **parallelizes** the search across many candidate solutions and uses **survival of the fittest** to drive the population toward the optimum.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use GA instead of calculus-based methods?** Traditional methods (gradient descent, Newton's method) require derivatives and convexity. GA needs **only a fitness function** and works on discrete, non-differentiable, and highly multimodal landscapes — exactly the messy problems in real-world engineering.

## 1.3 Why GA Matters in Engineering Decision Making

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Production & Operations** | Job-shop scheduling, inventory optimization |
| **Network Design** | Vehicle routing, telecom topology design |
| **Machine Learning** | Feature selection, hyperparameter tuning, neural architecture search |
| **Structural Engineering** | Truss optimization, parameter calibration |
| **Finance** | Portfolio optimization under non-convex constraints |

The key engineering strengths are **global search capability**, **derivative-free operation**, and **parallelism**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical GA Pipeline

A Genetic Algorithm operates through a strict iterative pipeline. Each generation executes the following stages:

### Stage 1 — Initialization
A population of $N$ chromosomes is generated. Each chromosome is an encoded candidate solution.

- For binary encoding: each gene is drawn from $\{0, 1\}$ uniformly at random.
- For real-valued encoding: each gene is drawn from a feasible interval $[L_i, U_i]$.

### Stage 2 — Fitness Evaluation
Each chromosome $\mathbf{x}_i$ is decoded into the problem domain and evaluated by the fitness function $\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$. Higher fitness = better candidate.

### Stage 3 — Selection
Parent chromosomes are probabilistically chosen, biased toward those with higher fitness. Common schemes:

**Roulette Wheel (Fitness-Proportionate Selection):**
The probability that chromosome $i$ is selected is:

$$p_i = \frac{\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)}$$

The expected number of copies of chromosome $i$ in the mating pool:

$$E_i = N \cdot p_i = \frac{N \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)}$$

**Tournament Selection:**
Pick $k$ chromosomes at random; the one with highest fitness wins. Tournament size $k = 2$ or $3$ is common.

**Rank-Based Selection:**
Chromosomes are sorted by fitness; selection probability is proportional to rank, not raw fitness. This prevents premature convergence when fitness values vary wildly.

### Stage 4 — Crossover (Recombination)
With probability $p_c$ (typically $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$), two parents exchange genes to form offspring.

**Single-Point Crossover:** Choose a cut-point $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$; swap the right segments.

$$\text{Parent 1: } 1011\,\vert\,00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 1: } 1011\,11$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } 0110\,\vert\,11 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 2: } 0110\,00$$

**Two-Point Crossover:** Two cut-points; swap the middle segment.

**Uniform Crossover:** Each gene independently inherited from either parent with probability 0.5.

### Stage 5 — Mutation
With probability $p_m$ (typically $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$), each gene is randomly altered.

- **Binary mutation:** Flip bit $0 \leftrightarrow 1$.
- **Real-valued mutation:** Add Gaussian perturbation $g' = g + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma)$.

### Stage 6 — Replacement & Termination
The new offspring replace (partially or fully) the old population. Common schemes:
- **Generational:** Replace entire population each generation.
- **Steady-State:** Replace only a few individuals per generation (elitism).
- **Elitist:** Always carry the best $e$ chromosomes to the next generation unmodified.

Termination occurs when:
- Maximum generations reached,
- Fitness plateau detected,
- Optimal solution found, or
- Computational budget exhausted.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Population size | $N$ | $20 \leq N \leq 200$ |
| Chromosome length | $L$ | Depends on encoding precision |
| Fitness (binary → integer) | $x = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | $0 \leq x \leq 2^L - 1$ |
| Real-value decoding | $x = L_b + \frac{(U_b - L_b)}{2^L - 1} \cdot \text{integer}$ | $L_b$ = lower bound, $U_b$ = upper bound |
| Roulette probability | $p_i = \Phi_i \,/\, \sum_j \Phi_j$ | $\sum_i p_i = 1$ |
| Expected count | $E_i = N \cdot p_i$ | Real-valued |
| Crossover rate | $p_c$ | $0.6 \leq p_c \leq 0.9$ |
| Mutation rate | $p_m$ | $0.001 \leq p_m \leq 0.05$ |
| Convergence criterion | $\vert \bar{\Phi}_g - \Phi_{\text{best},g} \vert < \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = small threshold |
| Schema theorem (Holland) | $E[m(H, g+1)] \geq m(H, g) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | $m$ = schema count, $f(H)$ = avg fitness, $\delta(H)$ = defining length, $o(H)$ = order |

> [!NOTE]
> **Holland's Schema Theorem (Fundamental Theorem of Genetic Algorithms):** Short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata (building blocks) grow exponentially across generations. This is the **theoretical justification** for why GA works.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering, GAs are routinely used for:
- **CNC machining parameter optimization** (cutting speed, feed, depth) to minimize surface roughness and tool wear.
- **Job-shop scheduling** where the solution space is discrete and NP-hard.
- **Supply chain network design** with non-convex cost functions.

The derivative-free nature is the decisive advantage — most real manufacturing problems have discontinuous, noisy objective functions where calculus fails.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Classic KTU-Style Question)

**Problem:** Maximize the function $f(x) = x^2$ over the integer domain $x \in [0, 31]$ using a Genetic Algorithm with a population size of $N = 4$ and 5-bit binary encoding.

### Step 1 — Initialization

Randomly generate 4 chromosomes (each of length 5 bits):

| Chromosome | Binary $b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ | Integer $x$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $C_1$ | 0 1 1 0 1 | 13 |
| $C_2$ | 1 1 0 0 0 | 24 |
| $C_3$ | 0 1 0 0 0 | 8 |
| $C_4$ | 1 0 0 1 1 | 19 |

### Step 2 — Fitness Evaluation

Apply $f(x) = x^2$:

$$\Phi(C_1) = 13^2 = 169$$
$$\Phi(C_2) = 24^2 = 576$$
$$\Phi(C_3) = 8^2 = 64$$
$$\Phi(C_4) = 19^2 = 361$$

Total population fitness:

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = 169 + 576 + 64 + 361 = 1170$$

### Step 3 — Selection Probabilities (Roulette Wheel)

$$p_i = \frac{\Phi_i}{\Phi_{\text{total}}}$$

$$p_1 = \frac{169}{1170} \approx 0.1444$$
$$p_2 = \frac{576}{1170} \approx 0.4923$$
$$p_3 = \frac{64}{1170} \approx 0.0547$$
$$p_4 = \frac{361}{1170} \approx 0.3085$$

Verification: $0.1444 + 0.4923 + 0.0547 + 0.3085 = 1.0000$ ✓

### Step 4 — Expected Count

$$E_i = N \cdot p_i = 4 \cdot p_i$$

$$E_1 = 4 \times 0.1444 = 0.5778$$
$$E_2 = 4 \times 0.4923 = 1.9692$$
$$E_3 = 4 \times 0.0547 = 0.2188$$
$$E_4 = 4 \times 0.3085 = 1.2342$$

Using stochastic sampling (assume actual drawn counts): $C_2$ selected twice, $C_4$ selected twice. Mating pool:

$$\text{Mating Pool} = \{C_2, C_2, C_4, C_4\} = \{11000, 11000, 10011, 10011\}$$

### Step 5 — Crossover (Single-Point at Position 3)

Pair 1: $11000$ and $10011$ → cut at position 3 (between bit 2 and bit 3):

$$\text{Parent 1: } 110\,\vert\,00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 1: } 110\,11 = 11011$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } 100\,\vert\,11 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 2: } 100\,00 = 10000$$

Pair 2: $11000$ and $10011$ → cut at position 2:

$$\text{Parent 1: } 11\,\vert\,000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 3: } 11\,011 = 11011$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } 10\,\vert\,011 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 4: } 10\,000 = 10000$$

New generation after crossover:

$$\{11011, 10000, 11011, 10000\}$$

### Step 6 — Mutation

Apply mutation rate $p_m = 0.01$ per bit. Assume one bit flips: position 4 of Offspring 4 flips from $0 \rightarrow 1$:

$$10000 \;\xrightarrow{\text{mutate}}\; 10001$$

Final new generation:

$$\{11011, 10000, 11011, 10001\}$$

### Step 7 — Fitness of New Generation

| Chromosome | Integer $x$ | Fitness $\Phi = x^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 11011 | 27 | **729** |
| 10000 | 16 | 256 |
| 11011 | 27 | **729** |
| 10001 | 17 | 289 |

**Result:** Best fitness improved from $576$ (Generation 0) to **$729$** (Generation 1). The optimal $x = 31$ would give $f = 961$ — GA will continue evolving toward this.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Operator Matters

| Operator | Purpose | Failure if Removed |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection** | Exploits good solutions | Random walk, no progress |
| **Crossover** | Combines useful gene combinations | Population cannot mix building blocks |
| **Mutation** | Maintains genetic diversity | Premature convergence to local optima |
| **Elitism** | Preserves best solutions | Best-found solution can be lost |

## 3.3 Convergence Behavior — A Closed-Form Insight

For a simple GA, the expected best fitness after $G$ generations can be modeled approximately as:

$$\mathbb{E}[\Phi_{\text{best}}(G)] \approx \Phi_{\text{opt}} - \left(\Phi_{\text{opt}} - \Phi_0\right) \cdot e^{-\lambda G}$$

where $\lambda > 0$ depends on $p_c$, $p_m$, and population diversity. This exponential convergence is the *asymptotic guarantee* Holland's schema theorem provides.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Genetic Algorithm. List its key genetic operators and explain the role of the fitness function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a population-based metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the principles of natural selection and genetics. It evolves a population of candidate solutions through successive generations using stochastic operators until an optimal or near-optimal solution is found.

**Key Genetic Operators:**

1. **Selection** — Probabilistically chooses parent chromosomes based on fitness, biasing toward high-quality solutions (exploitation).
2. **Crossover (Recombination)** — Combines genes from two parent chromosomes to produce offspring, enabling the mixing of building blocks.
3. **Mutation** — Introduces small random changes in genes, maintaining population diversity and enabling escape from local optima.

**Role of the Fitness Function $\Phi$:** The fitness function quantitatively measures how good a chromosome's decoded solution is. It serves as the *sole feedback signal* guiding the evolutionary search — GA's entire behavior is driven by maximizing (or minimizing) this scalar function across generations. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Crossover and Mutation in Genetic Algorithms. Why is mutation rate kept low?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Crossover | Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Combines genes from two parents | Randomly alters individual genes |
| Probability | High ($p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$) | Very low ($p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$) |
| Operates on | Two parents → two offspring | One chromosome at a time |
| Function | **Exploration** of solution space by recombination | **Diversity preservation** and escape from local optima |

**Why mutation rate is kept low:** A high mutation rate would destroy good building blocks already discovered, effectively turning the GA into a random search. Mutation is intended to provide occasional, small perturbations that maintain diversity without corrupting the accumulated progress of selection and crossover. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**
Explain the step-by-step procedure of a Genetic Algorithm. Use a block diagram to illustrate the flow.

**Model Solution:**

The Genetic Algorithm proceeds through the following iterative stages in each generation:

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Generate an initial population $P_0$ of $N$ chromosomes. For binary encoding, each gene is independently drawn from $\{0, 1\}$ with equal probability. The initial population should be diverse to cover the search space broadly.

**Step 2 — Decoding:** Each chromosome is decoded from its encoded form into the problem domain (e.g., binary string → integer → real value via affine mapping).

**Step 3 — Fitness Evaluation:** Each chromosome is evaluated by the fitness function $\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$. Higher fitness implies better suitability to the objective.

**Step 4 — Selection:** A mating pool is formed by selecting chromosomes with probability proportional to fitness (Roulette Wheel), via tournament, or via rank. The expected number of copies of chromosome $i$ is:

$$E_i = N \cdot \frac{\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)}$$

**Step 5 — Crossover:** With probability $p_c$, pairs of parents are recombined at one or more cut-points. Single-point crossover at position $k$ produces two offspring by exchanging the gene segments after position $k$.

**Step 6 — Mutation:** Each gene in each offspring is flipped (in binary encoding) with small probability $p_m$. This maintains genetic diversity.

**Step 7 — Replacement:** The new generation replaces the old one, optionally preserving the top $e$ elite chromosomes.

**Step 8 — Termination Check:** The loop terminates when a stopping criterion is met (max generations, fitness threshold, or convergence plateau), and the best chromosome is output.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Start] → [Initialize Population]
       ↓
[Decode] → [Evaluate Fitness]
       ↓
[Termination Met?] — Yes → [Output Best]
       ↓ No
[Selection] → [Crossover] → [Mutation]
       ↓
[New Generation] → back to [Decode]
```

**(7 marks — 1 mark per key step + 2 marks for diagram and flow coherence)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**
For the optimization problem **Maximize $f(x) = x^2$, where $x \in [0, 31]$**, perform **one generation** of a Genetic Algorithm with population size $N = 4$ and the following initial population:

| Chromosome | Binary |
|:---:|:---:|
| $C_1$ | 0 1 1 0 0 |
| $C_2$ | 1 0 1 0 1 |
| $C_3$ | 1 1 0 0 1 |
| $C_4$ | 0 1 0 1 0 |

Use **Roulette Wheel selection**, **single-point crossover at position 3**, and **mutation rate $p_m = 0.05$** (one bit flip only).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decode and Evaluate Fitness:**

$$\Phi(C_1) = f(12) = 144$$
$$\Phi(C_2) = f(21) = 441$$
$$\Phi(C_3) = f(25) = 625$$
$$\Phi(C_4) = f(10) = 100$$

Total fitness:

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = 144 + 441 + 625 + 100 = 1310$$

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities:**

$$p_1 = \frac{144}{1310} \approx 0.1099$$
$$p_2 = \frac{441}{1310} \approx 0.3366$$
$$p_3 = \frac{625}{1310} \approx 0.4771$$
$$p_4 = \frac{100}{1310} \approx 0.0763$$

**Expected counts:**

$$E_1 = 0.4396, \quad E_2 = 1.3466, \quad E_3 = 1.9084, \quad E_4 = 0.3053$$

**Stochastic selection outcome (illustrative):** Assume mating pool = $\{C_3, C_3, C_2, C_1\}$ = $\{11001, 11001, 10101, 01100\}$.

**Step 3 — Crossover at Position 3:**

Pair 1: $C_3$ and $C_3$ → no variation possible → Offspring 1 = 11001, Offspring 2 = 11001

Pair 2: $C_2 = 10101$ and $C_1 = 01100$:

$$\text{Parent 1: } 101\,\vert\,01 \Rightarrow \text{Offspring 3: } 101\,00 = 10100$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } 011\,\vert\,00 \Rightarrow \text{Offspring 4: } 011\,01 = 01101$$

**Step 4 — Mutation:** Flip one bit at position 0 of Offspring 4: $01101 \rightarrow 01100$.

**Step 5 — New Generation Fitness:**

| Chromosome | Integer | Fitness |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 11001 | 25 | 625 |
| 11001 | 25 | 625 |
| 10100 | 20 | 400 |
| 01100 | 12 | 144 |

**Observation:** The best fitness is preserved (625 = optimum at this generation), and the average fitness increases. Continuation across generations will converge toward $x^* = 31$, $f(31) = 961$.

**(7 marks — distributed across decoding, fitness, selection, crossover, mutation, and result interpretation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
Discuss in detail the various **selection mechanisms** in Genetic Algorithms. Compare Roulette Wheel selection with Tournament selection.

**Model Solution:**

Selection is the operator that biases the search toward high-fitness regions of the solution space. The principal selection schemes are:

**1. Roulette Wheel Selection (Fitness-Proportionate):**
Each chromosome occupies a slice of a "wheel" sized proportional to its fitness. Spinning the wheel $N$ times produces the mating pool.

Probability:

$$p_i = \frac{\Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)}$$

**Advantages:** Simple, intuitive, classical. **Disadvantages:** Premature convergence when one fitness dominates; requires non-negative fitness; scaling-dependent.

**2. Tournament Selection:**
Pick $k$ chromosomes uniformly at random; select the one with highest fitness. Repeat $N$ times.

**Advantages:** No scaling issues, works with negative fitness, simple to implement, tunable selection pressure via $k$. **Disadvantages:** Slightly more computation per draw.

**3. Rank-Based Selection:**
Chromosomes are sorted by fitness; the $i$-th best chromosome gets selection probability:

$$p_i = \frac{2 \cdot s}{N(N+1)} \cdot i$$

where $s$ is a selection pressure parameter.

**Advantages:** Removes scaling sensitivity; stable selection pressure.

**4. Truncation Selection:**
Only the top $T\%$ of chromosomes are allowed to reproduce.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Roulette Wheel | Tournament ($k=2$) |
|---|---|---|
| Selection pressure | Fitness-dependent | Tunable via $k$ |
| Sensitivity to scaling | High | None |
| Premature convergence risk | High (when fitness spikes) | Moderate |
| Negative fitness handling | Requires shift | Direct |
| Computational cost | $O(N)$ for wheel construction | $O(k)$ per draw |

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
Explain **encoding schemes** in Genetic Algorithms. Compare binary, real-valued, and permutation encodings with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

Encoding maps problem variables into chromosome representations. Choice of encoding determines which genetic operators are applicable.

**1. Binary Encoding:**
Each variable is represented as a fixed-length bit string. A 5-bit string represents integers $0$–$31$; for real-valued variables, the integer is mapped via:

$$x = L_b + \frac{(U_b - L_b)}{2^L - 1} \cdot \text{decoded integer}$$

**Example:** Chromosome `10110` (integer 22) representing $x \in [0, 3.1]$:

$$x = 0 + \frac{3.1}{31} \cdot 22 = 2.20$$

**Use:** Continuous variable optimization. **Operators:** Bit-flip mutation, single/multi-point crossover.

**2. Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding:**
Each gene is a real number $g_i \in [L_i, U_i]$. Mutation uses Gaussian perturbation:

$$g_i' = g_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma)$$

**Use:** High-dimensional continuous optimization where binary precision is inadequate.

**3. Permutation Encoding:**
Chromosome is a permutation of integers $1, 2, \dots, n$.

**Example:** Chromosome `[3, 1, 4, 2, 5]` represents the order of 5 jobs in a scheduling problem.

**Use:** TSP, job-shop scheduling, assignment problems. **Operators:** Order Crossover (OX), Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX), swap mutation.

**Comparison:**

| Encoding | Problem Type | Mutation | Crossover |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binary | Discrete / low-dim continuous | Bit flip | Single/two-point, uniform |
| Real-valued | Continuous high-dim | Gaussian perturbation | BLX-α, SBX |
| Permutation | Combinatorial ordering | Swap, insert, invert | OX, PMX |

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Fitness probability sum must equal 1.0000** — forgetting to normalize leads to a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Selection probability ≠ Expected count** — many students confuse $p_i$ with $E_i$. Expected count uses population size $N$.
> 3. **Cut-point indexing** — single-point crossover at position $k$ means the cut lies *between* genes $k-1$ and $k$. A 5-bit string has cut-points $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ only (never 0 or 5).
> 4. **Binary-to-integer decoding uses LSB-first convention:** $b_0$ is the least significant bit. Reversing this is a frequent error.
> 5. **Holland's Schema Theorem** — examiners often ask for the *intuitive meaning*, not the full equation. Memorize: "short, low-order, high-fitness building blocks grow exponentially."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a **population-based, stochastic metaheuristic** mimicking Darwinian evolution.
- The five core elements are: **population, fitness function, selection, crossover, mutation.**
- **Roulette Wheel probability:** $p_i = \Phi_i / \sum_j \Phi_j$ ; sum must be 1.
- **Expected count:** $E_i = N \cdot p_i$.
- **Binary decoding:** $x = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ (LSB-first).
- **Real-valued decoding:** $x = L_b + \frac{(U_b - L_b)}{2^L - 1} \cdot \text{integer}$.
- **Crossover rate** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ (high); **mutation rate** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ (low).
- **Single-point crossover** at position $k$ splits between gene $k-1$ and $k$.
- **Tournament selection** is preferred over Roulette Wheel when fitness scaling is uneven.
- **Holland's Schema Theorem** = fundamental theorem explaining *why* GA works.
- **Elitism** preserves the best solution across generations — always recommended.
- GA is **derivative-free** → ideal for non-convex, discontinuous, multimodal problems.
- Common failure modes: **premature convergence** (mitigated by mutation + rank selection), **slow convergence** (mitigated by elitism + tuning $p_c$, $p_m$).
- Termination criteria: **max generations**, **fitness threshold**, **convergence plateau**, or **computational budget**.
- KTU 2024 Scheme weightage: this topic typically carries **7–14 marks** in Part B and **3 marks** in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Application Areas** of heuristics and metaheuristics refer to the broad spectrum of real-world problem domains in which approximate, nature-inspired, or rule-of-thumb optimization techniques are applied to find high-quality (near-optimal) solutions within computationally tractable time limits. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PEIET634 - Heuristics for Decision Making**), Module 4 consolidates the practical deployment landscape of algorithms studied in Modules 1–3 (Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms, Ant Colony Optimization, Particle Swarm Optimization, Tabu Search, and Hybrid Heuristics) into recognized industrial, scientific, and managerial problem archetypes.

A heuristic application is characterized by **NP-hard combinatorial complexity**, **non-convex search spaces**, or **computationally expensive objective functions** where exact methods such as Branch-and-Bound, Dynamic Programming, or Integer Linear Programming become intractable for realistic problem sizes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to map each heuristic technique to its **most appropriate industrial application** and justify the selection based on problem characteristics such as **discreteness, constraint structure, objective modality, and evaluation cost**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hiker lost in a dense, fog-covered mountain range at night** who must reach the valley below before sunrise. The hiker cannot see the entire topography (cannot evaluate the global landscape), but can feel the slope beneath their boots (local information). A heuristic is analogous to a **decision rule** such as "always step in the direction of steepest descent." 

Different terrains demand different rules:
- **Smooth convex hills** → Gradient descent (deterministic heuristic) works well.
- **Rocky, crater-filled terrain with multiple valleys** → A metaheuristic like **Simulated Annealing** (occasionally accepting uphill moves to escape local minima) is required.
- **Team of hikers sharing information** → **Swarm Intelligence** (PSO, ACO).
- **Survival-of-the-fittest generation of trails** → **Genetic Algorithms**.

Each application area corresponds to a specific "terrain type" — and selecting the correct heuristic is the art of recognizing the terrain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Heuristic vs. Metaheuristic Application:**
> - A *heuristic* is **problem-specific** (e.g., 2-opt for TSP).
> - A *metaheuristic* is **problem-independent** (e.g., GA applied to scheduling, routing, and feature selection alike).

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU Board Favourite)

| Application Metric | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem Size (n)** | Number of decision variables | $10^1$ – $10^6$ |
| **Search Space Cardinality** | Total feasible/infeasible solutions | Exponential in $n$ |
| **Constraint Density ($\rho$)** | Ratio of active constraints to variables | $0 < \rho < \infty$ |
| **Objective Modality** | Number of local optima | Uni-modal, Multi-modal |
| **Evaluation Cost ($T_e$)** | Wall-clock time per solution evaluation | $\mu$s – hours |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Application Areas (KTU Module 4 Framework)

The application landscape is taxonomically organized into **nine core domains**, each with characteristic problem formulations and dominant heuristic preferences.

### 2.1.1 Combinatorial Optimization Problems

These are discrete decision problems where the solution set is finite but exponentially large. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies the following:

**a) Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**
- **Problem:** Find the shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting $n$ cities exactly once.
- **Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\min \; Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$

Subject to:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

$$
x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \sum_{i,j \in S} x_{ij} \leq \vert S \vert - 1, \; \forall S \subset V, \; 2 \leq \vert S \vert \leq n-2
$$

- **Preferred Heuristics:** **2-opt, 3-opt** (local search), **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)**, **Genetic Algorithm (GA) with OX crossover**.

**b) Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**
- **Problem:** Determine optimal routes for a fleet of vehicles serving a set of customers from a central depot, minimizing total distance subject to vehicle capacity $Q$.
- **Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\min \; Z = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{(i,j) \in R_k} d_{ij}
$$

Subject to:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{j \in \Delta^{+}(i)} x_{ijk} = 1, \quad \forall i \in C \; (\text{customers})
$$

$$
\sum_{i \in C} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q, \quad \forall k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}
$$

where $q_i$ is the demand of customer $i$ and $y_{ik}$ is a binary assignment variable.

- **Preferred Heuristics:** **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm**, **Tabu Search**, **ACO with pheromone decay $\rho = 0.1$ to $0.5$**.

**c) Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP)**
- **Problem:** Schedule $n$ jobs on $m$ machines such that each operation uses one machine for a fixed duration, no two operations of the same job overlap, and no machine processes two jobs simultaneously.
- **Makespan Objective:**

$$
\min \; C_{max} = \max_{j \in J} \{C_j\}
$$

where $C_j$ is the completion time of job $j$.

- **Preferred Heuristics:** **Giffler-Thompson Priority Rule**, **GA with permutation encoding**, **Simulated Annealing with geometric cooling $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ where $\alpha = 0.95$**.

### 2.1.2 Engineering Design Optimization

Heuristic methods are extensively applied to:
- **Structural optimization:** Truss design, topology optimization.
- **Aerodynamic shape optimization:** Airfoil design using CFD-coupled GAs.
- **VLSI circuit partitioning:** Min-cut placement using Simulated Annealing (TimberWolf algorithm).

The typical objective function involves **non-convex, multi-modal landscapes** with expensive function evaluations ($T_e \in$ minutes to hours), making **surrogate-model-assisted evolutionary algorithms** essential.

### 2.1.3 Machine Learning and Data Mining

| Sub-Area | Heuristic Role | Common Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Selection** | Reduce dimensionality $d$ from $D$ to $d' \ll D$ | GA, PSO, Binary Differential Evolution |
| **Hyperparameter Tuning** | Optimize learning rate, depth, regularization | Bayesian Optimization, GA, PSO |
| **Neural Architecture Search (NAS)** | Search over network topology space | Evolutionary NAS, Reinforcement Learning |
| **Clustering** | Optimize partition validity indices | K-means + GA, PSO-based clustering |

### 2.1.4 Bioinformatics and Computational Biology

- **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA):** Align $k$ sequences of length $L$ optimizing sum-of-pairs score.
- **Protein Structure Prediction:** Predict 3D fold from amino acid sequence — search space is astronomically large ($10^{300}$ conformations for a 100-residue protein).
- **Phylogenetic Tree Construction:** Optimize parsimony or maximum likelihood score.
- **Preferred Heuristics:** **ACO for MSA**, **GA for protein folding**, **Memetic Algorithms for phylogeny**.

### 2.1.5 Financial and Economic Decision Making

- **Portfolio Optimization** (Markowitz extension):

$$
\max \; \mu_p - \lambda \sigma_p^2, \quad \sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}
$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the asset weight vector, $\mu_p$ is expected return, $\sigma_p^2$ is variance, $\lambda$ is the risk aversion coefficient, and $\Sigma$ is the covariance matrix. **Cardinality and budget constraints** make this NP-hard.

- **Algorithmic Trading:** GA/PSO for parameter optimization of trading strategies.
- **Credit Scoring:** Feature selection via GA on borrower datasets.

### 2.1.6 Network Design and Telecommunications

- **Network Topology Design:** Minimize cost while meeting QoS constraints.
- **Frequency Assignment:** Minimize interference in cellular networks.
- **Routing in Ad-hoc and Sensor Networks:** ACO/PSO for energy-efficient paths.

### 2.1.7 Supply Chain and Logistics

- **Warehouse location-allocation** problems.
- **Inventory replenishment** with stochastic demand.
- **Multi-echelon distribution network** design.

### 2.1.8 Image and Signal Processing

- **Image segmentation:** GA-optimized thresholding (Otsu's method hybridization).
- **Filter design:** PSO-tuned FIR/IIR filter coefficients.
- **Compressed sensing:** Sparse signal recovery.

### 2.1.9 Game Playing and Artificial Intelligence

- **Game tree search** beyond depth $d$: Alpha-Beta pruning is a heuristic, while **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)** is a metaheuristic.
- **Real-time strategy games:** Evolutionary strategies for unit positioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The selection of a heuristic for an application follows the **No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem** — no single algorithm is universally superior. Therefore, the application area dictates algorithm selection through empirical benchmarking.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Problem Type | Objective Function | Key Constraint | Recommended Heuristic | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TSP | Minimize tour length $\sum d_{ij}x_{ij}$ | Subtour elimination | ACO, 2-opt, GA-OX | $O(n^2 \cdot \text{iter})$ |
| VRP | Minimize total route distance | Capacity $Q$, time windows | Clarke-Wright + Tabu | $O(n^2 \log n)$ |
| JSSP | Minimize makespan $C_{max}$ | Machine precedence | GA, SA, Giffler-Thompson | $O(m \cdot n^2)$ |
| Knapsack | Max $\sum v_i x_i$ | $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$ | GA-binary, PSO-binary | $O(n \cdot \text{pop} \cdot \text{iter})$ |
| Portfolio | Max $\mu_p - \lambda\sigma_p^2$ | $\sum w_i = 1$, cardinality $K$ | NSGA-II, MOEA/D | $O(N^2 \cdot \text{iter})$ |
| Truss Design | Minimize weight $W$ | Stress $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | GA-real-coded, DE | $O(\text{iter} \cdot T_e)$ |
| Feature Selection | Max accuracy | Select $k$ of $D$ features | Binary GA, Binary PSO | $O(2^D \cdot \text{iter})$ |
| Protein Folding | Minimize energy $E$ | Bond geometry | Memetic Algorithm | $O(L^{3} \cdot \text{iter})$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TSP Solution Construction via Ant Colony Optimization

This derivation shows how ACO — a metaheuristic covered in earlier modules — is **applied** to the canonical TSP problem (a core Module 4 application area).

### Step 1: Initialize Pheromone Matrix
Each edge $(i, j)$ in the complete graph $G = (V, E)$ receives an initial pheromone value:

$$
\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0 = \frac{1}{n \cdot L_{nn}}
$$

where $L_{nn}$ is the length of a tour produced by the **nearest-neighbor heuristic** (a constructive heuristic application).

### Step 2: Construct Probabilistic Transition Rule
Ant $k$ at city $i$ selects the next city $j$ with probability:

$$
P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}, \quad j \in N_i^{k}
$$

where:
- $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ is the **heuristic desirability** (visibility),
- $\alpha \geq 0$ controls **pheromone influence**,
- $\beta \geq 0$ controls **distance influence**,
- $N_i^{k}$ is the feasible neighborhood.

### Step 3: Local Pheromone Update
After each ant moves across edge $(i,j)$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \rho \cdot \tau_0
$$

where $\rho \in (0, 1]$ is the local evaporation rate (typically $\rho = 0.1$).

### Step 4: Global Pheromone Update (Best-Ant)
After all ants complete their tours in iteration $t$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+n) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ used edge } (i,j) \text{ in its tour} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $L_k$ is the tour length of ant $k$ and $Q$ is a constant.

### Step 5: Termination
Iterate Steps 2–4 until the maximum number of iterations $t_{max}$ is reached or **convergence criterion** is met:

$$
\frac{\vert L_{best}(t) - L_{best}(t-10) \vert}{L_{best}(t-10)} < \epsilon = 10^{-6}
$$

### Final Solution
The best tour $T^*$ is the shortest among all global-best updates:

$$
T^* = \arg\min_{t \in [1, t_{max}]} L_{best}(t)
$$

The following matrix provides the **KTU examiner's reference** for matching heuristics to application areas. This is a high-yield visual for the 14-mark question.

| Application Area | Primary Heuristic | Secondary Heuristic | Why This Choice? |
|---|---|---|---|
| TSP / VRP | **ACO** | GA, 2-opt | Graph-based, pheromone trails mimic shortest path reinforcement |
| Job-Shop Scheduling | **GA** | SA, TS | Permutation encoding handles precedence naturally |
| Portfolio Optimization | **NSGA-II** | PSO | Multi-objective (return vs. risk) needs Pareto front |
| Feature Selection | **Binary GA** | Binary PSO | Discrete binary decision space |
| Neural Architecture Search | **Evolutionary NAS** | RL | Discrete topology, expensive evaluation |
| Truss Optimization | **Real-coded GA** | DE | Continuous variables, real-valued design |
| Protein Folding | **Memetic Algorithm** | SA | Hybrid local + global search for rugged landscape |
| Image Segmentation | **PSO-Otsu** | GA | Multi-modal threshold optimization |
| Network Routing | **ACO** | PSO | Dynamic graph environment |
| Game Tree Search | **MCTS** | Minimax + α-β | Stochastic, large branching factor |
| Bin Packing | **First-Fit Decreasing** | GA | Greedy heuristic often near-optimal for $n \leq 50$ |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Apply a single iteration of the **2-opt heuristic** to a TSP tour. Given the distance matrix below, identify the improving 2-opt move.

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Current Tour:** $T_0 = 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$, with length $L(T_0) = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all 2-opt swaps:** There are $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ possible non-adjacent edge pairs.

**Step 2 — Evaluate reverse move 2-opt(2,4):** Remove edges $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$, replace with $(1,3)$ and $(2,4)$. Reversing segment $\{2,3\}$ yields new tour:

$$
T_1 = 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute new length:**

$$
L(T_1) = d_{13} + d_{32} + d_{24} + d_{41} = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95
$$

**Step 4 — Try 2-opt(2,3):** Remove $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$, replace with $(1,3)$ and $(2,4)$:

$$
T_2 = 1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2 \to 1
$$

$$
L(T_2) = 15 + 30 + 25 + 10 = 80
$$

**Step 5 — Selection:** Since $L(T_2) = 80 < 95 = L(T_0)$, accept move 2-opt(2,3). Net improvement $\Delta = 15$ units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Insight:** When two non-adjacent edges are swapped, the segment between them is **reversed**. This is the defining property of the 2-opt move.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**List and briefly explain three major application areas of heuristic methods in engineering decision making.**

**Model Answer:**

Three major application areas are:

1. **Combinatorial Optimization — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):** Finding the shortest tour visiting $n$ cities. Heuristics like 2-opt, ACO, and GA are widely used since exact enumeration requires $O((n-1)!)$ operations.

2. **Engineering Design Optimization:** Real-coded Genetic Algorithms and Differential Evolution optimize continuous design variables in trusses, airfoils, and mechanical components where exact gradient-based methods fail due to non-convexity.

3. **Machine Learning — Feature Selection:** Binary GAs and PSO search the $2^D$ subset space to identify the most discriminative $d' \ll D$ features, minimizing classifier error and computational cost.

(Any 3 valid applications with brief explanations — 1 mark per application)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]
**Why is heuristic optimization preferred over exact methods for NP-hard scheduling problems?**

**Model Answer:**

Heuristic optimization is preferred for NP-hard scheduling problems (e.g., Job-Shop Scheduling) because:

1. **Exponential Complexity:** Exact methods like Branch-and-Bound exhibit worst-case time complexity $O(b^d)$ where $b$ is the branching factor. For $n$ jobs on $m$ machines, the search space is $\frac{(nm)!}{\text{symmetry}}$.

2. **Polynomial-Time Approximability:** Metaheuristics like Genetic Algorithms and Simulated Annealing deliver near-optimal solutions in $O(n \cdot \text{pop} \cdot \text{iter})$ time, which is tractable for industrial-scale instances.

3. **Robustness to Landscape Modality:** Metaheuristics incorporate mechanisms (mutation, crossover, probabilistic acceptance) that escape local optima, unlike greedy priority rules that may get trapped.

(3 marks: 1 mark for complexity, 1 for tractability, 1 for robustness)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Formulate the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** as a mathematical optimization model. Identify the decision variables, objective function, and all constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe how **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is applied to solve the TSP. Provide the pheromone update rule and the probabilistic transition rule with all parameter definitions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mathematical Formulation of TSP:**

**Decision Variables:**

$$
x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if edge } (i, j) \text{ is included in the tour} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Objective Function:** Minimize the total tour length:

$$
\min \; Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$

**Constraints:**

- **Degree Constraints** (each city has exactly one predecessor and one successor):

$$
\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

- **Subtour Elimination Constraints** (Dantzig-Fulkerson-Johnson formulation):

$$
\sum_{i, j \in S} x_{ij} \leq \vert S \vert - 1, \quad \forall S \subset V, \; 2 \leq \vert S \vert \leq n-2
$$

- **Binary Constraint:**

$$
x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall i, j
$$

**Part (b) — ACO Application to TSP:**

ACO simulates artificial ants depositing pheromone on edges. Each ant $k$ at city $i$ selects the next city $j$ with probability:

$$
P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

where:
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ — pheromone concentration on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ — heuristic information (inverse distance).
- $\alpha$ — pheromone weight (typical: $\alpha = 1$).
- $\beta$ — heuristic weight (typical: $\beta = 2$ to $5$).
- $N_i^{k}$ — set of unvisited cities for ant $k$ at city $i$.

**Pheromone Evaporation and Deposition:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

where $\rho \in (0, 1]$ is the evaporation rate and:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed edge } (i, j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Algorithm Summary:** (1) Initialize pheromone, (2) Construct ant solutions probabilistically, (3) Apply local pheromone update, (4) Evaluate tours, (5) Apply global pheromone update (best-so-far ant), (6) Repeat until convergence.

**(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Supplementary Exam 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the application of **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** in solving the **Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP)**. Explain chromosome encoding, fitness function, and the OX crossover operator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs)** are used in **Portfolio Optimization**. Formulate the bi-objective problem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — GA for JSSP:**

**Problem:** Schedule $n$ jobs on $m$ machines, each with a defined operation sequence, minimizing the makespan $C_{max}$.

**Chromosome Encoding (Permutation with Repeats):** A chromosome is a sequence of $n \cdot m$ integers, each integer from $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ representing a job, with each job appearing exactly $m$ times (one for each operation). Example for 3 jobs, 3 machines: $(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3)$.

**Decoding:** The $k$-th occurrence of job $j$ in the chromosome corresponds to its $k$-th operation. Operations are scheduled in order using a **Gantt-chart-based** simulation.

**Fitness Function:** $f = \frac{1}{C_{max} + 1}$ or equivalently minimize $C_{max}$.

**Order Crossover (OX) Operator:**

1. Select two cut points $c_1$ and $c_2$ in parent chromosomes.
2. Copy the segment between $c_1$ and $c_2$ from Parent 1 to Child 1.
3. Starting from $c_2$, copy the remaining elements from Parent 2 in their order, wrapping around, skipping those already in the segment.
4. Repeat symmetrically for Child 2.

**Example:**
- Parent 1: $(1, 2, \vert 3, 1, 2, \vert 3, 1, 2, 3)$
- Parent 2: $(2, 1, \vert 3, 2, 1, \vert 3, 2, 1, 3)$
- Cut points between positions 3 and 6.
- Child 1 segment: $(3, 1, 2)$ from Parent 1. Fill remaining from Parent 2: $(3, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1)$ → Child 1: $(1, 3, \vert 3, 1, 2, \vert 3, 2, 1, 3)$.

**Part (b) — MOEA for Portfolio Optimization:**

**Bi-Objective Formulation:**

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; f_1(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \mu_i
$$

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}} \; f_2(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}
$$

Subject to:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i = 1, \quad 0 \leq w_i \leq 1, \quad w_i \in \{0, 0.05, 0.10, \ldots, 1\}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_N)^T$ is the expected return vector and $\Sigma$ is the $N \times N$ covariance matrix.

**MOEA Application:** NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II) is the most widely applied algorithm. It uses:
1. **Non-dominated sorting** to rank solutions into Pareto fronts.
2. **Crowding distance** to preserve diversity along the front.
3. **Binary tournament selection** based on rank and crowding distance.

**Output:** A Pareto front of portfolios representing the risk-return trade-off, from which the decision-maker selects the most preferred portfolio.

**(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing heuristic with metaheuristic:** A 1-mark deduction per occurrence. Remember — *heuristic = problem-specific*, *metaheuristic = problem-independent*.
> 2. **Forgetting the subtour elimination constraint** in TSP formulation: Costs **2 marks** in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Mixing up pheromone update (ACO) with position update (PSO):** Pheromone **decreases** via evaporation $(1 - \rho)$; position is **shifted** via velocity. Wrong equation = 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not specifying parameters** ($\alpha, \beta, \rho, Q$) in the ACO transition rule: KTU board deducts 1 mark for "incomplete mathematical formulation."
> 5. **Writing the OX crossover steps in wrong order:** The "fill from other parent starting at second cut point, wrapping around" must be explicit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 4: Application Areas**

- ☐ **Heuristic vs. Metaheuristic:** Heuristic = problem-specific (e.g., 2-opt, Clarke-Wright); Metaheuristic = problem-independent (e.g., GA, SA, ACO, PSO, TS).
- ☐ **Nine Core Application Domains:** Combinatorial Optimization, Engineering Design, ML/Data Mining, Bioinformatics, Finance, Networks, Supply Chain, Image Processing, Game AI.
- ☐ **TSP Formulation:** Decision variable $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$, minimize $\sum d_{ij} x_{ij}$, subject to degree + subtour elimination constraints.
- ☐ **VRP Variant:** Adds capacity $Q$ constraint; uses Clarke-Wright Savings + Tabu Search.
- ☐ **JSSP Encoding:** Permutation with repeats; OX crossover preserves relative order.
- ☐ **ACO for TSP:** Transition rule uses $\tau^{\alpha} \eta^{\beta}$; pheromone evaporates by $(1-\rho)$; global update rewards best ant.
- ☐ **GA Operators:** Selection (tournament/roulette), Crossover (single-point/OX/PMX), Mutation (bit-flip/gaussian).
- ☐ **Portfolio Optimization:** Bi-objective (return vs. risk); solved by NSGA-II for Pareto front.
- ☐ **Feature Selection:** Binary GA/PSO searches $2^D$ space; fitness = classifier accuracy - $\lambda \cdot (\text{features selected})$.
- ☐ **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No single heuristic dominates across all problem instances.
- ☐ **Engineering Utility:** Hybrid heuristics (memetic algorithms) combine global metaheuristic search with local refinement for NP-hard problems.
- ☐ **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing "GA is a heuristic" instead of "GA is a **metaheuristic** that can be **applied as a heuristic** to specific problems."
