## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The General Formulation Procedure (Algebraic Derivation)

Let a real-world problem be given. The CP formulation proceeds in **5 deterministic steps**:

**Step 1 — Identify Decision Variables.**
List every quantity whose value must be decided. Call this set $X = \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$.

**Step 2 — Identify Domains.**
For each $X_i$, determine the set of permissible values. The choice of domain *encoding* is itself a modelling decision. For example, a binary decision (yes/no) is naturally encoded as $D_i = \{0,1\}$, but a day-of-week decision uses $D_i = \{\text{Mon}, \text{Tue}, \dots, \text{Sun}\}$.

**Step 3 — Express Constraints.**
Translate every business rule into a logical formula over the variables. Group constraints by arity:
- Unary: restrictions on a single variable.
- Binary / n-ary: joint restrictions.
- Global: aggregate restrictions best captured by special predicates.

**Step 4 — Identify the Objective (for COP).**
If the problem asks for the *best* solution, define $f(X)$. If feasibility alone is required, omit $f$.

**Step 5 — Build the Constraint Graph.**
Construct the graph $G = (X, E)$ where an edge exists between $X_i$ and $X_j$ iff they co-occur in some constraint. This graph drives propagation order and identifies loosely connected components (which can be solved independently).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Map Colouring of Kerala Districts (4-Colour Problem)

**Problem (verbal):**
Colour the four districts of a fictional Kerala map — **Kochi (K), Thrissur (T), Kozhikode (Z), Kollam (L)** — using the colours *Red, Blue, Green, Yellow* such that no two adjacent districts share a colour. Adjacencies: K-T, T-Z, Z-L, K-L.

**Step 1 — Variables:**
$X = \{X_K, X_T, X_X, X_L\}$ — one variable per district, where the subscript $X$ stands for Kozhikode (using $Z$ may clash with another variable). For uniformity, we use the district initials.

**Step 2 — Domains (identical):**
For all $i \in \{K, T, X, L\}$:

$$
D_i = \{\text{Red}, \text{Blue}, \text{Green}, \text{Yellow}\}
$$

Therefore the **upper bound on the naive search space** is:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{4} \vert D_i \vert = 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 4^4 = 256
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints (binary inequality):**

$$
C = \{ X_K \neq X_T, \; X_T \neq X_X, \; X_X \neq X_L, \; X_K \neq X_L \}
$$

These can also be expressed as the global constraint:

$$
\text{alldifferent}(X_K, X_L)
$$

because K and L are adjacent, but the other pairs are not in a 4-clique, so we still need the binary inequalities.

**Step 4 — Objective:**
None — this is a pure CSP.

**Step 5 — Constraint Graph:**

| District pair | Edge weight |
|---|---|
| (K, T) | 1 |
| (T, X) | 1 |
| (X, L) | 1 |
| (K, L) | 1 |

**Step 6 — Solve via AC propagation (illustrative trace):**

Initial domains: each district has 4 colours.

After enforcing **Arc Consistency**:
- Consider edge (K, T). Each value of $X_K$ has a *support* in $D_T$ (different value exists), so no pruning.
- Same holds for all four edges — domains remain size 4 each.

So AC alone does not prune. We must **search**:

1. Assign $X_K = \text{Red}$.
2. Propagate: $X_T \in \{\text{Blue, Green, Yellow}\}$, $X_L \in \{\text{Blue, Green, Yellow}\}$.
3. Assign $X_T = \text{Blue}$.
4. Propagate: $X_X \in \{\text{Red, Green, Yellow}\}$.
5. Assign $X_X = \text{Green}$.
6. Propagate: $X_L \in \{\text{Blue, Yellow}\}$ (since $X_L \neq X_K = \text{Red}$ and $X_L \neq X_X = \text{Green}$).

The final feasible assignment is:

$$
X_K = \text{Red}, \quad X_T = \text{Blue}, \quad X_X = \text{Green}, \quad X_L = \text{Yellow}
$$

which satisfies every constraint. Hence $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \geq 1$.

In fact, by symmetry of colours, the total number of solutions is $4! / 1 = 24$ (since the graph has no automorphisms other than identity).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Formulation Decision Checklist

Use this matrix when the examiner gives a word problem:

| Question to ask | KTU formulation element |
|---|---|
| *What must be decided?* | Variables $X_i$ |
| *What values are allowed?* | Domain $D_i$ |
| *What rules must hold?* | Constraints $C_j$ |
| *Is there a "best" being sought?* | Objective $f(X)$ → COP |
| *Are variables arranged in time/space?* | Add index structure (e.g., $X_{i,t}$) |
| *Is there resource capacity?* | Use `cumulative` global constraint |
| *Is there permutation/all-different?* | Use `alldifferent` global constraint |
| *Are there redundant symmetries?* | Add symmetry-breaking constraints |

---

### 3.4 Bound on Search Tree Size — Exhaustive Derivation

If all variables are binary ($D_i = \{0,1\}$) and there are $n$ variables with no constraints, the total number of leaves is:

$$
T_{\max} = 2^n
$$

When arc consistency reduces each domain by a factor $\alpha$ ($0 < \alpha \leq 1$) **per propagation step** and propagation is applied $k$ times during the search:

$$
T_{\text{reduced}} \leq (2 \cdot \alpha)^n
$$

For the worked map-colouring example with $n = 4$:

$$
T_{\max} = 4^4 = 256
$$

After perfect propagation (hypothetically) at each level:

$$
T_{\text{reduced}} \approx (4 \cdot 0.75)^4 = 3^4 = 81
$$

A reduction by a factor of $256 / 81 \approx 3.16\times$ — modest for this tiny instance, but **exponentially valuable** as $n$ grows.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CSP Formulation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Problem Verbal Description] --> B[Step 1 Identify Decision Variables X]
    B --> C[Step 2 Specify Finite Domains D]
    C --> D[Step 3 Translate Rules to Constraints C]
    D --> E{Step 4 Objective Present?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Define Objective Function fX]
    E -- No --> G[Pure CSP Feasibility Only]
    F --> H[Step 5 Build Constraint Graph]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Propagate GAC arc consistency]
    I --> J{All Variables Assigned?}
    J -- No --> K[MRV Variable + Value Heuristic]
    K --> L[Branch Assign Value]
    L --> I
    J -- Yes --> M[Valid Solution Recorded]
    I --> N{Domain Wiped Out?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Backtrack to Previous Decision]
    O --> I
    N -- No --> I
```

---

### 4.2 Constraint Graph for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeK[Kochi]
    nodeT[Thrissur]
    nodeX[Kozhikode]
    nodeL[Kollam]
    nodeK --- nodeT
    nodeT --- nodeX
    nodeX --- nodeL
    nodeK --- nodeL
```

The graph is a 4-cycle with one chord (K-L), making it connected. AC propagation alone cannot solve it; backtracking search is necessary.

---

### 4.3 MAC Search Tree Trace (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Root All domains size 4] --> A1[Assign XK Red]
    A1 --> A2[Propagate XT BLU GRN YEL]
    A2 --> A3[Assign XT Blue]
    A3 --> A4[Propagate XX Red GRN YEL]
    A4 --> A5[Assign XX Green]
    A5 --> A6[Propagate XL BLU YEL]
    A6 --> A7[Assign XL Yellow SOLUTION]
    A6 --> A8[Assign XL Blue BACKTRACK CHECK]
```

---

### 4.4 Taxonomy of CP Constraints (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Constraints C]
    R --> R1[Hard vs Soft]
    R --> R2[Arity]
    R --> R3[Representation]
    R --> R4[Linearity]
    R1 --> R1a[Hard Must Hold]
    R1 --> R1b[Soft Penalty Weight]
    R2 --> R2a[Unary]
    R2 --> R2b[Binary]
    R2 --> R2c[Global n-ary]
    R2c --> R2c1[alldifferent]
    R2c --> R2c2[cumulative]
    R2c --> R2c3[element]
    R3 --> R3a[Extensional Table of Tuples]

    R4 --> R4b[Non-linear]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). Write the formal triplet used to represent it.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) is a declarative formulation in which the problem is stated purely by specifying the quantities to be decided and the rules they must obey — without specifying the algorithm to solve it.

**Formal triplet:**

$$
P = (X, D, C)
$$

where:

- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ — the **finite set of variables**.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ — the **finite set of domains**, with $X_i \in D_i$.
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ — the **finite set of constraints**, each being a relation over a subset of $X$.

A **solution** is an assignment $\theta : X \rightarrow \bigcup_i D_i$ such that every $C_j$ is satisfied.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1.5 marks triplet with explanation, 0.5 mark for solution statement.)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between *intensional* and *extensional* constraint representation with an example.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intensional | Extensional |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Logical formula / predicate | Enumerated list of allowed tuples |
| Compactness | Compact even for large domains | Explodes combinatorially |
| Solver use | Easier to propagate symbolically | Easier when scope is small |

**Example for $C(X, Y)$ with $D_X = D_Y = \{1,2,3\}$ and $X < Y$:**

- *Intensional:* $C(X, Y) \equiv (X < Y)$.
- *Extensional:* $C = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3)\}$.

*(1 mark definition each, 1 mark example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-internal-choice is offered. Both **Question A** and **Question B** are from the same module and carry identical marks. Solve **either** of them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Formulate the following problem as a CSP. Clearly state the variables, domains, and constraints.

> A software company must assign **three projects** $P_1, P_2, P_3$ to **three teams** $T_1, T_2, T_3$ such that:
> (i) Each project is assigned to exactly one team.
> (ii) Each team works on at most one project.
> (iii) Team $T_1$ cannot work on $P_2$.
> (iv) Project $P_3$ must be handled by $T_2$ or $T_3$.
> (v) If $T_2$ takes $P_1$, then $T_3$ cannot take $P_3$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the above CSP using backtracking search with arc consistency. Show the search tree and identify **all** valid solutions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Variables.** Let $X_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ denote whether team $T_i$ is assigned project $P_j$. We can equivalently let $Y_i \in \{1,2,3\}$ be the project assigned to team $T_i$ (using a *team-as-variable* encoding). Choose the team-as-variable encoding for elegance:

$$
X = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}, \quad \text{where each } T_i \in \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}
$$

**Step 2 — Domains.**

$$
D_{T_1} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad D_{T_2} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad D_{T_3} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints.**

(C1) *At most one team per project* (and exactly one team per project, since 3 teams and 3 projects):

$$
\text{alldifferent}(T_1, T_2, T_3)
$$

(C2) *$T_1$ cannot work on $P_2$*:

$$
T_1 \neq P_2
$$

(C3) *$P_3$ must be handled by $T_2$ or $T_3$* — implicit from `alldifferent` plus the fact every project must be covered, but explicit:

$$
(T_1 = P_3) \Rightarrow \text{false} \quad \text{(equivalently } T_1 \neq P_3 \text{ after (C2) and (C5) review)}
$$

Better expressed as a guard:

$$
T_2 = P_3 \;\; \text{OR} \;\; T_3 = P_3
$$

(C4) *Conditional* (C5 in original list):

$$
(T_2 = P_1) \Rightarrow (T_3 \neq P_3)
$$

Re-express via arc:

$$
\text{Reified constraint: } b = 1 \iff (T_2 = P_1), \quad \text{then } b \Rightarrow (T_3 \neq P_3)
$$

Or directly: $(T_2 = P_1) \rightarrow (T_3 \in \{P_1, P_2\})$.

**CSP triplet:**

$$
P = (\{T_1, T_2, T_3\},\; \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}^3,\; \{C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4\})
$$

*(1 mark for variables, 1.5 marks for domains, 3.5 marks for constraints — distribute via granularity of the four constraints.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

Initial state: each $T_i$ may take any of 3 projects. Apply `alldifferent` global constraint.

**Step 1 — Assign $T_1 = P_1$** (branch via MRV — all equal, pick $T_1$).

**Step 2 — Propagate `alldifferent`:**

$$
D_{T_2} = \{P_2, P_3\}, \quad D_{T_3} = \{P_2, P_3\}
$$

Apply (C2) — no pruning for $T_1$. Apply (C4) conditional — no pruning yet.

**Step 3 — Assign $T_2 = P_2$.** Propagate:

$$
D_{T_3} = \{P_3\} \quad (\text{via alldifferent}), \quad \text{then (C3) and (C4) both satisfied.}
$$

Check (C4): $(T_2 = P_1)$ is false (since $T_2 = P_2$), so (C4) is **vacuously true**.

**Solution 1:** $(T_1 = P_1,\; T_2 = P_2,\; T_3 = P_3)$. ✓

**Step 4 — Backtrack, try $T_2 = P_3$.** Propagate: $D_{T_3} = \{P_2\}$.
Check (C4): $(T_2 = P_1)$ is false → vacuously true. (C3) requires $T_2 = P_3$ or $T_3 = P_3$ → $T_2 = P_3$ ✓.

**Solution 2:** $(T_1 = P_1,\; T_2 = P_3,\; T_3 = P_2)$. ✓

**Step 5 — Backtrack to $T_1 = P_2$.** Forbidden by (C2). Dead branch.

**Step 6 — Backtrack to $T_1 = P_3$.** Check (C3): $T_1 = P_3$ is acceptable provided some team handles $P_3$ — but `alldifferent` will require $T_2 \neq P_3$ and $T_3 \neq P_3$. So $T_2, T_3 \in \{P_1, P_2\}$ and must differ.

Assign $T_2 = P_1$. Then $T_3 = P_2$. Check (C4): $(T_2 = P_1)$ true ⇒ must have $T_3 \neq P_3$. $T_3 = P_2$ ≠ $P_3$ ✓.

**Solution 3:** $(T_1 = P_3,\; T_2 = P_1,\; T_3 = P_2)$. ✓

Assign $T_2 = P_2$. Then $T_3 = P_1$. Check (C4): $(T_2 = P_1)$ false ⇒ vacuously true. ✓

**Solution 4:** $(T_1 = P_3,\; T_2 = P_2,\; T_3 = P_1)$. ✓

**All solutions:** $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 4$.

*(2 marks each for finding solutions; 1 mark for the search-tree diagram; full marks for showing backtracking branches.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Consider the **N-Queens problem for $N = 4$**. Formulate it as a CSP, identifying the variables, domains, and constraints explicitly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Show by arc-consistency propagation and backtracking that the $N=4$ problem has exactly **2 solutions**. List them. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Variables.** Let $Q_i$ be the column position of the queen in row $i$ (row index $i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$):

$$
X = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4\}
$$

**Step 2 — Domains.** Each queen can occupy any column 1–4:

$$
D_{Q_i} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \forall i
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints.**

(C1) *No two queens in the same column:*

$$
Q_i \neq Q_j \quad \forall \; i \neq j \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{alldifferent}(Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4)
$$

(C2) *No two queens on the same diagonal:*

$$
Q_i - Q_j \neq i - j \quad \text{and} \quad Q_i - Q_j \neq j - i \quad \forall i \neq j
$$

Equivalently, using absolute value:

$$
\vert Q_i - Q_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert \quad \forall i \neq j
$$

**CSP triplet:**

$$
P = \bigl(\{Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4\},\; \{1,2,3,4\}^4,\; \{C_1, C_2\}\bigr)
$$

*(2 marks variables, 1.5 marks domains, 3.5 marks constraints.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Search Tree (MRV variable, lexicographic value ordering):**

**Branch 1 — $Q_1 = 1$.**

- Apply `alldifferent`: each $Q_i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, all distinct.

**Branch 1.1 — $Q_2 = 3$** (skipping $Q_2 = 1$ since already used; $Q_2 = 2$ viable but leads to check).

Actually, systematic enumeration with `alldifferent` enforced:

| $Q_1$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ | $Q_4$ | Diagonal check | Feasible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | — | — | $\vert 3-1 \vert = 2 = \vert 2-1 \vert$ ⇒ same diag → fail | ✗ |
| 1 | 4 | 2 | 3 | All $\vert Q_i - Q_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert$ ✓ | **✓ Solution A** |
| 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | All ✓ | **✓ Solution B** |
| 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | duplicates check via backtracking tree | — |
| 3 | 1 | 4 | 2 | All ✓ | (mirror of A) |
| 4 | 1 | — | — | prune via diagonal | — |

**Final two solutions (excluding symmetric mirrors if not required):**

**Solution A:** $(Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4) = (1, 4, 2, 3)$ ⇒ queens at $(1,1), (2,4), (3,2), (4,3)$.

**Solution B:** $(Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4) = (2, 4, 1, 3)$ ⇒ queens at $(1,2), (2,4), (3,1), (4,3)$.

*(3 marks for showing the search tree, 2 marks for the diagonal checks, 2 marks for listing the two solutions.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks on CP formulation questions:**
> 1. **Confusing variables with values** — e.g., writing "$X = \{Red, Blue\}$" instead of "$X_i \in \{Red, Blue, Green\}$". Always specify the *variable name* and its domain *separately*.
> 2. **Forgetting the `alldifferent` global constraint** when the problem is essentially a permutation (N-Queens, assignment, graph colouring). Examiners reward recognising global structure.
> 3. **Mixing CP with LP notation** — do not write $\min z = \sum c_i X_i$ unless the problem has an objective; pure CSPs have *no* objective.
> 4. **Skipping the conditional/implication reformulation** — expressions like "If A then B" must be rewritten as $A \Rightarrow B$ and *also* the contrapositive checked when backtracking.
> 5. **Not stating that propagation reduces domains** — many students describe AC without showing the *before* and *after* domain sizes. Always include both.
> 6. **Symmetry breaking** — failing to mention or apply symmetry-breaking constraints loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Single solution claims** — when $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert > 1$, list **all** solutions or explicitly state symmetry reasoning for omitting mirrors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CSP triplet** $(X, D, C)$ is the *single most important formula* in this module — memorise it.
- **Variables** are decision quantities; **domains** are their allowable values; **constraints** are logical relations.
- **Constraint types to recognise:** unary, binary, ternary, **global** (`alldifferent`, `cumulative`, `element`).
- **Arc consistency (AC / GAC)** prunes domains without search; **backtracking** completes the work.
- **Formulation sequence:** Variables → Domains → Constraints → (Objective) → Constraint Graph.
- **`alldifferent`** is the most common global constraint in exam problems — recognise it instantly in permutation-style questions.
- **Conditional constraints** must be reified: $(A \Rightarrow B)$ becomes a pair of clauses or a Boolean indicator variable.
- **Search-tree traces** must show: domain state before assignment, chosen variable, chosen value, propagation effect, and backtracking points.
- **Naive search-space bound** = $\prod_i \vert D_i \vert$; propagation dramatically shrinks this.
- **COP vs CSP:** include an objective $f(X)$ for optimisation problems; omit for pure feasibility.
- **CSP vs LP:** CP is *declarative*; LP is *optimisation-oriented*. CP excels at highly combinatorial, structure-rich problems.
- **Real-world domains** to mention in 2-mark sub-parts: scheduling, rostering, configuration, frequency assignment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Search and Constraint Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Search and Constraint Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Basic Search in a CSP?

**Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs)** are mathematical models defined by a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ is a finite set of **variables**.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ is a set of **domains**, where $D_i$ is the finite set of possible values for $X_i$.
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ is a set of **constraints** that restrict the values the variables can simultaneously take.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Search (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Basic Search* refers to the systematic exploration of the assignment space of a CSP using **backtracking** — a depth-first search algorithm that incrementally builds partial solutions and backtracks when a constraint is violated. It is the foundational inference engine upon which all heuristic enhancements (MRV, LCV, Forward Checking, Arc Consistency) are layered.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sudoku Detective" 🕵️

Imagine you are solving a Sudoku puzzle alone in a quiet room:

- Each empty cell is a **variable** $X_i$ that needs a number.
- The allowed digits $\{1,2,\ldots,9\}$ for each cell form its **domain** $D_i$.
- The row, column, and 3×3 box rules are the **constraints** $C_j$.

You place a number, glance at the rules, and either **continue forward** (the placement is safe) or **erase and step back** to try another number (you backtrack). **Basic Search** is exactly this iterative "try-erase-retry" dance — formalised, made systematic, and turbo-charged with smart heuristics.

## 1.2 What is Constraint Propagation?

**Constraint Propagation** is the process of using the constraints of a CSP to **reduce the domains of variables** *before* or *during* search, thereby pruning the search tree. The most common form — **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** — repeatedly enforces that for every pair of variables connected by a constraint, every value in the first variable's domain has some supporting value in the second variable's domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The "Two Pillars" of CSP Solving:**
> 1. **Search (Backtracking)** — decides *where* to look in the assignment space.
> 2. **Inference (Constraint Propagation)** — decides *how much* can be deduced without searching.
> Modern solvers interleave the two: *propagate-then-search*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Party Guest List" 🎉

Suppose you are hosting a dinner and each guest has a list of dietary preferences (the **domain**), and the menu imposes **constraints** (e.g., "Veggie and Non-Veggie cannot sit together"). If a guest suddenly declares they are strictly vegan, you can immediately **strike off** all non-vegan dishes from their domain — and any friend who was relying on sharing a dish with them must also have their options narrowed. That cascading elimination is **constraint propagation** in action.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Vocabulary Used by KTU Examiners

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Number of variables | $\|X\|$ |
| $d$ | Maximum domain size | $\max_i \vert D_i \vert$ |
| $m$ | Number of constraints | $\|C\|$ |
| $\mathcal{N}(X_i)$ | Neighbours of $X_i$ in constraint graph | $\mathcal{N}$ |
| $D_i'$ | Reduced (filtered) domain of $X_i$ | $D_i' \subseteq D_i$ |
| **CSP** | Constraint Satisfaction Problem | $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ |
| **AC** | Arc Consistency | $\text{AC-3 algorithm}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of Basic Search — Backtracking Algorithm

A vanilla **backtracking search** works as follows:

1. **Choose an unassigned variable** $X_i$ (in the basic version: pick arbitrarily or in fixed order).
2. **Order its domain values** $D_i$ (in the basic version: try them in arbitrary order).
3. **For each value $v \in D_i$**:
   - **Check consistency:** Verify that assigning $X_i = v$ does not violate any constraint with already-assigned variables.
   - If consistent → **Recurse** on the remaining unassigned variables.
   - If inconsistent → **Prune** this branch and try the next value.
4. If all values fail → **Return failure** (the parent recursion will try a different value).

### Why Backtracking is the Right Backbone

A CSP with $n$ variables and max domain size $d$ has $d^n$ possible full assignments. Naïve brute force evaluates every assignment — $\mathcal{O}(d^n)$. Backtracking prunes subtrees early, giving a worst-case of $d^n$ but an *expected* exponential reduction in practice. With heuristics, it can solve problems with $n \geq 100$ efficiently.

## 2.2 Variable & Value Ordering Heuristics (The "Smart" Layer)

### 2.2.1 Minimum Remaining Values (MRV) — "Fail-First Heuristic"

Choose the variable with the **fewest legal values remaining** in its domain. Ties are broken randomly or by **Degree Heuristic** (variable involved in the most constraints on unassigned variables).

$$X_{\text{MRV}} = \arg\min_{X_i \in \text{unassigned}} \vert D_i' \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Fail-First"?** The intuition: detect failure as early as possible so that a huge subtree can be pruned immediately. If you have a 50/50 choice and a 1-value choice, tackle the 1-value choice first because it is the one that is *most likely* to fail.

### 2.2.2 Least Constraining Value (LCV)

For the chosen variable, try the value that **rules out the fewest choices** for the neighbouring unassigned variables.

$$v_{\text{LCV}} = \arg\min_{v \in D_i'} \sum_{X_j \in \mathcal{N}(X_i)} \vert D_j' \vert_{X_i = v} - \vert D_j' \vert$$

In plain terms: count how many domain values get eliminated from each neighbour — pick the value with the **smallest total elimination count**.

### 2.2.3 Degree Heuristic (Tie-breaker for MRV)

$$X_{\text{deg}} = \arg\max_{X_i \in \text{unassigned}} \vert \{C_k \in C : X_i \in C_k, \text{other vars unassigned}\} \vert$$

Used only to break MRV ties. It selects the variable that constrains the most *other* unassigned variables.

## 2.3 Inference During Search — Forward Checking

**Forward Checking (FC)** is the simplest form of constraint propagation *embedded inside* backtracking search.

**Mechanism:** Whenever $X_i$ is assigned a value $v$:
- For every **unassigned neighbour** $X_j \in \mathcal{N}(X_i)$:
  - Remove from $D_j$ any value that is **inconsistent** with $X_i = v$ (i.e., violates some constraint $C_k$ involving both $X_i$ and $X_j$).
- If any neighbour's domain becomes empty → **dead-end detected** → immediately backtrack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:**
> - *Arc Consistency* checks **every pair** of variables (even unassigned ones).
> - *Forward Checking* checks **only assigned → unassigned** pairs.
> Therefore, Forward Checking is **weaker** than full Arc Consistency but **cheaper** to maintain.

## 2.4 Constraint Propagation in Depth — Arc Consistency

A variable $X_i$ is **node-consistent** if every value in $D_i$ satisfies all unary constraints on $X_i$.

A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $v \in D_i$, there exists some value $w \in D_j$ such that the assignment $(X_i = v, X_j = w)$ satisfies the binary constraint $C_{ij}$.

$$ \forall v \in D_i, \exists w \in D_j \text{ such that } (v, w) \text{ satisfies } C_{ij} $$

A graph is **arc-consistent** if every arc $(X_i, X_j)$ is arc-consistent.

### 2.4.1 The AC-3 Algorithm (Mackworth, 1977)

AC-3 is the workhorse polynomial-time arc-consistency algorithm.

1. Initialise a queue $Q$ with **all arcs** $(X_i, X_j)$ in the constraint graph.
2. While $Q$ is not empty:
   - Dequeue an arc $(X_i, X_j)$.
   - Call $\text{Revise}(X_i, X_j)$: remove from $D_i$ every value $v$ for which there is **no** supporting $w \in D_j$.
   - If $D_i$ was revised (i.e., some value was removed):
     - If $D_i$ becomes empty → return **failure** (inconsistency detected).
     - Add to $Q$ all arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ where $X_k$ is a neighbour of $X_i$ *other than* $X_j$.
3. Return **success** (graph is arc-consistent).

### 2.4.2 Complexity of AC-3

Let $n$ be the number of variables, $d$ the maximum domain size, and $e$ the number of arcs (binary constraints).

- A single arc can be revised at most $d$ times before its domain is exhausted: $d$ times.
- Total number of arc revisions: bounded by $ed$ (each revision either empties the domain or stops).
- Cost of checking one arc $(X_i, X_j)$: $\mathcal{O}(d^2)$ (for every $v \in D_i$ scan every $w \in D_j$).
- **Total worst-case time complexity: $\mathcal{O}(e \, d^3)$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Korf's 2007 improvement (AC-3.1 / AC-2001)** drops this to $\mathcal{O}(e \, d^2)$ by recording supports, but AC-3 is the KTU-prescribed standard.

## 2.5 Path Consistency & $k$-Consistency (Advanced Note)

A graph is **path-consistent** if for every triple $(X_i, X_j, X_k)$ and every pair $(v, w)$ with $X_i = v, X_j = w$ satisfying $C_{ij}$, there exists a value $u \in D_k$ such that the triple satisfies both $C_{ik}$ and $C_{jk}$. Path consistency removes values from binary *relations*, not from variable domains — it is more powerful but exponentially more expensive and is rarely used in practice.

A CSP is **strongly $k$-consistent** if it is $j$-consistent for all $j \leq k$. Strong $n$-consistency implies that the problem can be solved in $\mathcal{O}(n^2 d)$ time without search.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Cost | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CSP size | $\vert X \vert = n$, $\vert D_i \vert \leq d$ | – | Finite-domain CSP |
| Brute-force assignments | $d^n$ | $\mathcal{O}(d^n)$ | Infeasible beyond $n \approx 25$ |
| Backtracking (no heuristics) | DFS over $n$ levels | Worst $\mathcal{O}(d^n)$ | Prunes on constraint violation |
| MRV variable pick | $\arg\min \vert D_i' \vert$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ per step | "Fail-first" heuristic |
| LCV value pick | $\arg\min \sum \Delta \vert D_j' \vert$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \, d^2)$ per step | "Succeed-last" heuristic |
| Forward Checking | $X_i$ assigned $\Rightarrow$ prune $\mathcal{N}(X_i)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \, d)$ per step | Weaker than AC |
| Arc Consistency (AC-3) | For each arc, revise if unsupported | $\mathcal{O}(e \, d^3)$ | Polynomial! |
| Strong $n$-consistency | Solvable in $\mathcal{O}(n^2 d)$ | – | Theoretical upper bound |
| Constraint graph arcs | $e \leq \binom{n}{2} = n(n-1)/2$ | – | For binary CSPs |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler register allocation** is formulated as a graph-colouring CSP solved by backtracking with constraint propagation.
- **Crew rostering and airline scheduling** (a multi-million-dollar industry) use MRV + LCV + AC-3 hybrids.
- **Hardware verification & VLSI testing** use CSPs with arc consistency to reduce state-space explosion.
- **Robotic motion planning** uses CSP variants (STRIPS, PDDL) where constraint propagation dramatically prunes infeasible action sequences.
- **Configuration problems** in telecom, automotive (option-package selection in BMW configurator), and supply-chain all use backtracking-with-propagation solvers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Map Colouring (Australia)

We have the classical Russell-Norvig example: colour the map of Australia (WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T) with three colours {Red, Green, Blue} such that no two adjacent regions share a colour.

### 3.1.1 Backtracking Without Heuristics (Naïve Order)

Assume variable order: $X = $ (WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T) and value order: $D = $ (R, G, B).

**Step 1 — Assign WA = R.** ✓
**Step 2 — Assign NT = R.** ✗ (WA and NT are adjacent) → backtrack, try NT = G. ✓
**Step 3 — Assign SA = R.** ✗ (adjacent to both WA and NT) → try G ✗ → try B ✓.
**Step 4 — Assign Q = R.** ✗ (adj. to NT) → G ✗ (adj. to SA) → B ✓.
**Step 5 — Assign NSW = R.** ✗ → G ✓.
**Step 6 — Assign V = R.** ✗ → G ✓.
**Step 7 — Assign T = R, G, B.** All allowed (T is isolated) → pick R ✓.

> [!NOTE]
> **Branching factor at each level:** at most 3, depth 7, so worst case $3^7 = 2187$. Backtracking + early pruning prunes most.

### 3.1.2 Same Problem with MRV + Forward Checking

Initial domains: $D_{\text{WA}} = D_{\text{NT}} = \ldots = \{R, G, B\}$.

**Step 1 — Pick MRV variable:** All domains have size 3. Tie. Pick SA (highest degree: SA is adjacent to WA, NT, Q, NSW — degree 4).

**Step 2 — Assign SA = R.** Forward check: remove R from $D_{\text{WA}}, D_{\text{NT}}, D_{\text{Q}}, D_{\text{NSW}}$.
Updated: $D_{\text{WA}} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{\text{NT}} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{\text{Q}} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{\text{NSW}} = \{G, B\}$.

**Step 3 — MRV next:** All unassigned non-T variables now have domain size 2. Pick WA arbitrarily. Try WA = G. FC prunes G from $D_{\text{NT}}$ → $D_{\text{NT}} = \{B\}$.

**Step 4 — MRV next:** $D_{\text{NT}} = \{B\}$ (size 1) → pick NT. Try NT = B. FC prunes B from $D_{\text{Q}}$ → $D_{\text{Q}} = \{G\}$.

**Step 5 — Continue:** Q must be G (FC forces it). NSW now has $\{G\}$ (R removed by WA, B removed by Q). V = R or G or B except: V adj. NSW, so remove G from V → $D_{\text{V}} = \{R, B\}$. Pick V = R. ✓ T = any. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Total nodes explored drops from ~50 to ~11.** This is the empirical power of MRV + FC.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Arc Consistency (AC-3) Step-by-Step

Consider the constraint graph with three variables and binary constraints:

$$X_1, X_2, X_3 \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$$
$$C_{12} : X_1 \text{ and } X_2 \text{ differ by 1}$$
$$C_{13} : X_1 \text{ and } X_3 \text{ differ by 1}$$
$$C_{23} : X_2 \text{ and } X_3 \text{ differ by 2}$$

**Initial domains:** $D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

**Initial queue:** $Q = \{(X_1,X_2), (X_2,X_1), (X_1,X_3), (X_3,X_1), (X_2,X_3), (X_3,X_2)\}$.

### Revise($X_1, X_2$) under $C_{12}$

For each $v \in D_1$, find supporting $w \in D_2$ with $\vert v - w \vert = 1$:

- $v = 1$: support $w = 2$ ✓
- $v = 2$: supports $w \in \{1, 3\}$ ✓
- $v = 3$: supports $w \in \{2, 4\}$ ✓
- $v = 4$: support $w = 3$ ✓

No value pruned. $D_1 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

### Revise($X_2, X_1$) under $C_{12}$ (Symmetric)

Same check in reverse: all $w \in D_2$ have supporting $v \in D_1$. No pruning.

### Revise($X_1, X_3$) under $C_{13}$

- $v = 1$: support $w = 2$ ✓
- $v = 4$: support $w = 3$ ✓
- All kept.

### Revise($X_3, X_1$) under $C_{13}$

- $w = 1$: support $v = 2$ ✓
- $w = 4$: support $v = 3$ ✓
- All kept.

### Revise($X_2, X_3$) under $C_{23}$

For each $v \in D_2$, find supporting $w \in D_3$ with $\vert v - w \vert = 2$:

- $v = 1$: support $w = 3$ ✓
- $v = 2$: support $w = 4$ ✓
- $v = 3$: support $w = 1$ ✓
- $v = 4$: support $w = 2$ ✓

No pruning.

### Revise($X_3, X_2$) under $C_{23}$

Similarly, all kept.

**Queue empty → AC-3 terminates successfully.** Final domains: $D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **In this example AC-3 prunes nothing** — the constraints are *already* arc-consistent. This illustrates that AC-3 is *sound but not complete* for solving CSPs: it does not guarantee a solution exists. It only prunes impossible values.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — When AC-3 *Does* Prune

Modify the problem: $C_{23} : X_2 = X_3$.

**Revise($X_2, X_3$) under $X_2 = X_3$:** For each $v \in D_2$, need $w = v \in D_3$. All $v \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ have a support. No pruning. (Symmetric.)

**Modify further:** Let $D_3 = \{1, 2\}$ (pre-pruned).

**Revise($X_2, X_3$):** Need $v = w \in D_3 = \{1,2\}$. So $v \in D_2$ must be in $\{1,2\}$. Remove $3, 4$ from $D_2$ → $D_2 = \{1,2\}$.

**Domain changed → re-queue arcs.** Add $(X_1, X_2)$ and $(X_3, X_2)$ — wait, AC-3 adds arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ for $X_k \neq X_j$, so we add $(X_1, X_2)$.

**Revise($X_1, X_2$) under $C_{12}$:** Need $\vert v - w \vert = 1$ for some $w \in D_2 = \{1,2\}$.
- $v = 1$: support $w = 2$ ✓
- $v = 2$: support $w = 1$ ✓
- $v = 3$: support $w = 2$ ✓
- $v = 4$: support $w = 3$ — but $3 \notin D_2$ ✗. So **remove 4 from $D_1$**.

$D_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$.

**Re-queue:** Add $(X_3, X_1)$.

**Revise($X_3, X_1$) under $C_{13}$:** Need $\vert w - v \vert = 1$ for some $v \in D_1 = \{1,2,3\}$.
- $w = 1$: support $v = 2$ ✓
- $w = 2$: support $v = 1$ ✓
- No pruning.

**Queue empty.** Final: $D_1 = \{1,2,3\}$, $D_2 = \{1,2\}$, $D_3 = \{1,2\}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** AC-3 has *cascading* effects — pruning $D_2$ forced pruning of $D_1$ via $C_{12}$. This cascading is what makes constraint propagation exponentially powerful in structured problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Method | When to Use | Strength | Weakness | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vanilla Backtracking | Teaching, tiny problems | Simple, no overhead | No early pruning | $\mathcal{O}(d^n)$ |
| BT + MRV | General CSPs | Drastically prunes at top of tree | Greedy, may not be optimal | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ per step |
| BT + LCV | Dense constraint graphs | Maximises remaining options | Costly to compute | $\mathcal{O}(n d^2)$ per step |
| Forward Checking | Real-time / interactive | Quick early dead-end detection | Doesn't catch indirect conflicts | $\mathcal{O}(n d)$ per step |
| Maintaining Arc Consistency (MAC) | Hardest CSPs | Strongest inference per step | Expensive overhead | $\mathcal{O}(e d^2)$ per step |
| Full Path Consistency | Geometric / temporal CSPs | Catches deep conflicts | Exponential relations | Rarely used |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CSP Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: CSP triple X, D, C] --> B[Build Constraint Graph]
    B --> C[Apply AC-3 Pre-Processing]
    C --> D{Any Domain Empty?}
    D -- Yes --> Z[Return: No Solution]
    D -- No --> E[Select Unassigned Variable]
    E --> F[Apply MRV Heuristic]
    F --> G[Order Domain Values via LCV]
    G --> H[For each candidate value v]
    H --> I{Consistent with assigned vars?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Assign v to X_i]
    J --> K[Run Forward Checking]
    K --> L{Any Neighbour Domain Empty?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Undo Assignment]
    M --> H
    L -- No --> N[Recursive Call: Backtrack]
    N --> O{All Variables Assigned?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Return: Solution Found]
    O -- No --> E
    N -- Failure --> M
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** This flowchart represents the canonical **MAC (Maintaining Arc Consistency)** loop — the workhorse of modern CSP solvers such as Choco, Gecode, and OR-Tools.

## 4.2 Constraint Graph for the Australia Map-Colouring Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    WA[Western Australia] --- NT[Northern Territory]
    WA --- SA[South Australia]
    NT --- SA
    NT --- Q[Queensland]
    SA --- Q
    SA --- NSW[New South Wales]
    SA --- V[Victoria]
    Q --- NSW
    NSW --- V
    T[Tasmania]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Tasmania is an isolated node (no edges). It is therefore *automatically* node-consistent and arc-consistent regardless of domain. AC-3 will not even enqueue arcs involving T.

## 4.3 The AC-3 Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start AC-3] --> initQ[Initialise Queue Q with all arcs]
    initQ --> checkQ{Q empty?}
    checkQ -- Yes --> done[Return: AC Achieved]
    checkQ -- No --> deq[Dequeue arc X_i, X_j]
    deq --> revise[Call Revise X_i, X_j]
    revise --> changed{D_i revised?}
    changed -- No --> checkQ
    changed -- Yes --> empty{D_i empty?}
    empty -- Yes --> fail[Return: Failure]
    empty -- No --> requeue[Re-queue all arcs X_k, X_i for X_k != X_j]
    requeue --> checkQ
```

## 4.4 Forward Checking Mechanism (Per-Step View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Solver
    participant V as Variable X_i
    participant N as Neighbours of X_i
    S->>V: Assign X_i = v
    loop For each X_j in Neighbours of X_i
        S->>N: Remove values in D_j inconsistent with X_i = v
    end
    alt Any D_j becomes empty
        S-->>S: Backtrack immediately
    else All D_j non-empty
        S->>S: Recurse to next unassigned variable
    end
```

## 4.5 Comparison: Pure Backtracking vs. MRV+LCV+FC Search Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1[Backtracking BT] --> a1[Try v1]
    root1 --> a2[Try v2]
    root1 --> a3[Try v3]
    a1 --> a1a[Subtree 1.1]
    a1 --> a1b[Subtree 1.2]
    a1 --> a1c[Subtree 1.3]
    a2 --> a2a[Subtree 2.1]
    a3 --> a3a[Subtree 3.1]
    a3 --> a3b[Subtree 3.2]
    root2[BT + MRV + LCV + FC] --> b1[Pick most-constrained var]
    b1 --> b1a[Try LCV-ordered values]
    b1a --> b1a1[Prune early via FC]
    b1a --> b1a2[Smaller pruned subtree]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left side shows a wide search tree with many fully explored subtrees. The right side shows a dramatically narrower tree — many branches are pruned *before* recursion begins.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). List its three components and explain what is meant by a "binary constraint" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a formal framework used to model problems whose solution requires assigning values to variables subject to a set of constraints. It is defined as a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

1. **Variables** $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ — the entities to which values must be assigned. *(1 mark)*
2. **Domains** $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ — the finite set of allowable values for each variable $X_i$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Constraints** $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ — restrictions on the legal combinations of values for the variables. *(0.5 marks)*

A **binary constraint** is a constraint that involves exactly **two variables** — for example, in the graph-colouring problem, the constraint "WA $\neq$ NT" is a binary constraint involving only the variables WA and NT. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Forward Checking and Arc Consistency. State one advantage of arc consistency over forward checking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Checking (FC) | Arc Consistency (AC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope of check | Only assigned → unassigned arcs | Every pair of unassigned variables |
| Triggered by | A variable being assigned | Any domain reduction |
| Strength | Weaker | Stronger |

**Advantage of AC over FC:** Arc Consistency can detect inconsistencies between *two unassigned* variables (e.g., $X_2$ and $X_3$) that Forward Checking — which only watches assigned variables — would miss. This leads to **earlier pruning of the search tree** and fewer dead-ends. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the Minimum Remaining Values (MRV) and Least Constraining Value (LCV) heuristics in CSP solving. How do they complement each other? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MRV (Minimum Remaining Values) — 3 marks:**

MRV is a **variable-ordering heuristic** that selects, at each step, the unassigned variable with the **fewest legal values remaining** in its domain. Formally:

$$X_{\text{MRV}} = \arg\min_{X_i \in \text{unassigned}} \vert D_i' \vert$$

This is also called the **"fail-first" heuristic** because tackling the most-constrained variable first means that if a solution cannot be found, the algorithm will discover this **as early as possible**, allowing it to prune an enormous subtree. Ties in MRV are usually broken using the **Degree Heuristic** — the variable involved in the largest number of constraints on the remaining unassigned variables.

**LCV (Least Constraining Value) — 3 marks:**

LCV is a **value-ordering heuristic** that, once a variable has been chosen by MRV, picks the value that **eliminates the fewest options** for the neighbouring unassigned variables. Formally, for each candidate value $v$:

$$\text{cost}(v) = \sum_{X_j \in \mathcal{N}(X_i)} \vert D_j' \vert_{\text{before}} - \vert D_j' \vert_{X_i = v, \text{ after}}$$

The chosen value is $v_{\text{LCV}} = \arg\min_v \text{cost}(v)$. This is the **"succeed-last"** philosophy: maximise the chance that subsequent recursive calls will have at least one viable move.

**Complementarity — 1 mark:**

MRV operates at the **outer loop** (decides *which* variable to expand), while LCV operates at the **inner loop** (decides *which* value to try first). They are orthogonal: MRV accelerates dead-end detection, LCV maximises the chance of a successful branch. Used together, they form the most common CSP heuristic pair.

---

**(b) Consider the following CSP: Variables A, B, C each have domain {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Constraints: A < B, B < C. Show step-by-step execution of the AC-3 algorithm. What are the final reduced domains? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build constraint graph and initialise queue.**

Constraint graph edges (one for each binary constraint direction): $(A,B), (B,A), (B,C), (C,B)$.

Initial domains: $D_A = D_B = D_C = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

Initial queue: $Q = \{(A,B), (B,A), (B,C), (C,B)\}$.

**Step 2 — Revise(A, B) under A < B.**

For each $v \in D_A$, need $w \in D_B$ with $w > v$. All five values $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ have such a $w$. No pruning. $D_A = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$.

**Step 3 — Revise(B, A) under A < B (i.e., $v = w$ would mean B > A).**

Wait — for an arc $(B, A)$ under constraint $A < B$ (i.e., $B > A$): for each $v \in D_B$, need $w \in D_A$ with $w < v$. All $v$ have such $w$. No pruning.

**Step 4 — Revise(B, C) under B < C.**

For each $v \in D_B$, need $w \in D_C$ with $w > v$. All have support. No pruning.

**Step 5 — Revise(C, B) under B < C (i.e., C > B).**

For each $v \in D_C$, need $w \in D_B$ with $w < v$. All have support. No pruning.

**Step 6 — Queue empty. AC-3 terminates.**

**Final reduced domains:** $D_A = D_B = D_C = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** AC-3 cannot prune any values here because the constraint $A < B$ has no upper bound violation — every value of A (resp. B, C) has some larger value in the next variable's domain. In such cases, **node-by-node backtracking** is still required to find a full solution (e.g., $A=1, B=2, C=3$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> ⚠️ Students often confuse the *direction* of arc revision. For arc $(X_i, X_j)$, you are removing values from $D_i$ — not from $D_j$. Always read the arc as "ensure $X_i$'s values have support in $X_j$."
> ⚠️ A common error is to forget that AC-3 is **incomplete** — it prunes impossible values but does not by itself *find* a solution. You must still run backtracking afterwards.
> ⚠️ Another common mark-loss point: failing to re-queue arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ when $D_i$ is revised. Forgetting this step breaks the cascading effect and can lead to **incorrect "solution found"** verdicts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) What is constraint propagation? Explain the concept of arc consistency with a suitable example. State the time complexity of the AC-3 algorithm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Constraint Propagation (2 marks):**

Constraint propagation is the process of **inferring domain reductions** from existing constraints, *without* assigning any variable. It is a form of local reasoning that prunes values which can never participate in any global solution, thereby shrinking the search space that backtracking must explore. The most widely-used form is **arc consistency**.

**Concept of Arc Consistency (3 marks):**

A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $v \in D_i$ there exists at least one value $w \in D_j$ such that the assignment $(X_i = v, X_j = w)$ satisfies the constraint $C_{ij}$. Formally:

$$\forall v \in D_i, \exists w \in D_j \text{ s.t. } (v, w) \models C_{ij}$$

A CSP is **arc-consistent** if every pair of variables connected by a binary constraint is arc-consistent in both directions.

**Example:** Let $X, Y \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with constraint $X + Y \leq 5$. Initially arc-consistent. Now change $D_X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $D_Y = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Arc $(X, Y)$: for $v = 4 \in D_X$, no $w \in D_Y$ gives $4 + w \leq 5$ (i.e., $w \leq 1$, so $w = 1$ works! ✓). Actually $v = 4$: need $w \leq 1$, so $w = 1$ is a support. All values of $X$ have support. Now reduce $D_Y$ to $\{4\}$. Revise $(X, Y)$ again: $v = 1$ needs $w \leq 4$ → $w = 4$ ✓. $v = 2$ needs $w \leq 3$ → no $w \in D_Y = \{4\}$. **Remove 2 from $D_X$.** Similarly remove 3, 4. Final: $D_X = \{1\}$, $D_Y = \{4\}$.

**Time Complexity of AC-3 (2 marks):**

Let $n$ = number of variables, $d$ = maximum domain size, $e$ = number of arcs in the constraint graph. The total worst-case time complexity of AC-3 is:

$$T_{\text{AC-3}} = \mathcal{O}(e \cdot d^3)$$

This is because each arc can be dequeued and revised at most $d$ times, and each revision costs $\mathcal{O}(d^2)$ to scan for supports.

---

**(b) Solve the following 4-queens problem using backtracking with MRV and forward checking. Show the search tree. Variables $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4$ represent columns, with domains $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (row positions). Constraint: no two queens attack each other. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Initial domains: $D_{Q_1} = D_{Q_2} = D_{Q_3} = D_{Q_4} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

**Step 1 — MRV: All domains have size 4. Tie. Pick $Q_1$ (or any). Try $Q_1 = 1$ (LCV order: all values equal, pick 1).**

Forward check: prune row 1 from $D_{Q_2}, D_{Q_3}, D_{Q_4}$, and prune diagonals.
- $D_{Q_2}$: remove 1, also diagonal-2 (col diff 1, row diff 1, so 1+1=2). $D_{Q_2} = \{3, 4\}$.
- $D_{Q_3}$: remove 1, diagonal 1+2=3, 1−2=−1 (invalid). $D_{Q_3} = \{2, 4\}$.
- $D_{Q_4}$: remove 1, diagonal 1+3=4. $D_{Q_4} = \{2, 3\}$.

**Step 2 — MRV: smallest domain is $D_{Q_2} = \{3,4\}$ (size 2). Pick $Q_2$. Try LCV: value 3 vs 4. Both eliminate 1 from neighbours — but value 3 in $Q_2$ attacks $Q_4$ diagonal? col diff 2, row diff = $\vert 3 - q_4 \vert = 2$ → $q_4 = 1$ or $5$; only $1 \in D_{Q_4}$, but $1$ was already removed. So value 3 is "least constraining" → pick $Q_2 = 3$.**

FC: $D_{Q_3}$ loses 3 (taken) and diagonal 3+1=4. $D_{Q_3} = \{2\}$. $D_{Q_4}$ loses 3 (taken) and diagonal 3+2=5 (invalid). $D_{Q_4} = \{2\}$.

**Step 3 — MRV: $D_{Q_3} = \{2\}$ (size 1). Pick $Q_3 = 2$.**

FC: $D_{Q_4}$ loses 2 (taken) and diagonal 2+1=3. $D_{Q_4} = \emptyset$. **DEAD-END.**

**Step 4 — Backtrack.** Undo $Q_3 = 2$. Undo $Q_2 = 3$. Try $Q_2 = 4$.

FC from $Q_2 = 4$: $D_{Q_3}$ loses 4 (taken) and diagonal 4+1=5 (invalid). $D_{Q_3} = \{2\}$. $D_{Q_4}$ loses 4 and diagonal 4+2=6 (invalid). $D_{Q_4} = \{2, 3\}$.

**Step 5 — MRV: $D_{Q_3} = \{2\}$. Assign $Q_3 = 2$.**

FC: $D_{Q_4}$ loses 2 and diagonal 2+1=3. $D_{Q_4} = \{?\}$. Initially $\{2,3\}$ after removing taken 2 → $\{3\}$. Diagonal 2+1=3 also removes 3. $D_{Q_4} = \emptyset$. **DEAD-END.**

**Step 6 — Backtrack.** Undo $Q_1 = 1$. Try $Q_1 = 2$.

(Full continuation omitted in summary — the key point is that the algorithm systematically explores the tree, and 4-queens has 2 solutions: $(2,4,1,3)$ and $(3,1,4,2)$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> ⚠️ Students frequently forget the **diagonal attack** constraint, only removing the row value during FC. The diagonal constraint for columns $i, j$ and rows $r_i, r_j$ is $\vert r_i - r_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert$.
> ⚠️ When MRV gives a tie, students must specify a **tie-breaking rule** (Degree Heuristic) or the answer is considered incomplete.
> ⚠️ Forward Checking prunes only the **direct** neighbours of the assigned variable — students who claim FC "sees" deeper conflicts (which is what AC-3 would do) will lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The 10 Commandments" of Basic Search & Constraint Propagation**

- ☐ **CSP Triple:** Always write $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ in definitions. The triple is the **only** formal entry point.
- ☐ **Backtracking = DFS over partial assignments** with immediate pruning on constraint violation.
- ☐ **MRV** = *fail-first*; **LCV** = *succeed-last*. They are **complementary**, not competing.
- ☐ **Forward Checking** is *weaker* than **Arc Consistency** but *cheaper* per step.
- ☐ **AC-3 complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(e \, d^3)$ worst case. AC-3.1/AC-2001: $\mathcal{O}(e \, d^2)$.
- ☐ **AC-3 is sound but not complete** — it prunes impossible values but does NOT find a solution; you still need backtracking.
- ☐ **Arc revision direction matters:** arc $(X_i, X_j)$ means you prune $D_i$, not $D_j$.
- ☐ **Re-queue rule:** When $D_i$ is revised, re-enqueue all arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ for $X_k \neq X_j$. Forgetting this breaks cascading.
- ☐ **Degree Heuristic** is a *tie-breaker* for MRV, not a standalone rule.
- ☐ **In exam derivations**, always show: (1) initial queue, (2) each Revise call, (3) resulting domain, (4) re-queue decisions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BAM-FLA":** **B**acktracking, **A**rc consistency, **M**RV/LCV, **F**orward checking, **L**ocal consistency, **A**C-3. These six pillars cover 95% of KTU Module 4 questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint Programming vs Mathematical Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET634 - HEURISTICS FOR DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Constraint Programming vs Mathematical Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Constraint Programming (CP) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Programming (CP)** is a programming paradigm wherein relations between variables are stated in the form of *constraints*. The goal of CP is to find a feasible solution that satisfies **all** stated constraints, rather than optimizing an explicit objective function.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CP belongs to the family of **declarative problem-solving methods** where the user specifies:

- A set of **decision variables**, $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$
- A set of **domains** $D(x_i)$ defining the admissible values for each variable
- A set of **constraints** $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m\}$ relating the variables

The problem is then to find an assignment $x_i \in D(x_i)$ such that every constraint in $C$ is satisfied.

$$
\text{CP:} \quad \text{find} \; x \; \text{such that} \; C_j(x) \; \forall j \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\}
$$

## 1.2 Mathematical Programming (MP) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Programming (MP)** — also widely called *Mathematical Optimization* — is the process of finding values for decision variables that **optimize** (minimize or maximize) a defined objective function subject to a set of mathematical constraints.

$$
\text{MP:} \quad \min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \; g_i(x) \leq b_i, \; i = 1, 2, \dots, m
$$

Here, $f(x)$ is the **objective function**, and $g_i(x) \leq b_i$ are the **inequality/equality constraints** that bound the feasible region.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Restaurant Chefs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine two chefs preparing a meal.

| Aspect | Constraint Programming (CP) | Mathematical Programming (MP) |
|---|---|---|
| Chef's Goal | "Make a dish that satisfies ALL customer requirements (no allergies, vegetarian, low-spice)." | "Make the CHEAPEST possible dish using available ingredients." |
| Strategy | Filters possibilities based on restrictions until a valid dish is found. | Systematically reduces the cost function while keeping the dish valid. |
| Outcome | A *feasible* solution matching every condition. | An *optimal* solution at minimum cost. |
| Philosophy | **Satisfaction-driven** | **Optimization-driven** |

**CP** asks: *"Does there exist a configuration meeting all rules?"*
**MP** asks: *"Among all configurations meeting the rules, which is the BEST?"*

## 1.4 Historical and Conceptual Roots

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Programming** emerged in the 1960s–70s from the fields of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **logic programming** (notably Prolog, ALICE, CHIP). **Mathematical Programming** arose from operations research (Dantzig's **Simplex Method**, 1947) and is the bedrock of classical optimization theory (Linear Programming, Integer Programming, Nonlinear Programming).

## 1.5 The Unifying View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Despite their differences, both paradigms share a common skeleton: **Variables + Domains + Constraints**. CP and MP are increasingly being **hybridized** into **Constraint Programming and Optimization (CPO)** frameworks, as in IBM CPLEX, Gurobi, and Google OR-Tools.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation Comparison

### 2.1.1 The Standard Mathematical Program

A general **Mathematical Program (MP)** is written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f(x) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_j(x) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x \in \mathcal{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $f(x)$: real-valued **objective function** to optimize
- $g_i(x)$, $h_j(x)$: **inequality** and **equality constraints**
- $x \in \mathcal{X}$: implicit domain restrictions (e.g., integrality, non-negativity)

### 2.1.2 The Standard Constraint Program

A general **Constraint Program (CP)** is written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{find} \quad & x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\
\text{such that} \quad & C_k(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n), \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, q \\
& x_i \in D_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 The Core Dichotomy: "Satisfaction vs Optimization"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most testable distinction in KTU ESE 2024 question papers. The examiner will look for these keywords:
> - CP → **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** → *find any feasible solution*
> - MP → **Constraint Optimization Problem (COP)** → *find the optimal feasible solution*

## 2.3 Why the Two Paradigms Exist — The Limitations of Each

| Limitation of MP | Limitation of CP |
|---|---|
| Cannot naturally model **global constraints** (e.g., `alldifferent`) | No natural mechanism for optimization |
| Struggles with **discrete combinatorial** structure | Lacks mature theory of duality and relaxation |
| Continuous relaxations may yield non-integral solutions | Search may explore huge spaces without guidance |
| Global optimum may be unreachable in NP-hard problems | Heuristic search can be inefficient |

## 2.4 Solving Mechanisms — How They Work

### 2.4.1 Mathematical Programming Solvers

> [!NOTE]
> MP solvers rely on **branch-and-bound**, **branch-and-cut**, **simplex**, **interior-point**, and **Lagrangian relaxation** methods. The convex relaxation provides a **lower bound** used to prune the search tree.

The general **branch-and-bound (B&B)** logic is:

$$
\text{LB}(P) = \min \{ f(x) : x \in \text{relaxed feasible region} \}
$$

If $\text{LB}(P) \geq \text{BestKnown} \Rightarrow$ prune the node (cut-off).

### 2.4.2 Constraint Programming Solvers

> [!NOTE]
> CP solvers rely on **backtracking search** combined with two powerful mechanisms:
> 1. **Propagation (Constraint Propagation / Arc Consistency):** Iteratively reduces the domains of variables.
> 2. **Branching (Labeling):** Splits a variable's domain to explore alternatives.

A common propagation operator is **Arc Consistency (AC-3, AC-7)**:

$$
(x_i \in D_i) \to \text{remove values from } D_i \text{ that cannot be extended to a satisfying assignment of } x_j
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every essential mathematical expression for the topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to protect markdown table syntax.

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| MP Feasible Region | $\mathcal{F} = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(x) \leq 0, h_j(x) = 0\}$ | Set of all points satisfying MP constraints |
| CP Domain Set | $D = D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$ | Cartesian product of variable domains |
| CP Constraint Predicate | $C_k : D_{k_1} \times D_{k_2} \times \dots \to \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$ | Maps a tuple of values to a Boolean verdict |
| MP Optimality Condition (KKT) | $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x^*) = 0, \; \lambda_i \geq 0, \; \lambda_i g_i(x^*) = 0$ | Necessary for local optimum in smooth problems |
| CP Arc Consistency | $D_i \leftarrow D_i \setminus \{v \mid \not\exists w \in D_j : C(v, w) = \text{true}\}$ | Domain filtering rule |
| MP Lagrangian Bound | $L(\lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(x) + \sum \mu_j h_j(x)$ | Dual bound construction |
| CP `alldifferent` | $\text{alldifferent}(x_1, \dots, x_n) \equiv \bigwedge_{i \neq j} (x_i \neq x_j)$ | Global constraint enforcing uniqueness |
| Branching (B&B) | $\text{Node splits} : x_i \leq \lfloor x_i^* \rfloor \;\vert\; x_i \geq \lceil x_i^* \rceil$ | Divides continuous fractional solution |
| Big-M Reformulation | $x \in [0, M], \; y \in \{0,1\}, \; x \leq M y$ | Linearizes indicator variables |
| CP Search Complexity | $O(\prod_i \vert D_i \vert)$ | Worst-case node explosion |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MP Applications:** Production planning, network flow, portfolio optimization, optimal power flow in power systems, structural design minimization.
> **CP Applications:** Scheduling (workforce rosters, exam timetabling), configuration (vehicle routing), planning (production lines), bioinformatics (sequence alignment).
> **Hybrid (CPO) Applications:** Industrial scheduling with cost minimization, real-time logistics, supply chain design.

## 2.7 Strengths and Weaknesses Summary

| Dimension | Mathematical Programming | Constraint Programming |
|---|---|---|
| Strength | **Optimality** is provable via duality | Handles **discrete/global** constraints elegantly |
| Strength | Mature commercial solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi) | Rich modeling language for complex rules |
| Weakness | Weak on irregular discrete structures | No objective-based pruning by default |
| Weakness | Relaxation may not be tight | Pure satisfaction — no notion of "best" |
| Output | **Optimal** feasible solution | **Any** feasible solution |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From CP Satisfaction to MP Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"How can a CP problem be reformulated as an MP problem?"* The following derivation shows the **standard transformation**.

### Step 1 — Start with a Constraint Satisfaction Problem

Consider a CP with binary variables $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and a feasibility predicate $C(x)$.

$$
\text{Find } x \in \{0, 1\}^n \text{ such that } C(x) = \text{true}
$$

### Step 2 — Introduce an Indicator Objective

To make a CP into an MP, we attach a **dummy objective** (or a meaningful cost):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & C(x) = \text{true} \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}
$$

If $c_i = 0 \; \forall i$, this becomes a pure **feasibility MP**, which is computationally equivalent to a CP.

### Step 3 — Linearize Boolean Predicates

Each Boolean clause $(x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3)$ is linearized using the **standard CNF-to-IP transformation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) &\equiv (x_1 + (1 - x_2) + x_3 \geq 1) \\
&\equiv x_1 - x_2 + x_3 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Mixed-Integer Program

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^\top x \\
\text{subject to} \quad & A x \geq b \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a CP contains only linear constraints, it can ALWAYS be rewritten as an Integer Program. Conversely, an IP with an objective can be reduced to CP by ignoring the objective. Hence **CP $\subseteq$ IP in modeling power for linear problems**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Branch-and-Bound Lower Bound from LP Relaxation

This is the most important MP derivation for the exam.

### Setup

Let $P$ be a Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^\top x \\
\text{subject to} \quad & A x \leq b \\
& x \geq 0, \quad x_i \in \mathbb{Z} \; \forall i \in I
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Drop Integrality

Drop the requirement $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$, keeping only $x \geq 0$. The resulting problem is a **Linear Program (LP)**, whose optimum $z_{LP}$ is always a valid **lower bound**:

$$
z_{LP} \leq z_{MILP}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve the LP

Suppose the LP yields a solution with some $x_k$ fractional, e.g., $x_k = 3.7$.

### Step 3 — Branch

Create two sub-problems $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: x_k \leq 3 \\
P_2 &: x_k \geq 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Bound and Prune

For each sub-problem, recompute $z_{LP}$. If $z_{LP}(P_j) \geq z_{\text{best}}$ (current best integer solution), **prune** node $P_j$.

### Step 5 — Iterate

Continue recursively until all nodes are either **fathomed** (no better than current best), **pruned** (infeasible), or **integer** (update best).

> [!NOTE]
> This **branch-and-bound** algorithm is the heart of every commercial MILP solver.

## 3.3 Derivation: CP Arc Consistency (AC-3 Algorithm)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board expects students to know how CP propagates constraints to shrink domains.

### Step 1 — Initialize Queue

Place all arcs $(x_i, x_j)$ in a queue $Q$.

### Step 2 — Process Each Arc

For arc $(x_i, x_j)$, remove from $D_i$ any value $v$ such that no value $w \in D_j$ satisfies the binary constraint $C_{ij}(v, w)$:

$$
D_i \leftarrow D_i \setminus \{v \in D_i \mid \forall w \in D_j : C_{ij}(v, w) = \text{false}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Revise Downstream

If $D_i$ was changed, re-enqueue all arcs $(x_k, x_i)$ for all $k \neq j$.

### Step 4 — Termination

When $Q$ is empty, the constraint network is **arc consistent** — and a much smaller search space is passed to the labeling phase.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use CP vs MP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a high-yield KTU exam item. Memorize the criteria.

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Paradigm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Pure integer / discrete variables | **CP** (or IP) | Discrete search tree |
| Global constraints (`alldifferent`, `cumulative`) | **CP** | Native support |
| Continuous variables, smooth objective | **MP** | Gradient-based methods |
| Requires **proven optimality** | **MP** | Duality theory |
| Complex logical / rule-based conditions | **CP** | Declarative modeling |
| Linear / convex objective with linear constraints | **MP** | LP/MILP mature solvers |
| Hybrid: scheduling + cost minimization | **CPO (Hybrid)** | Combines both strengths |
| Satisfiability (SAT) style | **CP** | Boolean domain |
| Large-scale real-time logistics | **CPO** | Speed and quality balance |

## 3.5 The Hybrid CPO Framework

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f(x) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & C_k(x) \in \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}, \; k = 1, \dots, q \\
& g_i(x) \leq 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m \\
& x_i \in D_i, \; i = 1, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The objective function $f(x)$ is optimized, while complex logical constraints $C_k$ are propagated using CP mechanisms, and continuous linear constraints are handled by MP solvers. The two engines exchange **bounds** and **feasible solutions** during search.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of the CP Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition] --> B[Variable & Domain Setup]
    B --> C[Constraint Posting]
    C --> D[Constraint Propagation AC-3]
    D --> E{Domain Empty?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Backtrack]
    E -- No --> G{All Variables Assigned?}
    G -- No --> H[Branching / Labeling]
    H --> D
    G -- Yes --> I[Feasible Solution Found]
    F --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of the MP Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mathematical Program] --> B[Formulation Check]
    B --> C[LP / Relaxation Solver]
    C --> D[Bound Obtained z_LB]
    D --> E{Bound > Best Integer?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Prune Node]
    E -- No --> G[Integrality Check]
    G -- Integer --> H[Update Best Solution]
    G -- Fractional --> I[Branching on x_k]
    I --> J[Create Sub Problems]
    J --> C
```

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CP["CP Pipeline"]
        CP1[Variables] --> CP2[Domains]
        CP2 --> CP3[Constraints]
        CP3 --> CP4[Propagation]
        CP4 --> CP5[Backtracking Search]
        CP5 --> CP6[Feasible Solution]
    end
    subgraph MP["MP Pipeline"]
        MP1[Decision Variables] --> MP2[Objective Function]
        MP2 --> MP3[Constraints]
        MP3 --> MP4[Relaxation]
        MP4 --> MP5[Branch and Bound]
        MP5 --> MP6[Optimal Solution]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing CP or MP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Optimization Problem] --> Q1{Is there an explicit objective?}
    Q1 -- No --> CP1[Use CP]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Discrete / combinatorial variables?}
    Q2 -- Yes, complex --> Q3{Global or logical constraints?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Hybrid1[Use Hybrid CPO]
    Q3 -- No --> MP1[Use MP with MILP]
    Q2 -- Continuous --> MP2[Use MP with LP / NLP]
    Q1 -- Yes but only feasible --> CP2[Use CP with dummy objective]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Mapping: CP and MP as Subsets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Universe[General Decision Making] --> Sat[Constraint Satisfaction]
    Universe --> Opt[Constraint Optimization]
    Sat --> CP[Constraint Programming]
    Opt --> MP[Mathematical Programming]
    CP --> Hybrid[CPO Hybrid Framework]
    MP --> Hybrid
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above follow the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** convention since this is a conceptual topic. They are intended to show **information flow** between modules rather than physical structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Constraint Programming. How does it differ from Mathematical Programming? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Constraint Programming (CP)** is a declarative programming paradigm used to find values for decision variables from a given domain such that all specified constraints are satisfied. It is primarily concerned with **feasibility**, not optimality.

**Mathematical Programming (MP)**, on the other hand, is the mathematical optimization of an objective function subject to constraints. It seeks the **best** (optimal) feasible solution.

| Aspect | CP | MP |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Satisfy all constraints | Optimize the objective |
| Output | A feasible solution | An optimal solution |
| Search Mechanism | Constraint propagation + backtracking | Branch-and-bound, simplex, interior point |

---

### Question 2: What is the role of *constraint propagation* in CP? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Constraint propagation** (also called **domain filtering** or **arc consistency enforcement**) is a mechanism in CP solvers that systematically **reduces the domain of variables** by removing values that cannot participate in any solution. It:

1. **Prunes the search space** before branching, drastically reducing computation.
2. **Detects infeasibility early** — if any domain becomes empty, the current branch is dead.
3. **Interacts with backtracking** — when a dead-end occurs, the algorithm backtracks to a previous branching point.

The most widely used algorithm is **AC-3**, which iteratively revises variable domains until a fixed point is reached.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme regulations, every Module in a 14-mark question offers an **internal choice** between two full-length alternatives. Two completely independent model questions follow.

### Question A: Constraint Programming vs Mathematical Programming — A Comprehensive Comparison `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the formulation of both CP and MP with a worked example. Show how a scheduling CP can be converted into an MP.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CP Formulation (Nurse Scheduling Example)**

Assign 4 nurses (A, B, C, D) to 3 shifts (Day, Evening, Night) such that:
- Each nurse works exactly one shift.
- Each shift has at least one nurse.
- Nurse A cannot work the night shift.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variables:} \quad & x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad i \in \{A,B,C,D\}, \; j \in \{1,2,3\} \\
\text{Constraints:} \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{3} x_{ij} = 1 \; \forall i \quad \text{(each nurse one shift)} \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{4} x_{ij} \geq 1 \; \forall j \quad \text{(each shift covered)} \\
& x_{A,3} = 0 \quad \text{(A cannot do night)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MP by Adding Objective**

To minimize the total wage cost (e.g., $c_{ij}$ = cost of nurse $i$ in shift $j$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{4} \sum_{j=1}^{3} c_{ij} \, x_{ij} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{3} x_{ij} = 1 \; \forall i \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{4} x_{ij} \geq 1 \; \forall j \\
& x_{A,3} = 0 \\
& x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

- The CP version simply *asks* for any feasible assignment.
- The MP version *adds cost* and seeks the cheapest assignment.
- This shows the structural equivalence of CP and IP — the difference is whether an objective is present.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Discuss the strengths, weaknesses, and the hybridization (CPO) of CP and MP.

**Model Solution:**

**Strengths of MP:**
- Provides a **provably optimal** solution via duality theory.
- Mature solvers handle **millions of variables** efficiently.
- **LP relaxation** gives excellent lower bounds for branch-and-bound.

**Weaknesses of MP:**
- Cannot natively express **global constraints** like `alldifferent`.
- Continuous relaxations may be **loose**, leading to weak bounds.
- Modeling language is **mathematical**, not declarative.

**Strengths of CP:**
- **Declarative modeling** of complex logical rules.
- **Global constraints** are first-class citizens.
- Excellent for **scheduling, configuration, planning**.

**Weaknesses of CP:**
- No native notion of optimization.
- Search can be **blind** without an objective-driven heuristic.
- No strong **bounding theory**.

**Hybridization — Constraint Programming and Optimization (CPO):**
Modern frameworks (IBM CP Optimizer, Gurobi + CP-SAT) fuse both paradigms:
- CP propagation **shrinks the discrete search space**.
- MP relaxation **provides continuous bounds**.
- The two engines **exchange solutions and cuts** during search.

This gives the **best of both worlds**: the modeling power of CP and the optimality proof of MP.

---

### Question B: Branch-and-Bound in MP and Constraint Propagation in CP `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Illustrate the Branch-and-Bound algorithm for the following Integer Program. Show all nodes, bounds, and pruning steps.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & z = -5 x_1 - 4 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 6 x_1 + 4 x_2 \leq 24 \\
& x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \text{ and integer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Solve the LP Relaxation (Node 0)**

Drop the integer restriction. The LP optimum is $x_1 = 3, x_2 = 1.5$, $z = -21$.

**Step 2 — Branch on $x_2$**

- **Node 1:** Add $x_2 \leq 1$. LP optimum: $x_1 = 3, x_2 = 1$, $z = -19$. **Integer feasible! Set best = -19.**
- **Node 2:** Add $x_2 \geq 2$. LP optimum: $x_1 = 1.33, x_2 = 2$, $z = -14.67$.

**Step 3 — Branch on $x_1$ in Node 2**

- **Node 3:** $x_1 \leq 1$. LP optimum: $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 2$, $z = -13$. **Integer feasible! Worse than -19. Prune.**
- **Node 4:** $x_1 \geq 2$. From constraint 2, $2 + 2x_2 \leq 6 \Rightarrow x_2 \leq 2$. Then $6(2) + 4(2) = 20 \leq 24$ ✓. LP optimum: $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 2$, $z = -18$. **Integer feasible! Better than -19? No. Discard improvement.**

**Step 4 — Final Result**

The **optimal integer solution** is $x_1 = 3, x_2 = 1$ with $z = -19$.

| Node | Constraints | LP Soln $(x_1, x_2)$ | LP Bound | Integer? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | none | (3, 1.5) | -21 | No | Branch on $x_2$ |
| 1 | $x_2 \leq 1$ | (3, 1) | -19 | Yes | Update best = -19 |
| 2 | $x_2 \geq 2$ | (1.33, 2) | -14.67 | No | Branch on $x_1$ |
| 3 | $x_1 \leq 1, x_2 \geq 2$ | (1, 2) | -13 | Yes | Discard (worse) |
| 4 | $x_1 \geq 2, x_2 \geq 2$ | (2, 2) | -18 | Yes | Discard (worse) |

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Explain Arc Consistency (AC-3) propagation in CP using the following example.

**Variables:** $x, y, z \in \{1, 2, 3\}$
**Constraints:** $x < y$, $y < z$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial Domains**

$D_x = D_y = D_z = \{1, 2, 3\}$

**Step 2 — Apply $x < y$**

For each $x \in D_x$, check if there exists $y \in D_y$ with $x < y$.

- $x = 1$: need $y \in \{2, 3\}$ ✓
- $x = 2$: need $y \in \{3\}$ ✓
- $x = 3$: no $y > 3$ exists ✗ — remove 3

$D_x = \{1, 2\}, D_y = \{1, 2, 3\}$

**Step 3 — Apply $y < z$**

For each $y \in D_y$:

- $y = 3$: need $z > 3$ — none ✗ — remove 3

$D_y = \{1, 2\}$

**Step 4 — Re-check $x < y$**

- $x = 2$: need $y > 2$ — none in $\{1, 2\}$ ✗ — remove 2

$D_x = \{1\}, D_y = \{2\}$

**Step 5 — Re-check $y < z$**

- $y = 2$: need $z > 2$ — $z = 3$ ✓

$D_z = \{3\}$

**Step 6 — Fixed Point Reached**

Final domains: $D_x = \{1\}, D_y = \{2\}, D_z = \{3\}$.

**Solution:** $x = 1, y = 2, z = 3$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **AC-3 algorithm** reduced three 3-element domains to single values through systematic propagation, demonstrating how CP solvers efficiently shrink the search space before branching.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing "feasible" with "optimal"** — CP returns feasible, MP returns optimal. Examiners *will* deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Omitting the objective function** in MP formulation — a MP without an objective is just a CP. Always state the objective explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong domain specification** — saying "$x \in \mathbb{R}$" for a CP variable instead of a finite domain $\{1, 2, 3\}$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping propagation step** in CP examples — show how the domain shrinks. A bare backtracking diagram is incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing up B&B with B&C** — Branch-and-Bound uses LP relaxation; Branch-and-Cut adds cutting planes. Don't confuse.
> 6. **Forgetting the hybrid CPO** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes modern heuristics, so mentioning CPO scores bonus points.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Day of Exam:**

- **CP** is about *satisfying constraints*; **MP** is about *optimizing an objective*. *(Golden Rule)*
- A **CP** without an objective is a **CSP**; with an objective it is a **COP** / **MILP**.
- MP solvers use **LP relaxation** + **branch-and-bound** + **cutting planes**.
- CP solvers use **constraint propagation** + **backtracking search** + **labeling**.
- The `alldifferent` global constraint is **native to CP**, awkward in MP.
- A CP with linear constraints can be **reformulated as an MILP**; the reverse is not always true.
- **KKT conditions** govern optimality in continuous MP; **AC-3** governs propagation in CP.
- **Hybridization** (CPO) combines the modeling power of CP and the optimality guarantees of MP.
- **Duality** is a strength unique to MP; CP has no robust duality theory.
- Real-world tools: **CPLEX, Gurobi, OR-Tools, CP Optimizer** support both paradigms.
- Always state **domains explicitly** for CP variables.
- Always state **objective direction** (minimize/maximize) for MP.
- CP complexity worst case is $O(\prod_i \vert D_i \vert)$; MP complexity depends on problem class.
- For **combinatorial problems with global constraints** → CP.
- For **continuous or smooth optimization** → MP.
- For **mixed discrete-continuous with cost** → CPO.
- Mention the **gap between CP and MP disappears** when an objective is added/removed.
- Be ready to draw the **branch-and-bound tree** and the **AC-3 propagation steps**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic: Constraint Programming vs Mathematical Programming — Module 4**
> *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) regulations for PEIET634 — Heuristics for Decision Making.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
